title: Attention at the Theoretical Minimum: A Mathematics of Arrays Framework for Memory-Optimal Transformer Kernels
summary:
The attention mechanism is the dominant computational bottleneck in modern transformer-based AI. Its standard implementation incurs quadratic memory traffic in the sequence length~$n$, and DRAM accesses cost 100--1000$\times$ more energy than arithmetic operations on contemporary hardware, so any analysis focused solely on FLOP counts fundamentally mischaracterises the bottleneck.
  We present a Mathematics of Arrays (MoA) reformulation of scaled dot-product attention and its numerically stable softmax, deriving a Denotational Normal Form (DNF) that eliminates all intermediate arrays -- including the implicit transposed-key buffer and every softmax temporary -- by algebraic construction rather than empirical tuning. The DNF achieves $O(n_{dk} + n{_{dv}})$ data movement versus $O(n^2 + n_{dk} + n_{dv})$ for the standard implementation, where $n$ is the sequence length, $dk$ is the key dimensionality and $dv$ the value dimensionality, and is verified numerically against PyTorch at full double-precision floating-point on concrete inputs.
  Unlike hardware-specific accelerators or empirical tiling schemes such as FlashAttention, MoA simultaneously provides array fusion, shape-transformation correctness, and predictive cost models from a single algebraic framework. Memory minimality is a theorem established before any code is written. A predictive performance model projects $2$--$100\times$ speedup and $2$--$50\times$ energy reduction, with the advantage widening at exascale. The derivation establishes a formally verified pipeline from Python specification through (ONF) Operational Normal Form, and dimension-lifted hardware mapping, providing performance-portable AI kernels of direct relevance to DARPA edge-deployment and DOE exascale priorities.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces a Mathematics of Arrays (MoA) reformulation for attention mechanisms in transformers, achieving significant reductions in data movement and energy use compared to standard implementations.
AI.motivation: Transformer-based AI models face significant memory traffic and energy costs due to the quadratic memory scaling of the attention mechanism. Existing analyses focusing solely on FLOPs overlook the true computational bottleneck.
AI.method: The authors use a Denotational Normal Form (DNF) derived via Mathematics of Arrays (MoA) to reformulate scaled dot-product attention, eliminating intermediate arrays and reducing data movement to linear scaling.
AI.result: The proposed method achieves $O(n_{dk} + n{_{dv}})$ data movement, verified numerically to match PyTorch implementations with high precision. Predictive models suggest $2$–$100\times$ speedup and $2$–$50\times$ energy savings.
AI.conclusion: The MoA framework offers a generalizable, hardware-efficient attention mechanism, providing array fusion, correctness guarantees, and performance predictability that align with major computational priorities like edge deployment and exascale computing.

title: An Empirical Comparison of General Context-Free Parsers
summary:
Parsing underpins a vast range of software engineering tasks, from compilers and static analyzers to language servers and fuzz testing tools. Yet most parsers deployed in practice are deterministic (LL or LR), forcing developers not only to contort their grammars to fit the parser, but to simplify the very languages they design sacrificing expressiveness for the sake of parseability. General context-free parsers eliminate this constraint. Yet, despite decades of algorithmic development, no rigorous head-to-head comparison exists across the major families of parsing algorithms.
  We present the first unified, controlled benchmark of six generalized parsing algorithms: CYK, Valiant, Earley, GLL, RNGLR, and BRNGLR, plus deterministic LL(1) and LR(1) baselines, all implemented in Rust with shared data structures and parse-tree extraction, and evaluated across 22 grammars ranging from simple expressions to full C++ and Java. Our results show that the cost of generality is lower than widely assumed. On deterministic grammars, the GLR family incurs only a 3x median slowdown over LR(1), with a narrow and predictable variance. GLR is the clear performance winner among generalized parsers and a practical default choice for software engineering tools.

AI.tldr: This paper conducts a unified benchmark of 6 generalized parsing algorithms alongside deterministic LL(1) and LR(1) baselines to evaluate their performance and practicality.
AI.motivation: To address the constraints imposed by deterministic parsers on language expressiveness and to provide a rigorous comparison of generalized parsing algorithms for real-world software engineering applications.
AI.method: Implemented six generalized parsers (CYK, Valiant, Earley, GLL, RNGLR, BRNGLR) and deterministic baselines in Rust, using shared data structures. Benchmarked them on 22 grammars including complex languages like C++ and Java.
AI.result: GLR parsers showed only a 3x median slowdown compared to LR(1) on deterministic grammars, with narrow variance. GLR emerged as the most performant among generalized parsers.
AI.conclusion: Generalized parsers are practical for software engineering, with GLR being an effective default choice due to its balance of generality and performance.

title: Fairness-Aware and Latency-Controllable Scheduling for Chunked-Prefill LLM Serving
summary: As large language models (LLMs) are increasingly deployed with highly heterogeneous workloads, chunked-prefill execution has emerged as a mainstream serving architecture. Balancing scheduling fairness and latency stability in such environments is critical; otherwise, severe head-of-line blocking and request starvation will degrade user experience. However, existing systems rely on rigid First-Come, First-Served (FCFS) policies and static token budgets, leading to fairness degradation and unpredictable latency jitter. To address these issues, we propose a fairness-aware and latency-controllable scheduling framework for chunked-prefill LLM engines. Specifically, we design a lightweight aging-based scheduling policy that dynamically calculates priorities using accumulated waiting time and remaining prefill work. Furthermore, we develop Latency-Prediction-Based Request Scheduling (LPRS) and Active Prefill Control (APC) to replace static budgets with target-time constraints and actively regulate prefill concurrency. We evaluated our scheduling framework on NVIDIA GPUs and Ascend accelerators using real-world workloads. Results show the aging policy reduces mean end-to-end latency by over 10\% compared to FCFS. Moreover, LPRS and APC significantly reduce P99 tail latency and suppress prefill fragmentation, confirming that the structural prefill control and the temporal latency constraints are fundamentally complementary. All codes have been released in Github.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes a new scheduling framework for chunked-prefill LLM engines to improve fairness and latency stability, addressing the issues with existing first-come, first-served (FCFS) systems.
AI.motivation: Existing LLM scheduling systems suffer from fairness degradation and unpredictable latency due to rigid FCFS policies and static token budgets, leading to poor user experience.
AI.method: The authors introduce a lightweight aging-based scheduling policy and develop Latency-Prediction-Based Request Scheduling (LPRS) along with Active Prefill Control (APC) to dynamically manage scheduling priorities and prefill concurrency.
AI.result: The framework demonstrates over 10% improvement in mean end-to-end latency, significant reduction in tail latency (P99), and minimized prefill fragmentation across tested hardware platforms.
AI.conclusion: The proposed scheduling framework effectively balances fairness and latency in chunked-prefill LLM systems, making it a practical alternative to traditional FCFS policies for real-world GPU and accelerator workloads.

title: Correlation Is Not Enough: Embedding Human Metadata for Individual Causal Discovery
summary:
Ask a pretrained biomedical language model whether "cortisol 28 ug/dL" and "stock-market volatility" are related, and it returns a cosine similarity of 0.83 on a scale where 1.0 means identical. The two share no mechanism. This is not a corner case: every off-the-shelf biomedical encoder we tested (BioBERT, PubMedBERT, BioM-ELECTRA) scores unrelated cross-domain pairs between 0.76 and 0.92 when the answer should be near zero. Accuracy on cross-domain discrimination is 0%.
  Retrieval systems survive this, because a language model downstream filters the noise. A Large Behavioural Model (LBM), a foundation model whose subject is a person rather than a sentence, does not: it reasons over a graph of a user's life and treats embedding proximity as evidence that two events are causally linked. False proximity writes a false causal edge, and everything downstream inherits the error. Here, embedding geometry is not a tuning knob; it is correctness.
  We report the fix. A contrastive pass over 72,034 pairs raises PubMedBERT BIOSSES correlation from 0.633 to 0.828 and within-vs-across-domain separation from 1.05x to 1.63x. A second pass, BODHI, mines hard negatives from edges absent in a biomedical knowledge graph and lifts separation to 2.30x and the discrimination gap to +0.392, at a 4.5% BIOSSES cost. On an Intel Xeon 6737P with AMX, OpenVINO cuts single-query latency from 1367 ms to 10 ms (133x) and reaches 555 sentences/sec. One finding contradicts standard advice: FP16 beats INT8 on this silicon at every serving batch size, and we explain why. The same model on a no-AMX Ice Lake instance runs 13-27x slower. We release the benchmark suite, training corpora, the BODHI generator, and the OpenVINO scripts.

AI.tldr: This paper analyzes the performance of biomedical language models in assessing cross-domain relationships, identifies significant gaps, proposes a fix with the BODHI model, and improves accuracy and operational efficiency.
AI.motivation: To address the current failure of pretrained biomedical language models in accurately discriminating unrelated cross-domain pairs, which has implications for downstream applications like causality reasoning in large behavioral models.
AI.method: The paper introduces a two-pass approach involving contrastive training and the use of hard negatives mined from a biomedical knowledge graph. Benchmarking and optimization, including OpenVINO acceleration, are used to evaluate and enhance performance.
AI.result: The proposed methods significantly improve PubMedBERT's performance in domain separation and discrimination, achieving a separation lift to 2.30x with a minor BIOSSES trade-off. System latency improves drastically with OpenVINO acceleration, reducing query time by 133x.
AI.conclusion: False proximity in embeddings can cause severe downstream errors, especially in causality reasoning systems. Effective contrastive training and operational optimizations can mitigate these issues, improving both accuracy and efficiency.

title: Information-Geometric Optimization on Spheres
summary: We consider the black-box optimization problem on a sphere. Two information-geometric optimization flows (IGO flows) are designed with rigorous calculation of natural search gradients based on hyperbolic (information) geometry of Poincar\' e and Bergman balls. We demonstrate that ensembles of generalized Kuramoto oscillators on spheres compute natural search gradients and realize IGO algorithms on both manifolds. The relationship between natural gradient policies in Bergman balls and quantum decision making is pointed out.

AI.tldr: The paper develops optimization flows using hyperbolic geometry, showing that Kuramoto oscillators on spheres compute natural gradients and connecting these mechanisms to quantum decision-making.
AI.motivation: To enhance black-box optimization methods using geometric and physical interpretations.
AI.method: Two IGO flows are constructed based on hyperbolic geometry (Poincaré and Bergman balls), and generalized Kuramoto oscillators on spheres are employed to compute natural gradients.
AI.result: Natural gradients were computed and linked to ensemble dynamics, demonstrating applicability of the approach to optimization problems.
AI.conclusion: The study establishes a connection between optimization on geometrically complex structures and quantum-inspired decision-making approaches.

title: Programming Domain-Specific FPGA Hardblocks from HLS: An RTL Blackbox Approach
summary: Domain-specific Field Programmable Gate Array (FPGA) architectures increasingly integrate specialized hardblocks, such as Tensor Slices, to accelerate artificial intelligence and machine learning workloads. Despite their efficiency benefits, these architectures remain difficult to program because designers typically rely on manual Register-Transfer Level (RTL) integration to access these hardblocks. This paper presents a compiler-agnostic methodology that enables high-level synthesis (HLS) tools to target custom FPGA hardblocks directly from C/C++ code. Architectural hardblocks are exposed as schedulable C-level operators using an RTL blackbox abstraction with explicit latency and initiation-interval contracts, allowing the HLS scheduler to optimize around specialized hardware without manual RTL orchestration. Unlike traditional uses of HLS blackboxes for external IP integration, our approach treats blackboxes as architectural abstractions, enabling scalable composition of C-level operators that target custom FPGA hardblocks without compiler modification. We evaluate the proposed flow using a Tensor Slice-based FPGA architecture with AMD Vitis HLS and the Verilog-to-Routing (VTR) toolchain. Across multiple matrix sizes, designs generated using the proposed C-Blackbox flow achieve lower area-delay product than behavioral HLS baselines while providing substantially higher productivity-adjusted efficiency than handwritten RTL implementations. These results demonstrate that domain-specific FPGA architectures can be made accessible through HLS while maintaining competitive hardware efficiency.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces a novel methodology to simplify programming domain-specific FPGA hardblocks using high-level synthesis (HLS) tools, directly from C/C++ code.
AI.motivation: Programming FPGA hardblocks, such as Tensor Slices, used to accelerate AI/ML workloads is challenging because it typically requires manual and labor-intensive RTL integration.
AI.method: The authors propose a compiler-agnostic approach that exposes FPGA hardblocks as schedulable C-level operators using an RTL blackbox abstraction, which has explicit latency and initiation-intervals.
AI.result: The C-Blackbox flow was evaluated on Tensor Slice-based FPGA architectures, showing improved area-delay product and better productivity-adjusted efficiency compared to RTL and behavioral HLS baselines.
AI.conclusion: The methodology enables efficient and accessible usage of domain-specific FPGA architectures using HLS tools, achieving competitive performance metrics compared to manual RTL designs.

title: Accelerating Birkhoff Projection for Manifold-Constrained Hyper-Connections
summary:
Manifold-constrained hyper-connections (mHCs) have recently been proposed as a principled extension of hyper-connections, where the residual mixing matrices are constrained to be doubly stochastic via projection onto the Birkhoff polytope. In practical mHC implementations, this constraint is enforced by Sinkhorn-Knopp iterations, and the backward pass relies on unrolling the iterative solver. This design introduces substantial computation and memory overhead, and may also yield inaccurate projections when the algorithm converges slowly on challenging inputs, undermining the intended norm-control and stability guarantees of mHCs.
  In this work, we focus on the practically important 4x4 Birkhoff projection setting and develop an end-to-end acceleration framework. By leveraging the dual formulation, we reduce the problem to a three-dimensional unconstrained convex problem and solve it with Newton's method, achieving fast convergence and high accuracy. For the backward pass, we replace the unrolled differentiation with implicit differentiation, yielding exact gradients without storing intermediate states. To exploit massive parallelism, we design a warp-level CUDA kernel that uses only register-level primitives, avoiding global and shared memory I/O.
  Extensive experiments against representative open-source baselines demonstrate that the proposed solver yields substantially more reliable doubly stochastic projections -- especially when the input magnitude is large -- and achieves significant end-to-end speedups (including the backward pass), reaching over 20x acceleration at large batch sizes while maintaining orders of magnitude smaller marginal errors.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces a fast and accurate acceleration framework for 4x4 Birkhoff projections in mHCs, improving computation speed and projection reliability.
AI.motivation: The authors address inefficiencies in the standard manifold-constrained hyper-connections (mHCs), particularly the computational and memory overhead caused by existing methods using Sinkhorn-Knopp-based projections.
AI.method: They reformulate the 4x4 Birkhoff projection as a dual convex problem with a three-dimensional space, solve it with Newton's method for efficiency, and replace unrolled differentiation with implicit differentiation for gradient accuracy. A warp-level CUDA kernel is designed for parallelism.
AI.result: The proposed solver achieves over 20x acceleration in large-batch scenarios while maintaining significantly smaller projection errors, thereby enhancing projection reliability.
AI.conclusion: The acceleration framework reduces computational overhead and ensures accurate doubly stochastic projections, making it practical for mHCs in challenging settings.

title: AgentCompile: An LLM-Guided Compiler for Direct CUDA Inference
summary: Transformer inference increasingly depends on specialized compiler and runtime support, but real model graphs still require semantic decisions about which regions are worth specializing and which CUDA implementation families are plausible. We present AgentCompile, an LLM-guided CUDA inference compiler that uses LLM outputs only as advisory search metadata. Given compiler-derived region summaries and bounded candidate spaces, the LLM proposes semantic labels, candidate priorities, parameter hints, and risk annotations; the compiler materializes CUDA candidates through templates, checks interface and hardware constraints, validates candidates empirically, selects implementations by measured latency, and falls back when specialization is unsupported or unprofitable. In end-to-end autoregressive generation, AgentCompile averages 5.66x, 4.05x, and 4.26x speedup over PyTorch eager on Qwen3-1.7B, Qwen3-4B, and Llama-3.2-1B-Instruct, respectively, across five representative workloads. We will open-source the project.

AI.tldr: AgentCompile is a CUDA inference compiler that uses LLM suggestions to optimize transformer model performance, with significant speedups over PyTorch eager execution.
AI.motivation: To address the need for optimizing transformer model inference through better compiler and runtime support that adapts to real model graph properties and benefits from LLM insights.
AI.method: AgentCompile leverages LLM-guided metadata for decisions and uses compiler-generated CUDA templates to validate and empirically test implementations. It dynamically selects specialized implementations based on latency while offering fallbacks for unsupported cases.
AI.result: AgentCompile achieves significant speedups in autoregressive generation, showing 5.66x, 4.05x, and 4.26x improvements on models Qwen3-1.7B, Qwen3-4B, and Llama-3.2-1B-Instruct, respectively, over PyTorch eager execution in five workloads.
AI.conclusion: AgentCompile demonstrates the feasibility and efficiency of LLM-informed CUDA specialization, making transformer inference faster and adaptable. The project will be open-sourced for broader community benefit.

title: This is how the Neocortex Learns
summary: A sufficient account of how the neocortex learns must meet three criteria: 1. Computationally, it must approximate a powerful, general-purpose learning algorithm known to scale to human-level intelligence; 2. Algorithmically, it must be implementable using known, well-established neural circuits within the neocortex and associated brain structures; 3. Implementationally, there must be a detailed account for how all of the algorithmic mechanisms actually function at a neurochemical level. At present, there is only one framework that meets all of these criteria: error-driven predictive learning via temporal derivatives, driven by corticothalamic circuits, based on competitive kinase synaptic plasticity induction mechanisms. This has been implemented in the Axon neural simulation framework using spiking neurons, and demonstrated to learn across a wide range of challenging cognitively motivated tasks.

AI.tldr: The paper discusses a framework for understanding how the neocortex learns, based on error-driven predictive learning via corticothalamic circuits, demonstrated with spiking neurons in the Axon framework.
AI.motivation: To establish a comprehensive model that explains how the neocortex learns, fulfilling computational, algorithmic, and implementational criteria.
AI.method: The use of a framework based on error-driven predictive learning through corticothalamic circuits, leveraging competitive kinase synaptic plasticity mechanisms, and implementing it in the Axon neural simulation framework.
AI.result: The framework has been successfully demonstrated to learn across various cognitively challenging tasks using spiking neurons.
AI.conclusion: Error-driven predictive learning driven by corticothalamic circuits represents a plausible and detailed explanation for neocortical learning, supported both biologically and computationally.

title: Systematic LLM Translation of Legacy Scientific Code to Differentiable Frameworks: Application to a Land Surface Model
summary: Differentiable programming offers transformative capabilities for scientific modeling, enabling gradient-based parameter estimation, sensitivity analysis, and data assimilation. Yet, migrating legacy codebases into differentiable frameworks remains a challenge. We present a five-phase LLM-based agentic pipeline that translates legacy Fortran into JAX: static dependency analysis determines module translation order from the full call graph; iterative compile-repair loops correct errors autonomously; and a Fortran reference oracle enforces numerical parity at the module level before integration and gradient verification. We instantiate and evaluate the pipeline on CLM-ml-v2, a 19,000-line Fortran land surface model, and analyze agent behavior across 73 module translation tasks. The resulting differentiable model computes the complete Jacobian in a single backward pass, recovers physical parameters in eight times fewer steps than gradient-free optimization, and achieves a 24 times wall-clock speedup over sequential Fortran at ensemble size N=2,048. Both the translated model and pipeline infrastructure are released as a reusable framework for differentiating other Earth system model components.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces a pipeline to translate legacy Fortran code into JAX for differentiable programming, with successful application to a land surface model.
AI.motivation: Migrating scientific legacy codebases to modern differentiable programming frameworks is challenging but vital for enhanced modeling capabilities.
AI.method: The paper presents a five-phase pipeline, involving static dependency analysis, iterative error correction, and numerical parity enforcement, applied to a specific Fortran land surface model.
AI.result: The translated model significantly improves performance, enabling faster parameter recovery and substantial computational speedup in ensemble simulations.
AI.conclusion: This framework demonstrates the feasibility of leveraging LLM-based pipelines for translating legacy codes to modern, differentiable environments, benefiting Earth system modeling.

title: Dynamics in a Low-Rank Separable Field Cellular Automaton
summary: Complex collective dynamics in cellular automata are usually associated with local-neighborhood combinatorics, yet it remains unclear whether long-lived dynamical organization requires such explicit local interaction structure. Here, we introduce a Separable-Field Cellular Automaton (SFCA), a normalized-field cellular automaton in which local neighbor counting is replaced by a rank-one-like row-column field. Each cell is updated according to a normalized field, with survival and birth governed by two threshold intervals. Systematic scans over interval widths and positions revealed four outcome classes: extinction, fixed points, cycles, and long transients. The outcome phase diagram was organized by the relative geometry of the survival and birth intervals: fixed points dominated when born interval was contained in survival interval, whereas long transients concentrated near the boundary between partial overlap and no overlap. A fine scan along this transition showed that the long-transient region forms a narrow but persistent ridge separating two qualitatively distinct cycle-dominated regimes. One side produced dense, high-change-rate cycles approximating global period-2 alternation, whereas the other produced sparse, low-change-rate, stripe-like cycles. Damage-spreading further supported a basin-competition interpretation, in which the long-transient ridge reflects delayed selection between two cyclic attractor families rather than random nonconvergence, while finite-size analysis shows that the long-transient ridge remains robust across tested grid sizes. These results show that structured long-transient dynamics can arise under compressed separable field coupling, suggesting that nontrivial collective organization does not necessarily require full local-neighborhood combinatorics.

AI.tldr: This study introduces a Separable-Field Cellular Automaton (SFCA) that generates complex dynamics without explicit local-neighborhood interactions. It identifies key patterns like extinction, fixed points, cycles, and long transients, highlighting how structured dynamics can emerge in a simplified model.
AI.motivation: The motivation is to explore whether complex and long-lived dynamical organization in cellular automata can occur without explicit local-neighborhood interactions, which are usually considered essential.
AI.method: The authors introduced the SFCA model, replacing traditional neighbor counting with a rank-one-like row-column field. They systematically varied birth and survival threshold intervals to map out outcome classes and conducted fine scans to examine the dynamics near key transitions.
AI.result: They identified four distinct outcomes: extinction, fixed points, cycles, and long transients, with the dynamical patterns strongly influenced by the relative geometry of the threshold intervals. A robust ridge of long-transient dynamics separated two distinct cycle regimes.
AI.conclusion: The findings demonstrate that long-transient dynamics and structured collective behavior can emerge with simplified separable field couplings, challenging the necessity of full local-neighborhood combinatorics for complex dynamical organization.

title: Disentangling Latent Risk Pathways via Bayesian Hypergraph Inference
summary: Electronic health records (EHR) pose large-scale multi-disease modeling problems in which many outcomes are rare and strongly influenced by shared risk factors. While modern approaches achieve strong predictive performance, they often treat diseases independently or rely on black-box architectures, offering limited insight into how risk factors organize disease risk and little principled uncertainty quantification. We introduce a Bayesian hypergraph inference framework that reframes multi-disease modeling around latent, risk-factor-modulated disease pathways. Risk factors act on hyperedges, latent disease subsets with shared risk patterns, allowing diseases to participate in multiple distinct pathways and enabling interpretable, higher-order structure beyond pairwise associations. A repulsion prior encourages parsimonious and identifiable structure, while posterior inference provides calibrated uncertainty over both disease groupings and risk-factor influence. To enable scalable inference on large EHR datasets, we develop a structured variational inference algorithm that preserves logical dependencies among hyperedge existence, disease membership, and pathway-level effects. Experiments on simulated data and UK Biobank demonstrate stable and interpretable disease pathway structure, well-calibrated uncertainty, improved estimation for rare diseases, and competitive predictive performance.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces a Bayesian hypergraph framework for modeling multi-disease interactions in electronic health records, focusing on interpretable risk-factor-modulated disease pathways and uncertainty quantification.
AI.motivation: The motivation is to address the challenge of multi-disease modeling in electronic health records, where diseases often share risk factors, and existing methods either treat them independently or use non-transparent approaches.
AI.method: The paper develops a Bayesian hypergraph framework that models latent disease pathways modulated by shared risk factors. It includes a structured variational inference algorithm for scalable, interpretable analysis and introduces a repulsion prior for parsimonious structure.
AI.result: The framework demonstrated accurate and interpretable disease pathway modeling, better estimation for rare diseases, well-calibrated uncertainty quantification, and competitive predictive performance in simulations and the UK Biobank.
AI.conclusion: The proposed Bayesian hypergraph framework offers a scalable and interpretable approach for multi-disease modeling, improving both performance and understanding of disease interrelationships.

title: VoLo: A Physical Orchestrator for Open-Vocabulary Long-Horizon Manipulation
summary: Open-vocabulary long-horizon manipulation requires robots to reason over flexible instructions and complex multi-object scenes while adaptively planning, executing, monitoring, and recovering from failures. We address these demands with a closed agent loop in which a VLM orchestrates heterogeneous robot capabilities as interruptible tools. Unlike in virtual AI agents, the timing of decisions, actions and tool calls is important in a physical world that does not pause for reasoning. We refer to this setting as Physical Orchestration, and propose VoLoAgent, a VLM that plans, monitors, and recovers by treating a VLA/WAM as an interruptible tool it steers mid-rollout alongside vision models and action primitives. To evaluate these long-horizon capabilities, we introduce RoboVoLo, a high-fidelity benchmark for open-vocabulary long-horizon manipulation across common sense, memory/state tracking, complex references, and world knowledge, with both task-level success and failure-mode diagnostics. Experiments show VoLoAgent substantially outperforms single VLA/VLM or tool-based systems, with validation on real-robot experiments. Project page: https://chicychen.github.io/VoLo/

AI.tldr: VoLoAgent enables flexible, long-horizon manipulation by physical robots using VLM-based orchestration of tools. Experiments show superior performance over traditional methods.
AI.motivation: The authors aim to tackle the challenges of open-vocabulary long-horizon manipulation, which requires robots to handle complex instructions and adapt to physical-world dynamics.
AI.method: The paper introduces VoLoAgent, a VLM-based system that orchestrates tools and integrates vision models and action primitives for adaptive reasoning, planning, monitoring, and recovery.
AI.result: VoLoAgent demonstrates improved long-horizon manipulation capabilities in both benchmark tests (RoboVoLo) and real-robot experiments, outperforming traditional systems.
AI.conclusion: This research advances robotic manipulation by integrating VLMs for adaptive control and tracking, offering a robust solution for physical orchestration tasks.

title: Page image classifier fine-tuned on century-spanning archives of scanned documents for further content-specific processing
comment: 29 pages, 19 figures, 13 tables. arXiv admin note: text overlap with arXiv:2507.21114
summary: Purpose: Digitization projects in the humanities produce vast, heterogeneous archives of historical documents, making manual sorting impractical at scale. This work addresses the need for an automated system to classify scanned page images based on visual content type - text, tables, and graphics - enabling content-specific downstream processing such as Optical Character Recognition (OCR) or structured data extraction. Methods: An image classification system was developed and evaluated on a dataset of over 48,000 annotated historical page images from century-old Czech archaeological archives, refined through four successive annotation stages with domain-expert review. A Random Forest Classifier baseline was established using hand-crafted image features. Subsequently, deep learning architectures were fine-tuned and compared: Convolutional Neural Networks (EfficientNetV2, RegNetY), Vision and Document Image Transformers (ViT, DiT), and multimodal CLIP models. An 11-category label scheme was designed collaboratively with domain experts and evaluated via five-fold cross-validation. Results: The feature-based baseline achieved approximately 75% accuracy. Fine-tuned CNNs and Transformers substantially outperformed it, with RegNetY-16GF achieving 99.16% and ViT-large 99.12% Top-1 accuracy on the held-out test set. CLIP ViT-B/16 reached 99.14% with optimized text descriptions. Conclusion: Image-only models, particularly RegNetY-16GF, deliver near-perfect classification accuracy and produce consistent labels across 649,508 unlabeled archival pages with over 90% inter-model agreement. Fine-tuned CLIP, despite competitive test-set accuracy, showed under 65% agreement with image-only models on unlabeled data, making it less suitable for deployment. The final models, annotated dataset, and software are publicly available under open-source licenses.

AI.tldr: This paper develops and evaluates an automated image classification system for classifying historical document page images into content types (text, tables, graphics), using deep learning models and achieving near-perfect accuracy.
AI.motivation: Large-scale digitization projects in the humanities generate massive, heterogeneous document archives, demanding automation for sorting and enabling further processing like OCR or structured data extraction.
AI.method: The study refined a dataset of 48,000 historical page images through successive annotations and domain-expert reviews. Traditional and advanced methods were compared, including Random Forest Classifiers, CNN architectures (EfficientNetV2, RegNetY), Transformers (ViT, DiT), and multimodal CLIP models. Models were tested on an 11-category label scheme via cross-validation.
AI.result: Deep learning models surpassed traditional baselines, with RegNetY-16GF achieving 99.16% accuracy. CLIP ViT-B/16 showed competitive performance but lower agreement on unlabeled data (65%). Consistent labels were generated for over 649,508 pages with >90% inter-model agreement.
AI.conclusion: Image-only models, specifically RegNetY-16GF, excelled in accuracy and consistency, proving suitable for deployment. Public availability of datasets, models, and software ensures accessibility for future research.

title: Bidirectional Small-Granularity Search between Code and Text
summary: We introduce the novel task of bidirectional small-granularity search between code and text, where the queries are small snippets of text or code and the results are also small fragments of the opposite modality, i.e., code or text. This task establishes direct links between text in scientific publications and corresponding code segments, in support of better and faster understanding of scientific methods. We introduce a large dataset for the proposed task that includes a training partition with textual descriptions of code generated automatically using GPT-4, and three testing partitions, one in-domain and two out-of-domain (OOD) that contain manually-annotated data as well as material from other domains. We also propose a modular approach to address this task. Our approach shares an encoder across four different subtasks that learn start/end of answer spans in both directions. We show that our method achieves good results in-domain, and encouraging results OOD. This suggests that addressing this task with automatically-generated data is possible, but there is exciting future work to be done.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces a task for linking small snippets of code and text bidirectionally, backed by GPT-4-generated data and evaluated with in-domain and out-of-domain testing.
AI.motivation: To bridge the gap between scientific text and code for improved understanding of scientific methods.
AI.method: A modular approach with a shared encoder, focusing on answering span start/end prediction tasks, using GPT-4 auto-generated data for training.
AI.result: Good results within the same domain and promising outcomes for out-of-domain tasks.
AI.conclusion: While the approach shows potential, there is significant room for future improvement in this novel task.

title: PathoSage: Towards Multi-Source Evidence Adjudication in Pathology via Experience-Aware Agentic Workflow
summary: Recent advances in Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs) and agent workflows have shown strong promise for computational pathology, yet reliable patch-level reasoning remains challenging. End-to-end pathology MLLMs often hallucinate morphological features, while recent agentic systems usually merge tool outputs and retrieved knowledge into a shared context, making decisions vulnerable to conflicting evidence and context contamination. We propose PathoSage, a three-stage framework that explicitly separates knowledge retrieval, evidence collection, and evidence adjudication for patch-level pathology multimodal reasoning. Its core component, Structured Evidence Deliberation, independently evaluates heterogeneous evidence from tools, performs conflict analysis, and generates the final judgment in a fresh context to reduce anchoring bias. We further introduce a training-free Beta-Bernoulli experience system with continuous credit assignment to model long-term tool reliability and construct similarity-weighted priors for future tool use. Experiments show that PathoSage effectively mitigates VQA hallucinations and classifier disagreement, outperforming strong pathology MLLM and agentic baselines. Our results highlight explicit evidence adjudication and reliability-aware tool modeling as key ingredients for robust pathology agents.

AI.tldr: PathoSage, a novel framework, enhances patch-level pathology reasoning by structuring evidence collection, deliberation, and adjudication, outperforming existing models.
AI.motivation: To address challenges in reliable patch-level reasoning in computational pathology, such as hallucinations in pathology MLLMs and issues with agentic systems blending conflicting evidence.
AI.method: Proposes a three-stage framework called PathoSage, featuring Structured Evidence Deliberation to independently evaluate tool outputs, analyze conflicts, and reduce bias, along with a training-free Beta-Bernoulli experience system for tool reliability modeling.
AI.result: PathoSage outperformed state-of-the-art pathology MLLMs and agentic systems by mitigating hallucinations and classifier disagreements, resulting in more robust decisions.
AI.conclusion: Explicit evidence adjudication and reliability-aware tool modeling are crucial for improving patch-level reasoning in computational pathology.

title: Offline Reinforcement Learning for Plasma Control in Nuclear Fusion: Codebase and Benchmark
summary: Offline reinforcement learning (RL) offers a promising route for developing plasma controllers from historical tokamak data, since online trial-and-error on real devices is costly and risky. However, progress in this direction remains difficult to measure due to the lack of a standardized offline RL benchmark for realistic multi-actuator, long-horizon plasma control problems in nuclear fusion. We introduce RL4F, an Offline Reinforcement Learning Benchmark for Plasma Control in Nuclear Fusion, providing closed-loop evaluation environments and baseline comparisons across four full-profile tracking tasks: rotation, density, temperature, and pressure. The dynamics function underlying the evaluation environment is built from historical discharge data from DIII-D, a real-world Tokamak. We evaluate a broad set of imitation learning and offline RL baselines under a unified protocol. We find that offline model-based RL methods obtain the best average performance on most objectives, although no single method dominates all tasks, highlighting the importance of dynamics modeling in complex, long-horizon plasma control tasks. To foster further research, we open-source the codebase, datasets, and evaluation framework, providing a benchmark not only for the fusion community but also for algorithm development in offline RL.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces RL4F, a benchmark aimed at developing offline RL for plasma control in nuclear fusion using historical tokamak data. It emphasizes the challenges and provides a dataset, model evaluation, and open-source tools for research.
AI.motivation: The motivation is to address the lack of a standardized offline RL benchmark for realistic, multi-actuator, long-horizon plasma control problems in nuclear fusion, enabling progress measurement and fostering algorithm advancement.
AI.method: The method involves developing RL4F, which includes closed-loop evaluation environments based on historical tokamak data, baseline performance comparisons across four plasma control tasks, and a unified evaluation protocol.
AI.result: Offline model-based RL methods showed the best average performance across most objectives, but no single method dominated all tasks, indicating the complexity and importance of accurate dynamics modeling for plasma control tasks.
AI.conclusion: The provided benchmark fosters further research in both the fusion and offline RL communities. Open-sourced tools, datasets, and environments aim to advance plasma control and offline RL algorithm development.

title: AutoMegaKernel: A Statically-Checked Agent Harness for Self-Retargeting Megakernel Synthesis
comment: 18 pages, 5 figures. Open-source code, data, and agent harness: https://github.com/RightNow-AI/AutoMegaKernel
summary:
AutoMegaKernel (AMK) compiles a HuggingFace Llama-family model into a single persistent cooperative CUDA kernel that runs the whole forward pass in one launch, with no per-model hand-written CUDA. The contribution is the system, not raw speed.
  A frozen schedule-IR validator statically certifies deadlock-freedom and race-freedom via static graph checks (not a mechanized proof), so an unsafe agent-proposed schedule is rejected before launch: across 7,160 adversarial schedules (6,091 unsafe) it had zero false-accepts and accepted all 360 real lowerings. The same source retargets sm_80/sm_90/sm_120 from one codebase, auto-generates correct megakernels for 10 of 10 supported models, and on a real SmolLM2-135M checkpoint reproduces HuggingFace greedy decode token-for-token (perplexity match 2.5e-7). An unattended, agent-drivable autoresearch loop self-improves the megakernel over its own baseline (1.25-1.72x).
  A search-found int8 (W8A16) megakernel beats CUDA-graphed cuBLAS bf16 at batch-1 decode across NVIDIA's datacenter inference fleet: L4 up to 1.33x, the current-gen L40S 1.25-1.27x, A10G up to 1.08x at scale, and the consumer RTX 5090 1.19-1.23x. The ordering is not a clean function of bandwidth (the 864 GB/s L40S beats the 600 GB/s A10G); the divide is inference-class vs training-class. AMK trails cuBLAS on the high-bandwidth training-class A100/H100, where the harness localizes the cross-SM-sync bottleneck; we report the gap plainly. This is a precision-asymmetric (W8A16 vs bf16) comparison at decode position 0; the largest real checkpoint is TinyLlama-1.1B. Code and the harness: https://github.com/RightNow-AI/AutoMegaKernel

AI.tldr: AutoMegaKernel (AMK) compiles HuggingFace Llama models into a single CUDA kernel for the entire forward pass, aiming for correctness and flexibility over raw speed.
AI.motivation: The paper aims to improve computational efficiency and flexibility in deploying models from the HuggingFace Llama family using a CUDA kernel approach that requires no hand-written code.
AI.method: AMK introduces a static schedule-IR validator to ensure correctness and automates retargeting across multiple architectures. It also employs a self-improving auto-research loop to enhance kernel performance.
AI.result: AMK correctly generates kernels for 10 supported models and demonstrates better decode performance compared to cuBLAS bf16 on NVIDIA's inference GPUs, but underperforms on training-class GPUs.
AI.conclusion: AMK validates new kernels with static checks to ensure reliability, achieves competitive decode performance on consumer and inference GPUs, and highlights areas for improvement in training-class GPUs.

title: QDS-SNN: Energy-efficient Quantum Deeply-Supervised Spiking Neural Network Algorithm for Traffic Sign Recognition
summary: Traffic sign recognition is crucial for intelligent transportation and autonomous driving, as it can improve driving efficiency and ensure road safety. However, traditional recognition methods are based on large datasets and intensive computation, which limits their real-time applicability. Spiking Neural Networks (SNNs) offer a biologically inspired, energy-efficient alternative due to their spatiotemporal processing capabilities, but suffer from information loss and vanishing gradients during training. To overcome these limitations, this study proposes a Quantum Deep-supervised Spiking Neural Network (QDS-SNN) that integrates Quantum Neural Networks (QNNs) for efficient, low-power deep supervision. Using quantum superposition and entanglement, QNNs enable expressive representations and parallel computation, thereby enhancing performance without compromising energy efficiency. The proposed QDS-SNN incorporates a temporally and spatially adaptive LIF (TSA-LIF) neuron and a quantum-assisted classifier module (QACM) to mitigate gradient issues and improve training effectiveness. This study conducts experiments on the PennyLane quantum simulation platform, and the results show that QDS-SNN achieves 99.72\% accuracy on the GTSRB dataset in only 6 time steps -- outperforming the MS-ResNet baseline by 1.32\% while reducing energy consumption by 55.77\%. In the TSRD dataset, it achieves 97.90\% accuracy while reducing energy use to 52.68\% of the baseline. These results demonstrate that QDS-SNN offers a high-performance, energy-efficient solution for traffic sign recognition in intelligent transportation systems.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes a Quantum Deep-supervised Spiking Neural Network (QDS-SNN) for traffic sign recognition to achieve high accuracy and energy efficiency using quantum neural networks and spiking neural network integration.
AI.motivation: The motivation is to improve driving efficiency and road safety in intelligent transportation systems while addressing limitations of traditional traffic sign recognition techniques, such as high computational costs and energy inefficiency.
AI.method: The method involves integrating Quantum Neural Networks (QNNs) with Spiking Neural Networks (SNNs), using a temporally and spatially adaptive LIF neuron and a quantum-assisted classifier module to address training challenges like gradient vanishing and information loss.
AI.result: The QDS-SNN achieves 99.72% accuracy on the GTSRB dataset in only 6 time steps, outperforming the MS-ResNet baseline by 1.32% and reducing energy consumption by 55.77%. It also achieves 97.90% accuracy on the TSRD dataset while lowering energy use to 52.68% of the baseline.
AI.conclusion: The findings demonstrate that QDS-SNN is an energy-efficient and high-performing solution for traffic sign recognition, suitable for applications in intelligent transportation and autonomous driving systems.

title: Accuracy-Configurable Floating-Point Multiplier Design for SRAM-Based Compute-in-Memory
summary: Digital Compute-in-Memory (DCiM) reduces data movement and has become a promising solution for energy-efficient edge AI. However, most existing DCiM frameworks still primarily target integer or fixed-point arithmetic, and provide limited support for compiler-integrated and accuracy-configurable floating-point computation. Directly integrating conventional IEEE 754 floating-point units into dense SRAM-based DCiM arrays, however, incurs high area and power overhead. To address this challenge, this work presents an accuracy-configurable floating-point multiplier integrated into the OpenACM framework for SRAM-based DCiM. An exact IEEE~754-compliant multiplier is first implemented as a baseline, and a mantissa-segmentation-based approximate multiplier is then proposed to reduce hardware cost while preserving numerical fidelity. Post-layout results show up to 69% logic area reduction and 72% power savings over exact floating-point designs without delay overhead. Evaluations on image processing tasks and ResNet-18 inference further demonstrate negligible accuracy degradation. These results indicate that compiler-integrated approximate floating-point multiplication is a practical approach for enabling efficient and configurable floating-point support in SRAM-based DCiM systems. The Floating-Point Multiplier is available on https://github.com/ShenShan123/OpenACM

AI.tldr: The paper introduces an accuracy-configurable floating-point multiplier for SRAM-based DCiM arrays, reducing area and power consumption while maintaining numerical fidelity.
AI.motivation: Efficient edge AI requires energy-efficient computation and reduced data movement. Current DCiM frameworks lack robust floating-point computation, and integrating existing solutions incurs significant overhead.
AI.method: The authors propose an exact IEEE 754 floating-point multiplier as a baseline and develop a mantissa-segmentation approximation technique to optimize hardware cost.
AI.result: Post-layout results demonstrate up to 69% reduction in logic area and 72% power savings compared to exact designs. Evaluation on ResNet-18 and image tasks shows negligible accuracy degradation.
AI.conclusion: Compiler-integrated approximate floating-point multiplication is an effective solution for enabling energy-efficient floating-point computation in SRAM-based DCiM systems.

title: Large-Scale Regularized Matching on GPU Clusters
summary: Production decision systems such as ad allocation or content matching involve millions of users and thousands of items, reducing to large-scale linear programs with sparse block-diagonal structure across users. These LPs are solved repeatedly on recurring cadences over slowly evolving inputs. Three system gaps stand out. Scale: production instances routinely exceed the memory capacity of GPU solvers such as cuPDLP and D-PDLP under fixed hardware budgets. Temporal instability: solution variability across runs induces downstream churn and complicates SLAs, yet existing solvers provide no explicit control. Extensibility: CPU-based solvers such as DuaLip-Scala converge slowly and couple problem formulation to fixed schemas, making new constraint families difficult to express. We present a distributed multi-GPU LP solver built natively in PyTorch with systems-algorithm co-design for this structure. It adopts column-sharded parallelism with fused Triton kernels and batched operations to reduce per-iteration overhead. As users grow, only local computation increases, while communication is limited to a reduction of item-level dual variables, yielding near-linear scaling with GPU count at fixed item size. We also adopt ridge-regularized LPs to improve stability, a control absent from existing GPU solvers. A continuation schedule over the regularization parameter balances convergence speed and solution fidelity. Finally, we introduce an operator-centric programming model that replaces DuaLip-Scala's schema-bound interface with composable primitives, enabling new formulations without modifying the solve loop or distributed infrastructure. On synthetic workloads, our system achieves order-of-magnitude wall-clock speedup over DuaLip-Scala, near-linear multi-GPU scaling (3.86x on 4 GPUs), and scales beyond the reach of existing GPU solvers.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces a scalable, multi-GPU LP solver built in PyTorch with innovations in distributed computation, solution stability, and extensibility, achieving significant speedup and scalability.
AI.motivation: Current production decision systems face challenges in scale, temporal instability, and lack of extensibility in existing solvers.
AI.method: The authors propose a distributed multi-GPU LP solver with fused Triton kernels, ridge-regularized LPs for stability, and an operator-centric programming model to overcome system gaps.
AI.result: The system achieves order-of-magnitude speedup over existing solvers, near-linear GPU scaling (3.86x on 4 GPUs), and tackles workloads beyond current GPU solvers' capability.
AI.conclusion: The proposed system effectively addresses scalability, stability, and extensibility issues, providing improved solution speed and robustness for large-scale linear programs.

title: SNN-MLIR: An MLIR Dialect for Compiling Neuromorphic SNNs from NIR to Bare-Metal C
summary: Spiking neural networks (SNNs) are increasingly trained in a wide range of frameworks (SnnTorch, Lava, Norse, and others) each with its own model format. The Neuromorphic Intermediate Representation (NIR) addresses this fragmentation by providing a common, framework-independent format for exchanging trained SNN models. NIR solves the exchange problem, but it stops there. It provides a description of a network, not a path to running one. Each backend is still left to implement deployment on its own, with no shared, transformable compiler representation in between. This paper presents snn-mlir, an outof-tree MLIR dialect for SNNs together with a NIR-MLIR-C compilation bridge. The dialect provides a small set of typepolymorphic operations that work identically on floating-point (f32/f64) and quantized data, so a single intermediate representation serves both simulation and hardware-oriented deployment. A Python front end reads any NIR file and emits dialect IR, automatically inserting rescaling operations to keep quantization scales consistent across layers. A reference lowering pass converts the dialect to standard linalg and arith operations, from which the toolchain produces self-contained, dependency free C11 code that compiles and runs on any C-capable CPU or embedded target. We evaluate numerical fidelity against reference outputs, portability across CPU targets, and the cost of quantization. The current scope is feedforward, fully-connected networks with a CPU backend. snn-mlir is released as open source under the Apache-2.0 license with LLVM-exception and it is already available on Github.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces snn-mlir, an MLIR dialect designed for compiling Spiking Neural Networks (SNNs).
AI.motivation: The fragmentation of SNN frameworks with incompatible formats creates issues in training, deploying, and exchanging models. NIR addresses some problems but lacks a unified path for model execution across platforms.
AI.method: snn-mlir offers a type-polymorphic intermediate representation for SNNs, supporting both simulation and hardware-oriented deployment. It converts NIR files into compatible dialect IRs and generates dependency-free C11 code for CPU or embedded targets.
AI.result: The approach maintains numerical fidelity, demonstrates portability across CPU backends, and provides insights into the impact of quantization. It currently supports feedforward, fully-connected networks.
AI.conclusion: snn-mlir fills a significant gap in the SNN workflow by providing an extensible and framework-independent compilation bridge, enabling efficient deployment with a unified toolchain.

title: Discovering Functionally Selective Brain Regions with a Deep Topographic Multimodal Model
comment: Preprint. First two author contributed equally
summary: Nearby neurons in cortex share similar response profiles, producing systematic spatial organization across sensory and cognitive systems. Recent topographic models reproduce aspects of this structure but remain unimodal and spatially constrain each layer separately, yielding fragmented maps that capture neither the contiguity of cortical processing streams nor their integration across modalities. We introduce Topo-Omni, a topographic multimodal model in which visual, auditory, and language/cognitive processing share a single contiguous in-silico sheet. Built by fine-tuning a pretrained foundation model with a spatial smoothness objective, this architecture develops clusters across modalities that are consistent with human neuroimaging, from sensory to cognitive systems. Driving or suppressing a cluster selectively biases or impairs perception, paralleling human intervention studies. Finally, we use our model to screen for novel clusters in-silico and discover new natural landscape and animal networks which we validate in human data. A single spatial principle thus organizes representations across modalities and processing stages, yielding testable hypotheses about cortical organization.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces Topo-Omni, a topographic multimodal model that reflects human neuroimaging and explores cortical representation across modalities.
AI.motivation: To address the limitation of unimodal, fragmented cortical models and unify the representation across sensory and cognitive modalities using topographic principles.
AI.method: The study constructed Topo-Omni by fine-tuning a pretrained foundation model with a spatial smoothness objective to create a single, contiguous multimodal architecture.
AI.result: The proposed model captures cross-modal cortical clusters consistent with human neuroimaging, mirrors effects seen in human intervention studies, and predicts novel clusters validated by human data.
AI.conclusion: A singular spatial principle can systematically organize multimodal processing across the cortex, leading to insights about cortical representation and further testable hypotheses on brain organization.

title: SWE-Marathon: Can Agents Autonomously Complete Ultra-Long-Horizon Software Work?
summary: AI agents are increasingly expected to complete long-horizon workflows that require sustained progress over hours, millions of tokens, and complex environments. Yet current agent benchmarks largely evaluate short-form tasks, such as single pull requests, small tickets, or 5-10 minute exercises, limiting our ability to measure agents' capabilities in planning, long-context understanding, and memory use. We introduce SWE-Marathon, a benchmark of 20 long-horizon tasks spanning software engineering and adjacent technical domains. Each task consists of a unique executable environment, a human-written reference solution, and a multi-layer verification suite. Logged agent attempts average 27.2M total tokens, making SWE-Marathon substantially longer-horizon than existing SWE and command-line agent benchmarks. Current frontier coding agents solve fewer than 30% of tasks. Failures often arise from poor self-verification, self-reported infeasibility, and premature termination. We also observe reward-hacking behavior in 13.8% of rollouts, where agents attempt to exploit the environment or verifier to bypass the intended workflow. SWE-Marathon includes adversarial review of test suites and execution environments, as well as multi-layer checks designed to prevent shortcut solutions. We release SWE-Marathon, evaluation code, and agent trajectories at https://swe-marathon.org/.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces SWE-Marathon, a benchmark to evaluate AI agents in long-horizon software engineering tasks.
AI.motivation: Current benchmarks primarily test AI agents on short-duration tasks, which do not adequately measure their capabilities in long-term planning, understanding, and memory.
AI.method: The paper presents SWE-Marathon, comprising 20 unique long-horizon tasks with executable environments, reference solutions, and robust verification systems.
AI.result: Existing top-tier coding agents solve fewer than 30% of the tasks, with common failures including poor verification, self-reported infeasibility, and reward-hacking behavior.
AI.conclusion: SWE-Marathon offers a more realistic and challenging framework for evaluating AI agents in extended workflows, aiming to advance their capabilities in long-horizon tasks.

title: Transfer learning for causal forest
summary: Transfer learning addresses the challenge of transfering knowledge from one domain to another. Traditional transfer learning focuses on adapting models trained on a source domain (with a lot of observations) to improve performance on a target domain (with few observations). In this work we consider the case of a model shift and we focus on the transfer learning applied to a causal forest namely HTERF. This causal forest aims to estimate the Conditional Average Treatment Effect (CATE). The approach considered is the offset method presented by Wang (2016) adapted to a causal context. This method relies on the use of intermediate models in order to estimate the offset between source and target distributions. Our main result is a bound on the CATE error of HTERF on target depending on the error of the intermediate models. Simulation studies show the good performances of this approach in different settings on simulations and on a real-world dataset.

AI.tldr: The paper explores transfer learning in the context of causal forests, specifically focusing on Conditional Average Treatment Effect (CATE) estimation using the HTERF model with an offset method.
AI.motivation: To address the challenge of transferring knowledge between domains when estimating Conditional Average Treatment Effects (CATE), particularly given model shifts and limited target data.
AI.method: Adaptation of Wang's offset method to the context of causal forests, using intermediate models to quantify offsets between source and target distributions.
AI.result: The study derives a bound on the CATE error for HTERF on the target domain, based on errors of intermediate models. Simulations and real-world data demonstrate the effectiveness of the approach.
AI.conclusion: The approach shows reliable performance in addressing transfer learning challenges for causal effect estimation, making it valuable in domains with domain shifts and limited target observations.

title: MinNav: Minimalist Navigation Using Optical Flow For Active Tiny Aerial Robots
comment: Accepted for publication at ICRA 2026. Link to Project page https://pear.wpi.edu/research/minnav.html
summary: Navigation using a monocular camera is pivotal for autonomous operation on tiny aerial robots due to their perfect balance of versatility, cost and accuracy. In this paper, we introduce MinNav, a navigation stack based on optical flow and its uncertainty to fly through a scene with static and dynamic obstacles and unknown-shaped gaps without any prior knowledge of the scene components and/or their locations/ordering. We further improve success rate by using the activeness of the robot to move around in an exploratory way to find obstacles and navigate. We successfully evaluate and demonstrate the proposed approach in many real-world experiments in various environments with static and dynamic obstacles and unknown-shaped gaps with an overall success rate of 70%. To the best of our knowledge, this is the first solution to tackle all the aforementioned navigation cases without prior knowledge using a monocular camera. Our approach is on par in performance with depth based methods with factors of magnitude less computation required and can readily run onboard tiny aerial robots. The accompanying video, supplementary material, code and dataset can be found at https://pear.wpi.edu/research/minnav.html

AI.tldr: This paper proposes MinNav, an optical flow-based navigation system for tiny aerial robots equipped with monocular cameras to navigate through environments with static/dynamic obstacles and unknown gaps, achieving a 70% success rate.
AI.motivation: Autonomous navigation for tiny aerial robots is challenging due to their hardware constraints. The authors aim to develop a cost-effective, accurate, and versatile navigation solution without relying on depth sensors or prior knowledge of the environment.
AI.method: The authors developed MinNav, which uses optical flow and uncertainty-based navigation to detect obstacles and gaps. The system leverages the robot's activeness for exploratory movements to improve navigation.
AI.result: MinNav demonstrated a 70% success rate in various real-world tests, managing dynamic/static obstacles and unknown-shaped gaps. It rivals performance of depth-based systems but requires far less computation.
AI.conclusion: MinNav is the first monocular camera solution for comprehensive navigation challenges, providing cost-effective, onboard usage for tiny aerial robots while delivering competitive results.

title: Multimodal Group Emotion Recognition In-the-Wild Towards a Privacy-Safe Non-Individual Approach
summary: This thesis addresses group emotion recognition (GER) in-the-wild with a focus on privacy preservation. Unlike traditional emotion recognition methods that rely on individual-level cues such as face, gaze, or voice analysis, this work uses collective audio-video signals to infer emotions at the group level, reducing risks of individual monitoring and surveillance. Two complementary frameworks are proposed. The first is a cross-attention multimodal architecture for audio-video fusion, combined with Frames Attention Pooling (FAP) for temporal aggregation. It is supported by synthetic data augmentation and validated through ablation studies, demonstrating robustness in real-world GER conditions. The second framework, Variational Encoder Multi-Decoder (VE-MD), learns a shared latent space for emotion classification and structural representation prediction, including body and face cues. Two decoding strategies, DETR-based and heatmap-based, are explored to analyze the role of structural representations in group and individual settings. The thesis makes three main contributions: it clarifies the role of multimodality and structural cues in group-level affective computing; introduces two architectures for privacy-preserving multimodal GER; and shows that competitive performance can be achieved without using individual features as input data.

AI.tldr: The thesis proposes two privacy-preserving frameworks for recognizing group emotions using audio-visual signals instead of individual cues, achieving robust results in real-world settings.
AI.motivation: To address privacy concerns in traditional emotion recognition methods by focusing on group-level analysis rather than individual monitoring.
AI.method: Introduced two frameworks: (1) Cross-attention multimodal architecture with audio-video fusion and Frames Attention Pooling, (2) Variational Encoder Multi-Decoder for shared latent space learning and structural prediction.
AI.result: The proposed frameworks demonstrated competitive performance and robustness in group emotion recognition (GER) while avoiding the use of individual-level features.
AI.conclusion: The research shows that it is possible to achieve effective and privacy-preserving GER through multimodal architectures and structural representation analysis without compromising on accuracy.

title: TinyJudge: Unverifiable Constraint Alignment via Lightweight Specialist Ensembles
summary: Instruction Following (IF) is a core capability of LLMs, requiring strict adherence to diverse constraints, ranging from verifiable ones (e.g., output length) to unverifiable ones (e.g., tone). Reinforcement learning with verifiable rewards has emerged as a paradigm for IF tasks, leveraging LLM-as-a-judge to assess unverifiable constraints. However, we empirically find that this approach remains a significant bottleneck, suffering from severe reward hacking and higher computational overhead. In this work, we first analyze the generalization capabilities of unverifiable constraints and discover that specific constraints exhibit distinct, high-generalization patterns. Motivated by this, we propose TinyJudge, a framework that employs an ensemble of specialized tiny language models ($\sim0.6B$) to provide rewards for soft constraints. By distilling expertise from frontier models into these tiny models, it achieves high-precision, lightweight evaluation. Extensive evaluations across five benchmarks demonstrate that TinyJudge outperforms the baselines by $\sim10\%$ in average performance and $12\%$ in reward precision. Crucially, it also achieves a $3\times$ speedup in total training time. Our work provides a scalable and robust path for aligning LLMs with unverifiable human instructions.

AI.tldr: The paper addresses challenges in LLM instruction following, proposing TinyJudge, an ensemble of smaller models for evaluating unverifiable constraints, offering performance and efficiency improvements.
AI.motivation: The need for scalable and precise handling of unverifiable constraints in LLM instruction-following tasks, overcoming issues like reward hacking and computational inefficiency.
AI.method: TinyJudge employs specialized small language models ($\sim0.6B$) that distill expertise from larger models, forming an ensemble to evaluate soft constraints with high precision and reduced overhead.
AI.result: TinyJudge achieves higher average performance (~10%), better reward precision (12%), and threefold faster training times compared to existing methods.
AI.conclusion: TinyJudge offers an effective and efficient framework to align LLMs with unverifiable instructions, addressing current bottlenecks and improving scalability.

title: OmniMem: Perturbation-aware Memory Compression for Streaming Audio-Visual LLMs
summary: Audio-visual large language models (LLMs) hold strong promise for long-form video understanding, yet their long-video inference is fundamentally limited by the linear growth of video tokens and key-value (KV) caches. We present OmniMem, a memory-efficient streaming framework designed specifically for audio-visual LLMs. Unlike existing compression methods that treat all tokens uniformly, OmniMem introduces a modality-aware memory allocation strategy that separately manages visual and audio contexts, addressing the severe token imbalance between the two modalities. OmniMem further preserves informative and non-redundant KV states through perturbation-aware memory selection, enabling compact memory without sacrificing long-range understanding. To strengthen compression under realistic deployment constraints, we also explore budget-aware fine-tuning, which encourages the model to consolidate useful information into retained memory. Experiments on VideoMME Long, LVBench, and LVOmniBench with video-SALMONN 2+ and Qwen-2.5-Omni show that OmniMem consistently improves over strong training-free compression baselines by 2-4% absolute accuracy under the same memory budgets, with an additional 1-2% gain after fine-tuning.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces OmniMem, a memory-efficient mechanism for audio-visual large language models, addressing challenges in processing long-form videos by incorporating modality-aware and budget-aware strategies.
AI.motivation: Current audio-visual language models struggle with processing long-form videos due to the exponential growth of video tokens and memory requirements, necessitating efficient computational strategies.
AI.method: OmniMem employs a modality-aware memory allocation for better handling visual and audio contexts, a perturbation-aware memory selection to preserve essential information, and a fine-tuning process for optimized memory compression.
AI.result: OmniMem achieves 2-4% improvement in accuracy over compression baselines under the same memory budget and gains an additional 1-2% performance after fine-tuning, validated on multiple benchmarks.
AI.conclusion: OmniMem offers a practical, efficient framework for enhancing audio-visual LLMs by smartly managing memory without compromising their ability to understand long-form video content.

title: MedicalRec: Medical recommender system for image classification without retraining
summary: The emergence of machine learning and deep learning has revolutionized the efficiency of diagnostic, therapeutic, and administrative systems in healthcare. However, this rapid adoption has come at the cost of requiring significant computing power and energy consumption, as well as e-waste disposal and carbon emissions. One of the challenges of these models is choosing the right model for classification tasks. To this end, researchers attempt to identify the optimal model using their data through trial and error, which involves energy consumption and waste. The goal of this study is to develop a model-based recommender system for medical image classification. For this purpose, a data set was collected from 3,000 articles in the field of medical image classification. This dataset, publicly available under the name MedicalRec-Bench, contains over 5,000 records of models tested in various tasks, including Skin Cancer Classification, Tumour Classification, Wound Classification, Breast Cancer, and MRI classification. The dataset was evaluated in four different modes, depending on the number of features: MedicalRec I (5 features), MedicalRec II (9 features), MedicalRec III (11 features), and MedicalRec IV (18 features). Collecting all values for the features is challenging due to non-reporting by the authors; hence, the dataset contains significant amounts of missing values. The Medical Recommender System (MedicalRec) is a transformer-based model used for item recommendations in this study. This model achieved remarkable results in the evaluation on the dataset and in the evaluation with 12 base models. This model achieved a maximum HitRate@100 of 75.5%. The dataset and implementations are available through the GitHub link: https://github.com/Ramin1Mousa/MedicalRec

AI.tldr: The authors propose MedicalRec, a transformer-based recommender system for selecting optimal models for medical image classification. They also introduce MedicalRec-Bench, a comprehensive dataset for this task.
AI.motivation: The motivation behind this study is to address the high energy consumption and waste generated by trial-and-error approaches in selecting machine learning models for medical tasks, and streamline this process with a reliable model recommender system.
AI.method: The authors developed MedicalRec, a transformer-based model, and introduced the MedicalRec-Bench dataset consisting of over 5,000 records of models for medical image classification. The dataset was evaluated in four versions with varying numbers of features, and the MedicalRec model was tested against 12 base models.
AI.result: The MedicalRec model demonstrated strong performance, achieving a maximum HitRate@100 of 75.5%. The dataset and model implementations are publicly available on GitHub.
AI.conclusion: MedicalRec and MedicalRec-Bench provide a robust solution to optimize medical image classification model selection, reducing energy costs and improving efficiency in healthcare AI applications.

title: An 84-Format Numeric Catalog with Bit-Exact Conformance Vectors: A Vendor-Neutral Reference for FP8, BF16, MXFP4, and Microscaling Formats
comment: 17 pages. Source repository: https://github.com/gHashTag/paper3-methodology tag v4.0-trinity. Paper CC BY 4.0; code MIT. ORCID 0009-0008-4294-6159
summary:
Numeric format proliferation in machine learning hardware -- FP8 (E4M3 and E5M2), BF16, MXFP4, microscaling block formats, and dozens of research variants -- has outpaced the availability of vendor-neutral, bit-exact reference material. Engineers porting models across accelerators encounter silent divergences that are difficult to diagnose without a shared ruler.
  This paper describes a catalog of 84 numeric formats spanning 13 families, a suite of six bit-exact conformance packs covering GF16, MXFP4 element, BF16, FP8 E4M3, FP8 E5M2, and E8M0 block scale, and an IEEE P3109 v3.2.0 cross-walk that maps each pack to its corresponding standards-track configured format. Each pack is a self-contained JSON document with a SHA-256 fingerprint, a shared row schema, and an anchor vector that encodes 3.0 -- the identity phi^2 + 1/phi^2 = 3 -- as a cross-pack sanity check. Packs are cross-validated against ml_dtypes 0.5.4 (Google/JAX); any divergence is documented explicitly and interpreted as a spec-permitted interpretation gap rather than hidden. The work is framed as registry filling: it does not propose new formats, make model-accuracy claims, or assert superiority over any vendor's implementation. All artifacts are publicly available at https://github.com/gHashTag/t27 under an open license.

AI.tldr: This paper catalogs 84 numeric formats for ML hardware, along with a suite of six bit-exact conformance packs to provide a vendor-neutral reference for resolving discrepancies in format interpretation.
AI.motivation: There is a lack of standardized, vendor-neutral, bit-exact references for the numerous numeric formats in ML hardware, leading to difficulties in diagnosing silent divergences when porting models across accelerators.
AI.method: The paper introduces a catalog of numeric formats, conformance packs, and a cross-walk system linking to IEEE standards. The conformance packs are self-contained JSON documents anchoring format specifications and allowing for sanity-check validations.
AI.result: The catalog and conformance packs fill gaps in numeric format documentation and enable explicit, documented handling of divergences without introducing new formats or asserting model accuracy claims.
AI.conclusion: This registry-filling work provides a critical reference for engineers working with heterogeneous accelerators, offering transparent, open-access tools for resolving numeric format divergences.

title: Seq103: A Unified Neuroevolution Framework for Compact Sequence Architecture Discovery
summary: Neuroevolution is a representative neural architecture search paradigm that evolves both network topology and weights through evolutionary algorithms. In this paper, we propose Seq103, a unified NEAT-style neuroevolution framework for compact sequence architecture discovery. Seq103 consists of a shared evolutionary backbone and an optional recurrent extension.
