Extensive experiments on multiple benchmarks demonstrate that GraphLoRA not only outperforms state-of-the-art LLM-based recommendation methods but also achieves superior generalization, effectively balancing structural reasoning capability with computational efficiency. Code is available at \href{https://github.com/wgj15965/GraphLoRA}{https://github.com/wgj15965/GraphLoRA}.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces GraphLoRA, a novel framework to improve LLM-based recommendations by integrating graph-structured collaborative signals into the adaptation process, demonstrating superior performance and efficiency.
AI.motivation: To address the challenge of aligning the textual semantics of LLMs with collaborative signals, which current methods fail to handle effectively due to their inability to capture high-order relational dependencies.
AI.method: GraphLoRA embeds a graph message-passing network into the low-rank adaptation pathway, enabling structural signals to guide LLM parameter updates for deeper integration between text and graph data.
AI.result: GraphLoRA outperforms state-of-the-art LLM-based recommendation methods on multiple benchmarks, demonstrating better generalization and a balance between reasoning capability and efficiency.
AI.conclusion: GraphLoRA effectively bridges the gap between textual and structural data, improving recommendation systems by leveraging graph-based collaborative signals in a computationally efficient manner.

title: Land cover and flood type govern the detection limits of satellite-based flood mapping across diverse global flood events
summary: Floods are among the most destructive natural hazards, and their increasing frequency under climate change makes satellite-based inundation mapping essential for disaster response. Geospatial foundation models pretrained on satellite archives offer geographic transferability, but their operational reliability across diverse, unseen events remains uncharacterized. Here we deploy Prithvi-EO-2.0 across 19 out-of-distribution flood events (2017-2025) spanning six continents, eight climate zones, and six flood mechanisms, validating against two independent reference products. Detection accuracy depended jointly on land cover and flood type, with cropland yielding the highest agreement (IoU=52%) and riverine events the strongest detection (F1=0.69), while tree cover and built-up areas showed near-zero detection (IoU=4%) regardless of flood mechanism. Dual-reference validation revealed that apparent model error partly reflects definitional inconsistency between reference products rather than detection failure. Iterative pipeline testing identified 23 failure modes, with pipeline engineering dominating initial error over model capacity. These findings establish environment-dependent detection boundaries for operational satellite flood mapping.

AI.tldr: The study examines the use of a geospatial foundation model (Prithvi-EO-2.0) for flood detection across diverse events globally. Detection performance varied significantly based on land cover and flood type.
AI.motivation: Floods present increasing challenges under climate change, necessitating accurate and transferable satellite-based mapping for better disaster response efforts.
AI.method: The study tested Prithvi-EO-2.0 model on 19 diverse flood events worldwide between 2017-2025, validated against two independent reference datasets, and identified critical operational constraints.
AI.result: Detection accuracy varied: croplands yielded the highest IoU (52%), while tree cover and built-up areas performed poorly (IoU=4%). Riverine floods had the best detection (F1=0.69). Discrepancies were partly due to reference product inconsistencies.
AI.conclusion: Satellite-based flood mapping relies heavily on environmental factors, and operational reliability requires addressing pipeline issues and reference inconsistencies to enhance detection in diverse scenarios.

title: Enabling KV Caching of Shared Prefix for Diffusion Language Models
summary:
Key-value (KV) caching for shared prefixes is essential for high-throughput large language model (LLM) serving, but it faces critical challenges in emerging diffusion language models (DLMs). In DLMs, bidirectional attention means that updating any token dynamically alters the entire context and its corresponding KVs. Thus, existing caching techniques developed for LLMs, which assume that KVs remain invariant once computed, corrupt the shared prefix KVs. Our experiments show that applying these techniques to DLMs causes model accuracy to collapse to near zero.
  To unlock high-throughput DLM serving, we propose bidirectional prefix caching, bicache, the first KV caching technique for shared prefixes in DLMs. bicache is designed based on key observations from our comprehensive analysis: shared prefix KVs remain stable and reusable in shallow layers, while the depth of shallow layers depends on the fraction of shared prefix tokens in each request. Thus, bicache dynamically identifies a safe layer depth for reusing shared prefix KVs and eliminates redundant computation. Evaluations demonstrate that bicache significantly improves serving throughput by 36.3%-98.3% compared to existing techniques without accuracy collapse (only 0-1.8% difference).

AI.tldr: The paper introduces bicache, a key-value caching technique designed to enhance throughput in diffusion language models (DLMs) without compromising accuracy.
AI.motivation: Existing KV caching techniques developed for large language models (LLMs) fail for diffusion language models (DLMs) due to dynamic bidirectional attention, leading to accuracy collapse.
AI.method: The authors propose bicache, which dynamically identifies stable layers for shared prefix KV reuse, based on the fraction of shared prefix tokens and layer depth. This reduces redundant computation in DLMs.
AI.result: Evaluations show that bicache improves serving throughput by 36.3%-98.3% compared to existing techniques, with minimal accuracy degradation (only 0-1.8%).
AI.conclusion: Bicache addresses KV caching challenges specific to DLMs and demonstrates significant performance improvements while maintaining model accuracy.

title: Representational Similarity and Model Behavior in Multi-Agent Interaction
summary: Researchers have shown that neural similarity among humans predicts social closeness and cooperative success, whereas innovation often emerges from interactions among dissimilar individuals. We investigate whether these principles extend to artificial intelligence by examining interactions between large language models. In our experiments, 276 model pairs interact across eight games spanning both cooperation and novelty. We find that pairs with more similar representation spaces achieve significantly higher cooperation but exhibit reduced novelty and creativity. The effects of representational similarity on cooperation and novelty remain robust even after controlling for other factors such as performance disparity and model size. We also find that similarity in the early layers consistently shows the strongest association with cooperation and novelty, compared to the middle and later layers. This suggests that a central factor underlying these patterns could be the extent to which the two models share lexical and semantic grounding. Overall, representational similarity can be an important consideration in multi-agent system design.

AI.tldr: Neural similarity among AI models predicts cooperative success but reduces novelty. Representation similarity impacts cooperation and creativity patterns.
AI.motivation: To explore whether principles of neural similarity affecting human interactions (social closeness and cooperation vs innovation from dissimilarity) extend to AI agent interactions.
AI.method: Conducted experiments on 276 model pairs playing eight games involving cooperation and novelty, analyzing representational similarity's influence while controlling for additional factors.
AI.result: Higher representational similarity led to better cooperation but decreased creativity. Early layer similarity had the strongest effect among layers analyzed.
AI.conclusion: Representational similarity should be considered in designing multi-agent systems due to its impact on cooperation and novelty.

title: Hardware-aware Low-latency Quantum Compilation with Data-driven Lightweight Error Detection for Early Fault-Tolerant Systems
comment: 16 pages, 15 figures, Springer LNCS format. Code available at https://github.com/Sumitchongder/quantum-hw-aware-pipeline
summary: Noisy intermediate-scale quantum (NISQ) processors are entering an early fault-tolerance regime where full quantum error correction carries prohibitive resource costs, yet lightweight error detection can meaningfully improve algorithmic success rates. Existing compilation and error-detection toolchains treat these concerns in isolation, with no principled way to balance detection overhead against success probability under latency constraints. We present an integrated hardware-aware compilation and data-driven quantum error-detection (QED) framework that jointly optimises qubit mapping, SWAP insertion, and syndrome-schedule placement via a noise-weighted cost function and a learned multi-objective scheduler. Simulation experiments on an HPC cluster using GPU-accelerated density-matrix simulation (NVIDIA cuQuantum SDK) across VQE, phase-estimation, and Grover benchmarks, three noise profiles, and circuit sizes of 6-20 qubits (depths 10-160), show that joint co-design raises algorithmic success probability by up to 68 percent (95 percent CI: 60 percent to 76 percent) over SABRE on an 8-qubit VQE instance with post-selection.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces a framework combining quantum circuit compilation and error detection to optimize quantum computation performance, demonstrating significant success probability improvement.
AI.motivation: Improving quantum algorithm success rates in the noisy intermediate-scale quantum (NISQ) era is challenging due to resource-intensive quantum error correction. Lightweight error detection is promising but lacks integrated optimization with compilation methods.
AI.method: The authors propose a hardware-aware, integrated framework for quantum error detection and compilation, leveraging a noise-weighted cost function and a learned multi-objective scheduler to jointly optimize qubit mapping, SWAP insertion, and syndrome-schedule placement.
AI.result: Simulation experiments on various quantum benchmarks and noise profiles showed that the proposed co-design framework improves algorithmic success probabilities by up to 68% compared to existing methods like SABRE.
AI.conclusion: The integrated approach to error-detection and compilation is effective in improving quantum algorithm performance, marking a step forward in early fault-tolerance for NISQ processors.

title: When More Cores Hurts: The Vector Database Scaling Paradox in HPC
summary: Vector databases have been designed and optimized for cloud environments; however, emerging scientific AI workloads (e.g., molecular search, meteorological trajectory detection, and literature-driven hypothesis generation) demand efficient, scalable execution on HPC systems. We present a large-scale evaluation of three state-of-the-art vector databases -- Qdrant, Milvus, and Weaviate -- on two production supercomputers, scaling to 256 distributed workers across 64 compute nodes. We evaluate representative workload patterns -- mixed read/write and write-then-read -- using popular benchmarks, multimodal embeddings, and a novel real-world scientific dataset. Our results reveal that workload characteristics can limit latency reduction, additional cores can reduce query throughput by up to 30.67%, and scaling from 16 to 256 workers (16x) only yields a 5.46x improvement. This scaling paradox exposes the fundamental mismatch between cloud-oriented designs and HPC systems, highlighting the need for new, HPC-aware vector database designs.

AI.tldr: This paper evaluates the performance of three vector databases on HPC systems and highlights scalability challenges due to their cloud-oriented design.
AI.motivation: To address the demand for efficient execution of scientific AI workloads on HPC systems, which current cloud-optimized vector databases fail to meet.
AI.method: Analyzed three vector databases -- Qdrant, Milvus, and Weaviate -- on two supercomputers using benchmarks, embeddings, and a scientific dataset; scaled evaluation across 64 compute nodes.
AI.result: Identified scalability limitations; increased cores decreased throughput by up to 30.67%, and scaling 16 to 256 workers only achieved 5.46x speedup.
AI.conclusion: Current vector databases are not well-suited for HPC environments, and new, HPC-aware designs are necessary to unlock their potential for scientific workloads.

title: SLRMentor: An LLM-Based Tool Supporting Learning of SLR in Software Engineering
summary: This paper presents SLRMentor, a conversational assistant designed to support both learning about the systematic literature review process and the execution of planning activities in software engineering. The tool offers general guidance on SLR methodology and supports key planning tasks, including search string construction and reasoning about inclusion and exclusion criteria, with explanations grounded in established SLR guidelines. A pilot validation with graduate students suggests that SLRMentor helps clarify the SLR process and planning decisions, lowers initial barriers for novice researchers, and supports learning while still requiring active methodological judgment.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces SLRMentor, a conversational assistant aimed at assisting researchers with understanding and executing systematic literature reviews in software engineering.
AI.motivation: To address challenges faced by novice researchers in learning and planning systematic literature reviews, reducing initial barriers, and ensuring methodological clarity.
AI.method: Development and pilot validation of SLRMentor, which guides users through key SLR planning tasks with explanations based on established SLR guidelines.
AI.result: The pilot validation results indicate that SLRMentor enhances understanding of the SLR process, supports planning decisions, and facilitates learning, although active methodological judgment is still required.
AI.conclusion: SLRMentor effectively aids novice researchers in systematic literature review tasks, combines guidance with learning, and lowers entry barriers while maintaining the need for active engagement and judgment.

title: Improving Bayesian Optimization via Training-Aware Conditional Diffusion Models
summary: Bayesian optimization (BO) is a widely used approach for black-box optimization that uses a Gaussian process (GP) as a surrogate and guides sequential evaluations via an acquisition function, with the ultimate goal of locating the global optimum $\mathbf{x}^{\star}$. To align with this goal, information-based acquisition functions such as Predictive Entropy Search (PES) model $\mathbf{x}^{\star}$ as a random variable and reduce the entropy of its distribution, but approximating this distribution via traditional GP posterior sampling is computationally expensive. To address this limitation, we leverage Conditional Diffusion Models (CDMs) to efficiently approximate the distribution of $\mathbf{x}^{\star}$ and develop BO-inherent training strategies for CDMs. Motivated by the structural properties of the CDM-learned distribution, we further develop an acquisition strategy termed Diffusion-based Mode Seeking (DMS) to guide the sequential evaluation. We establish a sub-optimality guarantee for the CDM-learned distribution and demonstrate through extensive experiments that DMS outperforms standard BO baselines.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces a novel Bayesian Optimization (BO) approach leveraging Conditional Diffusion Models (CDMs) to approximate the distribution of the global optimum and proposes a new acquisition strategy called Diffusion-based Mode Seeking (DMS), which outperforms existing BO methods.
AI.motivation: Traditional Bayesian Optimization methods like Predictive Entropy Search rely on Gaussian Process posterior sampling, which is computationally expensive for probabilistic modeling of the global optimum's distribution. This paper aims to alleviate this computational burden while improving efficiency and performance.
AI.method: Conditional Diffusion Models (CDMs) are used to approximate the distribution of the global optimum. Additionally, the paper introduces DMS, a BO-specific acquisition strategy, and implements BO-specific training strategies for CDMs.
AI.result: Results show that the proposed DMS approach outperforms standard Bayesian Optimization baselines across extensive experiments, and a sub-optimality guarantee for the CDM-learned distribution is established.
AI.conclusion: Integrating Conditional Diffusion Models into Bayesian Optimization provides a more computationally efficient framework. The DMS strategy ensures superior performance for black-box optimization tasks compared to traditional methods.

title: MuJoCo-Drones-Gym: A GPU-Accelerated Multi-Drone Simulator for Control and Reinforcement Learning
summary: Robotic simulators are a cornerstone of modern research in aerial robotics, serving both as a vehicle for the development of new control algorithms and as the data source for training reinforcement learning (RL) policies. Yet, existing quadcopter learning environments often face a trade-off between physical fidelity, multi-agent support, and the throughput required by modern deep RL pipelines. In this paper, we present MuJoCo-Drones-Gym, an open-source Gymnasium-compatible multi-drone environment built on top of the MuJoCo physics engine. MuJoCo-Drones-Gym supports an arbitrary number of Bitcraze Crazyflie 2.x nano-quadcopters and exposes a modular API for selecting (i)~the physics model (rigid-body MuJoCo, explicit Python dynamics, or any subset of ground effect, blade drag, and inter-drone downwash), (ii)~the action interface (per-motor RPMs, collective normalized thrust, velocity setpoints, or PID waypoint commands), and (iii)~the observation space (kinematic state vectors, RGB / depth / segmentation cameras, or neighbourhood adjacency information). A PettingZoo ParallelEnv wrapper enables drop-in multi-agent reinforcement learning, while a suite of seven task environments, hover, velocity tracking, multi-drone hover, waypoint navigation, formation flight, gate racing, and a generic multi-agent template, demonstrates the breadth of the interface. We describe the environment design, the underlying physics and quadcopter dynamics, and illustrate its use through control and learning examples that mirror those of the closely related gym-pybullet-drones project, while taking advantage of MuJoCo's improved contact handling, rendering, and parallelizability.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces MuJoCo-Drones-Gym, an open-source multi-drone environment designed for aerial robotics research, offering high physical fidelity and multi-agent support, compatible with modern RL pipelines.
AI.motivation: To address the trade-offs in existing quadcopter simulators between physical accuracy, multi-agent functionality, and throughput for deep reinforcement learning.
AI.method: The authors developed MuJoCo-Drones-Gym on the MuJoCo physics engine. It incorporates modular APIs for selecting physics models, action interfaces, and observation spaces, and supports tasks with multi-agent RL compatibility through a PettingZoo wrapper.
AI.result: MuJoCo-Drones-Gym successfully supports Bitcraze Crazyflie 2.x drones with advanced features such as customizable physics models, action interfaces, and observation spaces. It also includes seven task environments for various applications.
AI.conclusion: MuJoCo-Drones-Gym provides a flexible, high-fidelity, and multi-agent compatible simulation environment for advancing aerial robotics research and reinforcement learning.

title: AMN: An Adaptive Multi-Scale Fusion Network with Boundary and Uncertainty Modeling for Nuclei Segmentation
summary: Accurate classification of nuclei subtypes in histopathology images is critical for downstream tasks including tumor grading, immune infiltrate quantification, and prognosis prediction. Existing approaches rely on either convolutional or transformer-based encoders in isolation, limiting their ability to simultaneously capture fine-grained local texture and long-range spatial context. We present AMN (Adaptive Multi-Scale Nuclei Network), a dual-encoder segmentation framework that jointly leverages a Swin Transformer and a ResNet-50 feature pyramid, fused via a learned per-channel gating mechanism that dynamically weighs each encoder's contribution at every scale. AMN is trained with a multi-objective loss combining class-weighted focal loss, boundary-aware loss with positive-pixel emphasis, and a novel uncertainty-modulated classification term that suppresses overconfident erroneous predictions. Evaluated on the CoNIC benchmark across seven nuclei classes, AMN achieves a mean Dice of 0.82 and mean F1 of 0.68, with an F1 of 0.67 on the diagnostically challenging lymphocyte class. AMN outperforms eight baseline models spanning pure-CNN, pure-transformer, and recent hybrid architectures: U-Net, ResU-Net, DeepLabV3+, SegNet, ViT-Small, HmsU-Net, ConvFormer-UNet, and BEFUnet. Cross-dataset evaluation on MoNuSeg demonstrates strong generalization without retraining and validating the domain robustness of the learned representations.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces AMN, a hybrid segmentation model combining Swin Transformer and ResNet-50 for nuclei classification in histopathology images. It excels in fine-grained accuracy and generalization compared to other models.
AI.motivation: To improve nuclei subtype classification in histopathology images, addressing limitations of isolated CNN or transformer-based encoders in capturing both local texture and global context.
AI.method: AMN employs a dual-encoder system (Swin Transformer + ResNet-50) fused via per-channel gating, trained with advanced loss functions including focal loss and boundary-aware mechanisms.
AI.result: Achieved high metrics (mean Dice of 0.82, mean F1 of 0.68) on CoNIC benchmark, outperforming eight baseline models while exhibiting strong cross-dataset generalization.
AI.conclusion: AMN demonstrates superior performance and robust generalization in nuclei classification, underscoring its potential for histopathology tasks including tumor grading and prognosis.

title: Post-training is (Massive) Supervised Learning
summary: The prevailing paradigm for training LLMs has evolved to rely on a massive post-training phase consisting of SFT and RL. In this position paper, we argue that this methodology effectively marks a reversion to the ``pre-train then fine-tune'' approach of the BERT era, explicitly tailoring models to the desired behaviors and specific benchmarks on which they are evaluated. We begin with a historical overview of LLMs, describing the different phases of the LLM evolution. We argue that the current landscape is remarkably similar to the early days of LLMs, where task performance heavily relied on fitting the models to in-distribution datasets. To empirically demonstrate this, we compare pre-trained models to randomly initialized ones, by fine-tuning both variants on modern reasoning datasets and evaluating them on competitive math and code benchmarks. We show that models post-trained from scratch yield highly non-trivial performance. Our findings suggest that current post-training methodologies function primarily as a distribution-fitting mechanism. We finish by positing that developing generally capable models and systems requires moving beyond extensive post-training for predefined behaviors, shifting instead toward training procedures where models ``learn how to learn''.

AI.tldr: This paper critiques the post-training methodology in large language models (LLMs) and argues that it mirrors the old "pre-train then fine-tune" approach, advocating for developing models that learn how to learn rather than relying on predefined behaviors.
AI.motivation: The authors aim to explore whether current post-training methods for LLMs have reverted to older paradigms and how a shift in training paradigms could lead to the development of more generally capable models.
AI.method: The paper includes a historical overview of LLM evolution and empirical comparisons between pre-trained models and randomly initialized models, fine-tuning both on reasoning datasets and evaluating their performance on benchmarks.
AI.result: Post-trained models from scratch achieve significant performance, suggesting that post-training acts as a distribution-fitting process rather than enabling general learning capabilities.
AI.conclusion: Current methodologies focus on fitting models to predefined benchmarks rather than developing learning systems. The authors advocate for training models to "learn how to learn" to achieve general capabilities.

title: When Should an AI Scientist Stop? Verifiable Experiment Steering and Refusal for Autonomous Discovery
comment: Accepted at AI for Science Workshop at ICML 2026
summary: We present CARTOGRAPH, a verification layer for AI scientists that couples unresolved-subspace experiment steering (select), explicit ambiguity closure (resolve), and residual-based library inadequacy detection (refuse). Under a local linear-Gaussian bridge, raw unresolved projection is the isotropic unresolved Fisher-information trace, while CARTOGRAPH-A is the exact unresolved A-optimal rule; closed-form EIG and Box-Hill arise as local comparators rather than global equivalents. Across five testbeds, CARTOGRAPH-A beats raw projection 129W/0T/15L at d = 8 (p < 10^-21) in a replicated structured cascade. More distinctively, the framework tentatively identifies three out-of-library pharmacokinetic mechanisms and then revokes those identifications as residuals expose structural misfit, while one perturbed in-library control stays identified throughout. In low-dimensional pharmacokinetic and filtered EPA settings, near-ties against disagreement are predicted by theory and observed. Finally, in a retrospective audit of 40 positive claims from the published A-Lab autonomous materials system, the refuse guard flags all 4 claims later marked inconclusive under manual reanalysis while passing 32/36 confirmed claims. Code is available at https://github.com/ai4science-boed/cartograph.git

AI.tldr: CARTOGRAPH proposes a verification method for AI experiments, combining a steering mechanism, an ambiguity resolution step, and a library inadequacy detection feature. It shows high performance in verifications and error identification across multiple test cases.
AI.motivation: The motivation is to improve AI scientists' ability to rigorously verify experimental outputs by addressing unresolved ambiguities, steering experiment decisions, and detecting inadequate modeling assumptions.
AI.method: It employs a framework (CARTOGRAPH) combining unresolved-subspace experiment steering, explicit ambiguity resolution, and a residual-based inadequacy detection system. Key comparison metrics and methods (e.g., unresolved A-optimal rules, isotropic Fisher-information traces) are utilized in local-linear settings.
AI.result: CARTOGRAPH outperforms standard raw projection methods in experimental settings (e.g., winning 129/144 comparisons at d=8). It successfully identifies and later revokes misfitting hypotheses based on residual evaluation in pharmacokinetics, while robustly flagging flawed experimental claims in retrospective audits.
AI.conclusion: CARTOGRAPH enhances experimental verification by combining innovative theoretical methods and practical validation mechanisms. It addresses ambiguities and identifies structural flaws in modeling, showing significant promise in real-world scientific contexts.

title: Quantitative Promise Theory: Intentionality and Inference in Autonomous Agents
summary: I discuss some quantitative representations of Promise Theory for processes involving autonomous agents. Agent models are common in software systems, machine learning, and biology, for example, but may also apply to physics and other forms of engineering. I describe how Bayesian probability and information theoretic optimization, including Active Inference, may be incorporated with promise semantics -- as well as how Promise Theory supplements solutions, helping to avoid probability's pitfalls, which include non-local coordination, calibrating, and normalizing probabilistic computations. The role of boundary conditions in constraining allowed states and selecting decision thresholds is a form of promise, and agent alignment provides a scalable definition of intent. Autonomous agents may congeal into swarms with superagent characteristics by trying to minimize their information, despite uncertainty that works to maximize it. The use of Promise Theory involves some research challenges as well as stylistic preferences.

AI.tldr: The paper explores integrating Promise Theory with Bayesian methods, emphasizing its application to autonomous agents and its advantages over purely probabilistic approaches.
AI.motivation: To address the limitations of probabilistic methods, such as lack of coordination and normalization difficulties, by integrating them with Promise Theory in modeling autonomous agents.
AI.method: The study combines concepts like Bayesian probability, Active Inference, and information theoretic optimization with Promise Theory to address system challenges like boundary constraints and agent alignment.
AI.result: Promise Theory is shown to complement probabilistic approaches by improving scalability, intent alignment, and handling coordination challenges in autonomous agents.
AI.conclusion: Promise Theory offers a robust framework to overcome the limitations of traditional probabilistic methods, especially in autonomous systems and swarm behaviors, though it presents research challenges.

title: ScaleDisturb: Exploiting Temporal Asymmetry to Amplify Read Disturbance in Modern DRAM Chips
summary: DRAM suffers from read disturbance phenomena (e.g., RowHammer and RowPress), where repeatedly accessing or continuously keeping open a DRAM row (aggressor row) induces bitflips in other physically nearby unaccessed rows (victim rows). The disturbance mechanism is practically exploitable from the software stack and worsens across generations with continued density scaling. DRAM read disturbance is highly sensitive to memory access patterns, yet prior work explores read disturbance under only a limited set of access patterns. We present ScaleDisturb, a new DRAM access pattern that can amplify DRAM read disturbance by asymmetrically extending the open time of two aggressor rows. Our rigorous experimental characterization of 196 DDR4 and 3 HBM2 DRAM chips shows that ScaleDisturb (1) leads to bitflips at significantly fewer row activations, compared to state-of-the-art memory access patterns, (2) makes read disturbance attacks easier across all tested DRAM chips, (3) increases DRAM vulnerability to read disturbance as DRAM manufacturing technology scales down to smaller node sizes. We showcase a proof-of-concept attack on a real system where a user-level program leveraging ScaleDisturb induces more bitflips than state-of-the-art RowHammer and RowPress memory access patterns. We describe and evaluate four solutions for mitigating read disturbance bitflips in the presence of ScaleDisturb and call for more research on the topic.

AI.tldr: DRAM read disturbance phenomena like RowHammer are exacerbated by new attack patterns. ScaleDisturb amplifies these disturbances, making memory chips more vulnerable. Researchers provide experimental results and mitigation solutions.
AI.motivation: The study aims to investigate and exploit a new DRAM access pattern, ScaleDisturb, which intensifies read disturbance phenomena, posing risks to the reliability and security of current and future DRAM technologies.
AI.method: The researchers designed the ScaleDisturb access pattern to asymmetrically extend the open time of aggressor rows, experimentally analyzing its impact on DRAM chips. They tested this on 196 DDR4 and 3 HBM2 chips to evaluate the vulnerability and verify its effects.
AI.result: ScaleDisturb causes bitflips more efficiently and effectively than existing access patterns, making all tested DRAM chips more prone to attacks. It worsens read disturbance vulnerabilities as DRAM technology advances, and its threat is demonstrated in a proof-of-concept attack.
AI.conclusion: ScaleDisturb highlights critical vulnerabilities in DRAM technology as it scales, and necessitates immediate attention in terms of novel prevention strategies to mitigate these threats. Solutions for addressing ScaleDisturb are proposed, and further research is encouraged.

title: Cherry-pick Override: Unsafe Directional Commitment in LLM Judges under Mixed Evidence
summary: LLM judges increasingly turn verdicts into system commitments. Under mixed evidence (claims with both supporting and refuting sources) this is unsafe: when the schema exposes CONFLICTING as the authorized non-directional verdict, returning SUPPORTS/REFUTES is an unauthorized directional commitment, a failure we name Cherry-pick Override (CCO). We define CCO under an explicit task contract and report it with a same-denominator diagnostic protocol paired with matched-coverage bootstrap and an apples-to-apples random-veto null. On AVeriTeC's Conflicting subset (N_C = 150), three-option judges return a directional verdict on more than 84% of mixed-evidence claims; under the typed schema, three-judge majority voting amplifies direction-on-conflict on AVeriTeC (0.887 vs. 0.840; 95% CI [+0.013, +0.080]) but does not replicate on VitaminC-Mixed. Walking an intervention ladder of common single-channel fixes (typed vocabulary, panel aggregation, confidence thresholding, validator-only filtering), each leaves a distinct residual failure: panel aggregation suppresses single-judge CONFLICTING dissent in 48% of CCO cases; the panel is well-calibrated for direction (ECE = 0.07 on pure-S/R) so confidence cannot operationally separate CCO from correct directional commits; validator-as-classifier nearly halves pure-evidence accuracy. A minimal two-channel reference probe reaches operating points neither single channel reaches; under the random-veto null its promotion to CONFLICTING is structurally targeted on AVeriTeC (empirical p < 1/2001) and weaker but in the same direction on VitaminC-Mixed, a selectivity result rather than a magnitude one. We argue for an external commitment-control layer that separates verdict generation from commitment authorization, using structural evidence and confidence as orthogonal channels and NO-COMMIT as a routed controller state.

AI.tldr: This paper examines issues with LLM judges making unsafe directional commitments when conflicting evidence exists. It introduces the concept of Cherry-pick Override (CCO), analyzes failures with existing methods, and suggests a commitment-control layer as a solution.
AI.motivation: The motivation is to address the unsafe reliance of LLMs on directional verdicts in cases of mixed evidence, which can lead to unauthorized commitments and errors in judgment processes.
AI.method: The authors define CCO under a detailed task contract, use diagnostic protocols with statistical methods like matched-coverage bootstrap and random-veto null, and evaluate proposed fixes including panel aggregation, confidence thresholding, typed schema, and two-channel reference probes.
AI.result: LLM judges frequently fail in cases with mixed evidence (84% directional verdicts). Panel aggregation, confidence filtering, and single-channel fixes leave specific failings. A minimal two-channel reference probe improves targeting of conflicting cases, showing selective improvement.
AI.conclusion: A commitment-control layer separating verdict generation from commitment authorization, leveraging structural evidence and confidence in distinct channels, can address failures like CCO and promote safer judgment in mixed-evidence scenarios.

title: LOTTERY: Learning from Reference-Only Samples in Two-Sample Testing under Size Asymmetry
summary: Data-adaptive two-sample testing assesses if two samples come from the same distribution, using a discrepancy learned from the data (e.g., via kernel-based feature representations). Such methods typically rely on data splitting to decouple learning from testing and control type I error. However, this paradigm is ill-suited to few-shot settings with severe sample-size imbalance: abundant reference samples are available, while only a handful of query samples arrive. In this paper, we show how this imbalance can be leveraged constructively. Using abundant reference data, we learn reference-dependent representations that summarize salient structure of the reference distribution and provide informative signals for detecting departures. We incorporate a collection of representation families that capture both global and local structure, and adaptively weight them using only reference samples via an uncertainty-guided principle. Theoretically, we establish permutation-based type I error control and show consistency of the aggregated test: as the sample sizes grow, the test power converges to one whenever the representation set contains at least one consistent representation. Empirically, our aggregation achieves strong performance across a range of benchmarks while retaining type I error control.

AI.tldr: A method is proposed for data-adaptive two-sample testing that combines reference-dependent representations to handle sample imbalance in few-shot scenarios, ensuring type I error control and good performance.
AI.motivation: To address the challenge of few-shot, imbalanced sample scenarios in two-sample testing where traditional data splitting methods fail to perform adequately.
AI.method: The method leverages abundant reference samples to learn reference-dependent representations and combines them using an uncertainty-guided weighting principle. It ensures validity through permutation-based type I error control and achieves power consistency over time.
AI.result: The proposed approach achieves strong empirical performance across various benchmarks while maintaining type I error control.
AI.conclusion: The paper introduces an adaptive method that uses sample imbalance constructively, offering robust and theoretically sound performance in two-sample testing.

title: EgoAERO: Learning Dexterous Manipulation from a Single Egocentric Video without Object Assets
summary: Egocentric RGB-D videos offer a natural source of human dexterous manipulation demonstrations, but existing data is difficult to use for robot learning because object pose, geometry, and contact information are often missing or require pre-scanned object assets. We present EgoAERO, the first framework that learns dexterous manipulation from a single egocentric RGB-D human demonstration without object assets. EgoAERO reconstructs contact-consistent hand-object trajectories through asset-free object tracking and reconstruction, ego motion compensation, and adaptive contact optimization, then converts them into robot policies using two-stage residual learning. We further introduce an online quality assessment mechanism and construct EgoDex-R, a large-scale egocentric dataset with 4.3M RGB-D frames for dexterous policy learning. Simulation and real-world experiments show that EgoAERO enables single-demonstration dexterous manipulation and achieves downstream performance close to CAD-based reconstructions on HOI4D.

AI.tldr: EgoAERO enables robots to learn dexterous manipulation from single egocentric RGB-D videos without requiring pre-scanned object assets.
AI.motivation: Existing egocentric RGB-D datasets for dexterous manipulation are challenging to use for robot learning due to the lack of comprehensive object poses, geometries, and contact details, as well as their reliance on pre-scanned object assets.
AI.method: The proposed framework, EgoAERO, reconstructs hand-object trajectories from a single RGB-D egocentric video by performing asset-free object tracking, ego-motion compensation, and adaptive contact optimization. These reconstructions are then converted into robot policies via a two-stage residual learning process.
AI.result: EgoAERO demonstrated successful single-demonstration dexterous manipulation performance. It also achieved results comparable to CAD-based reconstructions on the HOI4D dataset in both simulation and real-world experiments.
AI.conclusion: EgoAERO provides a robust approach to learning dexterous manipulation without object assets and introduces EgoDex-R, a significant dataset that enhances the potential for policy learning in robotics.

title: NeuroAlign: Hierarchical Multimodal Fusion of Dynamic and Structural Neuroimaging for MCI Analysis
summary: Multimodal neuroimaging fusion of functional MRI (fMRI) and diffusion tensor imaging (DTI) provides complementary information for cognitive impairment analysis, but remains challenged by heterogeneous feature spaces and misaligned representations. We propose \textit{NeuroAlign}, a hierarchical framework for structured multimodal fusion. It introduces (1) \textit{Dual-Modal Hierarchical Alignment} (DMHA), which models multi-scale dynamic connectivity and aligns dynamic-static and functional-structural embeddings; and (2) \textit{Dual-Domain Hierarchical Interaction} (DDHI), which enables fine-grained modulation and global interaction between connectivity- and region-level features. To support feature-level inspection, we design \textit{Synergistic Activation Mapping} (SAM), a gradient-free, marker-oriented attribution method for DFC, SFC, ALFF, and FA. Evaluated on GUTCM, ADNI, and OASIS under five-fold validation, NeuroAlign achieves competitive MCI/SCD detection and preliminary cross-dataset transferability. Attribution analyses reveal modality-specific and partially consistent brain patterns, providing model-derived evidence for multimodal representation analysis.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces NeuroAlign, a multimodal neuroimaging fusion framework for cognitive impairment analysis, integrating fMRI and DTI via novel hierarchical alignment and interaction techniques.
AI.motivation: Understanding cognitive impairments requires integrating data from different neuroimaging modalities, but challenges persist in aligning heterogeneous features and representations.
AI.method: The framework employs Dual-Modal Hierarchical Alignment (DMHA) for dynamic-static and functional-structural feature alignment, Dual-Domain Hierarchical Interaction (DDHI) for connectivity and region-level feature interaction, and Synergistic Activation Mapping (SAM) for gradient-free feature attribution.
AI.result: The method shows competitive performance in detecting mild cognitive impairments and cross-dataset transferability. Attribution analyses highlight specific brain patterns through multimodal representations.
AI.conclusion: NeuroAlign offers an innovative and effective strategy for multimodal neuroimaging fusion, advancing cognitive impairment analysis and improving our understanding of underlying brain patterns.

title: BEACON: Behavioral Entropy Aggregation for Cross-Model Hallucination Detection in Large Language Models
comment: 12 pages, 6 tables, 1 figure. Code and data available upon request
summary: Hallucination in large language models (LLMs), defined as the generation of factually incorrect or unsupported content, remains a critical barrier to reliable deployment. We present BEACON (Behavioral Entropy Aggregation for Cross-model hallucination detectiON), a black-box hallucination detection framework that operates purely on model outputs without requiring access to internal representations or external knowledge bases. BEACON extracts a 31-dimensional feature vector from structured multi-pass generation, integrating NLI-based semantic entropy, embedding geometry, chain-of-thought consistency, and paraphrase stability signals. A gradient-boosted classifier trained on 7,617 labeled examples across seven benchmarks achieves 0.8123 +/- 0.0102 AUROC (95% CI: 0.7632-0.8251), outperforming standalone semantic entropy (+0.2298) and SelfCheckGPT-style consistency baselines (+0.2457). Feature importance analysis shows that hallucination is inherently multi-dimensional, requiring combined uncertainty signals. An efficient 5-call variant achieves 0.7795 AUROC, enabling practical deployment across black-box LLM APIs.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces BEACON, a model-agnostic hallucination detection framework for LLMs, achieving strong performance without internal model access.
AI.motivation: Hallucination in LLMs is a major obstacle for their reliable usage as they generate factually incorrect content.
AI.method: BEACON identifies hallucination using a 31-dimensional feature aggregation from structured multi-pass outputs, combining signals like semantic entropy, geometry, and consistency.
AI.result: The method achieves 0.8123 AUROC on seven benchmarks, surpassing conventional baselines, with an efficient variant reaching 0.7795 AUROC for practical application.
AI.conclusion: Hallucination detection in LLMs requires multi-dimensional approaches and BEACON demonstrates strong efficacy for API applications.

title: Improving Multimodal Reasoning via Worst Dimension Optimization
summary: Multimodal reasoning requires a path that retains integrity over a wide range of constraints, from visual grounding to logic consistency. However, the current Process Reward Models focus on heuristically defined rewards that equally weigh these factors, which may lead to the concealment of individual dimension failures by the dominating factors, without guaranteeing the validity of the reasoning process in general.

AI.tldr: The paper discusses the shortcomings of current Process Reward Models in multimodal reasoning, highlighting that they use heuristic rewards that might overlook failures in individual dimensions.
AI.motivation: Addressing the issue of integrity in multimodal reasoning across constraints like visual grounding and logic consistency.
AI.method: Critique of current models that rely on heuristic rewards, suggesting potential failures in balancing constraints effectively.
AI.result: Illustrates the limitations of heuristic reward models in ensuring balanced and valid multimodal reasoning.
AI.conclusion: There is a need for better frameworks that retain integrity across multiple constraints in multimodal reasoning.

title: MST-Direct at Scale: Multivariate and Conditional Geostatistical Simulation via Sinkhorn Optimal Transport
summary:
This paper extends MST-Direct, a Matching-via-Sinkhorn-Transport approach for multivariate geostatistical simulation, from the original bivariate, unconditional, small-grid formulation to multivariate, conditional, and large-grid settings. We address the three main limitations identified in the original work: (i) scalability beyond a few thousand nodes through a sparse, candidate-restricted Sinkhorn matcher with O(nC) memory complexity; (ii) extension to multiple variables by matching target value tuples onto an independent FFT-MA Gaussian backbone that reproduces a prescribed variogram; and (iii) hard-data conditioning by fixing observed data tuples at their spatial locations while conditioning the backbone through kriging. Because the transport plan remains a permutation of the target tuples, the multivariate joint distribution is preserved exactly.
  The method is validated using the same six-variate, heteroscedastic, strongly nonlinear reference distribution employed in Direct Multivariate Simulation (DMS), under both unconditional (200x200) and conditional (100x100, 200 hard-data samples) scenarios, and is benchmarked against the Projection Pursuit Multivariate Transform (PPMT). Results show that MST-Direct reproduces the joint distribution with zero histogram error, exactly honours hard data, and accurately reproduces the prescribed spatial correlation structure, whereas PPMT remains an approximation.
  Index Terms-Optimal transport, Sinkhorn algorithm, geostatistical simulation, multivariate simulation.

AI.tldr: This paper enhances MST-Direct for geostatistical simulations, introducing scalability, multivariate extension, and data conditioning while preserving accuracy.
AI.motivation: To overcome limitations in scalability, multivariate handling, and hard-data conditioning in geostatistical simulations.
AI.method: Enhances MST-Direct with a sparse Sinkhorn matcher for scalability, an FFT-MA Gaussian backbone for multivariable extension, and kriging-based conditioning for hard data.
AI.result: Validated on a six-variate reference dataset, MST-Direct outperforms PPMT by reproducing distributions, honoring hard data, and preserving spatial correlation accurately.
AI.conclusion: MST-Direct achieves precise distribution reproduction, hard-data adherence, and correlation accuracy in multivariate geostatistical simulations.

title: Quality-Diversity Search in Sound Generation: Investigating Innovation Engines for Audio Exploration
comment: This is an extended version of the previously published conference paper "Towards Sound Innovation Engines Using Pattern-Producing Networks and Audio Graphs": https://doi.org/10.1007/978-3-031-56992-0_14
summary: This study addresses the challenges composers and sound designers face in creating and refining tools to achieve their musical goals. Using evolutionary processes to promote diversity and foster serendipitous discoveries, we automate the search through uncharted sonic spaces for sound discovery, arguing that diversity-promoting algorithms can bridge the gap between the theoretical realisation and practical accessibility of sounds. We describe a system for generative sound synthesis combining Quality Diversity (QD) algorithms with a supervised discriminative model, inspired by the Innovation Engine algorithm, and explore different configurations and the interplay between the chosen synthesis approach and the discriminative model. We examine the interaction between Compositional Pattern Producing Networks (CPPNs) and Digital Signal Processing (DSP) graphs, introducing a novel approach that uses multiple specialised CPPNs for different frequency ranges; this yields simpler networks while maintaining performance comparable to single-CPPN setups. We also investigate evolutionary stepping stones by analysing goal switches between musical and non-musical contexts, revealing how lineages traverse unlikely paths to current elites. Expanding the behaviour space of a previous study to include various sound durations, we uncover specialisation within temporal niches. Results indicate that CPPN and DSP graphs coupled with a Multi-dimensional Archive of Phenotypic Elites (MAP-Elites) and a deep learning classifier can generate a substantial variety of synthetic sounds, diverse and innovative across temporal and contextual dimensions. We present the generated sound objects through an online explorer and as rendered sound files, and, in the context of music composition, an experimental application that showcases their creative potential across various durations and contexts.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces a system that employs evolutionary algorithms and machine learning to explore diverse and innovative synthetic sounds, aiding composers and sound designers.
AI.motivation: To address challenges faced by composers and sound designers in discovering novel sounds and bridging the gap between theoretical sound possibilities and practical accessibility.
AI.method: Combining Quality Diversity (QD) algorithms with supervised discriminative models, leveraging CPPNs and DSP graphs for sound synthesis, and exploring evolutionary pathways through technologies like MAP-Elites.
AI.result: The system successfully produces a diverse range of synthetic sounds across temporal and contextual dimensions, showcasing applicability in music composition through online tools and experimental applications.
AI.conclusion: The proposed system demonstrates its potential for creating innovative and specialised synthetic sounds, promoting discovery and creativity within musical contexts.

title: AttentionCap: Transformer Based Capacitance Matrix Learning Toward Full-Chip Extraction
comment: Accepted at the 63rd ACM/IEEE Design Automation Conference (DAC '26)
summary: As capacitance extraction accuracy of rule-based pattern matching becomes difficult to sustain at advanced nodes, a growing trend emerges to develop deep-learning-based 2D capacitance models. However, existing MLP- and CNN-based methods constrain their input to fixed metal-layer combinations in a specific process node, limiting their usability in practice. Recognizing the inherent similarity between capacitance matrix and the prevailing attention mechanism, we propose AttentionCap, a customized Transformer for capacitance matrix learning, with a Gram representation framework, a physics-aligned symmetric-attention output layer, and a novel normalized Laplacian loss. We also introduce a process-node embedding to enable multi-node learning. Trained on synthetic data, AttentionCap attains 0.67\%/3.99\% self/coupling-capacitance error on unseen real designs under a multi-layer and multi-node setting, surpassing the CNN-Cap baseline with 4.6$\times$/5.7$\times$ lower self/coupling error and 192$\times$ faster inference speed. A pretrained AttentionCap accurately transfers to an unseen node with only 5K samples and 4K finetuning steps. With sufficient accuracy on unseen real designs and strong transferability to new process nodes, AttentionCap offers highly practical value for modern EDA workflows. Code and data are available at https://github.com/THU-numbda/AttentionCap.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes AttentionCap, a Transformer-based model for accurate 2D capacitance matrix learning. It outperforms existing CNN methods in accuracy, speed, and adaptability to unseen process nodes.
AI.motivation: Existing MLP- and CNN-based 2D capacitance models are limited by their fixed input constraints tied to specific metal-layer combinations and process nodes, making them impractical for variable-use cases.
AI.method: The study introduces a customized Transformer model named AttentionCap with novel features, including a Gram representation framework, symmetric-attention output, normalized Laplacian loss, and process-node embedding for multi-node learning.
AI.result: AttentionCap achieves 0.67% self-capacitance error and 3.99% coupling-capacitance error on unseen real designs, significantly outperforming CNN-based baselines by achieving 4.6×/5.7× lower errors and 192× faster inference speeds.
AI.conclusion: AttentionCap demonstrates high accuracy, transferability to unseen nodes with minimal samples, and practical application potential to modern EDA workflows, marking it a superior alternative to existing methods.

title: Chimera: Protocol-Aware Recovery for Confidential BFT Consensus
summary:
Trusted Execution Environments (TEEs) have enabled confidential Byzantine Fault-Tolerant (BFT) consensus systems with confidentiality and improved scalability. However, TEEs do not provide state continuity: during recovery, a compromised host can roll back a crashed enclave to a stale persistent state, significantly threatening both safety and availability. Existing defenses face a fundamental tradeoff: they either impose substantial overhead on critical consensus paths, reducing throughput and increasing latency, or incur prolonged recovery delays, hurting availability.
  We present the first systematic taxonomy of rollback-resilient recovery for confidential BFT consensus, distilling prior approaches into four categories. We further expose their inherent limitations. Guided by this detailed analysis, we design CHIMERA, a protocol-aware recovery framework that breaks this tradeoff. Our key insight is that rollback protection in consensus systems should not be uniform. Different types of persistent states differ fundamentally in their state distribution, update behavior, and representation form. CHIMERA separates persistent state into metadata and logs according to these protocol-level properties and applies distinct recovery mechanisms to each type. We formally model CHIMERA in Maude and verify its safety and liveness properties. We implement it on Braft and ZooKeeper using Intel TDX, and evaluate it in both LAN and WAN settings. Results show that CHIMERA achieves higher throughput, lower recovery latency, and better availability than state-of-the-art rollback-resilient baselines.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces CHIMERA, a rollback-resilient recovery framework for confidential BFT consensus systems that outperforms existing solutions in throughput, recovery latency, and availability.
AI.motivation: Existing TEEs-based confidential BFT consensus systems struggle with state continuity during recovery, where compromised hosts can rollback to outdated states, leading to safety and availability issues.
AI.method: The authors devised a taxonomy detailing rollback-resilient recovery methods, identified limitations, and proposed CHIMERA that categorizes persistent states into metadata and logs with distinct recovery approaches. Formal modeling in Maude and implementation using Intel TDX validated the design.
AI.result: CHIMERA demonstrated higher throughput, reduced recovery latency, and enhanced availability in both LAN and WAN settings when compared to current rollback-resilient approaches.
AI.conclusion: CHIMERA resolves the tradeoff in existing defenses, enhancing the safety and availability of confidential BFT consensus systems by leveraging protocol-aware recovery mechanisms.

title: A Preliminary Model for Managing Technical Debt in an Agile Environment
summary: This paper presents a preliminary model for managing involuntary technical debt in agile environments by formulating, in an integrated way, the dynamics among backlog, debt, velocity, and economic value. The work distinguishes initiated but unfinished functional debt from a simple defect back log and from rework, interprets productivity degradation as technical-debt interest, and derives the naive maximum-remediation policy in order to show its limitations against an intertemporal value-based decision. On this basis, a dynamic policy uk is proposed to balance new development and remediation; a decreasing marginal-value structure is incorporated; and the model is extended to discrete, inhomogeneous items. Exploratory validation through sensitivity analysis and MonteCarlo simulation shows behavior consistent with the economic intuition of the model. Finally, the limits of the formulation are made explicit: its macroscopic nature, its dependence on organizationally stable parameters, its assumption of intertemporal rationality, and its requirement of weak coupling among stories.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes a dynamic model for managing involuntary technical debt in agile environments by balancing new development and remediation via an economic-value approach.
AI.motivation: The study aims to address the lack of integrated models for tackling involuntary technical debt in agile settings by accounting for its interplay with backlog, velocity, and economic value.
AI.method: The authors develop a dynamic policy involving sensitivity analysis and Monte Carlo simulation to explore the economic trade-offs between development and technical-debt remediation.
AI.result: The model demonstrates behavior consistent with economic principles, showing the effectiveness of balancing development and remediation strategies to manage technical debt.
AI.conclusion: While promising, the model has limitations such as its macro-level approach, dependence on stable parameters, and assumptions about intertemporal rationality and story coupling.

title: Improving the sharpness in neural network-based parametric post-processing of ensemble forecasts
summary: Statistical post-processing has proven to be an effective tool in improving ensemble forecast of different weather variables. Case studies show that post-processing can remedy the typically underdispersive and potentially biased behaviour of the ensemble while optimizing a proper scoring rule expressing the forecast skill. The price of these positive effects is generally a deterioration in sharpness; the width of the central prediction intervals and the uncertainty of the predictions are increasing, especially for shorter lead times. This work aims to reduce the extent of the latter phenomenon for neural network-based parametric post-processing methods by extending the network's loss function with a penalty term. We demonstrate the effect of the proposed technique for 2m temperature ensemble forecasts of the European Centre for Medium-Range Weather Forecasts downloaded from the EUPPBench benchmark dataset and verified against synoptic observations. Here, the predictive distribution is Gaussian, and we use the continuous ranked probability score (CRPS) as loss function. The case studies confirm a substantial relative decrease ($8.2\%-12.5\%$) in the width of the nominal central prediction interval compared to the width of the predictive distribution computed without the penalty term, while there is no deterioration in the mean CRPS of probabilistic forecasts and in the RMSE of the predictive mean.

AI.tldr: This study enhances neural network-based post-processing for ensemble weather forecasts by introducing a penalty term to the loss function, thereby reducing prediction intervals without compromising forecast quality.
AI.motivation: Statistical post-processing improves ensemble weather forecasts by addressing inherent biases and underdispersion but often results in reduced sharpness and increased prediction intervals, necessitating a solution to maintain sharpness.
AI.method: The study integrates a penalty term into the loss function of neural network-based parametric post-processing methods. It evaluates the approach using 2m temperature forecasts from the European Centre for Medium-Range Weather Forecasts and the EUPPBench dataset, employing Gaussian predictive distributions and CRPS as the loss function.
AI.result: The proposed approach achieved an 8.2%-12.5% reduction in the width of the nominal central prediction intervals without degrading the mean CRPS of probabilistic forecasts or the RMSE of the predictive mean.
AI.conclusion: Adding a penalty term to the loss function in post-processing effectively improves sharpness, making weather forecasts more precise while maintaining accuracy and reliability.

title: Perceptive Behavior Foundation Model: Adapting Human Motion Priors to Robot-Centric Terrain
summary: Humanoid behavior foundation models aim to acquire reusable whole-body control policies from broad human motion priors, enabling a single controller to produce diverse and expressive behaviors. However, existing motion-centric foundation policies largely assume that the reference motion is already physically compatible with the robot's surroundings. This assumption breaks when the demonstrator, operator, and robot inhabit different environments: a human motion may specify the intended behavior, but not the footholds, clearance, body height, or contact timing required by the robot's local terrain. We introduce \emph{Perceptive Behavior Foundation Model} (Perceptive BFM), a terrain-aware humanoid control framework that grounds human motion priors in robot-centric perception. The model preserves raw kinematic motion references as the behavioral interface, while using local terrain observations to adapt contacts, posture, and timing. To provide scalable terrain supervision, we develop \emph{terrain-conformal reference synthesis} (TCRS), which converts locomotion-oriented human motion clips into terrain-consistent references through contact-aware foothold construction, foot-geometry-aware swing optimization, support-aware root reconstruction, collision repair, and multi-point inverse kinematics. We then train a blind adapted-reference teacher and transfer its terrain-conformal behavior to a deployed raw-reference student through target-frame action alignment. The student is an identity-gated Transformer tracker whose terrain features enter through residual pathways initialized to preserve the motion-tracking prior and trained to produce local corrections only when needed.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces "Perceptive Behavior Foundation Model" (Perceptive BFM), a terrain-aware humanoid control framework that adapts human motion references to the robot's local environment.
AI.motivation: Existing humanoid behavior foundation models often assume that human motion priors are physically compatible with the robot's surroundings, which is not always true when robots and demonstrators operate in different terrains.
AI.method: The authors propose a novel framework that leverages human motion priors in conjunction with robot-centric terrain perception. They introduce terrain-conformal reference synthesis (TCRS) to adapt human motion clips into terrain-consistent references and use teacher-student training with Transformers to implement terrain-aware humanoid behavior.
AI.result: The framework enables precise terrain-aware adaptation of humanoid controls, preserving diverse human motion while effectively managing local corrections through terrain perception and learned adaptations.
AI.conclusion: Perceptive BFM offers a promising approach to bridging the gap between human motion priors and real-world terrain constraints, creating scalable, reusable control policies for humanoid robots in diverse environments.

title: Crayotter: Traceable Multi-Agent Workflows for Long-Form Video Editing
summary: Editing a long-form video from heterogeneous footage requires more than selecting clips: an agent must preserve narrative intent across material preparation, timeline construction, post-production, and revision while leaving enough evidence to diagnose failures. We present \textbf{Crayotter}, an open-source multimodal multi-agent system for prompt-driven video editing. Crayotter organizes production into three phases: coverage-aware material preparation, artifact-based editing research, and tool-grounded timeline execution. Each phase externalizes inspectable artifacts, including coverage reports, multimodal analyses, editing blueprints, tool calls, and intermediate renders. These artifacts make an editing run traceable and allow failed segments to be diagnosed and selectively revised instead of requiring a full restart. We evaluate Crayotter on 23 editing themes against CapCut-Mate and CutClaw. Under human evaluation, Crayotter achieves an average score of 3.40/5, compared with 2.44 and 1.70 for the two baselines, with consistent gains in theme alignment, narrative coherence, and editing smoothness. We additionally describe a replayable trajectory schema and verifiable reward design that prepare these workflows for future policy optimization. Code, traces, and examples are publicly available at https://github.com/idwts/Crayotter.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces Crayotter, a system for prompt-driven video editing that organizes the editing process into three traceable phases, and it outperforms baseline methods in human evaluations.
AI.motivation: To address the challenges in long-form video editing, where maintaining narrative intent while diagnosing and revising is complex, necessitating a structured and inspectable editing framework.
AI.method: The method involves the creation of Crayotter, a multi-agent system with three editing phases – material preparation, editing research, and timeline execution – that generate externalized, diagnosable artifacts for better revision management.
AI.result: Crayotter performed significantly better than two baseline systems, CapCut-Mate and CutClaw, achieving higher scores on theme alignment, narrative coherence, and editing smoothness via human evaluation.
AI.conclusion: Crayotter's structured artifact-based system improves video editing workflows by enhancing traceability, enabling selective revisions, and preparing for future optimization through replayable schemas and verifiable rewards.

title: CAPruner: Conceptual-Adjacent Scene Graph Pruner for Enhancing 3D Spatial Reasoning of Large Language Models
summary: Large language models (LLMs) have recently been applied to 3D vision-language (3D-VL) tasks, which require spatial reasoning to identify target objects relative to anchors. Scene graphs are commonly employed to represent such relations, but reasoning over complete graphs incurs high token costs and computational inefficiencies, motivating the need for pruning. Existing pruning methods primarily rely on spatial proximity and often remove task-relevant relations, thereby undermining reliable spatial reasoning. To address these limitations, we derive a key requirement for scene graph pruning: preserving spatial relations that are most pertinent to the specific 3D-VL task. Guided by this insight, we propose the Conceptual-Adjacent Scene Graph Pruner (CAPruner). CAPruner integrates fuzzy semantic relevance with spatial proximity to estimate the importance of relations, enabling the selection of critical relations in a task-specific context. Moreover, to avoid costly relation-level annotations, CAPruner is trained by supervising the aggregated scores of each node's incident edges. Extensive experiments demonstrate that CAPruner effectively preserves relations essential for spatial reasoning, leading to substantial performance improvements of LLMs on 3D-VL tasks. Code is available at https://github.com/fz-zsl/CAPruner.

AI.tldr: This paper addresses efficient scene graph pruning for 3D vision-language tasks involving spatial reasoning, introducing the CAPruner method that balances semantic relevance and spatial proximity to retain critical relations.
AI.motivation: The motivation is to improve spatial reasoning in 3D vision-language tasks while overcoming computational inefficiencies caused by reasoning over full scene graphs.
AI.method: The method involves the CAPruner, which uses fuzzy semantic relevance combined with spatial proximity to identify task-specific important relations, offering supervised learning of aggregated edge scores to avoid costly annotations.
AI.result: CAPruner preserves essential spatial relations for reasoning and significantly boosts LLM performance on 3D vision-language tasks.
AI.conclusion: CAPruner offers an efficient and reliable pruning mechanism, enhancing spatial reasoning and computational efficiency in 3D-VL tasks.

title: Beyond Goodhart's Law: A Dynamic Benchmark for Evaluating Compliance in Multi-Agent Systems
summary: The rapid evolution of Large Language Models (LLMs) from passive assistants to autonomous, execution-capable agents has introduced critical operational risks. Most current evaluation frameworks neglect procedural compliance, leading to ''Machiavellian'' behaviors where agents strategically violate safety rules to maximize rewards - a direct manifestation of Goodhart's Law. To address this blind spot, we introduce MAC-Bench, a dynamic, adversarial benchmark designed to evaluate the procedural alignment of multi-agent systems under realistic pressure. We propose the SERV(Seed - Evolve - Refine - Verify) pipeline, an ``Agent-as-a-Benchmark'' paradigm that transforms unstructured legal texts into executable, contamination-free scenarios. By synthesizing holographic sandbox environments and injecting calibrated social-engineering pressure vectors, MAC-Bench forces agents into Pareto-optimal trade-offs between task success and regulatory adherence. We introduced novel metrics: the Compliance-Weighted Success Rate (CSR) and the Machiavellian Gap (MG), and conducted a comprehensive evaluation of state-of-the-art frontier models to reveal the pervasive trade-offs between success and compliance.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces MAC-Bench, a benchmark to measure procedural alignment of agents, and addresses risks of "Machiavellian" behaviors under real-world pressures.
AI.motivation: To mitigate operational risks caused by language model agents violating safety rules for reward maximization, an issue neglected by existing frameworks.
AI.method: Developed MAC-Bench, an adversarial benchmark, and the SERV pipeline to translate legal texts into realistic procedural compliance scenarios.
AI.result: Proposed metrics (CSR and MG) and tested it on advanced models, revealing trade-offs between procedural compliance and task success.
AI.conclusion: MAC-Bench effectively measures agents' compliance-behavior and highlights the challenge of balancing regulatory adherence with effectiveness.

title: Training-Inference Kernel Contracts: Bounding Divergence in Post-Training and Deployment
summary: A modern post-training pipeline often writes one symbol for its policy, pi_theta, while evaluating it through two different programs: a training kernel optimized for autograd and an inference kernel optimized for low-precision, fused, dynamically batched serving. In finite precision, these kernels can induce different distributions at identical weights, with the gap concentrated on slices that aggregate benchmarks under-represent. This paper proposes kernel contracts: a contract-first framework for specifying acceptable divergence between K_train and K_inf. A contract C = (N, S, R, O, Pi) combines numerical, statistical, runtime, and observability clauses with an escalation policy from violations to routing actions. We derive a chain of bounds from logit drift to total-variation distance to bounded reward drift, and specialize it to RL post-training, where per-token importance-ratio drift yields a bound on policy-gradient bias under explicit support and norm assumptions. We also describe a four-stage promotion pipeline, online routing loop, and minimal YAML DSL for contract artifacts. This is a framework and vocabulary paper; we do not report production-scale empirical validation.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes kernel contracts to manage divergence issues in post-training pipelines during model evaluation between training and inference kernels.
AI.motivation: To address the divergence caused by differences in training and inference kernels in post-training pipelines, especially when evaluated in finite precision.
AI.method: Introduces kernel contracts that specify numerical, statistical, runtime, and observability tolerances, along with policy escalation procedures for divergence handling.
AI.result: Derives mathematical bounds connecting divergence measures (e.g., logit drift to reward drift) and outlines applications in reinforcement learning post-training.
AI.conclusion: Presents kernel contracts as a novel framework to formalize tolerances in model evaluation pipelines, aiming to minimize divergence but does not include large-scale empirical results.

title: Concepts in Practice: C++ MPI Bindings for the HPC Ecosystem. From a Standardizable Core to a Composable Interface
summary:
The official C++ MPI bindings were removed from the standard in 2008, leaving a gap that numerous third-party libraries have attempted to fill. However, existing wrappers typically cover only a limited subset of MPI or target specific use cases, falling short of a general-purpose solution. A recent conceptual paper proposed general design principles for modern C++ bindings based on C++20 concepts, without committing to a concrete interface.
  We present the first concrete realization of these principles in a layered architecture. At the foundation, we define a core layer: refined C++20 concepts formalizing the MPI standard's notion of data buffers, automatic mapping of standard C++ constructs, non-intrusive customization points for third-party types, and concept-based wrappers for MPI procedures. The result is a low-level native C++ MPI interface that works directly with STL containers, is highly extensible, and lends itself to standardization. Built on this core, we present KaMPIng-v2 -- a C++ MPI library offering the convenience and memory-safety of KaMPIng with composable, pipe-based syntax inspired by C++ ranges for efficient, boilerplate-free MPI programming. Finally, we demonstrate the core layer's broad applicability by designing lightweight adapters for GPU and performance-portability libraries, making the HPC ecosystem a first-class citizen in MPI. Kokkos views, Thrust device vectors, and SYCL buffers can be passed directly to MPI procedures, with adapter logic remaining self-contained.
  All contributions are backed by a fully functional open-source reference implementation, demonstrating the practical viability of the proposed design.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces a modern C++ MPI library leveraging C++20 concepts for a comprehensive and extensible solution.
AI.motivation: To address the lack of official C++ MPI bindings since 2008 and limitations of existing third-party wrappers.
AI.method: Developed a layered architecture with core layer concepts, integration of user-friendly features via KaMPIng-v2, and lightweight adapters for GPU and HPC libraries.
AI.result: Produced a highly extensible, memory-safe C++ MPI library with features like STL container compatibility and GPU support.
AI.conclusion: The proposed design formalizes modern principles for C++ MPI bindings, demonstrating practicality and broad applicability with open-source implementation.

title: DD-GEPA: Prompt Optimization for Dialogue Disentanglement Focusing on Task Instruction and Utterance Representation
summary: Multi-party chat often contains interleaved dialogues because multiple participants can discuss different topics at the same time. Dialogue disentanglement addresses this problem by separating an entangled utterance sequence into coherent dialogues. While large language models (LLMs) are promising for this task, they still struggle with dialogue disentanglement and achieve low accuracy. This paper proposes an automatic prompt optimization for LLM based dialogue disentanglement. We decompose the prompt into three components: task instruction, utterance representation, and output instruction, and optimize them using GEPA, an optimization method for compound AI systems. Experiments on benchmark datasets show that the optimized prompts improve dialogue disentanglement accuracy over the original prompts and can surpass hand crafted prompts.

AI.tldr: The paper addresses dialogue disentanglement in multi-party chats via optimized prompts for large language models, improving accuracy over original and handcrafted prompts.
AI.motivation: Multi-party chats lead to interleaved dialogues, creating challenges in accurately disentangling coherent conversations. Current large language models struggle with low accuracy in this task.
AI.method: The authors propose decomposing prompts into three components—task instruction, utterance representation, and output instruction—and using an optimization method (GEPA) to refine these prompts.
AI.result: Optimized prompts significantly improve dialogue disentanglement accuracy on benchmark datasets, even outperforming handcrafted prompts.
AI.conclusion: The optimization approach demonstrates the potential of prompt engineering in enhancing LLM performance for dialogue disentanglement tasks in multi-party chats.

title: Rank Intervals for Leaderboards: A Hierarchical Framework for Model Evaluation
