Our approach integrates three complementary components: (1) a Graph-Temporal Encoder that employs graph attention and recurrent units to aggregate spatial neighborhood effects and capture the co-evolving historical dynamics of environmental context and community structure; (2) a Context-Anchored Latent Alignment mechanism that structures the latent space using a label-activated mixture prior and supervised contrastive learning, actively clustering species based on shared environmental preferences; and (3) an Imbalance-Aware Decoupled Decoding module that utilizes Asymmetric Loss to focus learning on hard, rare species samples, preventing mode collapse in the long tail. Experiments on the large-scale eBird dataset, curated with domain experts, demonstrate that our framework significantly outperforms state-of-the-art baselines, particularly in predicting rare species and revealing interpretable species interactions.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces STELLAR, a framework addressing spatio-temporal biodiversity modeling challenges by combining graph-temporal encoding, latent space alignment, and imbalance-aware decoding to improve accuracy on rare species prediction.
AI.motivation: The study aims to enhance biodiversity modeling by addressing limitations in current approaches, which fail to consider the spatio-temporal nature of environmental drivers and species distributions jointly, and struggle with rarity-induced imbalance.
AI.method: STELLAR integrates spatio-temporal graph encoding, latent space alignment using contrastive learning, and imbalance-focused decoding to improve species distribution predictions with attention to rare species.
AI.result: STELLAR significantly outperforms existing methods, effectively predicting rare species and uncovering interpretable community interactions on the large eBird dataset.
AI.conclusion: STELLAR successfully addresses key challenges in JSDM by jointly optimizing environmental and species distribution dynamics, offering a robust tool for biodiversity monitoring and conservation.

title: Beyond Scalar Rewards by Internalizing Reasoning into Score Distributions
summary: Reward models are central to text-to-image post-training, but visual preference is subjective and better represented as a distribution over rubric scores than as a deterministic scalar. Existing scalar, score-token, and pairwise reward models over-compress uncertainty and fine-grained score differences, while reasoning-based generative rewards provide stronger judgments but are costly to deploy and difficult to use as direct optimization signals. We propose Z-Reward, a teacher-student reward modeling framework that decouples reasoning-heavy judgment from efficient reward deployment. The teacher is a large VLM that uses reasoning to infer rubric-aligned score distributions, and is trained with Group-wise Direct Score Optimization (GDSO), which combines policy-gradient rewards from distribution expectations with direct pointwise and pairwise supervision on score distributions and score gaps. The student is trained with Reasoning-Internalized Score Distillation (RISD), which transfers the teacher's reasoning-conditioned score distribution into a compact VLM without requiring explicit reasoning chains at inference time. On our internally annotated evaluation set, the 27B GDSO teacher reaches 89.6% human preference accuracy, outperforming SFT, RewardDance, and GRPO, while the 9B RISD student reaches 88.6%, outperforming the OPD baseline and closely matching the larger teacher. We further show that Z-Reward can serve as a differentiable reward signal for text-to-image optimization, yielding a 41.3% net human-preference improvement over the SFT baseline.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes a Z-Reward model framework for text-to-image post-training by decoupling heavy reasoning judgments from efficient reward deployment, achieving superior performance with scalable application.
AI.motivation: Text-to-image reward models struggle to handle subjective visual preferences and often compress uncertainties. A more nuanced, efficient approach is needed for robust visual preference representation.
AI.method: Z-Reward employs a teacher-student framework. The teacher uses a large vision language model (VLM) trained through Group-wise Direct Score Optimization (GDSO) for reasoning and score distributions. The student's Reasoning-Internalized Score Distillation (RISD) transfers this judgment into a smaller VLM for efficient deployment.
AI.result: The GDSO teacher model achieved 89.6% human preference accuracy, and the RISD student closely matched it at 88.6%, outperforming various baselines. Z-Reward also led to a 41.3% net improvement in human preference for text-to-image optimization.
AI.conclusion: Z-Reward effectively integrates reasoning-heavy models with scalable, efficient systems for robust text-to-image post-training, demonstrating significant improvements in human preference metrics.

title: Self-Harness: Harnesses That Improve Themselves
summary: The performance of LLM-based agents is jointly shaped by their base models and the harnesses that mediate their interaction with the environment. Because different models exhibit distinct behaviors, effective harness design is inherently model-specific. Yet agent harnesses are still largely engineered by human experts, a paradigm that scales poorly as modern LLMs become increasingly diverse and rapidly evolving. In this paper, we introduce Self-Harness, a new paradigm in which an LLM-based agent improves its own operating harness, without relying on human engineers or stronger external agents. We operationalize Self-Harness as an iterative loop with three stages: Weakness Mining, which identifies model-specific failure patterns from execution traces; Harness Proposal, which generates diverse yet minimal harness modifications tied to these failures; and Proposal Validation, which accepts candidate edits only after regression testing. We instantiate Self-Harness on Terminal-Bench-2.0 using a minimal initial harness and three base models from diverse families: MiniMax M2.5, Qwen3.5-35B-A3B, and GLM-5. Across all three models, Self-Harness consistently improves performance, with held-out pass rates increasing from 40.5% to 61.9%, 23.8% to 38.1%, and 42.9% to 57.1%, respectively. Qualitative analyses further show that Self-Harness does not simply add generic instructions, but effectively turns model-specific weaknesses into concrete, executable harness changes. These results suggest a path toward LLM-based agents that are not merely shaped by their harnesses, but can also participate in reshaping them.

AI.tldr: Self-Harness is introduced as a method for LLM-based agents to autonomously improve their harnesses, significantly boosting performance across various models.
AI.motivation: The diverse and rapidly evolving nature of modern LLMs makes human-engineered harnesses insufficient and inefficient for model-specific optimization.
AI.method: Self-Harness is implemented as an iterative process with three stages: Weakness Mining (identifying failures), Harness Proposal (modifying harnesses), and Proposal Validation (testing edits to prevent regressions).
AI.result: Self-Harness improves held-out pass rates across three distinct LLM models, achieving significant percentage increases, and effectively addressing model-specific weaknesses.
AI.conclusion: The approach enables LLMs to autonomously refine their own operational frameworks, reducing dependency on human engineers and paving the way for self-adaptive LLM systems.

title: Multi-Turn Evaluation of Deep Research Agents Under Process-Level Feedback
comment: Published as a workshop paper at SCALE - ICML 2026 (Oral)
summary: Existing benchmarks for deep research agents (DRAs) assess only single-shot outputs, ignoring a key question: can DRAs improve their reports when guided by feedback? To investigate this, we conduct a multi-turn evaluation of DRAs under two feedback settings: self-reflection, in which the agent revises its report without any external diagnostic signal, and process-level feedback, in which the agent receives guidance targeting gaps in its research strategy. To enable process-level feedback, we design Research Gap Inference (RGI), a method that analyzes patterns of satisfied and unsatisfied rubric criteria to infer research-process gaps. Our analysis reveals three key findings: (i) under self-reflection, agents incorporate and regress on rubric criteria at nearly equal rates, yielding negligible net improvement; (ii) a single round of process-level feedback yields substantial gains, raising the normalized score by approximately $8$-$15$ points and yielding a roughly $35$-$40\%$ incorporation rate; (iii) these gains do not compound over subsequent turns, as agents regress on up to $24\%$ of previously satisfied criteria when rewriting the full report to address remaining gaps. Even with targeted guidance, reliable multi-turn improvement remains out of reach for the DRA architectures we evaluate. Our code and results are publicly available at https://github.com/sabharwalrishabh/Multi-Turn-Evaluation-of-DRAs.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces a multi-turn evaluation for deep research agents (DRAs) assessing improvement from feedback, revealing that while feedback improves outputs, reliable multi-turn improvement remains challenging.
AI.motivation: The authors aim to address the gap in evaluating DRAs based on their ability to refine their outputs iteratively when provided with feedback, unlike existing benchmarks that only assess single-shot performance.
AI.method: The authors evaluate DRAs under `self-reflection` (internal revisions without external feedback) and `process-level feedback` (based on guidance extracted via a Research Gap Inference (RGI) method analyzing unmet rubric criteria).
AI.result: Key findings include negligible net improvement under self-reflection, significant gains from one round of process-level feedback (~8-15 points improvement in normalized scores), but a lack of compounding gains over multiple turns.
AI.conclusion: Despite the benefit of process-level feedback, multi-turn iterative improvement remains unreliable for current DRA architectures.

title: A Joint Finite-Sample Certificate for Adaptive Selective Conformal Risk Control
summary: Selective predictors answer on confident inputs and abstain elsewhere; deploying one safely needs a single finite-sample certificate that simultaneously upper-bounds the selected risk, lower-bounds the acceptance probability $\pacc$ above a floor $\pmin$, and lower-bounds the deployment utility. This certificate must be valid under adaptive threshold selection from a finite grid of $m$ pairs on $\ncert$ samples. We give such a certificate for bounded, possibly non-monotone losses by treating the selected risk directly as a ratio rather than through a Hoeffding-style range bound. The construction couples three confidence bounds: a variance-adaptive empirical-Bernstein bound on the ratio risk, a Clopper--Pearson bound on acceptance, and a two-sided closeness bound on utility. Together they lower-bound the certified policy's utility absolutely and to within $2\gammau$ of the best over the \emph{certified set}, both non-vacuous whenever feasible; a regime-scoped third leg matches an external oracle, informative only where the risk margin $\gammar < α$ and vacuous at the headline operating points. Relative to the range-only Hoeffding-ratio construction this sharpens the acceptance-floor dependence from $1/\pmin$ to $1/\sqrt{\pmin}$, and a closed-form corollary identifies a per-pair regime in which our risk bound dominates a Hoeffding conformal risk control (Hoeffding--CRC) selective bound. Empirically, on ImageNet (three ResNets) and COCO val 2017 panoptic, the certificate opens a $+22$ pp certified-acceptance frontier over Hoeffding--CRC and is ${\approx}10{\times}$ tighter than a non-vacuous matched-valid baseline; these gains are regime-scoped, not universal, and absent on ADE20K. The certifier runs in $O(\ncert m)$ time.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes a method for creating finite-sample certificates for selective predictors that ensure valid risk bounds, acceptance probabilities, and utility guarantees, outperforming prior methods in certain conditions.
AI.motivation: Selective predictors need robust certification to ensure their deployment is safe and effective under adaptive threshold selection.
AI.method: The authors provide a certificate based on three confidence bounds: empirical-Bernstein bound, Clopper--Pearson bound, and a closeness bound. This approach sharpens constraints compared to prior Hoeffding-style constructions.
AI.result: Empirical evaluation shows significant improvement in certified acceptance rates (+22 pp over Hoeffding--CRC) and tighter risk bounds (~10× improvement) on datasets like ImageNet and COCO, albeit with limitations on others like ADE20K.
AI.conclusion: The proposed certification method effectively improves selective predictor performance in specific regimes, offering sharper bounds and better utility under feasible conditions.

title: Edge-Constrained UAV Small-Object Detection with P2 Enhancement and Quantum-Inspired Lightweight Structure Search
summary: Unmanned aerial vehicle (UAV) object detection requires compact detectors that retain small-object details under onboard computation and memory constraints. Repeated downsampling inlightweight networks weakens shallow spatial information, while manually adding attention orfusion modules may increase cost without stable gains. This study analyzes YOLOX-Nano underedge-deployment constraints by combining a P2 high-resolution detection branch with a quantum-inspired evolutionary algorithm (QIEA) for lightweight structure screening. The search space isdefined by lightweight priority and task specificity, and the evaluation jointly considers accuracy,floating-point operations (FLOPs), latency, memory consumption, and recall. On VisDrone, theP2 branch increases APamall by 31.10% over the YOLOX-Nano baseline. Compared with NanoDet-Plus with similar model size, YOLOX-Nano+-P2 improves APs0.ss by 17.5% and APamal by 44.9%.The QIEA-selected candidate obtains the highest Recallso, but +P2 remains the strongest AP-oriented variant after full training. Full 100-epoch verification of Random-best, GA-best, andSA/QUBO-best candidates further shows that proxy rankings do not necessarily transfer to finalAPse9s. These results support using P2 as the main small-object enhancement path and QIEA as alightweight tool for candidate screening and accuracy-cost analysis. The source code, configurationfiles, diagnostic scripts, and summarized results are available at https://github.com/Ming23233/UAV-QIEA-Edge-Detection

AI.tldr: The study combines YOLOX-Nano with a high-resolution detection branch (P2) and a quantum-inspired evolutionary algorithm (QIEA) to improve small-object detection under UAV constraints. Results on VisDrone show significant performance enhancements.
AI.motivation: To improve lightweight UAV object detection, particularly for small objects, under limitations of onboard computation and memory while balancing detection accuracy and cost.
AI.method: YOLOX-Nano is enhanced with a P2 high-resolution detection branch. A QIEA is utilized to optimize structure screening. Evaluations consider multiple performance metrics like accuracy, latency, and memory usage.
AI.result: The P2 branch significantly increases performance metrics (e.g., APamall improves by 31.10%). Compared with NanoDet-Plus, YOLOX-Nano+-P2 shows a 17.5% gain in APs0.ss and 44.9% in APamal on the VisDrone dataset.
AI.conclusion: Using P2 enhances small-object detection, and QIEA proves effective for lightweight screening and performance-cost optimization. Proxy ranking methods may not directly predict final accuracy.

title: Emergence of Context Characteristics Sensitivity in Large Language Models
summary: During instruction fine-tuning (IFT), large language models (LLMs) learn to follow instructions by using the provided context to answer a query. While prior work has studied how context characteristics correlate with context usage by the LLM, this analysis has been limited to inference time, leaving open how these relationships are acquired in the first place. Here, we measure how models' sensitivity to such characteristics shifts across successive IFT stages: supervised fine-tuning (SFT), direct preference optimization (DPO), and reinforcement learning with verifiable rewards (RLVR). Experiments across four models and three datasets show that SFT makes models more likely to use contexts that are easy to understand, such as containing high length, context-query similarity, and fluency. Post-SFT dynamics may either reinforce or resolve these preferences depending on the training dataset. Our findings reveal that context usage is actively reshaped at each IFT stage, and designing a balanced IFT dataset is important in ensuring robust context utilization of instruction-tuned models.

AI.tldr: This paper investigates how large language models (LLMs) modify their context usage sensitivity throughout various instruction fine-tuning (IFT) stages: supervised fine-tuning (SFT), direct preference optimization (DPO), and reinforcement learning with verifiable rewards (RLVR).
AI.motivation: To understand how instruction fine-tuning stages influence an LLM's ability to use provided context effectively, given that prior research has mostly focused on this aspect during inference rather than training.
AI.method: Experiments were conducted across four language models and three datasets to analyze the changes in context sensitivity for attributes like length, context-query similarity, and fluency during SFT, DPO, and RLVR stages.
AI.result: Models trained via SFT were more likely to utilize easily interpretable contexts, and subsequent fine-tuning stages either reinforced or mitigated these preferences based on the training dataset used.
AI.conclusion: Each IFT stage actively reshapes how models utilize context, highlighting the need for designing balanced IFT datasets to ensure robust, instruction-following behavior in LLMs.

title: Collaborative Human-Agent Protocol (CHAP)
summary: Foundation models are moving from response generation into operational roles. They plan across steps, call tools, request human input, coordinate with other agents, and increasingly carry responsibility for work that affects customers, claims, code, contracts, and clinical decisions. Production deployments are no longer one human supervising one model. They are multi-human, multi-agent collaborations that cross teams, time zones, and trust boundaries. The technical surface for this collaboration remains weakly specified. When an agent drafts a response and a human edits it before it ships, the moment of human judgement is the most valuable signal in the system. In current practice it is recorded, if at all, in application code, chat threads, ticket comments, and tribal memory. Two protocol standards address adjacent concerns: MCP standardises agent access to tools and data, and A2A standardises agent-to-agent interoperability. Neither defines the shared workspace in which humans and agents perform accountable work together. This paper presents CHAP, the Collaborative Human-Agent Protocol. Under CHAP, the override that used to vanish into a chat thread becomes a structured event carrying a diff, a rationale, and a content hash. The handoff between shifts becomes a portable envelope rather than a pinned message. The human approval of an agent's draft becomes a non-repudiable signed decision that can be replayed years later. The protocol achieves this through a small Core (workspaces, participants, tasks, artefacts, and an append-only evidence log) together with composable profiles that add review, modes, routing, deliberation, handoff, identity, signatures, and transparency-backed audit as deployments require them. Specification, reference implementation, conformance suite, and worked examples are available at: https://github.com/BrightbeamAI/chap

AI.tldr: This paper introduces CHAP, a protocol for improving collaboration between humans and foundation AI models by formalizing interactions into structured events for better accountability and auditability.
AI.motivation: The motivation is to address the lack of structured collaboration frameworks for multi-human, multi-agent systems, ensuring clarity and accountability during joint tasks, especially in critical contexts.
AI.method: The authors propose CHAP, a protocol consisting of a Core system (workspaces, participants, tasks, artifacts, evidence logs) and composable profiles (review, handoff, audit, etc.), enabling structured collaboration between humans and AI agents.
AI.result: CHAP transforms previously informal events, like human edits or approvals of AI-generated content, into structured, auditable records, enhancing accountability and interoperability in human-agent collaborations.
AI.conclusion: CHAP fills a gap in the technical infrastructure for human-agent collaboration, offering a structured, audit-friendly protocol that supports transparency and accountability in complex, multi-agent tasks.

title: Driving Video Retrieval for Complex Queries with Structured Grounding
summary: Video retrieval at scale is central to data curation and safety validation in autonomous driving, where users want to find not only scenes but also dynamic events such as cut-ins and hard braking. Existing vision-language and keyword-based retrieval methods often miss these events because the relevant motion may not be explicitly described in text or captured by lexical overlap. Rule-based retrieval can encode such events more directly, but it is brittle: generated or hand-written rules often fail when their assumptions do not match real driving data. We propose STRIVE-D, a data-calibrated retrieval framework for driving videos. It uses weakly labeled in-domain videos to estimate when a query rule is reliable, adapt rules that mismatch observed data, and fuse calibrated rule scores with vision-language and keyword-based retrieval signals. Across three driving benchmarks, including newly released human-annotated event data on DrivingDojo, STRIVE-D delivers up to 84% relative improvement in top-1 accuracy over state-of-the-art methods.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces STRIVE-D, a framework for better video retrieval in autonomous driving by calibrating rule-based, vision-language, and keyword-based methods.
AI.motivation: Existing retrieval methods for autonomous driving videos struggle with accurately finding dynamic events due to lack of explicit textual overlap and limitations of rule-based systems.
AI.method: The STRIVE-D framework leverages weakly labeled in-domain videos to calibrate the reliability of retrieval rules, adapt mismatched rules, and fuse multiple retrieval approaches for improved accuracy.
AI.result: STRIVE-D achieves up to 84% relative improvement in top-1 accuracy compared to current state-of-the-art retrieval methods across multiple driving datasets.
AI.conclusion: STRIVE-D provides a robust and adaptable retrieval system for driving scenarios, significantly enhancing dynamic event detection and retrieval capabilities in autonomous driving contexts.

title: Overcoming Decoder Inconsistencies in Whisper for Dravidian and Low-Resource Languages
comment: Accepted at INTERSPEECH 2026, 5 pages, 1 figure, 5 tables
summary: Multilingual ASR models such as Whisper perform well on high-resource languages but exhibit substantially higher Word Error Rates (WER) for Dravidian languages compared to Indo-Aryan ones. Through linguistic and dataset analysis, we show that Dravidian languages have longer words, higher vocabulary diversity, and lower repetition, resulting in sparse token distributions and frequent character-level substitution errors. Baseline fine-tuning further reveals decoder imbalance between self-attention (linguistic context) and cross-attention (acoustic cues). Although synthetic token-repetition experiments indicate potential gains, they are impractical. Motivated by these observations, we introduce two decoder-level enhancements: Weighted-Attention, which adaptively balances attention sources, and Self-Conditioning, which reinjects intermediate predictions to improve token consistency. Experiments demonstrate consistent WER reductions for low-resource and agglutinative languages.

AI.tldr: This paper showcases methods to improve multilingual ASR performance, especially for Dravidian languages, using decoder-level enhancements.
AI.motivation: Address the relatively poor performance of multilingual ASR models like Whisper on Dravidian languages due to linguistic factors and imbalances in decoder attention.
AI.method: Introduce two enhancements: Weighted-Attention for balancing self- and cross-attention, and Self-Conditioning for reinjecting intermediate predictions.
AI.result: Experiments demonstrate reductions in Word Error Rates for low-resource and agglutinative languages.
AI.conclusion: Combining linguistic insights and novel decoder strategies, the methods improve ASR accuracy for challenging language groups.

title: SIGA: Self-Evolving Coding-Agent Adapters for Scientific Simulation
summary: Advanced scientific simulators expose specialized input languages that turn simulation goals into executable configurations, but learning them can cost domain scientists hours to days. We study simulator setup as a problem of agent-tool interface grounding: what minimal simulator-specific adaptations are needed for an off-the-shelf coding agent to operate real scientific software? Our intuition is that coding agents already know how to navigate files, edit code, run commands, and repair outputs, but they lack the simulator's executable contract: its vocabulary, structural constraints, validation rules, and termination conditions. We introduce SIGA, a Simulator-Interface Grounding Adapter that supplies this contract through retrieval, procedural memory, in-trajectory validation, and validation-enforced termination. We primarily evaluate SIGA on GEOS, an open-source multiphysics simulator used in subsurface science. SIGA produces a complete GEOS deck in about five minutes with TreeSim above 0.90, matching an extended-budget human expert who took about three hours, a roughly 36x wall-clock speedup. On a harder held-out set, grounding raises TreeSim from 0.720 to 0.789, a roughly 10% relative gain over the bare agent, and can reduce the across-seed standard deviation by 16x. Self-evolution further improves SIGA by rewriting adapter contents from prior trajectories, yielding the highest held-out GEOS mean and matching or outperforming the strongest hand-designed configuration. Transfers to OpenFOAM and LAMMPS show that the dominant mechanism shifts by interface: validation matters most when structural completeness is the bottleneck, while memory and retrieval matter most when domain correctness is the bottleneck. These results suggest that lightweight, self-improvable grounding layers can turn general coding agents into practical operators of scientific software.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces SIGA, a tool that enables coding agents to set up scientific simulators efficiently by providing essential simulator-specific guidance, significantly reducing setup time and improving accuracy.
AI.motivation: The authors aim to reduce the steep learning curve domain scientists face when setting up advanced scientific simulators by adapting coding agents to better understand and operate these tools.
AI.method: SIGA is developed as a grounding adapter that enriches coding agents with simulator-specific information, including retrieval, procedural memory, in-trajectory validation, and validation-enforced termination, allowing them to navigate and configure scientific simulation software effectively.
AI.result: SIGA achieved up to a 36x reduction in setup time compared to human experts on the GEOS simulator, improved configuration accuracy on harder tasks, and showed effective transfers to other simulators like OpenFOAM and LAMMPS while demonstrating scenario-specific strengths of its components.
AI.conclusion: Lightweight and self-improving grounding layers like SIGA can transform generic coding agents into capable operators of diverse scientific software, making simulator setup more efficient and accessible.

title: Routine laboratory trajectories encode the onset of organ-level complications in cancer
summary: Routine laboratory panels drawn during cancer treatment constitute longitudinal physiological recordings of organ function, yet their temporal structure is discarded by single-timepoint prognostic tools. A transformer trained on 2,777,595 laboratory measurements from 3,905 patients with multiple myeloma or ovarian cancer predicted the two-year onset of 162 treatment-associated complications, including therapy-related myelodysplastic syndromes, spanning eight clinical categories, achieving 1.5- to 6.1-fold enrichment above prevalence at the group level. It matched or outperformed non-sequential baselines across grouped endpoints (AUROC gains up to +0.11), demonstrating that longitudinal laboratory trajectories capture evolving complication-specific physiology inaccessible from isolated measurements. Predictions generalised across both cancers, divergence concentrating in disease-specific complications, and biomarker masking recovered signatures consistent with established pathophysiology. External validation on MIMIC-IV and MMRF CoMMpass confirmed transferability across independent healthcare systems (AUROC up to 0.85). Routine oncological laboratory data encode organ deterioration weeks to months before clinical onset, enabling complication-specific surveillance without additional testing infrastructure.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces a transformer model trained on longitudinal cancer treatment lab data to predict treatment-associated complications, achieving superior predictive performance and generalizability to independent datasets.
AI.motivation: To overcome limitations of single-timepoint prognostic tools by leveraging the temporal structure of longitudinal laboratory data for predicting treatment-associated complications in cancer patients.
AI.method: The study uses a transformer model trained on 2,777,595 laboratory measurements from 3,905 patients with multiple myeloma or ovarian cancer to predict the two-year onset of 162 treatment-related complications. The model incorporates longitudinal data trajectories, compares its performance with non-sequential baselines, and validates outcomes on external datasets.
AI.result: The model achieved 1.5- to 6.1-fold enrichment, AUROC up to 0.85 across external validations, and demonstrated superior performance over non-sequential baselines, with divergent performance seen in disease-specific complications.
AI.conclusion: Longitudinal laboratory data from routine cancer treatment contain predictive signals for organ deterioration and treatment complications, and can be harnessed for early complication-specific surveillance without requiring new testing infrastructure.

title: HDRAgent: An Agentic Framework for Multi-Exposure HDR Imaging
summary: Most existing multi-exposure HDR methods follow a fixed feed-forward reconstruction paradigm, making them prone to ghosting artifacts in complex dynamic scenes. To address this issue, we propose HDRAgent, the first agent-driven framework for HDR imaging, which adaptively selects reconstruction strategies according to the current scene conditions. Specifically, to provide scene-specific prior knowledge, we introduce a fine-grained contextual knowledge matching (FCM) module. This module leverages multimodal large language model (MLLM)-derived scene perception to retrieve relevant historical cases and tool knowledge, organizing them into structured evidence for MLLM-based adaptive tool scheduling. In addition, we propose a perception--distortion feedback mechanism that transforms post-execution quality assessment and artifact diagnosis into structured feedback, which is accumulated in historical memory to help subsequent contextual knowledge refinement and strategy selection. Furthermore, considering that extreme motion can invalidate alignment methods, we design an agent-guided generative alignment strategy that uses MLLM-based dynamic-region parsing to reconstruct unreliable contents in non-reference frames under reference-frame guidance. Experiments demonstrate that HDRAgent effectively reduces ghosting and local artifacts while achieving competitive or superior objective performance and visual quality.

AI.tldr: HDRAgent is a novel agent-driven framework that improves multi-exposure HDR imaging by adaptively choosing strategies for specific scenes, significantly reducing ghosting artifacts in dynamic situations.
AI.motivation: To overcome the ghosting artifacts commonly found in existing fixed feed-forward multi-exposure HDR methods when dealing with complex dynamic scenes.
AI.method: The method involves three innovations: a fine-grained contextual knowledge matching module, a perception--distortion feedback mechanism, and an agent-guided generative alignment strategy. These leverage multimodal large language models for scene-specific decisions, feedback-based quality refinement, and dynamic region reconstruction.
AI.result: The proposed HDRAgent achieves significant reductions in ghosting and local artifacts while maintaining or surpassing state-of-the-art methods in both objective performance and visual output quality.
AI.conclusion: HDRAgent successfully addresses the limitations of traditional HDR imaging methods in dynamic environments by introducing adaptive, feedback-driven, and scene-specific reconstruction strategies, setting a new benchmark for HDR imaging.

title: From Genes to Tokens: a GWAS-inspired Approach for Interpretable Stylometric Analysis
summary: This short paper introduces a stylometric interpretation method inspired by genome-wide association studies (GWAS). Each "gene" token's association with "phenotype" authorship is tested using logistic regression with multiple-comparison correction. Applied to English, German, and Russian corpora, the method detects statistically significant lexical markers distinctive of individual authors.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes a logistic regression-based method to identify distinctive lexical markers for authorship across languages.
AI.motivation: Inspired by genome-wide association studies (GWAS), the paper aims to explore how stylometric markers can systematically identify authorship traits within textual corpora.
AI.method: Utilizing logistic regression with multiple-comparison correction, the authors test associations between individual 'gene' tokens and authorship 'phenotypes.'
AI.result: The approach successfully identifies statistically significant lexical markers that distinguish individual authors in English, German, and Russian corpora.
AI.conclusion: The study demonstrates that GWAS-inspired stylometric methods can effectively detect author-specific lexical markers across multiple languages.

title: Evaluation Cards: An Interpretive Layer for AI Evaluation Reporting
summary: AI evaluation results are produced at scale but reported inconsistently across leaderboards, model cards, benchmark papers, and company blogs. The cost is interpretive: readers cannot reliably compare results across sources, identify what a report omits, or trace an aggregate claim to its underlying evidence. Recent efforts address isolated components but leave three gaps: they cover only narrow slices of the evaluation lifecycle and do not compose into a single interpretable record; they specify static representations that do not differentiate the questions different stakeholders bring to the same evidence; and they remain proposals on paper, lacking the extraction infrastructure required for adoption at scale. We present \EvalCards{}, an operational reporting layer that composes benchmark metadata, evaluation run data, and model metadata into a unified record. We (1) derive a reporting schema from a structured review of 52 papers and 10 stakeholder interviews, (2) implement four interpretive signals (reproducibility, documentation completeness, provenance and risk, and score comparability), rendered through reader modes calibrated to research and non-research audiences, and (3) deploy a monitoring tool that applies \EvalCards{} across 5,816 models, 635 benchmarks, and 101,843 results, surfacing systematic gaps in current reporting practice.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces EvalCards, a reporting tool designed to unify and standardize AI evaluation records by addressing current gaps in reporting practices.
AI.motivation: The motivation is to resolve inconsistencies in how AI evaluation results are reported across various mediums, which hinders reliable comparisons, omissions identification, and evidence traceability.
AI.method: The authors developed EvalCards by deriving a schema from literature reviews and interviews, implemented interpretive signals for diverse audiences, and deployed it to evaluate large-scale AI model data.
AI.result: EvalCards was applied to over 5,000 models and 100,000 results, exposing significant reporting gaps and demonstrating its capability to standardize evaluation practices.
AI.conclusion: EvalCards successfully provides a unified, interpretable reporting framework for AI evaluations, indicating its potential to streamline reporting standards and enhance reliability.

title: A Theoretical Analysis of Memory and Overfitting Phenomena in Stochastic Interpolation Models
summary: This paper provides a theoretical account of memorization in stochastic interpolation models. By leveraging closed-form expressions for the optimal velocity field and the associated score function, we show that, in the continuous-time oracle setting, both deterministic and stochastic generation processes recover training samples. Under Euler discretization, generated samples remain centered around training samples, with deviations controlled by the step size. We further analyze generation in the presence of estimation errors and show that accumulated estimation errors control the endpoint deviation from the training set. These results imply that the generated sample admits a representation as a training sample perturbed by three controlled terms: a discretization-induced bound, an estimation-error-induced bound, and stochastic Gaussian noise. Based on this characterization, we provide theoretical definitions of overfitting and underfitting in generative models. Synthetic simulations support our theoretical findings.

AI.tldr: The paper theoretically studies how memorization occurs in stochastic interpolation models and provides a characterization using controlled perturbations. It supports claims with synthetic simulations.
AI.motivation: The study seeks to understand the mechanism of memorization in stochastic interpolation models through a theoretical lens, aiming to shed light on how generated samples relate to training data.
AI.method: The approach involves deriving closed-form expressions for optimal velocity fields and score functions, analyzing continuous-time oracle settings, and examining discretization effects and estimation errors.
AI.result: The generated samples are shown to be perturbed versions of the training samples, with deviations influenced by discretization, estimation errors, and Gaussian noise. New definitions of overfitting and underfitting for generative models are proposed.
AI.conclusion: The findings help bridge theoretical understanding and practical outcomes in generative modeling, offering a refined way to evaluate quality and generalization with respect to overfitting and underfitting.

title: Illumination-Invariant Anomaly Detection for Sub-Canopy UAV Multispectral Point Clouds
summary: Unmanned Aerial Vehicle (UAV) multispectral point clouds (MPC) provide high-dimensional spatial-spectral data for sub-canopy target detection; however, their efficacy is significantly compromised by severe illumination heterogeneity caused by vegetation shadows. To address this, we propose a prior-free anomaly detection framework capable of robustly handling lighting variations. First, we formulate solar angle estimation as an inverse optimization problem. By coupling spectral indices with a ray-tracing model, this strategy achieves Prior-Free Shadow Extraction without relying on flight metadata, effectively distinguishing dark objects from true shadows. Second, to mitigate spectral distortions, we introduce an Illumination-Consistent Sparse Representation mechanism. Unlike standard reconstruction methods, we construct a background dictionary strictly from neighbors sharing the same illumination state. This constraint effectively disentangles spectral reflectance from lighting variations, ensuring that targets are represented solely by physically consistent background points. Experimental results indicate that the proposed method significantly improves the separability between anomalies and background in complex forest environments, demonstrating superior performance over state-of-the-art baselines. This framework is particularly suited for identifying camouflaged military targets, mapping fallen tree trunks, and uncovering archaeological ruins hidden beneath dense foliage.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes a comprehensive framework to enhance Unmanned Aerial Vehicle (UAV) multispectral point cloud data for detecting sub-canopy targets under uneven lighting conditions.
AI.motivation: Illumination heterogeneity caused by vegetation shadows limits the effectiveness of UAV multispectral point clouds for detecting sub-canopy objects.
AI.method: The framework involves two main strategies: (1) a solar angle estimation method using inverse optimization and spectral indices for prior-free shadow extraction, and (2) Illumination-Consistent Sparse Representation to mitigate spectral distortions by using a background dictionary derived from similarly illuminated neighbors.
AI.result: The proposed method improves anomaly detection performance in complex forest settings and outperforms existing methods.
AI.conclusion: The framework succeeds in robust target detection regardless of lighting variations and is useful for applications such as detecting camouflaged military targets, mapping tree trunks, and uncovering hidden archaeological ruins.

title: OpenBibleTTS: Large-Scale Speech Resources and TTS Models for Low-Resource Languages
summary: Recent advances in neural text-to-speech (TTS) and multilingual speech generation have substantially improved synthetic speech quality, yet these gains remain unevenly distributed across the world's languages. Existing models are still dominated by a small set of high-resource languages, while many studies of low-resource TTS are simulated on artificially downsampled high-resource corpora that do not reflect the orthographic variation and limited phonetic coverage encountered in genuinely underrepresented settings. As such, we introduce OpenBibleTTS, which is a large-scale benchmark for low-resource speech synthesis spanning 37 underrepresented languages. Moreover, a systematic comparison of various TTS architectures and large-scale speech generation models is conducted across in-domain Biblical text and out-of-domain material. Results show that no single system dominates across languages and metrics: Gemini-TTS achieves the highest listener ratings on most evaluated languages, but monolingual EveryVoice models trained on OpenBibleTTS remain strongest for intelligibility and are preferred in several African languages, while open from-scratch systems degrade sharply on out-of-domain text, revealing a persistent gap between broad multilingual coverage and reliable synthesis quality in underserved linguistic communities. We complement automatic evaluation with subjective human judgments, and open-source all processed datasets, alignments, and trained models to support future low-resource TTS research.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces OpenBibleTTS, a large-scale benchmark to study low-resource text-to-speech (TTS) synthesis across 37 underrepresented languages, revealing limitations in synthesis quality for underserved languages.
AI.motivation: The study addresses the uneven distribution of TTS advancements, largely benefiting high-resource languages while neglecting low-resource ones, and critiques the reliance on artificially simulated low-resource settings.
AI.method: The authors introduce OpenBibleTTS, a benchmark dataset for underrepresented languages, conduct systematic evaluations of TTS architectures and models using in-domain and out-of-domain text, and include both automatic and human evaluations.
AI.result: No single TTS system consistently outperformed others across languages and metrics. Gemini-TTS achieved high listener ratings for most languages, but monolingual EveryVoice models excelled for intelligibility, especially in African languages. Models trained from scratch demonstrated poor performance on out-of-domain text.
AI.conclusion: The study highlights a gap between achieving broad multilingual TTS coverage and ensuring reliable synthesis quality for low-resource languages. By open-sourcing datasets and models, the paper seeks to advance research in this area.

title: XAInomaly: Explainable and Interpretable Deep Contractive Autoencoder for O-RAN Traffic Anomaly Detection
comment: 22 pages, 9 Figures, Submitted to Journal (First revision completed)
summary: Generative Artificial Intelligence (AI) techniques have become integral part in advancing next generation wireless communication systems by enabling sophisticated data modeling and feature extraction for enhanced network performance. In the realm of open radio access networks (O-RAN), characterized by their disaggregated architecture and heterogeneous components from multiple vendors, the deployment of generative models offers significant advantages for network management such as traffic analysis, traffic forecasting and anomaly detection. However, the complex and dynamic nature of O-RAN introduces challenges that necessitate not only accurate detection mechanisms but also reduced complexity, scalability, and most importantly interpretability to facilitate effective network management. In this study, we introduce the XAInomaly framework, an explainable and interpretable Semi-supervised (SS) Deep Contractive Autoencoder (DeepCAE) design for anomaly detection in O-RAN. Our approach leverages the generative modeling capabilities of our SS-DeepCAE model to learn compressed, robust representations of normal network behavior, which captures essential features, enabling the identification of deviations indicative of anomalies. To address the black-box nature of deep learning models, we propose reactive Explainable AI (XAI) technique called fastshap-C.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes the XAInomaly framework, which leverages a Semi-supervised Deep Contractive Autoencoder and a reactive Explainable AI technique for anomaly detection in Open Radio Access Networks.
AI.motivation: The paper aims to address challenges in O-RAN management, such as complexity, scalability, and interpretability, by introducing an explainable method for effective anomaly detection.
AI.method: The research features a Semi-supervised Deep Contractive Autoencoder to capture normal network behaviors and anomalies, combined with the fastshap-C Explainable AI technique for interpretability.
AI.result: The XAInomaly framework demonstrates an ability to detect network anomalies reliably while ensuring the model’s predictions are explainable and accessible to operators.
AI.conclusion: XAInomaly integrates generative AI with explainable techniques for interpretable anomaly detection, improving the management of complex and dynamic O-RAN systems.

title: Physics-Guided Dual Decoding and Spectral Supervision for Global 3D Hydrometeor Prediction
summary: While global data-driven models excel at predicting continuous atmospheric variables, three-dimensional hydrometeor forecasting remains challenging due to the zero-inflated, long-tailed distributions of these variables. Standard deep learning optimization often yields overly smooth forecasts, attenuating extreme events and spatial textures. We propose PredHydro-Net, a physics-guided dual-decoding framework that mitigates this smoothing. To resolve multi-variable optimization conflicts, it employs a decoupled architecture where macroscopic thermodynamic and dynamic fields unidirectionally modulate hydrometeor generation. By integrating wavelet-based frequency decoupling, spectral amplitude matching, and adversarial training, the model achieves a favorable trade-off between quantitative accuracy and spatial fidelity. In a 72-h global evaluation, PredHydro-Net outperforms both spatiotemporal deep learning baselines (Earthformer and PredRNNv2) and the operational Global Forecast System (GFS) in extreme-event detection and spectral representation. Furthermore, it demonstrates strong climatological consistency with Global Precipitation Measurement (GPM) satellite retrievals. The model reasonably reproduces the three-dimensional cloud structures in extreme weather events, such as Hurricane Ian. Feature attribution confirms its dependence on physical precursors such as relative humidity and wind convergence, offering a robust, physics-informed approach to long-tailed atmospheric prediction.

AI.tldr: PredHydro-Net, a physics-guided dual-decoding framework, improves global 3D hydrometeor forecasting, particularly for extreme weather events, outperforming deep learning and operational models.
AI.motivation: 3D hydrometeor forecasting is challenging due to zero-inflated, long-tailed distributions, and standard deep learning approaches result in overly smooth predictions that miss extreme events.
AI.method: The authors propose PredHydro-Net, a decoupled dual-decoding architecture that uses wavelet frequency decoupling, spectral amplitude matching, and adversarial training, guided by physics-based modulations.
AI.result: PredHydro-Net outperforms baseline spatiotemporal models (Earthformer, PredRNNv2) and GFS in extreme-event detection, spectral representation, and consistency with GPM satellite data.
AI.conclusion: PredHydro-Net effectively improves the fidelity of hydrometeor predictions, particularly for extreme weather scenarios, by integrating physics-informed approaches and advanced training techniques.

title: An Enhanced Geometric-Spectral Feature Learning Framework for Airborne Multispectral Point Cloud Classification
summary: Multispectral point cloud (MPC) is composed of 3D spatial-spectral information, which holds tremendous potential for accurate land-cover classification. However, the representation power of classification models is limited by inherent high-dimensional and heterogeneous spatial-spectral information, unbalanced sample distribution, and inter-class spectral similarity of airborne MPCs. We build two MPC datasets and propose an enhanced geometric-spectral feature learning framework based on attentions for airborne MPC classification. A key component in our model is a two-stream feature fusion method with attention mechanisms, which enhances the representation capability of spatial-spectral features from high-dimensional heterogeneous MPCs. The first stream aims to extract position-encoded global spectral features with fusion self-attention, and the second stream comprises a multikernel point convolution and feature aggregation attention to extract spectral-guided geometric features. We then develop a residual attention fusion block to integrate the most informative geometric-spectral features from the two parallel streams. Another important contribution of this work is a joint loss function to improve the learning ability on unbalanced and interclass similar samples. Experimental results on two airborne MPC datasets demonstrate the effectiveness of the proposed method compared with the state-of-the-art methods. Furthermore, the codes and datasets used in this paper will be made available freely at https://github.com/HITlixian/TGRS_GSFF.

AI.tldr: This paper presents a novel attention-based feature learning framework for classifying airborne multispectral point clouds (MPCs). It addresses challenges like high-dimensional data, sample imbalance, and spectral similarity.
AI.motivation: The study addresses the challenge of accurately classifying airborne MPCs, which contain high-dimensional and heterogeneous spatial-spectral data, unbalanced sample distribution, and interclass spectral similarity.
AI.method: The method includes a two-stream feature fusion framework leveraging attention mechanisms for learning spatial-spectral features. One stream extracts global spectral features via fusion self-attention, while the other focuses on spectral-guided geometric features using multikernel point convolution and an attention mechanism. A residual attention fusion block integrates features, and a joint loss function tackles unbalanced and similar samples.
AI.result: The proposed framework demonstrated improved classification performance on two newly built airborne MPC datasets, outperforming state-of-the-art methods.
AI.conclusion: The study shows that enhancing spatial-spectral feature learning through attention mechanisms and addressing imbalanced data issues strengthens airborne MPC classification. Code and datasets are released for broader use.

title: UXBench: Benchmarking User Experience in AI Assistants
summary: As AI assistants serve millions of users daily, evaluating user experience (UX) beyond general model capability has become increasingly important. We present UXBench, the first user-centric benchmark grounded in real user feedback signals for evaluating preference alignment and dialogue generation. The benchmark consists of three interconnected tasks, UX Judge, UX Eval, and UX Recovery, with 7,400 test instances extracted from over 70K interaction logs of a mainstream Chinese AI assistant. The dataset closely reflects real user distributions, covering 8 scenarios, 83 domains, and diverse failure patterns that pose severe challenges. Extensive experiments on 26 frontier language models provide novel insights into how well models perceive user experience and how improvements in model capability contribute to better dialogue engagement. Through comprehensive analysis of model behavior and performance gaps, we show that user feedback prediction is a learnable capability, where a reward model trained from in-the-wild feedback signals can achieve well-calibrated accuracy. We further document the systematic biases of LLM-as-a-judge evaluation protocols and compare typical response strategies that directly affect user experience. UXBench establishes a new evaluation landscape and calls for greater attention to tailored UX optimization, contributing to a user-centric scaling law that shapes the success of AI assistants.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces UXBench, a user-centric benchmark incorporating real user feedback to evaluate AI assistant performance, particularly in preference alignment and dialogue generation.
AI.motivation: The paper is motivated by the need to assess AI assistants not just based on general model capabilities but on real-world user experience to better understand and enhance user interaction.
AI.method: The authors developed UXBench, a benchmark based on 7,400 test instances from over 70K interaction logs. It includes tasks in UX evaluation, such as user feedback prediction, and experiments with 26 language models are conducted.
AI.result: The study reveals that user feedback prediction is a learnable skill, and a reward model trained on real-world feedback can achieve accurate predictions. Additionally, biases in LLM-as-a-judge protocols were identified.
AI.conclusion: UXBench provides a new standard for evaluating AI assistant UX and emphasizes the importance of tailored UX optimizations for improving real-world interaction outcomes.

title: BRAIN: Bayesian Reasoning via Active Inference for Agentic and Embodied Intelligence in Mobile Networks
summary: Future sixth-generation (6G) mobile networks will demand artificial intelligence (AI) agents that are not only autonomous and efficient, but also capable of real-time adaptation in dynamic environments and transparent in their decisionmaking. However, prevailing agentic AI approaches in networking, exhibit significant shortcomings in this regard. Conventional deep reinforcement learning (DRL)-based agents lack explainability and often suffer from brittle adaptation, including catastrophic forgetting of past knowledge under non-stationary conditions. In this paper, we propose an alternative solution for these challenges: Bayesian reasoning via Active Inference (BRAIN) agent. BRAIN harnesses a deep generative model of the network environment and minimizes variational free energy to unify perception and action in a single closed-loop paradigm. We implement BRAIN as O-RAN eXtended application (xApp) on GPU-accelerated testbed and demonstrate its advantages over standard DRL baselines. In our experiments, BRAIN exhibits (i) robust causal reasoning for dynamic radio resource allocation, maintaining slice-specific quality of service (QoS) targets (throughput, latency, reliability) under varying traffic loads, (ii) superior adaptability with up to 28.3% higher robustness to sudden traffic shifts versus benchmarks (achieved without any retraining), and (iii) real-time interpretability of its decisions through human-interpretable belief state diagnostics.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes the BRAIN agent powered by Bayesian reasoning and Active Inference, demonstrating superior adaptability, transparency, and resilience compared to DRL methods for 6G mobile networks.
AI.motivation: To address limitations of existing DRL-based AI agents in 6G networking, such as lack of explainability and brittle adaptation under dynamic conditions.
AI.method: The authors introduce the BRAIN agent, which uses a Bayesian deep generative model and Active Inference to unify perception and action for real-time decision-making. It is implemented as an O-RAN xApp on a GPU-accelerated testbed.
AI.result: BRAIN outperformed standard DRL baselines in dynamic radio resource allocation, achieving 28.3% higher robustness to traffic shifts without retraining and providing interpretable decision-making.
AI.conclusion: BRAIN offers an advanced, adaptable, and transparent solution for AI challenges in 6G networks, outperforming existing DRL methods in robustness and decision interpretability.

title: EinSort: Sorting is All We Need for Tensorizing LLM
summary: Tensor networks provide efficient representations for compressing large neural networks. By carefully designing shapes and topologies, they can significantly reduce memory and computational costs. However, identifying implicit low-rank structures in large foundation models remains challenging due to their enormous scale and un-structured weight distributions. We propose an adaptive tensorization method that discovers inherent low-rank structure in a target tensor by index ordering. Experiments on weight and KV-cache compression demonstrate improved reconstruction quality compared to baselines.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces an adaptive tensorization approach for detecting low-rank structures in neural network tensors to enhance compression efficiency.
AI.motivation: Current methods struggle with identifying implicit low-rank structures in large foundation models due to their size and unstructured weight distributions.
AI.method: The paper proposes a tensor network-based approach that uses adaptive index ordering to uncover inherent low-rank structures in target tensors.
AI.result: Experiments reveal that this method enhances reconstruction quality in weight and KV-cache compression when compared to baseline approaches.
AI.conclusion: The adaptive tensorization method improves compression efficiency and reconstruction quality in large-scale neural networks.

title: A Geometric Framework for Absolute Pose and Velocity Estimation with Event Cameras
summary: Despite the rapid advancements in event-based motion estimation, current geometric methods primarily focus on velocity estimation. However, absolute pose estimation, which is equally crucial for key applications such as robotic navigation and augmented reality, remains relatively underexplored. Consequently, the simultaneous recovery of absolute pose and velocity from event streams remains an open and challenging problem. To address this gap, we propose a geometric framework for absolute pose and velocity estimation by leveraging 3D lines in the scene and the events they trigger. At the core of the framework lie two key geometric constraints: the orthogonality between a 3D line and the normal vector of its corresponding event plane, and the collinearity of an event with the 2D projection of its associated line. Based on these constraints, we present both linear and polynomial solvers for absolute pose estimation. The former enables efficient computation, while the latter provides a globally optimal solution for rotation. For velocity estimation, we develop an efficient linear solver and a more accurate optimization-based solver to recover both angular and linear velocities. Notably, our methods require a minimum of three event-line correspondences to determine the 6-DoF absolute pose or velocities independently. Extensive experiments in simulation and on real-world datasets demonstrate that our methods achieve state-of-the-art performance, with significant improvements in accuracy and computational efficiency compared to existing methods. The demo code is publicly available at https://github.com/Zibin6/EventPoseVelocity.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces a geometric framework to estimate both absolute pose and velocity simultaneously from event streams utilizing 3D lines and event-plane constraints.
AI.motivation: Current geometric methods emphasize velocity estimation while neglecting absolute pose estimation, an essential aspect for robotic navigation and augmented reality.
AI.method: The framework uses two key geometric constraints (orthogonality and collinearity) for linear and polynomial solvers for absolute pose and velocity estimation, requiring only three event-line correspondences.
AI.result: Extensive experiments validate the framework's state-of-the-art performance in accuracy and computational efficiency compared to previous methods.
AI.conclusion: The proposed method fills an important gap by providing highly accurate and efficient solutions for simultaneous pose and velocity estimation from event data.

title: Blockchain Infrastructure for Intelligent Cyber--Physical--Social Systems:Post-Quantum Security, Interoperability, and Trustworthy Data Economies in the Era of Embodied AI
summary: The deployment of embodied artificial intelligence via world-model-based robotics presents a transformative opportunity for blockchain infrastructure, establishing urgent demand for trustworthy data provenance, cross-organizational governance, and incentive-compatible sharing across decentralized ecosystems. Simultaneously, quantum computing advances recognized by the 2025 Nobel Prize in Physics and the Turing Award threaten the cryptographic primitives securing these data economies, creating an interdependent imperative: long-lived verification for embodied AI depends on crypto-agile architectures capable of withstanding quantum adversaries. This tutorial examines blockchain as the coordination layer bridging this dual transition, from financial substrate to foundational Cyber-Physical-Social Systems infrastructure that simultaneously secures against quantum cryptanalysis and enables scalable, trustworthy data economies. The session opens with an immersive AWS Braket demonstration engaging participants with superconducting, trapped-ion, and neutral-atom hardware to assess cryptographic threat timelines and witness ECDSA-to-post-quantum signature transitions. Five integrated modules progress from embodied AI and world-model requirements through quantum hardware reality and evidence-based security migration, to scalable cross-shard architectures via BrokerChain protocols, trustworthy data economies implementing Croissant metadata standards and robotic learning provenance, and industry ecosystem integration for multi-modal cloud deployment. By bridging quantum hardware realities with embodied AI data requirements, this tutorial charts blockchain as unified infrastructure for next-generation decentralized intelligent environments, providing open-source frameworks and roadmaps for architecting quantum-resistant, interoperable, and data-trustworthy systems.

AI.tldr: The paper explores the integration of blockchain as a coordination layer for embodied AI while addressing quantum computing threats to cryptographic security.
AI.motivation: The need for trustworthy data economies in decentralized ecosystems, coupled with threats to cryptographic security posed by quantum computing advances.
AI.method: A tutorial involving demonstrations on quantum hardware, modular sessions on topics like embodied AI and blockchain protocols, and open frameworks for system design.
AI.result: Provision of roadmaps, open-source frameworks, and strategies for architecting quantum-resistant, interoperable systems that support decentralized intelligent environments.
AI.conclusion: Blockchain can serve as a foundational infrastructure integrating quantum-resistant features to support embodied AI and decentralized data economies.

title: Titans-as-a-Layer: Test-Time Memory for Conversational Speech Emotion Recognition
comment: ICML 2026 Workshop on Machine Learning for Audio
summary: Speech emotion recognition (SER) is commonly formulated as utterance-level classification, although conversational emotion depends on a speaker's usual vocal range and the emotional context established by previous utterances. Speech-language models provide strong pretrained acoustic and semantic representations, and can adapts them to SER labels via finetune, but this mechanism still missing per-dialogue state. We study whether test-time neural memory can supply this missing context while leaving the large audio language models (LALMs) backbone intact. Building on Titans, we introduce a plug-and-play Memory-as-a-Layer (MAL) adapter that writes dialogue history into a small neural memory and reads it back as an audio-token-aligned residual update, avoiding changes to the host model's token positions. Across different audio LLMs and emotion recognition datasets evaluations, our design improves SER performs across different evaluation metrics, supporting test-time memory as a residual contextual mechanism for conversational SER.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes a Memory-as-a-Layer (MAL) adapter to enhance speech emotion recognition (SER) by integrating conversational context without altering large audio language models.
AI.motivation: The study aims to address the lack of per-dialogue state and context in conversational SER tasks, which current audio language models (LALMs) struggle to integrate effectively.
AI.method: A plug-and-play MAL adapter is introduced to store dialogue history in a neural memory. This memory is used as a residual update mechanism aligned with audio tokens, requiring no significant modifications to LALMs.
AI.result: The MAL adapter improves SER performance across various audio LLMs and datasets, achieving better results in conversational emotion recognition.
AI.conclusion: Test-time memory, implemented via the MAL adapter, provides an effective mechanism for incorporating conversational context into SER, enhancing the performance of existing LALMs.

title: DiffSight-Former: Modeling Structural Differences and Temporal Dynamics for Glaucoma Progression Prediction
summary: Glaucoma is a leading cause of irreversible blindness worldwide, and early detection from fundus images is critical for effective disease management. While deep learning has achieved promising performance in fundus image analysis, most existing methods rely on single time-point images and fail to capture longitudinal structural and vascular changes associated with disease progression. Sequential fundus images acquired during clinical follow-up provide valuable temporal information; however, current sequential models often struggle to detect subtle early progression signals and commonly depend on fixed-length inputs or diagnostic cues from already glaucomatous images, limiting their clinical utility for early prediction. To address these limitations, we propose DiffSight-Former, a framework for glaucoma progression prediction from sequential fundus images. It incorporates a time-variant feature extraction module based on a fundus-specific foundation model to obtain robust anatomical representations. A multi-structure difference modeling module is introduced to quantify progression-related changes in the optic disc/cup region and retinal vasculature. These representations are integrated with temporal interval embeddings and processed by a time-aware Transformer to model disease progression and estimate the probability of future glaucoma onset. Experiments were conducted on two longitudinal datasets, SIGF (405 sequences) and GRAPE (263 sequences). On SIGF, DiffSight-Former achieved an AUC of 91.54% and a sensitivity of 92.16% for progression prediction. On GRAPE, it achieved an average accuracy of 87.48% across three clinical visual-field progression criteria. Compared with existing approaches, DiffSight-Former demonstrates strong performance and robustness across different temporal settings, highlighting its potential for longitudinal glaucoma monitoring and early risk prediction.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces DiffSight-Former, a novel framework that combines deep learning, temporal modeling, and anatomical representation to predict glaucoma progression using sequential fundus images, achieving robust results across multiple datasets.
AI.motivation: Current methods for glaucoma detection rely on single time-point images and fail to leverage temporal information from sequential fundus images, limiting their utility in predicting early disease progression.
AI.method: The proposed DiffSight-Former framework utilizes a fundus-specific foundation model for time-variant feature extraction, a difference modeling module for anatomical and vascular progression changes, and a time-aware Transformer for temporal progression modeling.
AI.result: DiffSight-Former demonstrated strong performance, achieving a 91.54% AUC and 92.16% sensitivity on the SIGF dataset, and 87.48% average accuracy across three clinical criteria on the GRAPE dataset.
AI.conclusion: DiffSight-Former outperforms existing methods in predicting glaucoma progression, emphasizing its potential application for early and robust glaucoma monitoring in clinical settings.

title: Clinically Grounded Privacy Evaluation of Medical LMs
summary: Medical language models (LMs) can memorize and reproduce protected health information, but privacy evaluations often focus on recovery of training text rather than disclosure under realistic threat models. We introduce a clinically grounded framework that evaluates leakage along a graded axis of adversarial access, ranging from publicly inferable demographics to leaked note fragments. At each tier, we measure verbatim memorization of patient-specific text and semantic leakage of sensitive diagnoses. Applying the framework to an LM pretrained on 378k clinical notes, we find that routine encounter metadata (i.e. name, date of birth, provider, practice, visit date) elicits high rates of verbatim memorization across a patient's timeline and sensitive-diagnosis recovery (AUROC 0.91 for abortion, 0.81 for HIV). At the same time, exact-match memorization can overstate disclosure: 36% of memorized tokens reflect templated documentation. Our work highlights the risks of training on longitudinal clinical data, providing a practical framework for contextual privacy evaluation of medical LMs.

AI.tldr: The paper presents a privacy evaluation framework for medical language models, exposing risks on patient data leakage through both verbatim memorization and semantic inferences.
AI.motivation: To address the limitations of existing privacy evaluations of medical LMs that focus on recovery of training text rather than realistic adversarial disclosure scenarios.
AI.method: The authors propose a clinically grounded privacy evaluation framework along a graded axis of adversarial access, assessing both verbatim patient-specific text memorization and semantic leakage of sensitive diagnoses.
AI.result: Applying the framework to a language model trained on 378k clinical notes, they found high rates of memorization and sensitive diagnosis recovery. However, 36% of what was identified as memorized text reflected templated documentation.
AI.conclusion: Training on longitudinal clinical data poses significant privacy risks, and the proposed framework is a practical tool for assessing these risks in medical language models.

title: OrderDP: A Theoretically Guaranteed Lossless Dynamic Data Pruning Framework
comment: Published as a conference paper at ICLR 2026
summary: Data pruning (DP), as an oft-stated strategy to alleviate heavy training burdens, reduces the volume of training samples according to a well-defined pruning method while striving for near-lossless performance. However, existing approaches, which commonly select highly informative samples, can lead to biased gradient estimation compared to full-dataset training. Furthermore, the analysis of this bias and its impact on final performance remains ambiguous. To address these challenges, we propose OrderDP, a plug-and-play framework that aims to obtain stable, unbiased, and near-lossless training acceleration with theoretical guarantees. Specifically, OrderDP first randomly selects a subset and then chooses the top-$q$ samples, where unbiasedness is established with respect to a surrogate loss. This ensures that OrderDP conducts unbiased training in terms of the surrogate objective. We further establish convergence and generalization analyses, elucidating how OrderDP affects optimal performance and enables well-controlled acceleration while ensuring guaranteed final performance. Empirically, we evaluate OrderDP against comprehensive baselines on CIFAR-10, CIFAR-100, and ImageNet-1K, demonstrating competitive accuracy, stable convergence, and exact control -- all with a simpler design and faster runtime, while reducing training cost by over 40%. Delivering both strong performance and computational efficiency, our method serves as a robust and easily adaptable tool for data-efficient learning. The code is publicly available at https://github.com/shengze-xu/OrderDP.

AI.tldr: OrderDP is a novel data pruning framework designed to reduce training costs by over 40% while maintaining competitive performance. It tackles biased gradient estimation issues and provides theoretical guarantees for stable and unbiased training.
AI.motivation: Existing data pruning methods often select highly informative samples, which can introduce bias in gradient estimation and complicate the analysis of final model performance.
AI.method: OrderDP combines random sampling with the selection of top-$q$ samples to achieve unbiased training based on a surrogate loss, ensuring stable acceleration. The framework is supported by convergence and generalization theoretical analyses.
AI.result: OrderDP demonstrated competitive accuracy, stable convergence, exact control, and faster runtime on datasets like CIFAR-10, CIFAR-100, and ImageNet-1K. It significantly reduced training costs while maintaining strong performance.
AI.conclusion: OrderDP is a computationally efficient and robust data pruning tool with strong performance and adaptability, bolstered by theoretical guarantees and a straightforward design.

title: Decoding Pedestrian Crossing Intention from Egocentric Vision via Vision Language Models
summary: Egocentric vision offers a first-person view of human perception and decision making, yet its potential for traffic-safety prediction remains underexplored. In this work, we study the decoding of pedestrian crossing intentions from short egocentric video clips. We approach this by formulating the task as a closed-ended visual question answering (VQA) problem and leveraging vision language models (VLMs) to predict the pedestrians' intent. We first benchmark three families of state-of-the-art VLMs in a zero-shot setting, finding that they achieve moderate gains over random guessing but exhibit limited higher-level traffic reasoning. Motivated by these findings, we further adapt VLMs to the target task using parameter-efficient fine-tuning. Our results show that the fine-tuned models substantially outperform their zero-shot counterparts and achieve a 9\% accuracy improvement over a specialized transformer-based baseline. Finally, we demonstrate that incorporating additional contextual cues, including ego motion, vehicle motion, and eye gaze, further improves predictive performance. In particular, the fine-tuned Qwen3-VL-2B model guided by eye gaze and ego motion achieves a 14.5% accuracy improvement over the transformer baseline, establishing a new state of the art for egocentric pedestrian intent decoding.

AI.tldr: This paper focuses on predicting pedestrian crossing intentions using short egocentric video clips through vision-language models (VLMs).
AI.motivation: The study aims to enhance traffic safety prediction by leveraging egocentric vision, which provides a unique first-person perspective that has been underutilized for decoding pedestrian intentions.
AI.method: They benchmark state-of-the-art vision-language models (VLMs) in zero-shot settings initially. Subsequently, parameter-efficient fine-tuning is applied for task-specific adaptations. Additional contextual features such as ego motion, vehicle motion, and eye gaze are incorporated into the model.
AI.result: Fine-tuned VLMs outperform their zero-shot counterparts and improve prediction accuracy by 9% over a transformer baseline. Contextual cues like eye gaze further boost performance, leading to a 14.5% accuracy improvement with the Qwen3-VL-2B model.
AI.conclusion: Adapting VLMs and integrating contextual information significantly enhances the capability of egocentric models to predict pedestrian crossing intentions, setting a new standard for traffic safety research.

title: Automated IEP Generation from Traditional Chinese Parent-Teacher Interviews via Corpus-Grounded Feature Diffusion
summary: Writing Individualized Education Programs (IEPs) is a high-labor, knowledge-intensive document burden; English-language research has demonstrated that generative AI can significantly reduce drafting time, yet automated IEP generation in Traditional Chinese remains virtually unexplored due to domain data scarcity, strict privacy regulations, and the absence of local evaluation benchmarks.
