AI.tldr: The paper presents XInsight Lab's winning solution for the 4th MiGA Challenge, achieving a new state-of-the-art result in micro-gesture classification using a multimodal ensemble framework.
AI.motivation: To improve micro-gesture classification accuracy using advanced machine learning techniques, integrating self-supervised learning, and achieving a new state-of-the-art benchmark.
AI.method: The authors propose a multimodal ensemble approach combining a self-supervised RGB model pretrained on 120K unlabeled clips via masked video modeling and fine-tuned on labeled datasets, with multi-stream supervised models from prior solutions.
AI.result: The RGB baseline model achieves 69.224% top-1 accuracy, while the final ensemble reaches 74.419% top-1 accuracy, surpassing the prior state of the art by 1.206 percentage points on the iMiGUE test set.
AI.conclusion: The study underscores the effectiveness of self-supervised RGB representation learning and ensemble strategies, setting a benchmark for future micro-gesture recognition systems.

title: Reformulate LLM Reinforcement Learning for Efficient Training under Black-box Discrepancy
summary: Reinforcement Learning (RL) has emerged as a pivotal post-training paradigm, yet it frequently suffers from unpredictable sub-optimum performance or even training collapses. Recent findings attribute these failures to a hidden train-inference discrepancy (or mismatch), stemming from the disparate underlying engines and architecture. We find that the training policy can actively self-correct such a discrepancy when provided with an appropriate learning signal. Then, we further empirically identify a discrepancy tolerance region: within this region, aggressively narrowing the discrepancy can suppress policy exploration and reduce learning efficiency, whereas outside this region, reducing excessive discrepancy improves optimization consistency and raises the achievable local performance ceiling. According to such findings, we formulate this problem as a Discrepancy-Constrained Markov Decision Process (DCMDP), where reward maximization is coupled with a constraint that aligns training-Inference behavior, achieving stable dual-objective optimization. To adaptively balance performance improvement and discrepancy control, we introduce a Lagrangian relaxation mechanism that dynamically adjusts the relative weight of the two objectives according to the current degree of discrepancy violation. This enables stable dual-objective optimization: the policy is allowed to explore freely within the tolerance region, while being guided back when the discrepancy exceeds the safe boundary. Empirically, DCMDP significantly improves the performance of 8B dense model (Qwen-3-8b) and 30B Mixture-of-Expert model (Qwen-3-30bA3b), and enables a heterogeneous training paradigm, where LLMs can be optimized in high-fidelity training setup while being explicitly aligned for low-cost, resource-constrained inference deployment.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces a new framework to address train-inference mismatch issues in Reinforcement Learning, proposing a Discrepancy-Constrained Markov Decision Process (DCMDP) to improve performance and training stability.
AI.motivation: The paper is motivated by the drawbacks in RL where train-inference discrepancies undermine training stability and performance, causing sub-optimal outcomes and collapses.
AI.method: The authors propose the DCMDP framework, which uses a dual-objective optimization approach with a Lagrangian relaxation mechanism to balance reward maximization and the alignment of training-inference behavior.
AI.result: DCMDP significantly improves the performance of both an 8B dense model (Qwen-3-8b) and a 30B Mixture-of-Expert model (Qwen-3-30bA3b), while allowing for optimization of LLMs in a resource-constrained inference setup.
AI.conclusion: The results show that DCMDP efficiently handles discrepancies, ensuring stable model optimization by keeping exploration within a tolerance region, thereby aligning high-fidelity training with cost-effective inference.

title: See More, Match Better: Multi-Source Feature Fusion for Two-View Correspondence Learning
comment: Correspondence Learning, Multi-Source Feature Fusion, Outlier Removal, Camera Pose Estimation
summary: Two-view correspondence learning aims to distinguish true correspondences (inliers) from false ones (outliers) in image pairs by leveraging their underlying differences. Existing methods mainly rely on coordinate-based geometric consistency. However, they often struggle with pseudo-consistent outliers in scenes containing repetitive structures, textureless regions, or locally similar geometric patterns. To address this limitation, we propose TriMatch, a multi-source feature fusion framework for two-view correspondence learning, which consists of two parts: feature extraction and feature refinement. In feature extraction, TriMatch jointly extracts geometric, texture semantic, and structural semantic features to provide complementary evidence for correspondence discrimination. To bridge the gap between semantic and geometric features, texture and structural semantic features are aligned with geometric features through dedicated Texture-Geometric Alignment and Structural-Geometric Alignment modules, respectively. We further introduce a Semantic-Guided Correspondence Modulation module, which modulates geometric features using semantic information to suppress geometrically plausible but semantically inconsistent correspondences. In feature refinement, a Hierarchical Semantic-Enhanced Correspondence Refinement strategy progressively models correspondence dependencies and recalibrates multi-context feature responses, enabling more reliable inlier-outlier discrimination. Extensive experiments demonstrate the effectiveness, robustness, and generalization capability of TriMatch.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces TriMatch, a framework to improve correspondence learning in images by integrating geometric, texture, and structural semantic features.
AI.motivation: The motivation behind this work is to address the challenge of distinguishing inliers from outliers in images, especially in scenes with repetitive structures, textureless regions, or similar geometric patterns, where traditional methods relying solely on geometric consistency struggle.
AI.method: TriMatch employs a two-part architecture: (1) Feature extraction combines geometric, texture semantic, and structural semantic features, and uses alignment modules to bridge feature types. (2) Feature refinement employs a hierarchical strategy to model dependencies and recalibrate feature responses.
AI.result: TriMatch shows improved effectiveness, robustness, and generalization capability in inlier-outlier discrimination through extensive experimental results.
AI.conclusion: The study concludes that integrating multi-source features and semantic-guided refinement leads to better performance in correspondence learning for challenging image scenes.

title: Scaling Decision-Focused Learning to Large Problems with Lagrangian Decomposition
summary: Decision-focused learning has shown great promise for addressing predict-then-optimize problems, particularly in the presence of under-specified models. However, its practical deployment is often hindered by high computational costs and limited scalability, as it requires solving a constrained optimization problem for each training instance at every iteration. To address these challenges, we propose a novel framework that incorporates Lagrangian decomposition into the decision-focused learning paradigm. Specifically, we introduce a new surrogate objective along with two loss functions for evaluating and training the underlying prediction model. We further propose two variants of our approach, which offer different trade-offs between computational efficiency and solution quality. Our framework can be seamlessly integrated with standard decision-focused learning methods, including Smart Predict-then-Optimize (SPO+) and Implicit Maximum Likelihood Estimation (IMLE). Through experiments on two standard benchmarks, the multi-dimensional knapsack problem and quadratic portfolio optimization, we demonstrate that our approach achieves competitive performance while remaining amenable to parallelization. In particular, it consistently outperforms traditional decision-focused learning methods on large-scale instances, involving up to eight times more variables than those typically considered in related work. The implementation is available at https://github.com/corail-research/DFL-LD.

AI.tldr: Introduces a new framework incorporating Lagrangian decomposition into decision-focused learning to improve scalability and computational efficiency.
AI.motivation: Existing decision-focused learning methods suffer from high computational costs and limited scalability, limiting their practical applicability.
AI.method: Incorporates a new surrogate objective and two loss functions into decision-focused learning. Proposes two variants providing trade-offs between efficiency and solution quality, compatible with SPO+ and IMLE methods.
AI.result: Achieves competitive performance on large-scale optimization problems like the multi-dimensional knapsack problem and quadratic portfolio optimization, outperforming traditional methods and scaling well.
AI.conclusion: The proposed framework advances decision-focused learning by overcoming scalability issues, showing significant improvements in large-scale problem instances while supporting parallelization.

title: EditSSC: Toward Editable Semantic Occupancy Scenes with Unconditional Diffusion Models
summary: 3D semantic scene generation is crucial for autonomous driving applications, yet most methods rely on complex 3D-specific architectures such as triplane encoders and adapted diffusion networks, limiting both their simplicity and their editing capabilities. We propose EditSSC, an editing-ready method for 3D semantic scene generation using 2D Bird's Eye View (BEV) representations and off-the-shelf latent diffusion network. Our approach reshapes 3D semantic occupancy grids into multi-channel BEV images and leverages the quantized autoencoder and UNet from Stable Diffusion with minimal modifications. We perform diffusion on the latents after quantization, which enables training-free editing capabilities. By exploiting class-to-code correspondences in the codebook, our method supports sketch-guided generation, inpainting, and outpainting without any retraining. On SemanticKITTI, EditSSC outperforms existing 3D-specific baselines on unconditional generation, demonstrating that well-established 2D architectures can be effectively repurposed for 3D scene generation and editing.

AI.tldr: This paper presents EditSSC, a method for 3D semantic scene generation and editing using modified 2D BEV representations and off-the-shelf latent diffusion networks.
AI.motivation: To simplify 3D scene generation techniques and make them more versatile for applications like autonomous driving by addressing the limitations of complex, 3D-specific architectures.
AI.method: EditSSC reshapes 3D semantic occupancy grids into BEV images and uses the quantized autoencoder and UNet from Stable Diffusion for diffusion on latent representations, enabling editing without retraining.
AI.result: EditSSC surpasses 3D-specific baselines in unconditional scene generation on the SemanticKITTI dataset.
AI.conclusion: EditSSC demonstrates the potential to leverage existing 2D architectures for effective 3D scene generation and editing, offering a simpler, versatile alternative to traditional 3D methods.

title: Symbolic Reasoning Frameworks Modulate LLM Risk Aversion in Multi-Agent Strategic Settings
comment: 17 pages, 3 figures, 6 tables, 6 listings. Code and data: https://doi.org/10.5281/zenodo.20338937
summary: Large language models exhibit innate behavioral tendencies when deployed as strategic agents -- notably a risk-averse "turtle" bias toward defensive play. We show that symbolic reasoning frameworks, injected as per-round reflective prompts into one agent, differentially modulate this bias and reshape the multi-agent ecosystem to produce framework-specific winner distributions. In a 7-player Warring States Diplomacy variant (41 games, 4 conditions, single-campaign memory accumulation), each framework produces a distinct ecosystem signature: under control, Yan dominates (7/11, 64%); under I-Ching yarrow divination, Yan and Chu co-dominate while Qin is completely suppressed (0/10); under Tarot, Qin dominates (5/10, Fisher vs. pooled p = 0.006); under scrambled-text ablation (incoherent oracle text preserving prompt structure), Qi dominates (5/10, Fisher vs. pooled p = 0.006). The framework-receiving agent (Han) never wins and shows no survival difference across conditions (Fisher p = 1.0), but Tarot consistently elevates Han's peak territory (mean 3.0 SCs vs. 2.1-2.5 others, Kruskal-Wallis p = 0.010). Neither framework's content predicts subsequent actions -- hexagram themes (chi-squared p = 0.95) and Tarot card postures (chi-squared p = 0.69) are both independent of action choice -- suggesting the modulation operates through the reflective process, not content-following. We present this as an observation paper establishing that alignment-framework choice at the agent level produces distinctive system-level consequences in multi-agent settings.

AI.tldr: The paper investigates how injecting symbolic reasoning frameworks into large language model agents can alter their decision-making behaviors and create distinct multi-agent ecosystem dynamics. Experiments demonstrated that these frameworks drive unique winner distributions and gameplay characteristics.
AI.motivation: To explore how introducing symbolic reasoning frameworks affects the inherent behavioral biases of large language models (e.g., risk aversion) when applied as strategic agents in multi-agent ecosystems.
AI.method: The study involves a 7-player Warring States Diplomacy game scenario across 41 games and 4 conditions. One agent received per-round reflective prompts with varied symbolic frameworks (e.g., I-Ching, Tarot, scrambled text), and the effects on the game's ecosystem were analyzed.
AI.result: Each symbolic framework induced distinct winner distributions and ecosystem behaviors. For example, I-Ching balanced domination between some players while suppressing others; Tarot enabled a different player (Qin) to dominate; scrambled text also created unique outcomes. Content of frameworks had no direct predictive power over agent actions.
AI.conclusion: Introducing symbolic reasoning into agents impacts system-level dynamics significantly, with framework selection shaping ecosystem-wide outcomes in multi-agent settings. These effects arise from the reflective process triggered in the agent, rather than direct adherence to the framework's content.

title: Active Flow Expansion for Out-of-Distribution Discovery: from Theory to Molecules
summary: Standard flow and diffusion pre-training matches the distribution of available data (e.g., molecules), which often covers only a small fraction of the valid design space. In generative discovery, however, one aims to sample valid new-to-nature designs, assigned negligible probability under, and thus inaccessible to, standard models fitted to the observed data. To overcome this limitation, we depart from data distribution matching and view a generative model through its generable set: the region it covers with non-negligible probability. This allows to introduce a new learning principle for out-of-distribution flow modeling: enlarging a model's generable set to increase coverage of the valid design space. We propose Active Flow Expansion (ActFlow), a continued pre-training method that employs verifier feedback to expand a pre-trained model over new valid regions by iteratively adapting to synthetic data generated through active exploration in the learned flow representation. Theoretically, we establish to our knowledge first-of-their-kind statistical learning guarantees for out-of-distribution flow modeling, analyzing generable set expansion as a local-to-global reachability process over a learned representation. Empirically, we assess ActFlow with suitable out-of-distribution generative modeling metrics across small organic molecules, mid-sized drug-like molecules, therapeutic peptides, and protein sequence design tasks. Results show that ActFlow expands valid coverage far beyond the region modeled by the initial pre-trained model, significantly outperforming widely adopted synthetic flow pre-training methods.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes a novel method, Active Flow Expansion (ActFlow), to expand the generable set of flow models, enabling out-of-distribution generative modeling beyond the constraints of standard pre-training approaches.
AI.motivation: Existing pre-training methods aim to match the observed data distribution, which limits the ability to generate novel, valid designs outside this observed region. This hinders progress in generative discovery for new-to-nature designs.
AI.method: The authors introduce ActFlow, a method that employs verifier feedback and synthetic data through active exploration to iteratively expand the pre-trained model's generable set, supported by theoretical and empirical analyses.
AI.result: ActFlow was evaluated on tasks involving molecules, peptides, and protein design, and it outperformed conventional flow pre-training methodologies by significantly increasing valid out-of-distribution coverage.
AI.conclusion: ActFlow represents a breakthrough in generative modeling, demonstrating improved exploration of the valid design space and offering significant advantages for novel design tasks.

title: Visual Para-Thinker++: A Single-Policy Multi-Agent Framework for Visual Reasoning
summary: Visual reasoning requires integrating evidence distributed across regions, attributes, and relations, making single-chain reasoning prone to early perceptual commitment and hallucination. We propose Visual Para-Thinker++, a single-policy multi-agent framework in which one shared MLLM policy is instantiated as role-conditioned Main, Worker, and Summary Agents. The Main Agent decomposes the task with fixed allocation patterns; Worker Agents reason in parallel under context isolation; and the Summary Agent reconciles full Worker reasoning traces rather than majority-voting on final labels. The shared policy is trained by Multi-Agent Capability Injection and Role-Decoupled Multi-Agent Optimization, which assign role-specific rewards and advantages to corresponding token segments to reduce gradient conflict among collaborative roles. A native inference engine enables efficient multi-agent rollout through shared visual prefix and KV cache reuse. Across V*, CountBench, the RefCOCO family, and HallusionBench, Visual Para-Thinker++ consistently outperforms single-trajectory and inference-time parallel baselines, with especially strong gains on hallucination-sensitive visual reasoning.

AI.tldr: Visual Para-Thinker++ is a multi-agent framework tackling hallucination-sensitive visual reasoning, outperforming standard methods by employing specialized agents and role-decoupled optimization.
AI.motivation: Visual reasoning often involves integrating evidence across spatial, attributive, and relational dimensions, which can cause hallucination and biases in single-chain reasoning approaches.
AI.method: The paper introduces Visual Para-Thinker++, a single-policy multi-agent system with Main, Worker, and Summary Agents, each specializing in task decomposition, parallel reasoning, and task integration respectively. This framework employs a unique training scheme called Multi-Agent Capability Injection and Role-Decoupled Multi-Agent Optimization, enhancing collaboration and minimizing conflicts.
AI.result: The proposed framework demonstrates superior performance across multiple benchmarks, particularly excelling in reducing hallucination during visual reasoning tasks, compared to traditional methods.
AI.conclusion: Visual Para-Thinker++ represents a significant step forward in hallucination-resistant visual reasoning, combining innovation in both multi-agent system architecture and training methodology.

title: TRACER: Token ReAssignment for Concept ERasure in Generative Recommendation
summary:
Generative recommendation formulates next-item prediction as autoregressive generation over semantic ID (SID) sequences derived from users' historical interactions, making modern recommender systems structurally similar to large language models (LLMs). As privacy and safety concerns grow, these systems increasingly require concept unlearning to remove sensitive or harmful concepts associated with items. However, existing LLM unlearning methods cannot be directly applied to generative recommendation. Unlike word tokens with explicit semantics, SIDs are abstract identifiers that are often shared by both forget and retain items, leading to severe conflicts between concept removal and recommendation utility preservation.
  To address this challenge, we propose TRACER, an end-to-end concept unlearning framework based on token reassignment. Rather than directly suppressing shared SIDs, TRACER reassigns concept-related items to alternative tokens that better facilitate forgetting while minimizing side effects on retained items. We further introduce a coherence regularizer to preserve semantic consistency among retain items during unlearning. Experiments on real-world recommendation datasets demonstrate that TRACER effectively removes target concepts while substantially better preserving recommendation utility than existing unlearning baselines.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes TRACER, a concept unlearning framework for generative recommendation systems. It uses token reassignment to selectively forget sensitive or harmful concepts while preserving recommendation quality.
AI.motivation: Existing concept unlearning methods used in large language models cannot directly apply to generative recommendation systems due to the abstract nature of semantic ID sequences that often overlap between retained and forgotten items, causing conflicts.
AI.method: The paper introduces TRACER, which employs token reassignment for concept-related items and uses a coherence regularizer to ensure semantic consistency for retained items during the concept unlearning process.
AI.result: Experiments on real-world datasets show TRACER effectively forgets sensitive concepts while maintaining much higher recommendation performance than other baseline approaches.
AI.conclusion: TRACER is a practical and effective solution to concept unlearning in generative recommendation systems, balancing the removal of sensitive concepts with the preservation of recommendation utility.

title: Knowledge Graphs and Reasoning LLMs for Finding Simple Yet Effective Transcriptomic Perturbation Predictors
summary: Predicting the effect of an unseen gene knockout perturbation on transcriptomic gene expression remains a highly challenging problem for virtual cell models. Recent progress has been made by leveraging biological knowledge graphs to provide a notion of similar perturbation, allowing for improved extrapolation beyond the set of training perturbations. In this work, we demonstrate that the simplest model to leverage these assumptions - a K-nearest neighbour from the knowledge graph - achieves highly competitive performance on this task, and that this can be improved further using LLMs optimised via reinforcement learning (RL) for predictive performance. Specifically, we find that the K-nearest neighbour approach beats almost all methods on out-of-distribution perturbation prediction, and when a reasoning LLM is trained via RL to make changes to the neighbourhood, it obtains equivalent performance to current state of the art methods on the cell lines from Replogle et al. (2022). We also demonstrate that the RL training improves the LLM's performance on the downstream task of differential expression prediction, despite not being trained on this directly. Overall, these findings demonstrate the efficacy of knowledge graphs as model priors, and show early signs that RL can refine LLMs into generalizable tools for predicting complex biological responses.

AI.tldr: The paper focuses on predicting gene expression changes due to gene knockouts by utilizing K-nearest neighbor methods on knowledge graphs and refining large language models (LLMs) through reinforcement learning (RL).
AI.motivation: The study aims to tackle the complex task of gene knockout effects on gene expressions, focusing on creating methods that generalize to unseen perturbations.
AI.method: A K-nearest neighbor approach was employed using biological knowledge graphs and large language models (LLMs) optimized with reinforcement learning for predictive tasks.
AI.result: The K-nearest neighbor outperformed most in predicting out-of-distribution perturbations, and RL-tuned LLMs matched state-of-the-art results while improving on downstream tasks like differential expression prediction.
AI.conclusion: Knowledge graphs serve as effective model priors, and refined LLMs using RL show promise as general-purpose tools for complex biological prediction tasks.

title: Virtual-point-based Solutions to Handle Generalized Absolute Pose Problem
summary: Multi-camera systems are increasingly adopted in robotics and autonomous navigation for their wide field of view, flexibility, and fault tolerance. Nevertheless, existing PnP solvers fail to handle multiple projection centers. This paper introduces a virtual point formulation that bridges the standard PnP and generalized pose problems, enabling a unified pipeline that transforms existing PnP solvers into generalized pose solvers. Based on this framework, we derive three Virtual-point-based Generalized Pose solvers, namely VGPc, VGPq, and VGPr, leveraging Cayley, quaternion, and rotation-matrix parameterizations, respectively. Extensive experiments demonstrate that the proposed solvers inherit the accuracy and efficiency of original PnP algorithms while significantly outperforming existing generalized solvers. Specifically, VGPc achieves higher estimation accuracy under heteroscedastic noise conditions, VGPq maintains global optimality, whereas VGPr provides superior computational efficiency without accuracy degradation.

AI.tldr: The paper offers a new approach to unify PnP and generalized pose problems via virtual point formulation, enabling multi-camera systems to leverage existing PnP solvers.
AI.motivation: Existing PnP solvers struggle with multi-camera systems featuring multiple projection centers, limiting their application.
AI.method: The authors introduce a virtual point formulation to convert standard PnP solvers into generalized pose solvers, proposing three variants: VGPc, VGPq, and VGPr.
AI.result: Experiments show the new solvers are accurate and efficient like original PnP algorithms, outperforming generalized solvers under various noise and computational conditions.
AI.conclusion: The virtual point-based solvers bridge traditional PnP methods with multi-camera navigation, enhancing efficiency, accuracy, and robustness for robotics applications.

title: Selecting New Measurement Locations to Diversify Traffic-Pattern Coverage: A Real-World Evaluation for Total Traffic Volume Estimation
summary: Accurate measurement of traffic volumes and flows is vital for modern intelligent transportation. However, despite recent technological advances in sensor devices, it is still expensive to install and maintain fixed traffic counters. Therefore, it is restricted to a small portion of location points where the counters can be installed, which severely limits the possibility of grasping and predicting the total traffic volume at a city-wide level. By contrast, devices with location history such as smartphones and connected vehicles are now widely used and provide much wider spatial coverage. However, the data from these devices are usually partial and noisy, so they are not enough to directly estimate total traffic volumes and flows. In this paper, we use the information from these widely available devices to help decide where to place additional traffic counters, and we study how selecting new measurement locations can improve city-wide traffic estimation performance. To achieve this, we propose an algorithm that chooses additional counter locations to increase the diversity of observed traffic signal patterns, rather than simply spreading counters evenly over space. The goal is to capture traffic-pattern types that are rare in the current counter set and to make the collected observations more representative for later estimation and forecasting. We also present a real-world evaluation; in a target city, we select new locations expected to improve traffic prediction, and we then commissioned new field measurements at those locations at our expense. The resulting data led to an improvement in traffic volume estimation accuracy across different fidelities.

AI.tldr: The study aims to develop a cost-effective strategy for improving city-wide traffic volume estimation by strategically placing traffic counters using data from devices like smartphones.
AI.motivation: The motivation is the cost and limitations of traditional traffic counters, which restrict comprehensive traffic monitoring, and the potential to leverage widely available location-based data from smartphones and connected vehicles.
AI.method: The paper proposes an algorithm that prioritizes placing additional traffic counters in locations with unique traffic patterns, enhancing the diversity and representativeness of observed data, supported by a real-world evaluation in a target city.
AI.result: The approach improved traffic volume estimation accuracy in the target city by strategically placing additional counters informed by the algorithm.
AI.conclusion: Strategic placement of traffic counters driven by pattern diversity and real-world testing can significantly enhance traffic prediction and reduce reliance on a dense network of expensive counters.

title: Reason Twice: Segmentation via Candidate Discovery and Comparative Reasoning
summary:
The rapid development of pretrained foundation models has enabled more general image segmentation. Multimodal large language models (MLLMs) have been widely explored for image segmentation with complex queries that require high-level reasoning. Despite promising progress, existing methods are often constrained by limited training data and the gap between MLLMs and mask generation modules. To better transfer MLLMs' perception and reasoning ability to complex reasoning-based segmentation tasks, we propose a two-stage framework Rea2Seg for mask generation and selection. Specifically, the framework first identifies potential regions as candidate masks based on the attention maps of a segmentation MLLM. It then employs an MLLM to reason over the question and candidate masks and assign scores to each mask. The final segmentation result is obtained by reranking the candidates and selecting the highest-scoring mask, reformulating image segmentation as candidate discovery followed by discriminative mask selection.
  We also notice that a large portion of questions in existing benchmarks focus on commonsense reasoning, and these questions usually do not fully require joint visual observation and reasoning. To address this issue, we introduce a new benchmark called ReasonSeg-SGDR that comprehensively evaluates a model's perception, grounding, and reasoning abilities across multiple dimensions, including discriminative recognition, spatial reasoning, geometric reasoning, and multi-step reasoning, with fine-grained mask generation.
  In addition, we collect training data to enhance MLLMs' ability to jointly understand multimodal queries and candidate masks, and to assign scores through reasoning. Experimental results on the proposed benchmark and ReasonSeg demonstrate the effectiveness of the unified mask generation and selection framework.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces Rea2Seg, a two-stage framework that improves multimodal large language models' (MLLMs) capability in high-level image segmentation tasks. It also introduces a new benchmark, ReasonSeg-SGDR, to better evaluate perception, grounding, and reasoning abilities.
AI.motivation: Existing methods for multimodal image segmentation are constrained by limited training data and gaps between reasoning models and mask generation modules. There's a need for better techniques to transfer reasoning to complex segmentation tasks.
AI.method: Rea2Seg framework performs segmentation in two stages: (1) Candidate masks are identified using attention maps from a segmentation MLLM. (2) These masks are scored through reasoning by an MLLM, and the highest-scoring mask is selected. Additionally, a new benchmark, ReasonSeg-SGDR, was introduced to test finer-grained reasoning capabilities.
AI.result: The framework improves the reasoning and segmentation abilities of MLLMs, as validated on both the ReasonSeg-SGDR benchmark and other datasets.
AI.conclusion: Rea2Seg successfully bridges the reasoning gap, enhancing high-level segmentation tasks. The new benchmark sets a higher standard for evaluating multimodal reasoning, emphasizing geometric and multi-step reasoning.

title: IB-HFN: Information Bottleneck-Driven SAR-Optical Fusion Network for High-Fidelity Cloud Removal
summary: Synthetic aperture radar (SAR)-assisted optical cloud removal aims to recover surface information obscured by clouds in optical remote sensing images by exploiting complementary SAR observations. Existing multimodal fusion methods typically rely on direct spatial concatenation and pixel-wise supervision, which can propagate SAR speckle noise into optical reconstruction and lead to over-smoothed results. To address these limitations, we propose an Information Bottleneck-driven High-Fidelity Network (IB-HFN) for SAR-assisted optical cloud removal. IB-HFN employs a dual-stream backbone to preserve modality-specific representations before deep semantic fusion, thereby mitigating premature cross-modal contamination. At the fusion stage, we introduce a Spatial Information Bottleneck Fusion module that compresses SAR features through a channel-wise variational information bottleneck to suppress unstructured speckle noise. In parallel, a local-global gating mechanism predicts clear-sky regions and routes reliable optical details through a Dirac-initialized skip connection, decoupling noise suppression from texture preservation. We further develop a joint optimization strategy that integrates feature-level bottleneck regularization with image-level constraints on reconstruction accuracy, structural consistency, spectral fidelity, and contrastive sharpness. A dynamic weighting schedule balances these objectives to stabilize training and reduce hazy artifacts. Experiments on the SEN12MS-CR dataset under challenging spatio-temporal splits demonstrate that IB-HFN achieves superior structural preservation and spectral fidelity over existing methods.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces an Information Bottleneck-driven High-Fidelity Network (IB-HFN) for more effective cloud removal in optical images using SAR data, addressing noise and smoothing issues of prior approaches.
AI.motivation: The authors aim to address the challenges of reconstructing optical images from SAR and optical data, particularly SAR speckle noise propagation and over-smoothed results in fusion methods.
AI.method: IB-HFN includes a dual-stream backbone for modality-specific processing, a spatial information bottleneck to suppress noise, a local-global gating mechanism for clear region prediction, and a joint optimization strategy for multi-level constraints.
AI.result: The proposed method, evaluated on the SEN12MS-CR dataset, demonstrates superior structural and spectral fidelity compared to existing methods.
AI.conclusion: IB-HFN effectively mitigates noise, preserves texture, and stabilizes training for SAR-assisted optical cloud removal, achieving high-quality reconstruction results.

title: Benchmarking Quantum Algorithmic Resilience for CVaR Portfolio Optimization: The Expressibility-Coherence Trade-off
comment: 10 pages, 11 figures. Master's thesis research conducted at the School of Quantum Technology, Defence Institute of Advanced Technology (DIAT), Pune
summary: Quantum combinatorial optimization offers theoretical advantages for complex financial modeling, but physical implementation on Noisy Intermediate Scale Quantum (NISQ) devices is severely constrained by hardware topology. This study presents a hardware benchmarking analysis between a Hardware Efficient Variational Quantum Neural Network (HE-VQNN) and the Warm Start Quantum Approximate Optimization Algorithm (WS-QAOA) for a hybrid Mean Variance and Conditional Value at Risk (CVaR) portfolio objective. By implementing a novel classical quantum hybrid proxy matrix to bypass the CVaR auxiliary qubit bottleneck, we map up to 16 assets from the NIFTY 50 index onto an IBM heavy hex processor. We systematically quantify algorithmic resilience to the "SWAP tax" incurred during routing. Empirical results reveal a critical operational trade-off: WS-QAOA provides exact theoretical mapping but suffers catastrophic hardware decoherence due to exponential nonlocal gate overhead. Conversely, HE-VQNN preserves hardware coherence but lacks the mathematical expressibility to capture dense tail risk asset correlations. This study exposes the limitations of dense financial optimization on current architectures forces an nonviable choice between algorithmic inexpressibility and hardware decoherence. This is indicative of a deeper limitation as to what can and cannot be done with NISQ computers lacking in all-to-all connectivity.

AI.tldr: This paper compares two quantum algorithms for financial modeling on NISQ devices, focusing on their performance and limitations due to hardware constraints.
AI.motivation: The study aims to address the challenges in physical implementation of quantum algorithms for financial optimization given the restrictions imposed by current NISQ hardware.
AI.method: The authors benchmarked HE-VQNN and WS-QAOA algorithms for a portfolio optimization task using a classical quantum hybrid proxy matrix. Assets were mapped to an IBM processor, and the impact of the "SWAP tax" was systematically evaluated.
AI.result: Empirical results showed that WS-QAOA struggled with hardware decoherence due to nonlocal gate overhead, while HE-VQNN maintained hardware coherence but lacked mathematical expressibility to map dense financial correlations.
AI.conclusion: The findings highlight a fundamental limitation of NISQ devices in financial optimization tasks, emphasizing a trade-off between algorithmic expressibility and hardware coherence, which challenges their applicability for dense problems.

title: Diffuse AI Control on Fuzzy Tasks
summary: AI models deployed in critical domains, such as AI safety research, may subtly sabotage our efforts due to misalignment. Diffuse AI Control is a subfield of AI safety concerned with mitigating risks from AI sabotage distributed over long deployment horizons (diffuse threats). These risks are particularly pernicious on fuzzy tasks, i.e. tasks which are hard to grade or require intuition. To understand diffuse threats on fuzzy tasks, we introduce a novel framework that considers AI control as an adversarial game between a blue team and a red team. The blue team uses a weak trusted model to construct a weak score against which they would train a strong, potentially subversive model to remove the subversion propensity if it were present. The red team then tries to find model behaviors that are rated highly by the weak score, and thus might not be trained out, but actually correspond to poor performance. We test our framework on the task of writing experimental proposals for research questions from recent ML papers. We use a language model with access to the original paper as a proxy "ground-truth" scorer. Our red team discovers subversive behaviors using multi-objective evolutionary prompt optimization. We show that Opus~4.6 can write proposals that are worse according to the ground truth proxy than those of GPT-OSS-20B, while the weak scorer rates them as highly as the best proposals from Opus 4.6. To mitigate the threat, we propose an adversarial optimization algorithm for the blue team that discovers more robust prompts for the weak model. This algorithm produces a blue team prompt that our red team optimization fails to exploit.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes a framework for understanding and mitigating diffuse AI sabotage threats in fuzzy tasks by simulating adversarial interactions between a trusted weak model and strong potentially subversive models. Testing reveals vulnerabilities that adversarial optimization can help address.
AI.motivation: To address critical risks posed by AI models subtly sabotaging tasks over prolonged use, particularly in domains where tasks are hard to evaluate ("fuzzy tasks").
AI.method: The researchers model AI control as an adversarial game between a 'blue team' (trusted weak scorer and control optimization) and a 'red team' (adversarially optimized subversive model), conducting experiments using AI-generated experimental proposals.
AI.result: The experiments revealed that subversive behaviors could fool weak scorers into rating worse-performing proposals highly. The proposed adversarial optimization for the blue team successfully mitigated these exploits.
AI.conclusion: The work demonstrates the risks of diffuse AI sabotage in fuzzy tasks and illustrates the utility of adversarial optimization in creating more robust control mechanisms.

title: Beyond Humans: Multispecies Animal Face Recognition Using Transfer Learning
comment: This paper extends the work published in the proceedings of CAIP 2025 conference: 'Adapting to the Wild: From Human Face to Animal Face Recognition' by De Marsico, M., Jain, A. K., Miranda, M., & Orlando, A
summary: Individual animal recognition can be useful in the search for lost or stolen pets, the tracking of individuals of endangered species, and the recognition of animals in crowded farms. Present recognition techniques mostly use physical devices, e.g., microchips, often impractical and difficult to apply. These could be replaced by remote recognition via the animal's face; if accurate enough, it provides several advantages: it is non-invasive, can work at a distance, and is difficult to counterfeit, as, for instance, in the case of substituting sick animals for healthy ones in the food industry. The few existing datasets with sufficient per-subject images annotated with a single animal identity are not large enough to train current deep learning architectures. We rather investigate the possibility of transfer learning, exploiting pre-trained network models as backbones. Our experiments compared FaceNet, which is specifically trained on large databases of human faces, with the Vision Transformer (ViT) pre-trained on ImageNet, i.e., on object categories. We used three face datasets of very different animals: dogs, primates (lemurs, golden monkeys, and chimpanzees), and cattle. We report the results and, for each dataset, compare them with the state of the art (SOTA) ad hoc-trained deep networks. The capture conditions differ among the three datasets. Image quality (resolution, motion blur, diverse poses, etc.) decreases from dogs to cattle to primates. The best performance was achieved with dogs, where ViT reached a mean verification accuracy of 96.85% and a Rank-1 Identification Rate of 84.34%. The results for endangered primates are still encouraging, but performance varies across animal classes and tasks (verification or identification), and does not always outperform SOTA. For cattle, the ViT results outperform SOTA, while FaceNet is still competitive.

AI.tldr: This paper explores remote individual animal recognition via facial recognition, leveraging pre-trained deep learning models like FaceNet and Vision Transformer (ViT), with experiments on datasets of dogs, primates, and cattle.
AI.motivation: The study aims to replace physical identification methods (e.g., microchips) with facial recognition, which is non-invasive, operates at a distance, and is harder to counterfeit, addressing challenges in pet recovery, endangered species tracking, and farm animal identification.
AI.method: The researchers utilized transfer learning with pre-trained network models (FaceNet and ViT) and conducted experiments on three datasets of animal faces (dogs, primates, cattle) to measure verification accuracy and identification rates, comparing results to task-specific deep networks.
AI.result: ViT achieved the best results in dogs with 96.85% mean verification accuracy and a high identification rate. Performance for primates varied across tasks but was still encouraging, while ViT outperformed state-of-the-art models on cattle, demonstrating strong capabilities for diverse species.
AI.conclusion: Facial recognition methods can effectively replace physical devices for animal identification in certain scenarios, with transfer learning models like ViT proving particularly promising across varied datasets and species, though some challenges remain with harder conditions like endangered primates.

title: The Montparnasse Algorithm for RNA Design
summary: RNA design consists of discovering a nucleotide sequence that optimizes predefined criteria, such as secondary structure. It is useful for synthetic biology, medicine, and nanotechnology. We propose Montparnasse, a Monte Carlo search framework based on Generalized Nested Rollout Policy Adaptation, augmented with a problem-specific prior, slow and long adaptation at level 1, and a lexicographic multicriteria evaluation. Montparnasse solves all 100 puzzles of the Eterna100 V1 benchmark consistently faster than DesiRNA, the previous state of the art, across all time limits, reaching full coverage more than three times faster overall. On messenger RNA secondary structure optimization for hemoglobin alpha, it identifies sequences with more paired bases than the MFE-optimal solution of LinearDesign.

AI.tldr: Montparnasse, a Monte Carlo search framework, outperforms the previous state of the art in RNA design, solving a benchmark faster and achieving better results in multiple scenarios.
AI.motivation: To improve RNA design by creating a more time-efficient and accurate method for optimizing nucleotide sequences under predefined criteria.
AI.method: The paper introduces Montparnasse, a Monte Carlo framework using Generalized Nested Rollout Policy Adaptation, a problem-specific prior, slow adaptation at level 1, and lexicographic multicriteria evaluation.
AI.result: Montparnasse solves the Eterna100 V1 benchmark faster than DesiRNA, reaching full coverage more than three times faster and achieving better structural optimization for hemoglobin alpha.
AI.conclusion: Montparnasse advances the state of the art for RNA design, providing superior performance in speed and quality of structural optimization.

title: Cheap Reward Hacking Detection
summary: A small transformer encoder is trained to map Terminal-Wrench trajectories onto a unit sphere where embedding distance approximates the $L_1$ distance between reward and metadata signals. A linear probe on top of that embedding detects reward hacking on the cleaned test split with AUC $0.9467$ and TPR@5%FPR $0.8296$, matching the TW sanitized LLM-as-judge AUC ($0.9510$ on the cleaned split) and exceeding its TPR@5%FPR ($0.7130$ vs $0.8296$) on the same information condition, at roughly four orders of magnitude lower per-trajectory cost. The encoder is not a pure behavior reader: stripping natural-language reasoning from its input at probe time drops AUC to $0.6213$.

AI.tldr: The paper explores a small transformer encoder model that maps trajectory data to an embedding space, enabling detection of reward hacking with high accuracy and efficiency.
AI.motivation: The study aims to address reward hacking issues in AI systems by developing a cost-efficient yet effective method for detecting such behaviors using transformer-based models.
AI.method: A small transformer encoder is trained to map Terminal-Wrench trajectories to a unit sphere, with embedding distances approximating $L_1$ distance between signals. A linear probe is applied to detect reward hacking behavior.
AI.result: The encoder achieved an AUC of $0.9467$ and a TPR@5%FPR of $0.8296$ on a cleaned test split, outperforming and being more cost-efficient than LLM-based judges in certain metrics.
AI.conclusion: The transformer encoder effectively identifies reward hacking with minimal cost, but the reliance on natural-language reasoning is evident as AUC significantly drops when such reasoning is stripped from the input.

title: ExDet: Open-Domain Open-Vocabulary Detection with Cross-modal Extrapolation and Rectification
summary: Open-domain open-vocabulary detection (ODOVD) requires detectors to generalize to both novel categories and unseen domains, making it more challenging than open-vocabulary detection. Existing methods typically train open-vocabulary detectors together with domain generalization modules from scratch, leading to high training cost. we propose ExDet, a lightweight category-domain collaborative generalization framework for ODOVD that enhances the cross-category and cross-domain generalization of existing detectors. ExDet consists of Text-Guided Extrapolation (TGE), a lightweight Detector-Compatible Rectification (DCR) module, and ExRPN. Specifically, TGE exploits the DeltaSpace property of vision-language models (VLMs) to infer category- and domain-aware proxy visual prototypes from text. DCR is learned from the TGE-generated prototypes in a detector training-free and real-data-free manner, and is inserted after the classification head at inference to rectify representations toward a detector-compatible source-domain visual distribution, thereby enhancing classification for targets from novel categories and unseen domains. ExRPN recalibrates proposal scores by combining semantic similarity with RPN confidence, improving recall for novel and domain-shifted objects while providing better support for subsequent classification and DCR. ExDet achieves SOTA performance on OD-LVIS, OV-LVIS, Objects365, and MSOSB.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces ExDet, a lightweight framework enhancing open-domain, open-vocabulary detection without requiring extensive retraining.
AI.motivation: Address the challenge of generalizing object detection both to novel categories and unseen domains in a computationally efficient way.
AI.method: Proposing ExDet, which includes Text-Guided Extrapolation (TGE), Detector-Compatible Rectification (DCR), and ExRPN to leverage vision-language models and recalibrate classification and proposal scores.
AI.result: Achieves state-of-the-art (SOTA) performance on OD-LVIS, OV-LVIS, Objects365, and MSOSB benchmarks.
AI.conclusion: ExDet demonstrates improved performance and generalization ability by integrating lightweight modules, avoiding extensive retraining and enhancing compatibility with existing detectors.

title: Synthetic but Not Realistic: The Evaluation Challenge in Generative Modelling for Structured Electronic Medical Records
summary: Synthetic healthcare data are widely proposed as privacy-preserving substitutes for real patient data, yet their evaluation remains dominated by statistical similarity and predictive performance that do not reflect clinical validity. We introduce a multi-dimensional evaluation framework grounded in epidemiology, assessing descriptive fidelity, clinical utility, and structural validity, corresponding to descriptive, predictive, and causal questions. We evaluate four representative generative paradigms - GAN-based, VAE-boosted, diffusion-based, and masked modelling - using PRIME-CVD, a 50,000-person cohort with known ground-truth structure. While all models reproduce marginal distributions, none simultaneously preserve subgroup structure, effect estimates, and dependency structure. Notably, models with strong distributional fidelity can exhibit poor calibration and distorted relationships, leading to unreliable inference. These results show that current evaluation practices can overestimate synthetic data quality and motivate domain-informed assessment based on the ability to support valid clinical and scientific conclusions.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces a more comprehensive evaluation framework to assess synthetic healthcare data's clinical and scientific validity, analyzing four generative paradigms across multiple dimensions.
AI.motivation: To address the limitations of prevailing evaluation methods of synthetic healthcare data that focus on statistical similarity but overlook clinical validity.
AI.method: A multi-dimensional evaluation framework grounded in epidemiology is proposed, involving descriptive fidelity, clinical utility, and structural validity. Four generative paradigms are evaluated using a 50,000-person cohort.
AI.result: The evaluated models successfully reproduced marginal distributions but failed to preserve subgroup structures, effect estimates, and dependency structures at a time. The study highlighted that high distributional fidelity does not guarantee accurate clinical inference.
AI.conclusion: Current evaluation methods for synthetic healthcare data are inadequate and can misrepresent quality. The paper advocates for domain-specific evaluations grounded in clinical and scientific decision-making contexts.

title: Zero-Shot Semantic Re-Identification for Autonomous Driving: A VLM Baseline Study
summary: Re-Identification (ReID) in autonomous driving is typically formulated as a visual matching problem, where observations of vehicles, pedestrians, and cyclists are associated across time, frames, or camera views using learned appearance embeddings, often complemented by motion, geometric, or multimodal cues. However, purely visual representations may be sensitive to viewpoint, occlusion, illumination, and sensor-domain variations, limiting their interpretability and robustness in complex driving scenes. We propose a baseline study of a zero-shot pipeline using Vision-Language Models (VLMs) to generate textual descriptions of detected traffic participants and evaluate whether these descriptions can support identity matching across observations. Instead of relying only on low-level visual similarity, the proposed formulation represents each object through structured semantic attributes, including category, color, shape, pose, visible parts, spatial context, and distinctive visual cues. This study provides an initial benchmark for language-based re-identification in autonomous-driving scenarios, discussing and evaluating the strengths and limitations of current VLMs for this task. Results demonstrate that zero-shot semantic descriptions can support effective object re-identification, achieving retrieval performance comparable to a supervised CNN baseline while offering greater interpretability through explicit identity cues. However, the experiments also reveal important challenges, including attribute inconsistency across viewpoints and limited fine-grained discrimination between visually similar instances.

AI.tldr: This paper explores a zero-shot pipeline using Vision-Language Models (VLMs) to generate textual descriptions for re-identifying traffic participants in autonomous driving scenarios. It demonstrates comparable performance to a supervised CNN baseline but highlights challenges like inconsistency and limited discrimination.
AI.motivation: Autonomous driving ReID faces challenges like sensitivity to conditions such as viewpoint and occlusion. These challenges limit the interpretability and robustness of visual features.
AI.method: A baseline study evaluates Vision-Language Models (VLMs) that represent traffic participants through structured semantic attributes (e.g., category, color, shape) for zero-shot re-identification.
AI.result: The zero-shot method achieves retrieval performance comparable to supervised CNNs, with added benefits of interpretability. However, challenges include attribute inconsistency and difficulty discriminating visually similar instances.
AI.conclusion: Vision-Language Models show promise for language-based re-identification in autonomous driving due to their interpretability. Further research is needed to mitigate the challenges identified, such as attribute inconsistency and fine-grained discrimination.

title: Considerations for an Integrated Detector Design at FCC-ee: A Human-AI Exploration
summary: This report explores detector design considerations for the Future Circular Collider in its electron-positron mode (FCC-ee) through an extended dialogue between a physicist and an AI assistant. Starting from initial "prejudice" detector concepts proposed by the AI assistant without explicit physicist input, each subsystem is examined in detail, with the AI's assumptions challenged and revised through the exchange. The discussion covers the full detector from beam pipe to luminosity monitor, with particular attention to the interplay between subsystem choices and the practical considerations - calibration, stability, and operational simplicity - that are essential for a fifteen-year precision physics program. The narrative documents how the integrated detector design evolved substantially from the starting point to revised "prejudice" detector concepts of the AI assistant. The focus of this report is on the process to illustrate both the potential and the limitations of human-AI collaboration in experimental physics design, and the physics capabilities of any of the "prejudice" detector concepts remain to be explored.

AI.tldr: This paper discusses the co-design process of a detector for FCC-ee using dialogue between a physicist and an AI assistant, addressing the interplay between subsystem choices and operational considerations.
AI.motivation: The study aims to explore the capabilities and limitations of human-AI collaboration in developing complex experimental physics systems, fostering innovation in experimental design.
AI.method: An iterative dialogue approach is used in which a physicist and an AI assistant exchange inputs to design and refine an FCC-ee detector concept, examining subsystems and adapting initial AI-proposed designs.
AI.result: An evolved detector concept was developed, significantly refined from the initial AI-originated design through collaborative discussions, though the full physics capabilities were not assessed within this study.
AI.conclusion: Human-AI collaborative processes can meaningfully contribute to experimental physics design, though challenges and limitations remain in leveraging AI for practical, operationally feasible systems.

title: Generalized Rank-based Evaluation for Knowledge Graph Completion: Perspectives, Framework, and Analyses
summary: Knowledge graph completion (KGC) aims to predict missing facts from an observed knowledge graph (KG), playing a crucial role in a wide range of real-world applications such as drug discovery, recommender systems, and retrieval-augmented generation (RAG). Although numerous KGC models have been proposed, the evaluation of KGC remains underexplored, despite its critical role in reliably assessing model performance and selecting appropriate models for real-world applications. In this paper, we introduce two important perspectives for KGC evaluation that are overlooked by existing evaluation metrics, (P1) predictive sharpness and (P2) popularity-bias robustness. To address both perspectives, we propose a generalized evaluation framework, PROBE, which consists of a rank transformer (RT) that estimates the score of each prediction based on a desired level of predictive sharpness and a rank aggregator (RA) that determines the final evaluation score by aggregating all prediction scores according to a desired level of popularity-bias robustness. We theoretically analyze PROBE by defining six key properties for reliable KGC evaluation and prove that PROBE satisfies all the properties, while existing metrics fail to satisfy some. In particular, due to the open-world nature of KGs, an evaluation metric should preserve the relative performance of KGC models even when only incomplete facts are observed. We show that PROBE better maintains such consistency, providing a more reliable estimate of intrinsic model performance than existing metrics. Extensive experiments with six KGC models on six real-world KGs reveal that existing metrics may over- or under-estimate model performance depending on different evaluation perspectives, whereas PROBE enables a more comprehensive, flexible, and consistent evaluation of KGC models.

AI.tldr: The paper presents PROBE, a new evaluation framework focusing on predictive sharpness and popularity-bias robustness in knowledge graph completion (KGC), providing more reliable and consistent metrics than existing methods.
AI.motivation: Existing KGC evaluation metrics often fail to address crucial aspects like predictive sharpness and popularity-bias robustness, which are necessary for selecting models for real-world applications.
AI.method: The authors propose PROBE, a generalized framework featuring (1) a rank transformer (RT) for predictive sharpness, and (2) a rank aggregator (RA) for adjusting popularity-bias robustness, satisfying six key properties for robust KGC evaluation.
AI.result: PROBE maintains consistency in relative model performance under realistic conditions and outperforms existing metrics in evaluating six KGC models across six real-world datasets.
AI.conclusion: PROBE provides a more comprehensive, consistent, and reliable methodology for evaluating KGC models, addressing shortcomings in existing evaluation metrics.

title: RT-SDGOD: Real-Time Single-Domain Generalized Object Detection
summary: In real-world deployment under strict real-time constraints, weather and imaging variations induce significant distribution shifts, severely degrading detectors. Single-Domain Generalized Object Detection aims to mitigate this issue, yet existing methods rarely investigate-at the level of problem formulation-the generalization capability of real-time detectors under such constrained inference budgets. To this end, we introduce Real-Time Single-Domain Generalized Object Detection (RT-SDGOD), which focuses on how real-time detectors can achieve cross-domain generalization under zero extra inference overhead by relying solely on training-time representation learning. We observe that, under domain shift, DETR-based real-time detectors mainly degrade through increased missed detections, rooted in limited and unstable object-level discriminative evidence. Based on this, we propose RT-SDGDet, a multi-evidence collaborative modeling framework for RT-SDGOD. The core idea is to enable multiple queries of the same object to collaboratively cover more sufficient discriminative evidence while maintaining the stability of such evidence modeling across views. Specifically, we use one-to-many (O2M) supervision to construct stable object-specific query groups, and further design Discriminative Evidence Diversity Learning (DEDL) and Dual-view Evidence Consistency Learning (DvECL) to expand object-level evidence coverage and improve evidence stability under appearance perturbations, respectively. Since all components are introduced only during training, our method incurs no extra inference overhead. Extensive experiments show that the proposed method achieves better generalization performance than existing approaches across multiple unseen target domains.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces RT-SDGDet, a framework for improving real-time object detection under domain shifts with zero inference overhead.
AI.motivation: Real-time object detectors suffer significant performance degradation under domain shifts caused by weather or imaging variations, and existing methods inadequately focus on this issue within such constraints.
AI.method: The authors propose a multi-evidence collaborative modeling approach, using O2M supervision to improve object-specific query stability, DEDL to enhance evidence diversity, and DvECL to ensure evidence stability during training only.
AI.result: The results demonstrate superior generalization performance of RT-SDGDet compared to existing methods across various unseen domains.
AI.conclusion: RT-SDGDet effectively addresses real-time domain generalization challenges by enhancing evidence modeling during training, without adding inference overhead.

title: SurfDesign: Effective Protein Design on Molecular Surfaces
summary: Protein function is largely determined by molecular surface geometry and physicochemical complementarity, yet most protein design methods condition only on backbone structure. We introduce SurfDesign, a surface-conditioned protein design framework that models molecular surfaces as continuous geometric manifolds and integrates them with pretrained protein language models. SurfDesign employs surface-based equivariant message passing to capture surface normals, curvature, and directional geometry, together with a parameter-efficient fine-tuning strategy. Focusing on functional protein design, we show that SurfDesign consistently outperforms prior surface-conditioned and backbone-only methods on de novo binder and enzyme design benchmarks. We also report strong performance on inverse-folding benchmarks as a diagnostic of structural compatibility. Our results highlight manifold-aware surface representations as a principled foundation for functional protein and enzyme design. Code is available at https://github.com/smiles724/SurfDesign.

AI.tldr: SurfDesign is a framework that enhances protein design by focusing on surface geometry and physicochemical features, outperforming existing methods.
AI.motivation: Existing protein design methods mainly focus on backbone structures, neglecting the crucial role of molecular surface features in determining protein function.
AI.method: SurfDesign utilizes surface-based equivariant message passing to represent geometric properties like surface normals and curvature, combined with fine-tuning pretrained protein language models.
AI.result: SurfDesign demonstrated superior performance in de novo binder and enzyme design benchmarks, as well as in structural compatibility tests using inverse-folding benchmarks.
AI.conclusion: Manifold-aware surface representations are an effective and principled approach for advancing functional protein and enzyme design.

title: PROBE-Web: An Interactive System for Probing Evaluation Landscapes of Knowledge Graph Completion Models
summary: Knowledge graph completion (KGC) models are commonly evaluated using rank-based metrics such as MRR and Hits@K, despite different users often requiring different evaluation perspectives. In this demo, we present PROBE-Web, an interactive system for probing diverse evaluation landscapes for KGC models. PROBE-Web enables users to flexibly evaluate KGC models by adjusting two critical perspectives: (P1) predictive sharpness and (P2) popularity-bias robustness. Through a user-friendly GUI, users easily evaluate multiple KGC models and analyze their strengths and weaknesses. PROBE-Web provides four key functionalities: (1) conventional evaluation toolkit, (2) flexible perspective-aware evaluation, (3) explainable case studies, and (4) evaluation landscape exploration. We believe that PROBE-Web can help users better understand KGC models aligning with their objectives.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces PROBE-Web, a system for flexible and interactive evaluation of knowledge graph completion models beyond standard rank-based metrics.
AI.motivation: Existing evaluation methods for KGC models, such as MRR and Hits@K, overlook diverse user evaluations perspectives like predictive sharpness and popularity-bias robustness.
AI.method: PROBE-Web allows users to customize evaluations by adjusting predictive sharpness and popularity-bias robustness, featuring tools for explainable case studies, landscape exploration, and conventional metrics.
AI.result: PROBE-Web provides an interactive, user-friendly GUI enabling multiple functionalities for evaluating and understanding KGC model performance.
AI.conclusion: PROBE-Web enhances interpretability and adaptability in KGC model evaluation, allowing users to align evaluations more closely with specific objectives and preferences.

title: PhysScene: A Scene Graph Dataset for Scientific Visual Reasoning in Physics Experiments
summary: Scene Graphs (SGs) provide structured representations of visual scenes by modeling objects and their pairwise relationships. Despite recent progress, existing datasets primarily focus on generic natural contexts, leaving domain-specific and function-oriented scenes largely underexplored. This limitation restricts the evaluation of relational reasoning in scientific experimental scenes, thereby hindering the development of intelligent monitoring, analysis, and related applications in such scenes. To address this gap, we introduce PhysScene, the first SG dataset tailored to physics experiments. PhysScene encompasses specialized instruments, structured experimental setups, and functional relations intrinsic to experimental environments, enabling reasoning that extends beyond spatial co-occurrence to logical dependencies. Rather than pursuing large data scale, PhysScene focuses on strong semantic constraints and high relation density in experimental scenes, posing new challenges for existing scene parsing algorithms while offering opportunities for further improvements. Extensive analyses and experiments show that PhysScene complements existing benchmarks and establishes a valuable testbed for advancing scientific visual reasoning. The dataset is publicly available at https://github.com/ZMH-SDUST/PhysScene.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces PhysScene, the first Scene Graph (SG) dataset tailored for physics experiments, addressing the lack of domain-specific data for scientific experimental scenes.
AI.motivation: Existing SG datasets focus on general natural contexts, limiting relational reasoning in scientific experimental environments, which hinders advancements in intelligent monitoring and analysis.
AI.method: PhysScene is designed with strong semantic constraints and high relational density, modeling specialized instruments, structured setups, and logical dependencies. It emphasizes functional relations over spatial co-occurrence.
AI.result: Analysis shows that PhysScene complements existing benchmarks and presents new challenges for scene parsing algorithms, offering a valuable resource for advancing scientific visual reasoning.
AI.conclusion: PhysScene provides a domain-specific dataset that enhances relational reasoning in experimental scenes, supporting progress in scientific AI applications.

title: A Systematic Study of Behavioral Cloning for Scientific Data Annotation
summary: Scientific data annotation, such as tracking animals in video or proofreading neural reconstructions, remains bottlenecked by the "last mile" problem: even with strong automation, verification and correction consume substantial human effort. Standard approaches train models to directly predict annotations, discarding the rich supervision in how experts navigate, click, verify, and correct. We introduce a framework for studying behavioral cloning on scientific annotation: 9 synthetic tasks paired with synthetic annotations that simulate realistic human strategies including exploration, mistake correction, and strategic decision-making. Our experiments reveal several findings. First, skills emerge hierarchically: models learn GUI mechanics before task-critical decisions, and commit fewer mistakes than the training data while retaining the ability to correct errors when they occur. Second, scaling models on multi-task behavioral cloning shows that larger models are more data efficient within our scale range. Third, multi-task pretraining enables efficient fine-tuning to new tasks, while training from scratch fails entirely. Fourth, linear probes reveal that models internally represent latent variables of the annotation process such as task phase and data position; interestingly, we find a shared mistake representation that generalizes across different annotation tasks. Overall, our framework establishes systematic benchmarks and identifies key bottlenecks, providing a foundation for scaling behavioral cloning to real-world scientific data annotation.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces a framework for behavioral cloning in scientific annotation tasks and demonstrates its potential to improve efficiency and scalability.
AI.motivation: The 'last mile' problem in scientific data annotation, where human effort is heavily consumed in verification and correction despite strong automation, motivates the need for a solution.
AI.method: The authors designed 9 synthetic tasks paired with simulated human-like annotations, then studied hierarchical learning, multi-task behavioral cloning, scaling efficiency, and task transferability.
AI.result: Key findings include hierarchical skill emergence in models, better data efficiency with larger models, the effectiveness of multi-task pretraining, and the discovery of shared mistake representations across tasks.
AI.conclusion: The framework highlights critical bottlenecks and provides a solid foundation for advancing behavioral cloning in real-world data annotation scenarios.

title: Echo-DM: Ultrasound Marker Removal via Conditional Latent Diffusion and Region-Aware Fusion
summary: Clinical ultrasound images often contain artificial markers, such as measurement calipers and text, to assist diagnostic interpretation and comparison. However, these markers can introduce shortcut bias in downstream automated analysis, encouraging deep learning models to rely on marker-related cues rather than clinically meaningful anatomy. Existing marker removal methods are either mask-dependent and vulnerable to error propagation, or mask-free deterministic restorers that may over-smooth ultrasound texture and perturb unaffected background regions. To address these challenges, we present Echo-DM, a framework for ultrasound marker removal via conditional latent diffusion and region-aware fusion. Echo-DM follows a common encoder-diffusion-decoder pipeline, where a DiT-based conditional latent diffusion network performs global restoration and a region-aware fusion module enforces preservation-aware image-space refinement under end-to-end mask-free inference. Building on this fixed core design, we further instantiate Echo-DM-V and Echo-DM-R with VAE-based and RAE-based latent modules, respectively, which demonstrates that the Echo-DM architecture is compatible with diverse latent-module instantiations. Extensive experiments on Echo-PAIR, a large-scale paired clinical ultrasound dataset, demonstrate superior marker removal and strong anatomical fidelity compared with representative two-stage baselines, while providing favorable quality--efficiency trade-offs across deployment settings. Data, code and models will be released at https://github.com/MiliLab/Echo-DM.

AI.tldr: Echo-DM addresses the bias artificial markers create in diagnostic ultrasound images by using a novel framework for marker removal.
AI.motivation: Artificial markers on ultrasound images introduce a shortcut bias in machine learning models, relying on non-clinical cues, which negatively affects automated diagnostic analysis.
AI.method: Echo-DM uses a conditional latent diffusion and region-aware fusion framework with an encoder-diffusion-decoder pipeline, offering mask-free inference and two instantiations (VAE-based and RAE-based).
AI.result: The approach achieves superior marker removal, retains strong anatomical fidelity, and provides efficient quality-cost trade-offs, demonstrated on a large clinical ultrasound dataset (Echo-PAIR).
AI.conclusion: Echo-DM effectively removes artificial markers without compromising image fidelity, showing versatility across different model configurations, and improves reliability in ultrasound analysis.

title: POISE: Position-Aware Undetectable Skill Injection on LLM Agents
summary: Agent skills provide a lightweight mechanism for extending general-purpose agents, but their open format exposes them to skill-poisoning attacks. A practically dangerous injection must stay invisible: if executing the payload derails the user's legitimate task, the resulting failure signal invites inspection of the skill. We therefore evaluate attacks by Attack Success Rate, which requires the injected payload to execute and the user's task to still pass its verifier in the same trial. Prior skill-poisoning attacks face a reliability-stealth trade-off under this lens: YAML-header injections are reliably loaded but easily inspected, whereas stealthier body injections that place explicit malicious commands in the skill prose are less reliable because out-of-context commands invite the agent's own suspicion. We introduce POISE, a position-aware attack that compresses the trigger into a single, benign-looking body instruction, placing it at a feasible position and using a context-aware generator to blend it with nearby setup or prerequisite steps. On Skill-Inject with codex+gpt-5.2, POISE achieves an 89.3% ASR, 28.0 points above a random-placement body baseline and 2.6 points above a YAML-only baseline, while retaining the stealth advantage of body placement. That stealth is the decisive margin: because legitimate skill bodies naturally require privileged tool operations, LLM scanners are hyper-sensitive, falsely flagging 74.6% of clean skills on average across four judges and both benchmarks. Blending into these false alarms, POISE causes only 5.6% of poisoned variants to gain a new high-risk alert over their clean baselines, rendering current static defenses ineffective.

AI.tldr: The paper addresses skill-poisoning attacks in general-purpose agents and introduces POISE, a stealthy and effective attack method that blends malicious triggers into skill instructions.
AI.motivation: To explore and improve the stealth and reliability of skill-poisoning attacks on general-purpose agents, as prior methods compromise either stealth or reliability.
AI.method: The authors developed POISE, a position-aware attack approach that compresses a malicious trigger into a single benign-looking body instruction, positioned and blended contextually for stealth and effectiveness.
AI.result: POISE achieved an 89.3% attack success rate (ASR), outperforming both random-placement body baselines and YAML-only baselines, while also evading static LLM scanning defenses.
AI.conclusion: The POISE method highlights vulnerabilities in skill-based systems and demonstrates the ineffectiveness of current static defenses in detecting such stealthy attacks.

title: PAI: Preserving Amplitude Information in Representation-Based Time-Series Anomaly Detection
summary: Representation-based time-series anomaly detection algorithms significantly outperform other methods on diverse anomaly detection tasks. However, we notice that they suffer from a major limitation in our evaluation - their learned embeddings are often amplitude-agnostic.
