We validate our model through hypergraph reconstruction simulation, hypergraph classification, and node-level anomaly detection, where it demonstrates superior performance over existing state-of-the-art graph and hypergraph deep learning methods.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces U-Net architectures for hypergraph data using Parallel Hierarchical Pooling (PHPool) and Unpooling (PHUnpool) operators to better retain hypergraph structure and improve performance in tasks like hypergraph classification.
AI.motivation: While convolution has been extended to hypergraph data, the U-Net architecture has not been explored due to challenges in pooling/unpooling operations. The motivation was to design effective methods for these operations and bring the powerful U-Net architecture to hypergraph processing.
AI.method: The paper introduces PHPool and PHUnpool operators. PHPool is inspired by hierarchical clustering and provides a parallel and global way to pool hypergraph structures, while PHUnpool performs the corresponding inverse operation. These methods aim to maintain structural fidelity during processing.
AI.result: The proposed model shows superior performance compared to state-of-the-art graph and hypergraph deep learning methods across multiple tasks, including hypergraph reconstruction, classification, and node-level anomaly detection.
AI.conclusion: The study effectively introduces and validates U-Net architectures for hypergraphs via PHPool and PHUnpool, addressing pooling and unpooling challenges and demonstrating the potential of this approach with strong empirical results.

title: A VideoMAE-v2 Approach to Zero-Shot Traffic Accident Anticipation
summary: Traffic accident anticipation -- predicting the likelihood of an imminent collision at every frame of a dashcam video -- is safety-critical yet difficult to scale, because collecting in-domain annotated accident footage for every deployment scenario is prohibitively expensive. We study this task under a zero-shot setting where no target-domain training data is available: the model must learn exclusively from a publicly available binary-labelled driving-accident dataset and generalise to unseen dashcam footage. We propose a framework that bridges the gap between the frame-level temporal risk estimation task and coarsely labelled binary accident datasets by coupling a VideoMAE-v2 backbone with a per-frame prediction head under a sliding-window protocol. Our method achieves 2nd place in the 2026 CVPR@AUTOPILOT Zero-Shot Accident Anticipation competition. Code is available at https://github.com/TimeSouth/zero-shot-taa-solution.

AI.tldr: The paper addresses zero-shot traffic accident anticipation using dashcam videos and proposes a novel framework tailored for temporal risk estimation based on coarse, binary-labelled datasets.
AI.motivation: The motivation is to develop scalable methods for predicting imminent collisions in dashcam videos without requiring domain-specific, annotated accident footage, which is costly to obtain.
AI.method: The method involves a framework that integrates a VideoMAE-v2 backbone with a per-frame prediction head employing a sliding-window protocol to handle the temporal risk estimation task.
AI.result: The approach earned 2nd place in the 2026 CVPR@AUTOPILOT Zero-Shot Accident Anticipation competition.
AI.conclusion: The study introduces a scalable zero-shot accident anticipation framework, demonstrating its effectiveness in generalizing to unseen dashcam footage. The presented method shows promise for safety-critical applications.

title: Streaming Interventions: Can Video Large Language Models Correct Mistakes as They Occur?
comment: Qualcomm Interactive Cooking: Ego-MC-Bench -- available at https://huggingface.co/datasets/neuripsedtracksub/ego-mistake-corrections and Ego-CoMist -- available at https://huggingface.co/datasets/neuripsedtracksub/ego-counterfactual-mistakes
summary: Learning everyday skills, like cooking a dish, relies increasingly on instructional media such as online videos. This opens the door to the use of video (and multimodal) large language models (LLMs) as task guidance assistants. A crucial capability for the real-world success of a prospective task guidance assistant is it's ability to intervene proactively as soon as a mistake is apparent in order to guide the user. To evaluate this crucial capability, we introduce Ego-MC-Bench (Mistake Corrections), a benchmark for evaluating reactive, step-by-step task guidance in realistic cooking scenarios. Extensive experiments show that Ego-MC-Bench is highly challenging for state-of-the-art video LLMs. We argue that a key reason is the limited availability of training data for fine-tuning models on this task. Although there exists a wide range of cooking video datasets, existing datasets lack examples of mistakes along with appropriately timed interventions. To help address this data limitation, we also introduce Ego-CoMist, a counterfactual synthetic dataset created by transforming non -interactive cooking videos into supervised training examples showing proactive interventions. We show that fine-tuning on Ego-CoMist yields performance gains especially for smaller and more efficient video LLMs that are well suited for delivering assistance on edge devices.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces Ego-MC-Bench to evaluate video LLMs' ability to provide step-by-step guidance and proactive mistake interventions in cooking scenarios. It also introduces Ego-CoMist, a synthetic dataset for training models to handle such mistakes.
AI.motivation: To provide better task guidance through video LLMs by addressing the need for proactive mistake correction abilities, especially in everyday activities like cooking.
AI.method: The authors created Ego-MC-Bench, a benchmark to assess LLMs on step-by-step mistake correction tasks in realistic cooking scenarios. Additionally, they generated Ego-CoMist, a synthetic dataset with counterfactual cooking interventions, to enhance training.
AI.result: Experiments showed that existing video LLMs underperform on Ego-MC-Bench, highlighting the task's complexity. Fine-tuning with Ego-CoMist significantly improved the performance of smaller, efficient models.
AI.conclusion: Current video LLMs struggle with proactive mistake corrections due to training data limitations. Ego-CoMist offers a potential solution by providing synthetic training data for this task, improving model performance notably.

title: TinyGiantALM: A Compact Audio-Language Model for Intent-Aware Reasoning under Resource Constraints
summary: Current advancements in Audio Reasoning rely on massive Large Audio-Language Models (LALMs), hindering deployment in resource-constrained environments. We introduce TinyGiantALM, a compact 1.5B efficiency-oriented alternative. Instead of brute-force scaling, we propose an Instruction-Aware Feature Refinement framework using a Query-guided Projector and Semantic Gating to filter acoustic signals based on user intent. On the MMAR benchmark, TinyGiantALM achieves 46.4% zero-shot accuracy, significantly outperforming 7B-13B baselines. While a reasoning gap in logical narrative remains versus 30B+ models and certain trade-offs exist in overly dense or spatial scenes, our approach notably surpasses models up to 8x larger in disentangling mixed-modality environments. These findings demonstrate that architectural precision offers a tangible pathway to secure robust perception capabilities on edge-friendly scales.

AI.tldr: TinyGiantALM, a 1.5B parameter compact Audio-Language Model using efficiency-focused methods, outperforms larger models in many aspects of reasoning accuracy while remaining suitable for resource-limited settings.
AI.motivation: Large Audio-Language Models (LALMs) require significant resources, making them unsuitable for constrained environments. There is a need for efficient models that balance performance and scalability.
AI.method: TinyGiantALM employs an Instruction-Aware Feature Refinement framework with Query-guided Projector and Semantic Gating to filter acoustic signals effectively based on intent, enabling superior reasoning in compact models.
AI.result: TinyGiantALM achieved 46.4% zero-shot accuracy on the MMAR benchmark, beating larger 7B-13B models. Although it performs less well in dense or spatially complex scenes, it shows unmatched strength in processing mixed-modality environments, outperforming models 8x its size.
AI.conclusion: Efficient architectural refinements, as used in TinyGiantALM, provide a feasible way to power robust audio reasoning in environments with limited resources, establishing an exciting step toward scalable and capable compact models.

title: Stage-1 Controls the Entropy Regime, Not the Outcome
summary: Two-stage post-training -- a Stage-1 warm-start (supervised fine-tuning, SFT, or on-policy distillation, OPD) followed by Stage-2 reinforcement learning (RL) -- is increasingly used for vision-language models (VLMs). We ask what Stage-1 actually controls in a small-data study using Qwen2.5-VL-7B with a same-modality 72B VLM teacher for OPD. First, the three warm-starts reach a narrow $53$--$54\%$ band on Geometry3K internal validation, consistent with the narrow range reported by recent specialized methods; this setup provides little evidence that Stage-1 changes the in-domain endpoint. Second, a matched-recipe, early-stopped SFT improves out-of-domain MathVista by $+2.1$ points, reversing the $-9.5$-point drop of an over-trained variant. The clearest difference is the \emph{entropy regime}: OPD enters RL with substantially higher policy entropy than either SFT initialization, and the separation remains visible through the available trajectories. At the in-domain initialization, OPD also has higher answer diversity and pass@16 ($+2.0$ to $+5.2$ points over SFT), although problem-level bootstrap intervals show that the smaller contrast is uncertain. The advantage is absent after RL (endpoint pass@16 values within $1.1$ points) and on MathVista (six models within $1.2$ points). Our contribution is therefore a bounded empirical characterization: Stage-1 is strongly associated with the entropy regime in this setup, but the downstream payoff is small, localized, and not evidence that OPD is a better RL warm-start.

AI.tldr: This paper studies the impact of the Stage-1 warm-start method on vision-language models in a two-stage post-training process, finding that while entropy differences are notable, the downstream performance impact is negligible.
AI.motivation: To investigate the actual role and influence of Stage-1 warm-start (e.g., supervised fine-tuning or on-policy distillation) in two-stage training for vision-language models, particularly in small-data scenarios.
AI.method: The study uses a small-data setup involving Qwen2.5-VL-7B and a larger same-modality teacher model for on-policy distillation, comparing different Stage-1 warm-starts (SFT and OPD) based on their effects on performance metrics, entropy levels, and downstream reinforcement learning outcomes.
AI.result: All warm-start strategies resulted in similar in-domain performance (53–54% range) and minor out-of-domain improvements, but the entropy regime was the most distinct difference between methods. However, this had negligible impact after Stage-2 RL fine-tuning.
AI.conclusion: Stage-1 primarily influences the entropy regime of the models, but this does not translate to significant advantages in downstream performance, questioning the broader utility of one warm-start method (e.g., OPD) over another in these scenarios.

title: TUDSR: Twice Upsampling-Diffusion for Higher Super-Resolution
summary: Diffusion-based generative models have achieved remarkable success in real-world image super-resolution (SR). With tiled diffusion techniques, these models can produce high-resolution images that exceed their native-supported resolution. However, the quality of such high-resolution (e.g $2048^2$) outputs often remains extremely poor, primarily due to two factors we consider: the image upsampling ratio (e.g $\times8$) exceeding the model's native-supported upsampling ratio (e.g $\times4$), and the model's native-supported resolution. In practice, training a native high-resolution model requires larger architectures, which incur significant computational overhead and GPU memory costs, making it hard on limited-resource equipment. Thus, we present TUDSR, a Twice Upsampling-Diffusion framework for higher SR. The TUDSR framework mainly consists of two stages: the first involves training at $R$-resolution, and the second introduces a looped chunk-based training strategy at $NR$-resolution. Each stage adapts a one-step GAN architecture comprising a generator and a discriminator. Based on SD2.1-base, we develop TUDSR-S, which achieves state-of-the-art performance across multiple benchmarks. Extensive experiments further demonstrate that TUDSR-S generates high-quality images at the resolutions of $1024^2$ and even $2048^2$, significantly outperforming existing approaches. Code is available at https://github.com/wuer5/TUDSR.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes TUDSR, a framework for improving diffusion-based image super-resolution, enabling high-quality results at high resolutions ($1024^2$, $2048^2$), overcoming computational and memory challenges.
AI.motivation: The motivation is to address the poor quality of high-resolution image super-resolution outputs from conventional diffusion-based models due to limitations in upsampling ratio and resolution, while minimizing associated computational and memory costs.
AI.method: The method involves a Twice Upsampling-Diffusion framework (TUDSR) incorporating two stages: training at $R$-resolution and using a looped chunk-based strategy at $NR$-resolution. Each stage uses a one-step GAN with a generator and discriminator.
AI.result: The proposed TUDSR-S achieves state-of-the-art performance on multiple benchmarks, producing high-quality images at resolutions of $1024^2$ and $2048^2$.
AI.conclusion: TUDSR successfully addresses challenges in high-resolution image super-resolution by enhancing resolution quality while reducing computational overhead, outperforming existing methods.

title: OnlyDense: Reduced-Order Modeling for Lagrangian simulation
summary: In science and engineering, Lagrangian simulation methods such as Smooth Particle Hydrodynamics (SPH) or Material Point Method (MPM) are often employed to study the behavior of dynamic systems. However, these methods can be prohibitively computationally expensive, particularly when simulating multi-scale spatial or temporal phenomena, e.g., void growth and coalescence within macro-scale geometries, structural failure of spacecraft components resulting from hypervelocity impact of space debris particles, etc. In contrast to graph-based methods, where the state of the system is understood as a discrete set of particles, we propose a learning framework for scalable representation and dynamics modeling of massive particle systems by treating the system state as a function and its evolution as a trajectory in Hilbert space. Rather than representing the state as a discrete set of particles or embedding it in a nonlinear latent manifold, we approximate the state space with a linear subspace spanned by learned neural basis functions. This parameterization enables direct projection to obtain latent coefficients and explicit access to the basis functions, avoiding optimization over a nonlinear latent space. The resulting representation admits a natural interpretation: latent variables correspond to coefficients in Hilbert space, and basis functions correspond to spatial modes, analogous to Proper Orthogonal Decomposition. The framework thus unifies classical projection-based reduced-order modeling with modern deep learning, while remaining invariant to the number of discretization points. Experiments on large-scale SPH simulations with over one million particles, including dynamic events with extreme deformation and fragmentation, demonstrate that the proposed method accurately reconstructs and predicts dynamics, achieving an R$^2$ score above $0.99$ with as few as $32$ basis functions.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces a scalable learning framework for dynamic systems simulations, moving from discrete particle-based to function-based modeling for enhanced efficiency.
AI.motivation: Lagrangian methods like SPH and MPM are computationally expensive when simulating multi-scale and dynamic phenomena, which limits their scalability and application scope.
AI.method: The authors propose a framework where the system state is represented as a trajectory in Hilbert space using learned neural basis functions, combining reduced-order modeling with deep learning principles.
AI.result: The method achieves high accuracy (R² > 0.99) in reconstructing and predicting dynamics using as few as 32 basis functions, validated on SPH simulations with over one million particles.
AI.conclusion: The framework provides a scalable and interpretable approach to dynamic simulation, bridging classical and modern techniques, and is invariant to the number of discretization points.

title: ATN3D: Density-Aware LiDAR-Radar Early 3D Object Detection Under Extreme Sparsity
summary: 3D object detection is the backbone of perception for automated vehicles (AV) and broader intelligent transportation systems applications. Long-range detection is challenging because sensing evidence is sparse; yet this ``long-range'' scenario is routine in traffic. Although >30m is often labeled long-range in computer vision, on roadways it affords only approx. 1-2s for perception and decision-making. Under such extreme sparsity, two core challenges arise. First, early multimodal fusion tends to discard sparsity information and inject noise from empty or falsely occupied cells, degrading long-range recall. Second, context-agnostic uniform channel supervision favors dense and near-range samples, leaving far and small objects under-optimized, delaying the earliest detection of distant objects. We propose ``Ask The Neighbor'' (ATN3D), a LiDAR-Radar framework tailored for sparse-range conditions. ATN3D introduces (i) Density-aware early fusion with cross-modal gating that conditions fusion on per-voxel density/sparsity and Radar evidence, (ii) Occupancy-gated neighborhood aggregation with circular kernels to aggregate only from credible cells, (iii) Evidence-conditioned channel self-attention to adapt channel weights with weather/range, and (iv) a Range-aware loss that re-balances classification and localization by distance, aligning training with distance-stratified evaluation. On the VoD benchmark across clear and foggy conditions, ATN3D surpasses strong baselines: +3.55% mAP in clear weather and +8.41% mAP under simulated heavy fog; for >30m objects, gains are +3.33% (clear) and +2.09% (heavy fog). These results indicate earlier and more reliable long-range detections under sparse sensing in on-road traffic.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces ATN3D, a novel LiDAR-Radar framework designed for long-range 3D object detection in automated vehicles under sparse sensing conditions, overcoming challenges such as noise in sparse data and lack of adaptation to small/far objects.
AI.motivation: Long-range object detection in automated vehicles is essential due to the sparse nature of sensing evidence in traffic scenarios. Existing methods struggle because they discard valuable sparsity information, introduce noise, and fail to effectively optimize detection for distant objects.
AI.method: ATN3D leverages density-aware early fusion with cross-modal gating, occupancy-gated neighborhood aggregation, evidence-conditioned channel self-attention, and a range-aware loss function to enhance the training and performance of long-range 3D object detection.
AI.result: ATN3D achieves significant performance improvements on the VoD benchmark, with +3.55% mAP in clear weather and +8.41% mAP in foggy conditions. It also demonstrates better detection of >30m objects, with gains of +3.33% in clear weather and +2.09% in heavy fog.
AI.conclusion: ATN3D notably improves early and reliable detection of distant objects under sparse sensing conditions, making it a valuable contribution to the development of perception systems for automated vehicles.

title: A Unifying Lens on Reward Uncertainty in RLHF
summary: Reinforcement learning from human feedback (RLHF) is bottlenecked by \emph{reward hacking}, where the policy exploits errors in a proxy reward model (RM) and produces high RM scores without genuine quality gains. A natural mitigation is \emph{pessimism}: penalizing rewards in regions where the RM is uncertain. However, standard scalar RMs provide no principled notion of uncertainty. We argue that the right object is a \emph{distributional} reward model $p(r\mid x,y)$. Under either a Bayesian inference or a KL-distributionally robust optimization (KL-DRO) lens, the KL-regularized RLHF objective admits a closed-form effective reward $\tilde r(x,y) = \pmβ\log\mathbb{E}_p[e^{\pm r/β}]$. The pessimistic branch unifies the prior heuristics for RM ensemble aggregation: mean aggregation, worst-case optimization (WCO), and uncertainty-weighted optimization (UWO) all emerge as limits or truncations of this single expression. This also clarifies the implicit assumptions of each existing rule.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes a distributional reward model framework to address reward hacking in reinforcement learning, introducing KL regularization as a unifying approach for handling reward uncertainty.
AI.motivation: Current reinforcement learning from human feedback (RLHF) struggles with reward hacking, where policies exploit proxy reward model errors, leading to poor quality outputs despite high reward scores.
AI.method: The study advocates for a distributional reward model $p(r|x,y)$ to encapsulate uncertainty. Through Bayesian inference or KL-distributionally robust optimization (KL-DRO), they derive a KL-regularized RLHF objective producing an effective reward formula.
AI.result: The effective reward formula aligns with and generalizes prior heuristics like mean aggregation, worst-case optimization (WCO), and uncertainty-weighted optimization (UWO), while also clarifying their underlying assumptions.
AI.conclusion: A distributional perspective on reward modeling and KL regularization robustly addresses reward hacking and synthesizes existing heuristic methods into a single principled framework.

title: CineDance: Towards Next-Generation Multi-Shot Long-Form Cinematic Audio-Video Generation
summary: The fidelity and structural diversity of training datasets fundamentally determine the capabilities of video generation models. While commercial systems showremarkableabilitytogeneratecinematicnarratives, the progress of open-source models remains limited by the scarcity of high-quality training data. To bridge this gap, we introduce CineDance-1M, a large-scale, open research Text-to-Audio-Video (T2AV) dataset designed specifically for multi-shot, long-form joint audio-video generation. Averaging 92.8 seconds and 24.2 continuous shots per video, it provides configurable, structured annotations for both audio and video modalities. This exceptional quality is achieved through a rigorous three-stage curation pipeline: i) diverse sourcing and comprehensive cleansing, ii) film-theory-inspired narrative parsing, and iii) hierarchical dual-modal captioning. For a comprehensive assessment, we propose CineBench, featuring a diverse prompt suite and a six-dimensional, human-aligned metric system tailored for complex narrative audio-video evaluation. Furthermore, we adapt LTX-2.3 into CineDance, which demonstrates exceptional single-modality quality alongside precise audio-video alignment and robust subject and environment consistency, effectively validating our curation strategy and the high quality of CineDance-1M. We anticipate that this work will serve as a solid foundation for accelerating future research in multi-shot, long-form joint audio-video generation. Our project page is available at https://aliothchen.github.io/projects/CineDance/.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces CineDance-1M, a dataset for generating long-form, multi-shot audio-video content, and proposes CineBench for multi-modal evaluation.
AI.motivation: The lack of high-quality training data limits the development of open-source video generation models, particularly for long-form, multi-shot audio-video generation.
AI.method: To address this, the authors created CineDance-1M, a dataset curated via a three-stage process involving sourcing, narrative parsing inspired by film theory, and hierarchical captioning. They also proposed CineBench with diverse prompts and a metric system for evaluation.
AI.result: The study validates the quality of CineDance-1M using the adapted LTX-2.3 model, which exhibits strong audio-video alignment and consistency in subjects and environments.
AI.conclusion: CineDance-1M and CineBench provide a high-quality foundation for advancing open research in long-form, joint audio-video generation.

title: Friend or Foe? Language as an ideological switch in open-weight LLMs under Russian disinformation stress
summary: As Russia's war against Ukraine extends into generative AI, large language models (LLMs) adapted for local post-Soviet languages are deployed in contested information environments. Policy and industry discourse assumes that culturally aligned adaptation encodes the political orientation of the target community: a Ukrainian-oriented model will resist Russian narratives, a Russian-oriented one will reinforce them. Does it? This article systematically disconfirms that assumption. We run a controlled audit of four openly available LLMs sharing a common base model but fine-tuned for different linguistic communities, querying them in Ukrainian, Russian and English across ten contested wartime narratives: Crimea, "denazification", the "one people" thesis, and atrocity denial at Bucha and Mariupol. The result is a Fine-Tuning Paradox: the Ukrainian-oriented model shows the weakest resistance to Russian disinformation in Russian, while the Russian-oriented one exhibits the strongest rejection. Corpus composition, language coverage and prompt format prove more decisive than nominal cultural provenance. We situate these findings within debates on hybrid warfare, digital sovereignty and post-imperial information orders, arguing that the principal threat to regional information sovereignty is not adversarial fine-tuning but the untested assumption that cultural alignment guarantees resilience.

AI.tldr: The paper examines how large language models adapted for Ukrainian and Russian contexts handle wartime narratives, discovering a paradox where adaptation does not guarantee expected resistance to disinformation.
AI.motivation: The research investigates whether fine-tuning LLMs for local linguistic and cultural alignment can effectively counter disinformation in the context of the Russia-Ukraine war.
AI.method: The study conducted a controlled audit of four LLMs fine-tuned for different linguistic communities, using queries across ten contested wartime narratives in Ukrainian, Russian, and English.
AI.result: Contrary to expectations, the Ukrainian-oriented model showed weaker resistance to Russian disinformation when queried in Russian, while the Russian-oriented model performed better at rejecting it. Factors such as corpus composition and prompt format were shown to be more impactful than cultural alignment.
AI.conclusion: Cultural alignment of LLMs does not inherently ensure resilience against disinformation, challenging the assumption that nominally aligned models can reinforce targeted community resistance. The main threat lies in untested reliance on this assumption.

title: Neural Legendre-Fenchel transform with Hessian Preconditioning
summary: The Legendre-Fenchel (LF) transform is a fundamental tool in convex analysis and machine learning that maps lower semi-continuous functions to their convex conjugates. In practice, when closed-form formula are not available for expressing convex conjugates of given functions, one must approximate them using various techniques. One recent such versatile numerical method is the deep Legendre transform method which relies on neural networks although it remains challenging particularly for tackling ill-conditioned functions. This work builds on the reformulation of the LF transform as a projective polarity. A notable property of this framework is its affine invariance. We leverage this affine invariance to introduce a Hessian-based preconditioning strategy. Specifically, we apply an affine deformation around a minimizer so that the second-order Taylor approximation of the function coincides with the canonical paraboloid, whose conjugation map is the identity. A residual network initialized near the identity can then learn this simplified mapping, while the original conjugation map is recovered through the inverse deformation. The proposed preconditioning incurs only a modest computational overhead, consisting of a single eigendecomposition during initialization and two matrix-vector multiplications per query. Experiments on a diverse set of convex functions, including high-dimensional benchmarks, demonstrate improved convergence rates and enhanced numerical accuracy of the conjugation, with particularly significant gains for ill-conditioned problems. Finally, we discuss the scope of applicability of our proposed method and highlight several of its limitations.

AI.tldr: The paper improves the deep Legendre transform method used for convex conjugates by introducing a Hessian-based preconditioning strategy for better efficiency and accuracy, particularly on ill-conditioned functions.
AI.motivation: The motivation is to address the challenges of computing convex conjugates of functions, especially ill-conditioned ones, where closed-form expressions are not available and existing methods are inefficient.
AI.method: The authors reformulate the Legendre-Fenchel transform as a projective polarity and apply an affine deformation around a minimizer using Hessian-based preconditioning. Residual networks initialized near identity are used to learn a simplified mapping, reducing complexity for ill-conditioned problems.
AI.result: The proposed method achieves better convergence rates and higher numerical accuracy for computing convex conjugates. Significant improvements are observed in handling high-dimensional and ill-conditioned problems with modest computational overhead.
AI.conclusion: The paper presents an effective and computationally efficient approach to improving the deep Legendre transform method. However, it also discusses the limitations and scope of the approach.

title: MAVIS: Multi-Agent Video Retrieval via Structured Video Understanding
summary: The dominant paradigm in video retrieval relies on embedding-based full-corpus scanning, which suffers from inherent computational inefficiency and the semantic asymmetry between information-dense videos and sparse textual queries. To bridge this gap, we introduce \textbf{MAVIS}, a novel multi-agent framework that rethinks retrieval as cooperative reasoning rather than brute-force search. MAVIS first bridges the granularity mismatch by parsing raw videos into a \textbf{Structured Semantic Library}, enabling explicit attribute-level indexing. During retrieval, a planner decomposes complex user intents into atomic sub-tasks, dispatching specialized agents to independently nominate candidates. Crucially, MAVIS employs a \textbf{Logic-aware Debate} mechanism with a strict veto protocol, where agents collaboratively prune logical mismatches to identify a compact set of ``controversial'' candidates for fine-grained verification. This agentic workflow effectively bypasses the inefficiency of full-library traversal. Extensive experiments on MSR-VTT, MSVD, and ActivityNet demonstrate that MAVIS achieves competitive performance without task-specific fine-tuning, offering a scalable and interpretable alternative to traditional dual-encoder approaches.

AI.tldr: MAVIS is a multi-agent framework for video retrieval that emphasizes cooperative reasoning over brute-force search, offering improved efficiency and interpretability.
AI.motivation: Traditional video retrieval methods relying on full-corpus scanning face inefficiencies and challenges due to the semantic gap between dense video content and sparse textual queries.
AI.method: MAVIS employs a multi-agent framework: videos are parsed into a Structured Semantic Library, and a planner decomposes user queries into sub-tasks. Specialized agents suggest candidates, and a Logic-aware Debate mechanism refines these to a compact, accurate set.
AI.result: Experiments on datasets like MSR-VTT, MSVD, and ActivityNet show MAVIS achieves competitive performance with no task-specific fine-tuning.
AI.conclusion: MAVIS provides a scalable, interpretable alternative to existing dual-encoder video retrieval approaches by focusing on efficiency and logical reasoning.

title: The Hidden Bias of Process Reward Models:PRISM for Rewarding the Right Reasoning
summary: Process Reward Models (PRMs) improve credit assignment for reasoning by providing step-level feedback. However, we identify a hidden bias in PRMs caused by severe imbalance in step-level training data. Standard cross-entropy training amplifies this bias, causing PRMs to overcredit plausible but incorrect steps and produce high false-positive rates. We show that these false positives have an asymmetric downstream effect: false negatives mainly slow exploration, whereas false positives actively steer Best-of-N selection, guided decoding, and policy optimization toward flawed reasoning. This suggests that PRM training should shift from pointwise label fitting to reliable relative comparisons. To address this, we propose PRISM (Precision Ranking for Improved Step Modeling), a policy-aware PRM training framework that learns from contrastive step-level comparisons and hard negatives generated by a temporal lookahead strategy, requiring no new human labels. We further use a difficulty-aware curriculum to optimize the contrastive step margin. Across PRMBench and ProcessBench, PRISM substantially reduces false positives (22% on PRMBench) and improves macro F1 over strong discriminative PRMs. When applied to policy optimization and search tasks, including guided decoding and Best-of-N selection, it consistently improves accuracy (up to 22% for guided decoding and 33% for Best-of-N) and robustness. More broadly, trustworthy process supervision is not just about assigning high rewards, but about rewarding the right reasoning for the right reasons.

AI.tldr: The paper identifies bias in Process Reward Models (PRMs) due to imbalanced step-level data and proposes a new training framework, PRISM, which significantly reduces false positives and improves task performance.
AI.motivation: The authors aim to address a hidden bias in PRMs caused by imbalance in step-level training data, which leads to overcrediting flawed reasoning and results in poor downstream performance.
AI.method: They propose PRISM, a training framework using contrastive step-level comparisons, hard negatives from temporal lookahead, and a difficulty-aware curriculum, eliminating the need for new human labels.
AI.result: PRISM reduces false positives by 22% on PRMBench, improves macro F1, and increases accuracy across tasks like guided decoding (up to 22%) and Best-of-N selection (up to 33%).
AI.conclusion: Trustworthy process supervision involves not just rewarding high-quality reasoning but doing so for the correct reasons. PRISM demonstrates improved reliability and task performance by tackling bias in PRMs.

title: Do Video Foundation Models Understand Intuitive Physics? A Layerwise Probing Analysis
summary: We study whether pretrained video foundation models encode intuitive-physics information in their frozen representations, and how this information varies across model families, layers, and probe types. Using frozen-feature probing on IntPhys2 and Minimal Video Pairs (MVP), we compare predictive joint-embedding models (V-JEPA), masked reconstruction models (VideoMAE), and a diffusion-based video generator (LTX-Video). V-JEPA achieves the strongest overall results across benchmarks, especially with probes that model temporal dynamics, while VideoMAE remains competitive and LTX-Video recovers weaker but non-trivial signal. Layerwise analyses show that physics-relevant information is weakest in early layers and becomes most accessible at intermediate-to-late depth, and temporal controls show that disrupting frame order substantially reduces performance, especially on MVP. Together, these results suggest that intuitive-physics knowledge emerges reliably in pretrained video representations, but its accessibility depends strongly on pretraining paradigm, representational depth, and readout mechanism.

AI.tldr: This paper explores whether pretrained video foundation models inherently encode intuitive physics and examines the factors influencing this encoding.
AI.motivation: To investigate how pretrained video models capture intuitive-physics information and to examine variations across model families, layers, and probe types.
AI.method: The study uses frozen-feature probing on IntPhys2 and Minimal Video Pairs (MVP) benchmarks, comparing joint-embedding models (V-JEPA), masked reconstruction models (VideoMAE), and diffusion-based models (LTX-Video).
AI.result: V-JEPA performed best, followed by VideoMAE, while LTX-Video showed weaker but noticeable results. Physics-relevant information was strongest at intermediate-to-late layers, and frame order disruption significantly reduced performance.
AI.conclusion: Pretrained video models encode intuitive-physics information, but its accessibility depends on the pretraining approach, layer depth, and probe mechanisms.

title: FlashMemory-DeepSeek-V4: Lightning Index Ultra-Long Context via Lookahead Sparse Attention
comment: Technical report. 11 pages. Code and model available at https://github.com/libertywing/FlashMemory-Deepseek-V4 and https://huggingface.co/libertywing/FlashMemory-Deepseek-V4
summary:
Conventional LLMs keep the full KV cache loaded during decoding, causing a severe GPU memory bottleneck for ultra-long context serving. In this report, we propose Lookahead Sparse Attention (LSA), a novel inference paradigm powered by a Neural Memory Indexer built upon the DeepSeek-V4 architecture. Rather than passively attending to all historical tokens, LSA proactively predicts future context demands and preserves only the query-critical KV chunks in the GPU memory. Crucially, we instantiate this architecture via a backbone-free decoupled training strategy. By formulating the indexer as a standard dual-encoder architecture, we train it independently using standard retrieval training frameworks without ever loading the massive backbone model into GPU memory.
  We demonstrate that this "less is more" paradigm significantly maximizes serving efficiency while acting as an effective attention denoiser in tasks that rely on long-term global memory. Across primary long-context evaluation suites (e.g., LongBench-v2, LongMemEval, and RULER), FM-DS-V4 compresses the average physical KV cache footprint down to merely 13.5% of the full-context baseline, while consistently preserving or slightly elevating downstream accuracy (+0.6% absolute margin on average). Crucially, at extreme 500K scales, FlashMemory suppresses the physical KV cache overhead by over 90% without destabilizing the backbone's core reasoning capacities.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces Lookahead Sparse Attention (LSA), a method that improves GPU memory efficiency in long-context LLM decoding by predicting future context needs and retaining only critical parts of the KV cache.
AI.motivation: The study addresses the GPU memory bottleneck faced by LLMs when dealing with ultra-long contexts due to the full KV cache loading during decoding.
AI.method: The authors propose LSA, powered by a Neural Memory Indexer based on DeepSeek-V4 architecture. This approach involves a decoupled training strategy with a dual-encoder model trained separately from the main backbone model, avoiding GPU memory bottlenecks.
AI.result: The proposed approach reduces the KV cache usage to 13.5% of the baseline on average, preserving or slightly improving accuracy. At extreme context lengths (500K), it cuts KV cache overhead by more than 90% without impacting core reasoning.
AI.conclusion: LSA enhances efficiency by significantly reducing memory usage while maintaining or improving performance, making it particularly viable for tasks requiring long-term memory and context handling.

title: Visual Prompting Meets Feature Reconstruction-Based Anomaly Detection with Dual-Teacher Supervision
summary: Recent Anomaly Detection methods achieve perfect detection and segmentation scores on well-established datasets, such as MVTec. However, many of these methods face challenges when foundational assumptions - such as consistent object scale, viewpoint, background, illumination, and centered placement - are violated. Those variations that occur render anomaly detection methods unusable in many real-world scenarios. To address these limitations, we introduce three key contributions: (1) a visual prompting pipeline that isolates objects using foreground-background masking; (2) a mechanism for unfreezing the teacher in student-teacher models to improve domain adaptability; and (3) a data augmentation strategy leveraging diffusion-generated synthetic images to enhance anomaly detection performance. We achieve a 3.5 percentage point improvement over the previous state-of-the-art on the challenging AeBAD dataset by using the Masked Multiscale Reconstruction (MMR) model as our backbone.

AI.tldr: The paper addresses limitations in anomaly detection methods under real-world variations and introduces three key contributions for improving robustness and performance.
AI.motivation: Existing anomaly detection methods struggle under real-world conditions where foundational assumptions like consistent scale and illumination are violated.
AI.method: The authors propose three methods: (1) visual prompting with foreground-background masking, (2) domain adaptation via teacher unfreezing, and (3) diffusion-based synthetic data augmentation.
AI.result: A 3.5 percentage point improvement over the previous state-of-the-art on the challenging AeBAD dataset was achieved by leveraging the MMR model.
AI.conclusion: The proposed strategies enhance anomaly detection robustness and adaptability, making the methods suitable for varied real-world scenarios.

title: Beyond FLOPs: Benchmarking Real Inference Acceleration of LLM Pruning under a GEMM-Centric Taxonomy
summary: Pruning has emerged as a dominant paradigm for accelerating large language model (LLM) inference, spanning a broad spectrum of methods that remove computation across tokens, layers, heads, dimensions, and attention patterns. Despite sharing the same objective, these pruning approaches induce fundamentally different execution behaviors, causing realized speedups to depend heavily on hardware and kernel implementations. Consequently, the practical acceleration benefits of different pruning families remain poorly understood. In this work, we introduce a GEMM-centric taxonomy that reorganizes existing pruning methods according to the logical \textbf{M}, \textbf{N}, and \textbf{K} dimensions of general matrix multiplication (GEMM). Leveraging this abstraction, we build a unified benchmarking framework that enables implementation-consistent comparison across the pruning design space and systematically characterizes the acceleration--quality Pareto frontier. Our results show that static depth pruning remains the strongest Pareto-optimal baseline and stays closest to its theoretical acceleration upper bound in memory-bounded scenarios. During prefill, the frontier transitions from static depth at low quality loss (0\%--4\%), to dynamic depth at moderate loss (5\%--16\%), and finally to static width pruning at higher loss levels (17\%--26\%). These findings establish the first unified view of the practical limits of pruning-based LLM acceleration and provide guidance for future pruning research.\footnote{Code is available at https://github.com/EIT-NLP/LLM-Pruning/tree/main/PruningInferSim}

AI.tldr: This paper examines pruning techniques for large language models (LLMs) through a unified framework and GEMM-based taxonomy, analyzing their practical acceleration benefits across different scenarios.
AI.motivation: To address the gap in understanding the practical acceleration benefits of diverse LLM pruning techniques across hardware and implementation settings.
AI.method: The authors propose a GEMM-centric taxonomy for categorizing pruning methods and develop a benchmarking framework to systematically compare their acceleration-quality trade-offs.
AI.result: Static depth pruning emerges as the most Pareto-optimal approach in memory-constrained settings, with dynamic depth and static width pruning showing better performance under certain quality-loss thresholds.
AI.conclusion: The work creates a unified understanding of the practical capabilities and trade-offs of various pruning approaches, offering insights and benchmarks to guide future research.

title: SoccerNet 2026 Player-Centric Ball-Action Spotting:Retraining and Post-Processing Extensions to the FOOTPASS Baselines
comment: CVPR 2026 SoccerNet Player Centric Ball Action Spotting Challenge, Rank 7
summary: We describe our system for the SoccerNet 2026 Player-Centric Ball-Action Spotting Challenge, which requires predicting who performs which action and when, across eight classes in broadcast soccer. Building on the three FOOTPASS baselines [1] (TAAD, TAAD+GNN, and TAAD+DST), we contribute four extensions: (1) gradient check pointing to enable full-backbone fine-tuning on a single GPU; (2) fusion of GNN logits into the DST encoder, combining graph-based tactical context with per-player visual features; (3) square-root frequency class weighting to address the 213:1 pass-to-tackle imbalance in the training data; and (4) a post processing pipeline comprising per-class logit gating, temporal frame refinement, jersey re-assignment, and a two-model ensemble. Our system achieves 0.548 Macro F1 on the test set and 0.446 on the challenge set (server evaluation).

AI.tldr: The paper presents a system for predicting player-centric actions in soccer using enhanced models and post-processing techniques, achieving notable evaluation scores.
AI.motivation: To improve predictions of player actions within soccer matches, enabling detailed analysis and addressing imbalances in training data.
AI.method: The system builds on FOOTPASS baselines with four key improvements: gradient check-pointing, fusion of GNN logits with DST encoders, class weighting based on frequency, and an advanced post-processing pipeline.
AI.result: Achieved 0.548 Macro F1 on the test set and 0.446 on the challenge set according to server evaluation.
AI.conclusion: The integrated approach enhances player-centric ball-action spotting in soccer, demonstrating the effectiveness of their model extensions and techniques.

title: Position: Anthropomorphic Misalignment Research Needs Stronger Evidence
summary: We argue that many Anthropomorphic Misalignment Research (AMR) studies need stronger evidence to ensure that they can provide a robust foundation for critical safety decisions, such as model deployment and regulation. By evaluating failure modes across different misalignment concepts, such as deception, emergent misalignment, and sycophancy, we show how conceptual ambiguity, non-robust datasets, experimental design, and insufficient causal interventions can lead to overinterpretation of model behaviors. This position paper aims to offer guidance on evidentiary considerations that can help improve methodological rigor in AMR. To achieve this, we provide a clear call to action through a proposed framework of evidence levels and a diagnostic checklist. These shared standards will enable more productive scientific discourse and ensure that claims about AI risks rest on solid empirical foundations.

AI.tldr: The paper critiques Anthropomorphic Misalignment Research (AMR) for needing stronger empirical foundations to substantiate safety-related decisions, proposing a framework and checklist for methodological rigor.
AI.motivation: To enhance the scientific rigor of AMR studies and ensure critical safety decisions like model deployment and regulation rely on robust evidence.
AI.method: The authors evaluate failure modes across misalignment concepts and propose a framework of evidence levels and a diagnostic checklist for improved methodological rigor.
AI.result: The paper identifies issues like conceptual ambiguity and insufficient causal interventions in AMR studies, proposing shared evidentiary standards to address them.
AI.conclusion: By adopting the proposed framework and checklist, the AMR field can better ensure claims about AI risks are empirically substantiated, fostering productive discourse and safer AI practices.

title: Stabilizing On-Policy Distillation for MLLM Reasoning with Global Normalization
summary: On-policy distillation (OPD) has recently emerged as an important post-training paradigm. By using a stronger teacher model to provide dense, fine-grained supervision for sampled trajectories, OPD offers a clear advantage over reinforcement learning with verifiable rewards (RLVR), which typically depends on sparse binary or outcome-based environmental feedback. However, naive token-level distillation can suffer from gradient instability, due to magnitude misalignment in outlier states. To address this issue, we propose Globally Normalized Distillation Policy Optimization (GNDPO), a practical method that stabilizes optimization by transforming raw KL scores into batch-level relative advantages. This normalization effectively mitigates gradient explosions while retaining the benefits of token-level guidance. Experimental results show that GNDPO substantially improves training robustness and downstream performance across multimodal reasoning tasks. The code is released at https://github.com/OPPO-Mente-Lab/GNDPO.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes Globally Normalized Distillation Policy Optimization (GNDPO) to stabilize on-policy distillation (OPD) by mitigating gradient instability.
AI.motivation: To address the limitations of reinforcement learning with verifiable rewards (RLVR) and the gradient instability in naive token-level distillation for OPD.
AI.method: Introducing GNDPO, which applies batch-level normalization to KL scores, improving optimization stability and retaining fine-grained token-level guidance.
AI.result: GNDPO enhances training robustness and boosts downstream task performance, particularly in multimodal reasoning scenarios.
AI.conclusion: GNDPO resolves key challenges in OPD, offering effective and stable optimization with experimental validations demonstrating its benefits.

title: GenEyePose: Patient-Free, Knowledge-Based Saccadic Eye Movement Modeling for Digital Neurophysiologic Biomarker Development
summary: Eye movements, including saccades, are widely regarded as highly sensitive and objective biomarkers of neurophysiologic states. Detecting saccadic signatures in neurologic diseases offers a rapid, portable alternative to brain imaging, avoiding access and cost barriers. Currently, there are no robust AI-enabled video-oculographic solutions (e.g., digital biomarkers) for screening, triaging, or localizing brain abnormalities due to privacy issues and scarce datasets. In this work, we propose the first fully synthetic, patient-free, multimodal eye movement generation pipeline for generalizable saccade analysis. Using this synthetic dataset, we trained a deep learning classifier to distinguish between normal and abnormal (hypometria and hypermetria) saccadic accuracies and evaluated its performance on real-world clinical data. The model achieved an AUROC of 0.76 and a sensitivity of 0.71, showing that the synthetic data has strong potential to generalize for clinical applications, including as a screening tool in at-home and emergency room settings or a tool for precise neuroanatomic localization.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces an AI-driven synthetic dataset to analyze saccadic eye movements, achieving promising results for detecting abnormal neurophysiological states.
AI.motivation: The paper aims to address the lack of AI-enabled tools for analyzing saccadic eye movements as biomarkers for neurological diseases due to privacy concerns and scarce datasets.
AI.method: The authors developed a fully synthetic, multimodal eye movement generation pipeline and used it to train a deep learning model for identifying normal and abnormal saccadic accuracies.
AI.result: The model demonstrated an AUROC of 0.76 and a sensitivity of 0.71 when applied to real-world clinical data, showcasing the synthetic dataset's potential for generalization.
AI.conclusion: Synthetic datasets can be a viable solution for creating AI-based digital biomarkers for neurological disease screening and localization in various clinical and non-clinical settings.

title: From Shortcuts to Reasoning: Robust Post-Training of Theory of Mind with Reinforcement Learning
summary: Theory of Mind (ToM) is a must-acquire skill for modern foundation model systems to operate effectively and safely in the real world. Recent works have explored honing ToM via post-training; however, we show that such progress is confounded by a pervasive "shortcut" issue: tasks can reach up to 99% accuracy by simply exploiting spurious causal correlations, leading to a false sense of ToM. Motivated by this, we first develop a framework to systematically examine ToM datasets for shortcuts and provide guidance for future development. We find that questions reducible to pure state tracking, such as "belief," are especially shortcut-prone compared to mind questions, such as "intention," where reasoning beyond tracking is required. Using four shortcut-free datasets across three ToM contexts, we then comprehensively study whether Reinforcement Fine-Tuning with verifiable rewards and explicit reasoning chains, called Thinking-RFT, elevates ToM beyond Supervised Fine-Tuning, or SFT. Our key findings are as follows. First, Thinking-RFT effectively improves ToM in all scenarios, with a 6% improvement over SFT, particularly in complex higher-order reasoning, with a 10% improvement over SFT, and multimodal cases, with a 7% improvement over SFT. It also generalizes notably better to unseen domains and higher-order queries while being more robust to counterfactuals. Second, ToM benefits specifically from the joint effect of reasoning and RL: Thinking-RFT outperforms Non-Thinking-RFT by 7% on average. Third, RFT works by learning to ground its reasoning on anchor cues, such as keywords and state changes, that correspond to causal factors. We believe our study is useful for developing effective and robust ToM post-training datasets and advancing critical ToM capabilities.

AI.tldr: The study investigates improving Theory of Mind (ToM) in foundation models through Reinforcement Fine-Tuning (RFT), addressing issues of shortcut exploitation and evaluating the effectiveness of reasoning-based tuning strategies.
AI.motivation: To address the limitations of current approaches in enhancing Theory of Mind (ToM) for foundation models, as existing methods often fall prey to shortcut exploitation, undermining genuine ToM reasoning.
AI.method: A framework was proposed to identify and eliminate shortcut-prone datasets, followed by the use of a reasoning-enhanced version of Reinforcement Fine-Tuning (Thinking-RFT) compared to Supervised Fine-Tuning (SFT) across different ToM contexts.
AI.result: Thinking-RFT produced improvements over SFT in ToM tasks, notably achieving 6% overall gains and excelling in complex higher-order reasoning and multimodal scenarios, with significant robustness and generalization capabilities.
AI.conclusion: The joint use of reasoning and reinforcement learning in Thinking-RFT significantly enhances ToM capabilities, providing an effective strategy for eliminating shortcuts and advancing model reasoning capabilities.

title: Cranio-Diff: Diffusion-based Cross-domain Craniofacial Reconstruction with 2D X-ray Skull Guidance and Structural Identity Constraints
summary: The state-of-the-art generative models, such as CycleGAN, Pix2Pix, and diffusion models have demonstrated remarkable performance in the face generation task. However, they fail to effectively capture cross-modality semantic information in craniofacial reconstruction when translating from the skull (x-ray) to the face (optical) domain, due to a mismatch in the alignment of structural identity across modalities. To address this issue, we propose Cranio-Diff, a diffusion-based framework for cross-domain cranio-facial reconstruction from 2D X-ray skull images. The proposed approach integrates skull-conditioned structural guidance through ControlNet with biometric text conditioning to generate a face which is more semantically and structurally aligned with the given skull. The proposed Cranio-diff method is evaluated on skull-face dataset obtained from X-ray scans of 120 subjects in lateral and frontal views. To enable controlled evaluation, each face image is synthesised across three age groups (25, 45, 65) and three BMI variations of -10%, baseline and +10%, yielding 4320 paired samples. To the best of our knowledge, this is the only X-ray-face dataset with this magnitude. Extensive experiments showed that the proposed method outperforms recent existing approaches in both generated image quality and retrieval task. Finally, to evaluate the performance of our proposed method, we have evaluated the quality of the generated image using FID, IS, SSIM, LPIPS, PSNR and ArcFace score. Additionally, retrieval performance is evaluated using recall@k, mAP@k and MRR@k. Obtained experimental results demonstrate that the proposed method can be used as an alternate tool in providing aid in forensic investigations.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces Cranio-Diff, a diffusion-based model for reconstructing faces from 2D X-ray skull images, outperforming current models in alignment and reconstruction quality.
AI.motivation: Current state-of-the-art generative models fail to align structural identity in cross-modality craniofacial reconstruction due to mismatched semantics between the skull (X-ray) and face (optical) domains.
AI.method: The Cranio-Diff method uses ControlNet for skull-conditioned structural guidance and biometric text conditioning to generate semantically and structurally aligned faces. It is evaluated using a newly created extensive paired dataset.
AI.result: Cranio-Diff outperforms existing approaches on image quality metrics like FID, IS, SSIM, LPIPS, PSNR, and retrieval performance using metrics like recall@k, mAP@k, and MRR@k.
AI.conclusion: This method demonstrates strong potential for forensic applications by effectively reconstructing faces from skull X-rays with improved structural alignment and image quality over previous models.

title: Addressing Market Regime Changes and Heavy-Tailed Returns in Portfolio Optimization via Bayesian VAR and Elliptical Black-Litterman
comment: 9 pages, 3 figures, 4 tables. Extends our prior work [Mikriukov et al., ICIC 2025] on Black-Litterman under Elliptical Distributions (BLED). Manuscript under review
summary: Deep reinforcement learning (DRL) frameworks for portfolio optimization have shown promise for their ability to learn allocation rules dynamically from market data. However, these models fail to account for fat-tailed returns, which characterize actual market behavior with more frequent extreme events. Furthermore, historical data is treated homogeneously, without accounting for temporal importance, leading models to fail during regime changes. We propose a new BAVAR-BLED algorithm that combines methods derived from Bayesian-Averaging Vector Autoregressive (BAVAR) and the Black-Litterman model using Elliptical Distributions (BLED) within a TD3 architecture. BAVAR captures a set of vector autoregressive representations that consider multi-scale temporal features, enabling adaptive allocation decisions based on regime-aware estimates of return expectations and dispersion matrices. These estimates serve as prior inputs to BLED, a model that uses Student's t-distributions, allowing for more realistic fat tail return estimates. The BAVAR-BLED algorithm uses transformer networks for view construction and CNNs for risk-aversion estimates, which modify dynamic allocation decisions based on market conditions. An evaluation of 29 Dow Jones Industrial Average constituents over a decade-long market period shows that BAVAR-BLED significantly outperforms state-of-the-art methods, achieving Sharpe and Sortino ratios of 1.72 and 2.70, respectively, and total returns of 57.26%.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces BAVAR-BLED, a deep reinforcement learning (DRL) algorithm for portfolio optimization, which integrates Bayesian-Averaging Vector Autoregressive (BAVAR) and Black-Litterman models to account for fat-tailed returns and market regime changes.
AI.motivation: Existing DRL models for portfolio optimization fail to handle market characteristics like fat-tailed returns and temporal regime shifts, reducing their effectiveness during significant market events.
AI.method: The proposed method combines BAVAR for multi-scale temporal feature analysis and regime-aware metrics with BLED leveraging Student's t-distributions for realistic return modeling, integrated using a TD3 framework. Transformers and CNNs are incorporated for dynamic allocation decisions based on evolving market data.
AI.result: The BAVAR-BLED algorithm significantly outperformed contemporary models in a 10-year evaluation of Dow Jones stocks, achieving high Sharpe and Sortino ratios (1.72 and 2.70) and producing 57.26% total returns.
AI.conclusion: BAVAR-BLED provides a robust and adaptive solution for portfolio optimization by effectively addressing fat-tailed returns, temporal dynamics, and market regime variations, outperforming existing methods.

title: HDSL: A Hierarchical Domain-Specific Language for Structured 3D Indoor Scene Generation and Localized Editing with LLM Agents
summary: Text-driven indoor scene generation and editing require an intermediate representation that language models can both produce and revise. Existing LLM-based systems often rely on scene graphs or global constraint lists, which are compact but underspecify local geometry and make instruction-based edits difficult to localize. We frame this problem as structured program generation and local program repair, and propose Hierarchical Descriptive Scene Language (HDSL), an XML/CSS-style domain-specific language for structured 3D indoor scenes. HDSL represents rooms, regions, objects, and support surfaces as a tree with local coordinates, making complex scenes easier to plan recursively and easier to retrieve for editing. Our pipeline uses LLM agents to generate HDSL subtrees with bounded verification, grounds non-virtual nodes through multimodal asset retrieval, and applies force-directed layout optimization to repair boundary and collision errors. For editing, Hierarchical Retrieval-Augmented Generation retrieves the relevant subtree, asks the LLM to rewrite only that local context, and merges the result back through a deterministic three-way merge. In our reproduced benchmark, HDSL improves average object coverage, text-scene alignment, and generation time over full text-to-scene baselines while remaining competitive with recent layout-only reproductions on geometry metrics; for editing, HRAG reduces token use by $5.22\times$ and runtime by $6.19\times$, produces valid DSL for all eight paired edits, and better preserves unrelated scene objects.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes Hierarchical Descriptive Scene Language (HDSL), a structured language for generating and editing 3D indoor scenes using LLMs, improving text-scene alignment and efficiency.
AI.motivation: Existing systems for text-driven 3D scene generation struggle with underspecified geometry and localized edits due to reliance on scene graphs or global constraint lists.
AI.method: The authors introduced HDSL, a tree-structured, XML/CSS-style representation for 3D scenes. The method involves LLM agents for HDSL generation, asset retrieval, layout optimization, and supports local edits with Hierarchical Retrieval-Augmented Generation.
AI.result: HDSL improves object coverage, text-scene alignment, generation time, reduces token usage by 5.22×, runtime by 6.19×, produces valid DSL, and better preserves unrelated scene objects during edits.
AI.conclusion: HDSL and HRAG enable more efficient and accurate text-driven scene generation and localized editing, addressing limitations of previous approaches and advancing structured representations in 3D scene modeling.

title: Can LLMs understand LilyPond? A benchmark for symbolic music generation and understanding
summary: Symbolic music evaluation for large language models remains fragmented across representations, datasets, and metrics. We introduce LilyBench, a LilyPond-based benchmark that jointly evaluates symbolic music generation and music understanding on the same family of open-weight LLMs. The benchmark includes a 200-prompt generation suite and ten understanding tasks adapted from ABC-Eval, covering syntax, metadata prediction, structural sequencing, and music recognition. Generation quality is evaluated using compile rate, MusPy descriptor distributions via Jensen-Shannon similarity, and LilyBERT-based Fréchet Music Distance (FMD). Experiments on four open-weight models show that executable LilyPond generation is achievable in zero-shot settings, while structural understanding tasks remain challenging despite strong performance on composer and genre recognition. Our experiments also reveal systematic disagreements between descriptor-based and embedding-based metrics, suggesting that symbolic music evaluation benefits from metric triangulation rather than single-score ranking. We release the benchmark, prompt bank, and evaluation code to support future research in symbolic music generation and understanding at https://github.com/CSCPadova/lilybench

AI.tldr: LilyBench is a benchmark for evaluating symbolic music generation and understanding with open-weight language models, using both generation and understanding tasks alongside new evaluation metrics.
AI.motivation: The motivation behind the paper is to address the fragmented state of evaluating symbolic music with large language models by standardizing the process across representations, tasks, and metrics.
AI.method: The authors introduce LilyBench, consisting of a 200-prompt generation suite and ten understanding tasks, assessed through compilation success, descriptor similarity, and other metric triangulation methods.
AI.result: Experiments demonstrated that generating executable LilyPond music is feasible in zero-shot setups, but structural music understanding remains challenging. The study also identified metric disagreements, pointing to the need for triangulated evaluations.
AI.conclusion: LilyBench provides a unified way to evaluate symbolic music capabilities in LLMs, highlighting both strengths and limitations while advocating for multi-metric evaluation approaches.

title: Hybridizing Equilibrium Propagation with Ising Machines for Efficient Energy-Based Learning
summary: The rapid evolution of artificial intelligence has led to substantial advances in deep neural networks. Nonetheless, conventional GPU-based training remains highly energy-demanding, motivating the exploration of physical dynamics and compatible energy-based learning schemes, such as equilibrium propagation (EP). EP-based training, however, frequently suffers from convergence to local minima due to phase-space contraction. Here we introduce an Ising-dynamics-inspired equilibrium-propagation framework in which dissipative Hopfield relaxation is replaced by an extended phase-space dynamics with conjugate variables. The resulting training paradigm keeps the local two-phase learning rule of EP while changing the physical route by which neural states reach equilibrium. We show that this dynamics lowers effective energy barriers, accelerates convergence, improves noise robustness, and trains deep convolutional Hopfield networks on MNIST, FashionMNIST, and CIFAR-10 with performance comparable to backpropagation.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces an Ising-inspired equilibrium propagation framework to address energy inefficiency and local minima issues in neural network training.
AI.motivation: The study aims to reduce the energy-intensive nature of GPU-based training by adopting physical dynamics and energy-based learning schemes such as equilibrium propagation.
AI.method: The research replaces dissipative Hopfield relaxation in equilibrium propagation with an extended phase-space dynamics based on Ising dynamics.
AI.result: The proposed framework shows improved convergence speed, noise robustness, and comparable performance to backpropagation on MNIST, FashionMNIST, and CIFAR-10 datasets.
AI.conclusion: The Ising-inspired equilibrium propagation presents a viable alternative to traditional GPU-based training, overcoming phase-space contraction issues while maintaining efficiency.

title: Hybrid Robustness Verification for Spatio-Temporal Neural Networks
comment: Accepted at the 9th International Symposium on AI Verification (SAIV 2026)
summary: With AI increasingly deployed in safety-critical systems, providing formal robustness guarantees for the underlying models is essential. Existing verification methods either rely on overly conservative approximations or incur prohibitive computational costs. For example, the use of lp-norm perturbations in video settings encodes the belief that the adversary can inject noise in every video frame. In practice, adversarial perturbations exhibit structured spatial and temporal correlations, constrained to lower-dimensional, semantically meaningful subspaces. In this work, we study robustness verification of 3D CNNs processing video and volumetric inputs, targeting applications in action recognition (UCF-101), autonomous driving (Udacity), and medical imaging (MedMNIST) exploiting realistic assumptions on adversarial strength by modelling them as spatio-temporal constraints - where the attacker can modify either a subset of frames or patches within a set of consecutive frames. We demonstrate that modelling realistic constraints enables tighter approximations. We introduce Spatio-Temporal Bound Propagation (STBP), a verification framework that computes an exact closed-form characterization of the first convolutional layer and propagates certified bounds through subsequent layers using scalable approximations. Computing the exact closed form provides the tightest bounds for the first convolutional layer. Thus, we utilise approximation methods in the remainder of the network. To spur further progress in this field, we propose ST-Bench, a verification benchmark for autonomous driving and activity recognition, to systematically evaluate verifiable robustness. Compared to existing verification-based approaches, STBP provides stronger robustness guarantees with significantly improved scalability, achieving 1.7x higher certified robust accuracy under identical perturbation budgets.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces Spatio-Temporal Bound Propagation (STBP), a verification framework for ensuring robustness in 3D CNNs processing video/volumetric inputs, achieving tighter, scalable guarantees and releasing ST-Bench as a benchmark.
AI.motivation: With AI increasingly deployed in safety-critical applications, formal robustness guarantees for models are essential, as current verification methods are either overly conservative or computationally expensive. More practical approaches that consider realistic adversarial perturbations are needed.
AI.method: The paper introduces Spatio-Temporal Bound Propagation (STBP), a verification framework for 3D CNNs, modeling adversarial perturbations with realistic spatio-temporal constraints. It computes exact bounds for the first convolutional layer and propagates through subsequent layers using scalable approximations.
AI.result: STBP achieves significantly improved scalability and provides stronger robustness guarantees compared to existing methods, attaining 1.7x higher certified robust accuracy under the same perturbation budgets.
AI.conclusion: Realistically modeling adversarial perturbations with spatio-temporal constraints allows tighter and more scalable robustness guarantees. The introduction of STBP and the ST-Bench benchmark promotes further development in robust AI applications for safety-critical systems.

title: Counterfactual Transport Flows for Offline Conservative Trajectory Refinement
summary: Offline reinforcement learning (RL) offers a path to policy improvement from logged data alone, using historical returns or other measurable outcomes as world feedback. A key difficulty is improving observed behavior without extrapolating beyond what the offline data supports. We propose \emph{counterfactual transport flows}, a source-conditioned trajectory refinement framework for offline decision-making guided by world feedback. Given a low-feedback candidate trajectory, we construct local preference pairs from offline data by retrieving nearby trajectories in latent trajectory space with higher task-specific feedback, and use them as weak supervision for conservative refinement. The framework learns instance-specific refinement directions: at inference time, a refinement strength parameter controls how far the candidate trajectory is transported, enabling a trade-off between preserving the original behavior and applying stronger improvement. Experiments on D4RL benchmarks, including AntMaze and MuJoCo tasks, show that our method improves behavior from historical returns as world feedback, while providing interpretable trajectory-level refinement paths.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces counterfactual transport flows, which refine trajectories in offline reinforcement learning to improve performance while staying within the limits of historical data.
AI.motivation: The challenge lies in improving decision-making based only on offline data without overgeneralizing beyond what the data supports.
AI.method: The authors propose constructing local preference pairs by comparing trajectories from historical data, and then using these pairs for instance-specific trajectory refinement to improve policy outcomes.
AI.result: The approach is shown to enhance behavior on standard D4RL benchmarks like AntMaze and MuJoCo, while offering interpretable paths for trajectory refinement.
AI.conclusion: Counterfactual transport flows provide a structured way to refine offline RL policies by balancing the original behavior and improvement, with demonstrated performance gains and interpretability.

title: SemDINO: A DINOv3-Driven Network for Cross-Temporal Semantic Alignment in Change Detection
summary: Semantic change detection (SCD) aims to simultaneously locate land-cover changes and identify semantic categories before and after transition. However, existing methods suffer from insufficient cross-temporal alignment, weak multi-scale representation, and poor robustness to pseudo-changes caused by illumination, season, and registration noise. To address these issues, we propose a novel end-to-end semantic change detection network named SemDINO, which integrates a dual-branch encoder, multi-scale temporal interaction, semantic purification, change enhancement, and decoupled multi-task prediction into a unified framework. Specifically, we construct a dual-branch encoder that combines a CNN backbone and frozen DINOv3 features via gated pyramid fusion, enabling rich multi-scale semantic representation. Then, a multi-scale temporal bidirectional transformer interaction (M-TBTT) module is proposed to achieve global cross-temporal feature alignment and information interaction. To further enhance genuine changes and suppress pseudo-variations, we introduce semantic purification (SCP), bidirectional change enhancement (BiChangeEnhance), and multi-scale change enhancement (MCE) modules collaboratively. Finally, a multi-branch CD prediction head is designed to jointly output binary change mask, bi-temporal semantic maps, and edge constraint. Extensive experiments on public remote sensing CD datasets demonstrate that SemDINO achieves superior performance and generalization ability against state-of-the-art methods, especially in complex scenarios with interference factors.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces SemDINO, an advanced semantic change detection network that effectively detects and identifies land-cover changes with robust cross-temporal alignment and enhanced handling of pseudo-changes.
AI.motivation: To address the limitations of existing semantic change detection methods, including insufficient cross-temporal alignment, weak multi-scale representation, and vulnerability to pseudo-changes caused by natural variations.
AI.method: The paper proposes SemDINO, a network that incorporates a dual-branch encoder combining CNN and DINOv3 features, multi-scale temporal interaction via a transformer module, semantic purification, change enhancement, and a multi-branch prediction head for detailed outputs.
AI.result: SemDINO outperforms state-of-the-art methods on remote sensing change detection datasets, demonstrating superior detection accuracy, generalization, and robustness in challenging scenarios.
AI.conclusion: SemDINO is an effective and generalizable solution for semantic change detection, excelling in handling complex scenarios and mitigating pseudo-changes caused by external factors.

title: Optimizing Energy-based Neural Network Training with Coherent Ising Machine
summary: While Ising machines serve as advanced physical solvers for the Ising model,enabling applications in combinatorial optimization and neural network training,their scalability for large-scale neural networks remains constrained by hardware connectivity limitations and suboptimal training methodologies. In this work,we leverage a Coherent Ising Machine (CIM) to train an energy-based neural network using Equilibrium Propagation, achieving performance comparable to existing software-based implementations. We further enhance the algorithm by integrating the Adam optimizer to solve for the ground state of a Hopfield energy network, significantly improving convergence speed and solution accuracy. Additionally, we demonstrate the scalability of our approach across deeper network architectures and convolutional operations. Our results highlight the potential of CIM dynamics as a scalable platform for training complex neural networks, offering a pathway toward energy-efficient implementations via analog circuits, optoelectronics, or integrated photonics. This work establishes a novel physical framework for next-generation AI hardware development.

AI.tldr: This paper explores using a Coherent Ising Machine (CIM) to train energy-based neural networks, achieving comparable performance and improved scalability.
