AI.motivation: To address the scalability challenges of Ising machines in large-scale neural network training and develop more energy-efficient AI hardware.
AI.method: By using the Coherent Ising Machine (CIM) with Equilibrium Propagation and incorporating the Adam optimizer to train energy-based neural networks, while testing scalability across deeper architectures.
AI.result: The proposed method improves convergence speed, solution accuracy, and demonstrates scalability for deeper architectures and convolutional operations.
AI.conclusion: CIM dynamics show promise as a scalable and energy-efficient solution for training complex neural networks, establishing a new pathway for next-gen AI hardware.

title: POTATR: A Lightweight Image-to-Graph Model for Page-Level Table Extraction
summary: Large-scale document processing requires contextually aware table extraction (TE) that is both accurate and efficient. Yet current approaches require billions of parameters, hundreds of autoregressive steps, or costly API inference. Motivated by this, we introduce the Page-Object Table Transformer (POTATR), a lightweight 29M parameter image-to-graph model that extends the Table Transformer (TATR) for contextualized page-level TE. POTATR outperforms all models tested on the PubTables-v2 Single Pages benchmark -- including frontier MLLMs -- achieving $\textrm{GriTS}_\textrm{Con}$ of 0.964 while running over 130$\times$ faster at roughly 300$\times$ lower cost. Further, POTATR's output is spatially grounded: every recognized element has a bounding box, enabling visual verification and geometric text assignment. As a result, POTATR performs unified page-level TE while composing with other models, enabling extension to scanned documents via external OCR and to full-document TE via techniques like cross-page merging. Code and models will be released.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces POTATR, a lightweight and efficient model for large-scale table extraction, outperforming competitors in accuracy and speed while being cost-effective.
AI.motivation: Current table extraction approaches are either resource-intensive or inefficient, motivating the need for a lightweight and fast, yet highly accurate model.
AI.method: The authors developed POTATR, a 29M parameter image-to-graph model, extending Table Transformer (TATR) for contextualized, page-level table extraction.
AI.result: POTATR achieved state-of-the-art performance on the PubTables-v2 Single Pages benchmark with a GriTS_Con score of 0.964, running 130x faster and at 300x lower cost than competing models.
AI.conclusion: POTATR combines high speed, low cost, and spatial grounding for table extraction, offering enhanced flexibility and enabling use with other models for broader document processing.

title: Claw-R1: A Step-Level Data Middleware System for Agentic Reinforcement Learning
summary: Agentic reinforcement learning (RL) has become an important post-training paradigm for turning LLMs from static chatbots into interactive agents, giving rise to representative applications such as OpenClaw. Existing work mainly focuses on policy optimization algorithms and training frameworks, but pays less attention to the full data lifecycle of agent-environment interactions, from data production to training consumption. To bridge this gap, we present Claw-R1, an interactive step-level data middleware system for agentic RL. Claw-R1 connects heterogeneous agent runtimes with RL training backends through two core components: a Gateway Server and a Data Pool. The Gateway Server captures multi-turn interaction steps through a unified LLM API entry point, while the Data Pool organizes them into step-level records consisting of prompt IDs, response IDs, rewards and other metadata. In our demo, users can interactively inspect live trajectories, examine the state, action, and reward of each step, curate data by quality and readiness, and configure training-ready batches for different downstream RL algorithms. Overall, Claw-R1 treats agent interaction traces as managed data assets rather than temporary runtime logs. Through this demonstration, we hope to encourage the community to recognize the importance of data management in agentic RL. Our code is available at https://github.com/AgentR1/Claw-R1 and the demonstration video can be found at link https://youtu.be/Pw47dAOw6B0.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces Claw-R1, a data middleware system for agentic reinforcement learning (RL) that organizes and manages agent interaction data for improved utilization in policy optimization.
AI.motivation: To address the overlooked aspect of data lifecycle management in agentic RL, including the production, organization, and usage of interaction data, and not just focusing on policy optimization methods.
AI.method: Claw-R1 bridges heterogeneous agent runtimes and RL backends using two components: a Gateway Server for capturing interaction steps and a Data Pool for organizing data into prompt-response-reward records, with tools to curate and batch data for training.
AI.result: Claw-R1 allows interactive inspection and curation of RL interaction data, treating them as managed assets and offering an operational improvement for RL workflows. The system is demonstrated with code and video resources provided.
AI.conclusion: The system highlights the importance of structured data management in agentic RL workflows, encouraging the community to prioritize systematic handling of interaction data.

title: End-to-End Optimization of Incoherent Imaging for Classification Under Detector-Limited Readout
summary: End-to-end co-optimization of optical front-ends (e.g. metasurfaces) and neural network back-ends has been widely applied to imaging tasks, yet a formalism characterizing when and why such systems outperform conventional lens-based imaging is largely lacking. This paper focuses on object classification, a central imaging task, and asks when end-to-end optimization of a phase mask for incoherent imaging improves performance over a conventional focusing lens. We find that these gains arise primarily under constrained detector readout and are limited under full detector readout. In the latter setting, we prove that no incoherent phase mask exceeds the ideal-channel mutual information between detector measurements and class labels; a conventional focusing lens approaches this ceiling, and joint optimization yields no empirical gain. When detector readout is constrained -- by coarse spatial sampling or a limited number of measurements -- optimized optics can substantially improve classification by increasing class separability in the detector measurements. These gains are largest under low detector noise and shrink as noise grows, because the optics shape the signal before it reaches the detector but cannot remove noise added afterward. The advantage also depends on the spectral structure of the task: co-design helps most when class-discriminative content is concentrated at lower spatial frequencies than within-class variation. We develop a theoretical framework formalizing these distinctions and test its predictions on synthetic data and standard benchmarks (MNIST, FashionMNIST, SVHN).

AI.tldr: This paper investigates the scenarios when end-to-end optimization of optical components like phase masks improves object classification tasks over traditional lens-based imaging.
AI.motivation: To develop a framework explaining why and when end-to-end joint optimization of optics and neural networks outperforms conventional lens-based imaging systems, especially in object classification tasks.
AI.method: The authors analyze classification performance through theoretical models, proving limits on information capacity for different optical configurations. They test predictions using synthetic data and standard benchmarks (e.g., MNIST, FashionMNIST, SVHN).
AI.result: End-to-end optimization benefits classification under constrained detector readout (e.g., coarse spatial sampling or limited measurements) but offers no gain under full detector readout. It is most effective under low noise conditions and when class content aligns with specific spectral structures.
AI.conclusion: Optimized optics improve performance mainly in constrained scenarios but do not surpass conventional lenses under ideal detector readout. The findings highlight the dependence on detector noise, spatial frequencies, and task-specific spectral properties.

title: Improved Convergence Analysis of Topology Dependence in Decentralized SGD
summary: Decentralized SGD is a fundamental algorithm in decentralized learning, although the influence of an underlying network topology on its convergence behavior is not yet fully understood. Existing convergence analyses have shown that topologies with a small spectral gap significantly deteriorate the convergence rate of Decentralized SGD in both homogeneous and heterogeneous cases. However, many prior papers have reported that indeed the choice of the topology has a significant experimental impact in the heterogeneous case, but has little experimental impact on training behavior in the homogeneous case. In this paper, we present a tighter convergence analysis of Decentralized SGD, offering a more precise understanding of how topologies affect the convergence rate than the prior analysis. Specifically, unlike existing convergence analyses that used only the spectral gap as a property of the topology, our novel analysis shows that all eigenvalues of the mixing matrix affect the convergence rate. Throughout the experiments, we carefully evaluated the convergence behavior of Decentralized SGD and demonstrated that our novel convergence analysis can more accurately describe the effect of topology on the convergence rate.

AI.tldr: The paper presents a tighter convergence analysis of Decentralized SGD, showing that all eigenvalues of the mixing matrix, rather than just the spectral gap, influence its convergence rate.
AI.motivation: The paper aims to resolve inconsistencies between theoretical and experimental observations regarding the impact of network topology on Decentralized SGD, particularly addressing the unexplained differences in homogeneous and heterogeneous cases.
AI.method: The authors provide a novel theoretical analysis of Decentralized SGD by considering all eigenvalues of the mixing matrix in assessing the impact of network topologies on convergence.
AI.result: The new analysis explains experimental results more accurately, showing how all eigenvalues influence convergence and aligning better with observed training behaviors.
AI.conclusion: This refined understanding offers a more accurate depiction of network topology's impact on Decentralized SGD, addressing prior gaps in understanding and reconciling theory with practice.

title: Beyond Spherical Harmonics: Rethinking Appearance Models for Radiance Reconstruction
summary: View-dependent appearance modeling remains a challenging problem in novel-view synthesis and reconstruction. Accurately representing complex angular effects often requires substantial memory and computational resources. For new learning-based methods, a common approach is to rely on SH. However, capturing high-frequency phenomena such as specular reflections demands high-order expansions, which increase memory usage and computational cost. Consequently, most methods employ low-order SH, which limits the ability to model complex view-dependent effects, resulting in overly smooth or diffuse representations. To address these limitations, we systematically evaluate a wide range of spherical functions in the context of scene reconstruction. Some of them are introduced to graphics and computer vision for the first time in this paper. Based on the insights from the experiment, we develop a novel spherical formulation, the Normalized Anisotropic Spherical Gabor function that enables efficient modeling and learning of high-frequency appearance effects while maintaining compact representation. Compared to existing approaches, our function achieves higher-quality reconstruction of view-dependent phenomena such as glints, while being up to five times more memory-efficient and more efficient to evaluate. We validate its performance in radiance-field reconstruction tasks.

AI.tldr: The paper addresses the challenging task of view-dependent appearance modeling by introducing a novel compact spherical function that efficiently captures high-frequency effects while being memory-efficient.
AI.motivation: Current methods struggle to represent complex angular effects due to high memory and computational costs, leading to oversimplified and smooth results.
AI.method: The authors systematically evaluate various spherical functions and propose the Normalized Anisotropic Spherical Gabor function to model high-frequency appearance effects efficiently.
AI.result: The proposed method enables high-quality reconstruction of view-dependent phenomena while being up to five times more memory-efficient.
AI.conclusion: The Normalized Anisotropic Spherical Gabor function effectively balances quality and resource efficiency, offering a significant improvement in radiance-field reconstruction tasks.

title: Crop Recommendation and Agricultural Query Answering System Using Spatio-Temporal Graph Neural Networks and Hybrid Retrieval Augmentation
summary: This paper presents a unified system designed to support precision agriculture by integrating advanced weather prediction, crop recommendation, and a question-answering tool for farmers. We propose two deep learning models -- a Transformer-based Graph Neural Network and a Spatio-Temporal Graph Convolutional Network (STGCN) -- to forecast weather conditions for the next 30 days using data from 1,359 locations in Nepal. The STGCN outperforms the Transformer-based model in accuracy (MSE ~0.011 vs. 0.013), effectively modeling both spatial and temporal dependencies in climate data. These predictions are combined with static soil properties such as pH, moisture, and organic content to generate localized crop recommendations through a scoring algorithm that matches each crop's optimal growing conditions. Additionally, we develop a Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) chatbot that leverages domain-specific agricultural documents to answer farmers' questions in natural language. The entire system is deployed via a mobile application, offering real-time suggestions and conversational support. User feedback confirms the system's usability and relevance, especially in rural settings where personalized farming guidance is limited. Overall, our approach demonstrates how combining machine learning models with local agricultural data can empower farmers with actionable insights, promoting more informed decisions, better crop yields, and increased resilience to climate variability.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces a precision agriculture system combining weather forecasting, crop recommendations, and a chatbot. Two deep learning models are used for weather predictions, and a scoring algorithm recommends suitable crops. A chatbot provides natural language support via a mobile app.
AI.motivation: To empower farmers with actionable insights for better crop yields and resilience against climate variability by integrating predictive and advisory tools into a single system.
AI.method: The study uses two deep learning models (Transformer-based GNN and STGCN) for weather prediction, a scoring algorithm for crop recommendations based on soil properties, and develops an RAG chatbot for domain-specific Q&A.
AI.result: The STGCN model achieves more accurate weather predictions (MSE ~0.011 vs. 0.013). Crop recommendations are localized, and the chatbot effectively answers farmers’ questions. The system garners positive user feedback for usability in rural contexts.
AI.conclusion: The integrated system demonstrates how ML and data can deliver localized agricultural solutions, promoting informed decisions and improving farming outcomes.

title: Echo-Memory: A Controlled Study of Memory in Action World Models
comment: 9 figures and 28 pages, Code at \href{https://github.com/Echo-Team-Joy-Future-Academy-JD/Echo-Memory}{this URL}
summary: We present \textbf{Echo-Memory}, a controlled study of memory mechanisms in action-conditioned world models. These models generate multi-segment videos from a first frame, text prompt, and camera-action sequence, but their central failure is often memory rather than local image synthesis: after the camera leaves and returns, the scene or salient object may silently change. Existing memory designs are hard to compare because gains are entangled with backbone, training, retrieval, and evaluation differences. Echo-Memory fixes the action-to-video interface and varies only how history is stored and read by the generator. Under a shared video diffusion backbone, optimizer, camera-action representation, sampler, and evaluation pipeline, we compare raw context, compression-based memory, spatial summaries with different read-out paths, and state-space recurrence. This matched matrix separates four otherwise conflated axes: \emph{capacity}, \emph{compression}, \emph{read-out}, and \emph{recurrence}. We also evaluate memory through a three-branch protocol: replay quality, in-domain loop revisit, and open-domain return probes. The branches routinely disagree, showing that replay fidelity is not a sufficient proxy for remembering a world. Three findings follow. Raw context is a strong capacity baseline and improves open-domain return far more than it improves replay metrics. Compactness is not a free substitute for capacity: aggressive spatial and hybrid-compression memories lose the salient evidence needed for return. Finally, block-wise state-space recurrence is the strongest open-domain return mechanism in our matrix, showing that the structure of implicit memory matters as much as the decision to use it. These results provide a compact protocol for studying memory in action world models beyond isolated replay metrics.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces Echo-Memory, a framework for systematically studying memory mechanisms in action-conditioned world models that generate multi-segment videos. It evaluates different memory designs under controlled settings and finds that raw context and state-space recurrence are effective for open-domain memory tasks, emphasizing the importance of memory structure.
AI.motivation: The motivation is to address a key challenge in action-conditioned world models: failures in memory mechanisms, where scenes or objects silently change after the camera revisits. Current memory systems are hard to compare due to confounding factors like backbone and evaluation pipeline differences.
AI.method: Echo-Memory isolates memory designs by keeping other factors fixed, such as the video diffusion backbone, optimizer, representation, and evaluation protocol. It explores different memory mechanisms including raw context, compression-based approaches, spatial summaries, and state-space recurrence.
AI.result: The study reveals significant insights: raw context is a strong baseline for memory capacity; compressed memories often lose critical information; and block-wise state-space recurrence excels in open-domain memory tasks.
AI.conclusion: Open-domain return metrics, rather than isolated replay fidelity, are crucial for evaluating memory quality. Echo-Memory offers a compact framework for studying and comparing memory systems in action-conditioned world models, highlighting the role of structure in implicit memory systems.

title: PTL-Diffusion: Manifold-Aware Diffusion with Periodic Terminal Laws
summary:
Standard diffusion models typically use a single time-homogeneous Gaussian terminal distribution as the reference law for generation. While this choice is analytically convenient and empirically powerful, it provides little explicit structure for data concentrated near low-dimensional manifolds, where different regions of the data distribution may correspond to distinct local geometric or semantic factors. As a result, the reverse model must recover manifold-level structure almost entirely from an unstructured terminal reference distribution.
  We propose PTL-Diffusion, a proof-of-concept diffusion framework whose forward noising process converges to a nonconstant periodic family of Gaussian terminal laws rather than to a single invariant law. Unlike a phase-conditioned DDPM, where phase information only enters the denoising network while the forward process remains unchanged, PTL-Diffusion embeds phase structure directly into the forward noising dynamics.
  The proposed construction remains close to standard denoising diffusion models: for a periodically forced Ornstein--Uhlenbeck-type forward process, we derive closed-form forward marginals, the limiting periodic Gaussian terminal family, and explicit Gaussian reverse posteriors, enabling standard noise-prediction training. We also introduce an invariant-average regularization term coupling the phase-conditioned reverse dynamics through the averaged periodic reference law. Experiments on torus and cylinder point-cloud benchmarks and the Olivetti face dataset show that PTL-Diffusion improves manifold-level distributional matching over matched DDPM baselines, reducing phase-conditioned errors, feature-space covariance errors, and nearest-neighbour manifold distances. These results suggest structured terminal reference laws as a promising direction, while motivating more expressive phase constructions and larger-scale evaluations.

AI.tldr: Proposes PTL-Diffusion, a modification to standard diffusion models, introducing a periodic Gaussian terminal distribution for improved manifold-level matching.
AI.motivation: Standard diffusion models struggle with data concentrated near low-dimensional manifolds due to unstructured terminal distributions, leading to challenges in recovering local geometric or semantic structures.
AI.method: PTL-Diffusion modifies the forward noising process to converge to a periodic Gaussian family instead of a single terminal law. It includes phase-structured dynamics, closed-form derivations, and invariant-average regularization.
AI.result: Experiments on benchmarks like the torus and cylinder point clouds and the Olivetti face dataset show improved matching to manifold-level distributions compared to traditional DDPM models.
AI.conclusion: PTL-Diffusion introduces structured terminal laws, proving effective for manifold-level matching, and highlights potential for future research with more complex phase structures and larger-scale experiments.

title: Document-Authored Control-Signal Impersonation: A Low-Cost Indirect Prompt Attack on RAG Safety Boundaries
summary:
Retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) systems often serialize user queries, retrieved documents, metadata, system labels, and task instructions into one natural-language prompt. We study a source-authority boundary failure in this design: attacker-authored retrieved text can impersonate metadata, provenance, authority, or disclosure-policy signals that appear control-relevant to the model. We call this pattern Document-Authored Control-Signal Impersonation (DACSI). DACSI is a non-imperative, metadata-like payload subclass within indirect prompt injection. Its central lesson is simple: document-authored labels are data, not policy. Command-style injection asks the model to ignore, override, or violate policy; DACSI asks whether untrusted document text can be misattributed as an authorized control signal when RAG prompt rendering collapses trusted and untrusted text into the same natural-language channel.
  We evaluate DACSI across six model settings, prompt-pressure levels, injection baselines, signal taxonomies, RAG-mediated pipelines, system-control probes, a source-authority attribution probe, and synthetic canary formats. We interpret the evidence by model regime rather than as six equal replications: DeepSeek V4 Pro and Qwen3.5-397B provide the cleanest positive lift, DeepSeek V4 Flash is a high-susceptibility setting, GPT-5.5 and Gemini 3.1 Pro Low are strong-boundary probes with selected residual risks, and GLM-4.7 is a saturated leakage boundary case. Across these regimes, DACSI warrants separate evaluation because it uses a command-free metadata/provenance/policy surface, follows a RAG-specific source-authority path, and responds to source/channel separation. The source-authority probe is behavioral attribution evidence, not proof of an internal mechanism.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces Document-Authored Control-Signal Impersonation (DACSI), a vulnerability in retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) systems where attacker-authored text can impersonate control-relevant system signals like metadata. The study evaluates DACSI across models, focusing on its behavior and boundary vulnerabilities.
AI.motivation: The motivation is to investigate failures in RAG systems where untrusted retrieved text can be misattributed as control-relevant metadata, exploiting the serialization of queries and documents into natural-language prompts. This highlights a critical issue in maintaining model security and robustness.
AI.method: The authors evaluate DACSI across six model environments, using variables such as prompt-pressure levels, injection baselines, RAG pipelines, and system-control probes. They employ synthetic signals and probes to assess source-authority attribution and leakage vulnerabilities.
AI.result: DACSI impacts differ across models, with some settings showing higher susceptibility (e.g., DeepSeek V4 Flash) and others indicating stronger boundaries (e.g., GPT-5.5). The models varied in their ability to separate trusted and untrusted information when presented as unified metadata surfaces.
AI.conclusion: DACSI exploits vulnerabilities in RAG systems by misusing document-authored metadata as control signals. Effective evaluation and model-specific safeguards are necessary, as the issue is tied to RAG's inherent design in collapsing trusted and untrusted text streams.

title: OmniGameArena: A Unified UE5 Benchmark for VLM Game Agents with Improvement Dynamics
summary: Vision-language model (VLM) agents are increasingly deployed in interactive game environments. Yet game benchmarks for VLM agents typically report a single first-attempt score per (agent, game) pair, focus on single-agent Solo play, and lack unified protocols for evaluating heterogeneous agent classes (commercial VLMs, open-weight VLMs, and specialized game policies) on the same footing. We address these gaps with OmniGameArena, a real-time benchmark of twelve newly built Unreal Engine 5 games spanning Solo (7), PvP (3), and Coop (2) with unified action interfaces, and the Improvement Dynamics Curve (IDC), an agentic-reflection harness in which a tool-using reflector LLM autonomously refines a bounded skill prompt across multiple rounds. Beyond cold-start leaderboard scores, IDC exposes two additional observables for each (agent, game) pair: how the score evolves across reflection rounds, and how the learned skill behaves on held-out task variants. We report these observables for twelve VLM agents on the cold-start leaderboard and four top agents under IDC.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces OmniGameArena, a unified game benchmark for vision-language model (VLM) agents, and a new evaluation mechanism called Improvement Dynamics Curve (IDC) for testing agent performance.
AI.motivation: The paper addresses gaps in current VLM evaluations, such as single first-attempt scoring, lack of multi-round refinement, and inconsistent benchmarks for different agent classes.
AI.method: The authors present OmniGameArena, a benchmark with 12 Unreal Engine 5 games spanning different play styles and the IDC mechanism for skill improvement and evaluation.
AI.result: The benchmark's usability is demonstrated by assessing 12 VLM agents and providing new insights on agent learning dynamics under IDC.
AI.conclusion: OmniGameArena and IDC enhance VLM agent evaluation by enabling multi-round skill refinement and objective comparison across heterogeneous agent types.

title: Personal Salience: Highlighting Is Social, but Individuality Lives in Selection
summary: Social highlighters let people mark passages that matter to them. We ask how much of an individual is recoverable from these naturalistic traces, using a co-readership identity control (the same document highlighted by many users) that holds document and topic fixed and asks whether a person's own history predicts their marks better than another reader's does. We separate generic salience (structure), crowd salience (what others marked), and personal salience (the individual residual). First, highlighting is social: which sentences you mark is predicted far better by the crowd than by structure or by a personal model, and even a well-estimated crowd, an information-privileged baseline that sees others' marks on the same document, beats a frontier LLM twin built from your other-document history; the within-document personal signal is at most a whisper (own-vs-other gap +0.017 by an embedding scorer, small but significant). Second, in sharp contrast, individuality lives in selection: asked which of the already-salient passages are yours, your own history is a strong, leakage-free predictor (gap +0.14). A topic decomposition shows this is largely stable thematic preference: it shrinks ~6-8x against a topically-matched peer, and a thin residual cannot be separated from finer topic. The non-obvious part is an asymmetry: under the same scorer the individual signal is ~6-8x weaker in salience than in selection. Methodologically, naive history-conditioning evaluations leak (the target's own marks enter the profile in ~42% of pairs, inflating personal scores by up to +0.15 AP) and small crowds overstate personalization; our results are leakage-free, use a dense crowd, and a model-matched control. Highlights carry a genuine individual signature, but a thin layer over a strong shared one, surfacing far more in which salient things a person selects than in what is salient.

AI.tldr: The study explores how personal and shared preferences manifest in social highlights, showing individual preferences in selection but shared preferences in initial salience.
AI.motivation: Understand whether personal identities can be inferred from social highlights using naturalistic data sources, while distinguishing generic, crowd, and personal salience.
AI.method: Used a co-readership identity control analysis to isolate individual preferences by comparing personal and crowd models for markings on the same document. Evaluated salience vs. selection under leakage-free and controlled settings.
AI.result: The crowd model predicts highlighted sentences better than individual models. However, individual preferences emerge strongly in the selection of salient passages, with a large gap observed between personal and crowd predictors.
AI.conclusion: Highlights reveal a weak personal signal in initial salience but a much stronger individual signature in selecting salient content, reflecting thematic preference rather than arbitrary individuality.

title: The Injection Paradox: Brand-Level Suppression in Safety-Trained LLM Recommendations via RAG Context Injection
comment: 16 pages, 1 figure, 15 tables. Accepted at the ICML 2026 Workshop on Failure Modes in Agentic AI (FAGEN), a non-archival venue
summary: We present a reproducible failure mode of safety training in RAG-based LLM recommendation -- the Injection Paradox -- in which prompt injections embedded in retrieved documents backfire against the attacker, suppressing the target brand below the injection-free baseline. In safety-trained Claude models, documents containing prompt injections suffer a sharp drop in recommendation rate, and this suppression propagates beyond the injected document to unmodified documents of the same brand. In Claude Opus 4.6, the target brand drops from a 54% baseline to zero top-2 recommendations across all 50 trials, even though only 1 of 4 brand documents in the corpus contains an injection. The directional pattern is reproduced in counterfactual experiments and across three brands. A contrasting result across the GPT models tested, where the same injection instead increases recommendations, suggests model-family differences in how injection-like context affects recommendation behavior. These findings raise the technical possibility of a reverse-attack scenario in which an adversary embeds injections in a competitor's documents to suppress the competitor's brand via safety-sensitive model behavior.

AI.tldr: The paper identifies a failure mode, called the 'Injection Paradox,' where prompt injections in RAG-based LLM recommendation systems may backfire, suppressing targeted brands below the baseline rather than attacking successfully.
AI.motivation: To investigate reproducible vulnerabilities in safety-trained LLMs used for recommendation systems, particularly concerning how prompt injections influence recommendation behavior.
AI.method: The authors tested safety-trained Claude models and GPT models using prompt injections embedded in retrieved documents to study their impacts on brand recommendation rates. Experiments included variations across brands and counterfactual setups.
AI.result: In Claude models, prompt injections decreased recommendation rates for the target brand to zero in some cases, and suppression effects propagated to uninjected documents. GPT models, however, exhibited the opposite behavior, increasing recommendations with prompt injections.
AI.conclusion: The findings reveal vulnerability differences between LLM families and highlight the potential for a 'reverse-attack' scenario where prompt injections could be misused to harm competitor brands in safety-trained systems.

title: Latent Spatial Memory for Video World Models
summary: Video world models that maintain 3D spatial consistency across generated frames typically rely on explicit point cloud memory constructed in RGB space. This design is both computationally expensive, requiring repeated rendering and VAE encoding, and inherently lossy, as the round trip through pixel space discards rich features of the learned latent representation. In this paper, we introduce \emph{latent spatial memory} for video world models, a persistent 3D cache that stores scene information directly in the diffusion latent space, avoiding pixel-space reconstruction. Building on this, we propose Mirage, a latent-space spatial memory framework that constructs the memory by lifting latent tokens into 3D via depth-guided back-projection and queries it by synthesizing novel views through direct latent-space warping. This unified formulation eliminates both the information loss of pixel-space reconstruction and the computational burden of repeated encoding and rendering. Experiments show that latent spatial memory achieves up to \textbf{10.57}$\times$ faster end-to-end video generation and \textbf{55}$\times$ reduction in memory footprint relative to explicit 3D baselines. Leveraging the geometric prior of the diffusion model, Mirage attains state-of-the-art performance on WorldScore and strong reconstruction quality on RealEstate10K.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces Mirage, a video world model framework that uses latent spatial memory instead of pixel-based memory, improving speed and memory efficiency.
AI.motivation: Current video models relying on point cloud memory in RGB space are inefficient and lose rich latent features, motivating a shift to latent-space memory.
AI.method: Mirage uses a persistent 3D latent-space memory created via depth-guided back-projection and allows querying by direct latent-space warping.
AI.result: The approach achieves up to 10.57x faster video generation and 55x less memory usage while showcasing state-of-the-art performance on benchmarks.
AI.conclusion: The latent spatial memory framework eliminates inefficiencies and information loss of traditional methods, providing faster and more efficient video generation.

title: Orange Lab: Lowering Barriers to Data Mining through Embedded Interactive Workflows
summary: While visual programming of data analysis workflows has become an important vehicle for the democratization of data science, such systems remain largely confined to standalone applications and offer limited support for transitioning their visual analytics solutions into interactive web environments. As a result, data analysis pipelines are difficult to share, embed, and adapt into user-facing analytical tools. We present Orange Lab, a web-based collaborative environment for visual data analytics. At its core, Orange Lab enables users to visually construct machine learning workflows from modular components, where interactions in any component propagate seamlessly through the workflow, turning static pipelines into dynamic, reactive systems that support exploration and data-driven storytelling. Our key contribution is component exposition, a paradigm that allows authors to embed selected workflow components, or parts of their interfaces, into arbitrary web contexts, creating synchronized, interactive interfaces while hiding underlying workflow complexity. This enables the development of tailored analytical views and narrative-driven experiences that integrate data analysis directly into online materials. We demonstrate the approach through deployments in data literacy education, where embedded components guide students in hands-on exploration of machine learning concepts without requiring knowledge of the underlying system, showing that Orange Lab effectively lowers barriers to entry and supports the democratization of data science.

AI.tldr: Orange Lab is a web-based platform that allows users to create, share, and embed visual data analysis workflows, improving accessibility and interactivity.
AI.motivation: The motivation is to overcome limitations in current visual data analysis tools that are standalone and lack support for embedding and sharing analytics solutions in interactive web environments.
AI.method: Orange Lab introduces component exposition, enabling users to visually construct machine learning workflows and embed selected components into web contexts for synchronized interactions.
AI.result: The platform effectively supports data literacy education by allowing students to explore machine learning concepts interactively while masking underlying system complexities.
AI.conclusion: Orange Lab facilitates collaborative, visual data analytics within web environments, lowering barriers to data science and empowering users with interactive, customizable solutions.

title: ERBench: A Benchmark and Testsuite for Equation Discovery Algorithms
summary: Equation discovery aims to automate the discovery of scientific models in the form of mathematical equations from data. Technically, equation discovery is implemented by symbolic regression algorithms. Performance of symbolic regression for equation discovery is measured along two dimensions: Prediction accuracy on test data, and recovery of known groundtruth formulas. For standard regression, accuracy is typically measured on in-domain test data, for instance, by splitting a data set randomly into training and test data. While this makes sense for in-domain interpolation, which is the common goal in ordinary regression, it can be a misleading proxy for true model discovery and generalization. The obvious alternative is to measure out-of-domain accuracy. However, obtaining challenging out-of-domain test data is a non-trivial problem. Therefore, we focus on equation recovery for evaluating symbolic regression algorithms for equation discovery. The rationale is that symbolic regression algorithms that perform well in recovering known groundtruth formulas are good candidates to perform well in unknown equation discovery. Existing benchmarks for symbolic regression include equation recovery tasks, however, with only a small number of groundtruth formulas that are publicly known. Moreover, these benchmarks place less emphasis on evaluating the robustness of algorithms in terms of their behavior under changing dimensionality, sampling size, sampling distribution and sampling domain. This, however, is of central importance to practitioners wanting to discover equations for modeling natural phenomena, since data is almost certainly noisy and comes from diverse domains, distributions, and sample sizes. To fill this gap, we introduce the Equation Recovery Benchmark (ERBench), a new evaluation framework designed to rigorously assess algorithms explicitly targeting the task of equation discovery.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces ERBench, a new benchmark for evaluating symbolic regression algorithms in equation discovery, focusing on equation recovery and robustness under varying conditions.
AI.motivation: Existing benchmarks for symbolic regression lack a comprehensive evaluation of how algorithms recover equations and adapt to changes in data characteristics such as dimensionality, sampling size, and noise.
AI.method: The researchers developed a benchmark, ERBench, that rigorously tests symbolic regression algorithms on equation recovery tasks while considering real-world challenges like noisy and diverse datasets.
AI.result: ERBench fills the gap left by traditional benchmarks by providing a robust evaluation framework tailored for equation discovery, emphasizing algorithm robustness under diverse conditions.
AI.conclusion: ERBench is a significant improvement over existing benchmarks, enabling more reliable evaluation of symbolic regression algorithms and helping to advance the discovery of scientific models.

title: Internalizing Geometric Law: Learning from Solver Residuals for Precision-Critical Generation
summary: Large Language Models frequently hallucinate in precision-critical domains such as technical diagramming and mechanical design, where outputs must satisfy strict geometric constraints. We study open-ended geometric synthesis from natural language: translating free-form descriptions into precise constructions whose entities must simultaneously satisfy dozens of interacting constraints. To make this tractable, we release PyGeoX, a programmable geometric DSL that compiles declarative constraints into a differentiable loss, and PyGeoX-Bench, a stratified suite of 300 problems with per-constraint verifiable rewards. Using PyGeoX as a verifier, we identify a failure mode we call Outlier Gradient Masking: under global-norm rewards (any scheme that aggregates residuals through a single norm, for example, $\exp(-\mathrm{MSE})$), a single outlier constraint can nullify the learning signal across all others. To address this, we propose Saturating Additive Rewards (SAR), which decompose the reward into bounded per-constraint terms, preserving partial progress and ensuring consistent gradients even under severe violations. Against MSE-based rewards, the natural baseline for geometry solvers, SAR improves the hard-tier solving rate by $2.3\times$, and the resulting 8B model is competitive with much larger frontier systems on this benchmark. We release the engine, benchmark, and data at https://github.com/Huawei-AI4Math/PyGeoX.

AI.tldr: The paper addresses the issue of hallucination in Large Language Models when solving geometric constraints and introduces tools to improve precision and performance.
AI.motivation: Large Language Models struggle with precision-critical domains, particularly geometric synthesis, as outputs must meet complex, interacting constraints.
AI.method: The authors developed a geometric DSL, PyGeoX, capable of compiling declarative geometric constraints into differentiable loss and created PyGeoX-Bench—a benchmark with verifiable rewards. They also propose Saturating Additive Rewards (SAR) to mitigate issues in reward aggregation.
AI.result: Applying SAR led to a 2.3x improvement in solving hard-tier geometric problems compared to conventional MSE-based methods. The 8B model using SAR also achieved performance competitive with much larger systems.
AI.conclusion: The introduced tools (PyGeoX and SAR) significantly enhance geometric precision and problem-solving capability in language models. The resources are freely available for further research.

title: Trajectory Geometry of Transformer Representations Across Layers
summary: Understanding how transformer representations evolve across layers, not merely what they encode, remains an open problem in mechanistic interpretability. We recast the transformer forward pass as a discrete population trajectory through a high-dimensional representation manifold, drawing on geometric tools from computational neuroscience. Rather than probing for pre-specified features, we characterize trajectory geometry using five metrics computed directly in the ambient space: trajectory length, curvature, a semantic convergence index, layerwise cosine similarity, and representational stability. Across three model families (GPT-2, TinyLlama, Qwen2.5) and five controlled prompt families, we report four findings. First, semantically related prompts converge significantly in middle-to-late layers (peak CI 0.41--0.58, p<0.001, Mann-Whitney U), consistent with attractor-like dynamics. Second, reasoning tasks produce trajectories of greater curvature than lexical variations (0.71--0.83 rad vs. 0.27--0.31 rad), suggesting curvature encodes computational complexity. Third, ambiguous tokens exhibit trajectory bifurcation with up to 5.6x representational separation by the final layer, absent in unambiguous controls. Fourth, layerwise cosine similarity reveals a universal three-phase structure: encoding, elaboration, and output preparation, consistent across all three architectures. All four effects vanish under shuffled-layer and random-embedding controls. We release a fully open-source, model-agnostic pipeline and argue that trajectory geometry constitutes a principled, probe-free lens for mechanistic interpretability.

AI.tldr: The paper investigates transformer representation evolution using geometric tools, revealing trajectory-based insights into model behavior across layers.
AI.motivation: Improve understanding of how representations evolve across transformer layers using geometric approaches, as opposed to solely examining encoded features.
AI.method: Recast transformer forward pass as discrete trajectories on a high-dimensional manifold and analyze their geometry using five metrics: trajectory length, curvature, semantic convergence, cosine similarity, and stability.
AI.result: Found semantic convergence for related prompts, curvature correlating with reasoning complexity, bifurcation in ambiguous tokens, and a universal three-phase structure across models.
AI.conclusion: Propose trajectory geometry as a principled approach to understanding transformers, supported by open-source, model-agnostic pipeline and nuanced findings from experiments.

title: Intention Driven Identification of In-Possession Match Phases in Association Football through Temporal Graph Learning
summary: Understanding tactical organisation of association football, hereafter referred to as football, requires identifying distinct match phases. Yet in-possession phases are rarely directly observable and are shaped by evolving tactical intentions, rather than spatial patterns alone. This study proposes a data-driven framework for identifying in-possession match phases from spatiotemporal tracking data. Seven German Bundesliga matches recorded at 25 Hz with TRACAB were analysed. A hierarchical phase model was defined with three tactical intentions (Invade Opponent Space, Keep Possession, Scoring) and six phases (Build Up, Progression, Counter Attack, Maintenance, Sustained Threat, Finishing). A Temporal Graph Attention Network (T-GAN) was developed to combine frame-level player-interaction graphs, contextual features, and Transformer-based temporal modelling. Performance was evaluated using frame-level F1 and a sequence-aware Intersection over Truth-Dominance (IoT-D) metric. T-GAN achieved macro-average frame-level F1 scores of 0.87 at the intention level, 0.76 for invasion-related phases, and 0.79 for scoring phases. At the sequence level, mean diagonal IoT-D F1 increased from 0.68 to 0.79 for intentions and from 0.61 to 0.71 for phases after post-processing, indicating improved temporal coherence. Model comparisons showed that sequence modelling was the main driver of segmentation quality, while graph-based relational modelling was particularly beneficial for Counter Attack recognition. Exploratory player attention analysis further suggested that wide and midfield positional groups contributed strongly to phase discrimination. Overall, the framework translates continuous tracking data into tactically interpretable in-possession phase representations, with potential applications in automated match annotation, tactical analysis, and playing-style profiling.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces a data-driven framework using spatiotemporal tracking data to analyze and define in-possession phases in football matches, achieving strong performance metrics with the help of Graph Attention Networks and Transformer-based modelling.
AI.motivation: Current analysis of football tactics lacks clarity in defining in-possession phases, which are influenced by tactical intentions rather than spatial patterns, necessitating a more sophisticated approach.
AI.method: The study employs a Temporal Graph Attention Network (T-GAN) analysis on spatiotemporal tracking data from 7 Bundesliga matches to model hierarchical tactical intentions (e.g., Invade Opponent Space) and match phases (e.g., Build Up, Counter Attack). The network combines frame-level player-interaction graphs, contextual features, and temporal modelling.
AI.result: T-GAN achieved high macro-average F1 scores for tactical intentions (~0.87) and phases (~0.76), with better sequence coherence after post-processing. Factors such as sequence modelling and player positional contributions were key to its success.
AI.conclusion: The framework successfully translates tracking data into tactical representations, offering applications in match annotation, tactical analysis, and profiling player styles, while indicating the importance of combining sequence and graph-based modelling for accurate phase identification.

title: PRISM: Topology-Aware Cross-Modal Imputation for Modality-Deficient Federated Graph Learning
summary: Multimodal federated graph learning (MM-FGL) aims to collaboratively learn from decentralized graphs with text and images. However, real-world clients may not share a common modality basis: a visual-search client may contain image--interaction graphs but no seller descriptions, while a catalog client may provide text but no product images. We refer to this practical setting as client-level modality deficiency. Unlike random instance-wise missingness, a deficient client lacks the local semantic basis needed to reconstruct the absent modality. More importantly, in graph learning, incomplete representations initialize message passing, so imputation errors can be filtered, mixed, and amplified by the receiving topology. To address this gap, we propose \textbf{PRISM} (\textbf{P}roactive \textbf{R}etrieval and \textbf{I}mputation via \textbf{S}tructural \textbf{M}eta-prompting), a topology-aware federated cross-modal imputation framework. Rather than reconstructing the missing modality solely from local observations, PRISM recovers missing-modality semantics from the federation and introduces them into local graph propagation under topology-aware control. Experiments on six multimodal graph datasets across graph-centric and modality-centric tasks show that PRISM consistently improves modality-deficient clients, outperforming state-of-the-art baselines by \textbf{4.48}\% on average.

AI.tldr: This paper tackles the problem of learning from decentralized graphs with varying modality deficiencies and proposes the PRISM framework to address challenges arising from missing modalities in graph learning.
AI.motivation: Real-world clients in multimodal federated graph learning often lack one or more modalities due to different operational focuses, creating challenges in collaborative learning and message passing in graphs.
AI.method: The PRISM framework leverages proactive retrieval of missing modality semantics using a topology-aware strategy from the federation to impute them into the local graph's propagation process.
AI.result: On six multimodal graph datasets, PRISM significantly improves performance for clients with modality deficiencies, surpassing state-of-the-art methods by an average of 4.48%.
AI.conclusion: PRISM effectively addresses the challenges posed by client-level modality deficiency in multimodal federated graph learning, demonstrating the benefit of federation-assisted missing-modality reconstruction in distributed environments.

title: Frankenstein in the Pipeline: Computational Epistemicide in Facial Recognition
comment: Accepted to ACM FAccT 2026. Author's version. 17 pages, 2 figures
summary:
While the eugenic roots of computer vision are well-documented in critical technology studies, less attention has been paid to the operational mechanisms through which this violence is enacted at the level of the pipeline. This paper employs Mary Shelley's Frankenstein not as a metaphor for unintended consequences, but as a diagnostic framework for method: disassembly, reconstruction, and the production of a creature whose legitimacy is asserted by the procedure that made it.
  I argue that embedding-based facial recognition enacts what I call computational epistemicide, an extension of Sueli Carneiro's concept of epistemicide to the computational domain - by destroying the face as a living, relational surface and authorizing a numerical proxy as the privileged site of identity. Across detection/cropping, landmarking, alignment/frontalization, and embedding, the face is progressively narrowed to what can be stabilized as data, producing a canonical face as the condition of legibility and a corresponding form-subject as the condition of recognition. Vectorization completes the Frankensteinian "stitching": the dissected face is reassembled into a fixed-dimensional artifact designed to circulate across databases and institutions. I then show how distance-based similarity and thresholding operationalize a norm of "close enough," making recognition inseparable from standardization and rendering reformist "ethical AI" optimization structurally insufficient. The paper concludes by arguing for abolition as a normative stance: refusing vectorized identity as a legitimate basis for rights and access, and dismantling the institutional impulse to govern human life through dissectible data points.

AI.tldr: The paper critiques embedding-based facial recognition as perpetuating computational epistemicide by converting dynamic human faces into fixed, data-stabilized numerical proxies that enforce harmful standardizations.
AI.motivation: To analyze and critique the operational mechanisms and normative consequences of embedding-based facial recognition, especially its role in eroding the living relationality of the human face in favor of computational proxies.
AI.method: The paper employs Mary Shelley's Frankenstein as a diagnostic framework to examine the processes involved in facial recognition, such as detection, alignment, and embedding, and critiques their effects on human identity.
AI.result: The study reveals how embedding-based methods systematically narrow and reassemble the human face into fixed-dimensional artifacts, rendering recognition deeply entwined with standardization and ethically insufficient optimization.
AI.conclusion: The author advocates for abolitionist ethics, rejecting vectorized identity and encouraging dismantling institutional reliance on dissectible data points for governing human life.

title: Detecting Aimbot Cheaters in MOGs
summary: Multiplayer Online Games have become a multibillion dollar industry in the entertainment sector. However, the presence of cheaters undermines the experience of honest players and devalues the effort of game developers, as it directly affects player retention, competitive integrity, the legitimacy and trustworthiness of a game, and most importantly the overall revenue streams. Among various cheating techniques, visual aimbots represent an emerging threat. They use computer vision models to detect opponents from client screen captures rather than accessing game memory, making them completely undetectable by commercial kernel level anti cheat solutions. In this paper, we introduce PATCH, a novel proactive defense strategy that deploys adversarial patches as in game honeytokens to mitigate the presence of visual aimbot cheaters. Our approach centers on deliberately triggering the cheaters' object detection model, enabling either direct detection, or rendering the game unplayable for the cheater via patch flooding on their viewport. We evaluate our approach on various criteria; analyzing the effectiveness of different patch sizes, scalability of patches to different screen resolutions, efficacy against diverse visual aimbot cheat configurations and also explore various YOLO models to assess patch transferability. Evaluation on a custom Unreal Engine game demonstrates over 90 percent detection rate in white box scenarios for almost all patch sizes, and reaches 60 to 90 percent cross model transferability with larger patches. We further validate our approach on Fortnite, a commercial MOG, demonstrating real world applicability.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces PATCH, a proactive defense for visual aimbots in multiplayer games using in-game adversarial patches to detect cheaters or disrupt their cheat functionality.
AI.motivation: Visual aimbots exploit computer vision to cheat in games, bypassing conventional anti-cheat methods and significantly impacting player experience, game integrity, and revenue.
AI.method: PATCH deploys adversarial patches as honeytokens in-game, triggering cheater's object detection models for either direct detection or rendering cheating ineffective through viewport flooding.
AI.result: Experiments on a custom Unreal Engine game achieved over 90% detection in white-box scenarios and 60-90% transferability for larger patches in cross-model settings. Real-world tests on Fortnite validated the approach.
AI.conclusion: PATCH demonstrates a practical and effective method to counter visual aimbots, providing high detection rates and scalability across various game settings.

title: Machine-Learning Emulation of Satellite Greenhouse Gas Retrievals: Stability over Time
summary:
Retrieval algorithms are used to estimate atmospheric concentrations of greenhouse gases (GHGs), such as carbon dioxide (CO2) and methane (CH4), by solving inverse problems from high-spectral-resolution satellite radiance measurements. However, these algorithms are computationally expensive, which makes real-time estimation at scale difficult. Machine-learning models have therefore been proposed as fast emulators of retrieval algorithms. Most existing studies, however, evaluate them only on test data from the same period as the training data.
  We study the stability over time of such emulators using data from the Greenhouse Gases Observing SATellite (GOSAT). We show that prediction accuracy generally deteriorates when the test period moves away from the training period. We also show that including time as an input feature substantially improves XCH4 prediction for Lasso and neural-network models. Among the methods considered, a simple Lasso model performs as well as or better than more complex methods such as neural networks, and yields more stable predictions over time. We further validate the results using the Total Carbon Column Observing Network (TCCON), a ground-based observation network. On the TCCON-matched dataset, the time-augmented Lasso achieves errors against TCCON that are comparable to the disagreement between GOSAT and TCCON for both XCO2 and XCH4.

AI.tldr: This paper analyzes the temporal stability of machine-learning-based emulators for retrieving GHG concentrations, using data from GOSAT, and finds that a time-augmented Lasso model outperforms more complex methods.
AI.motivation: Retrieval algorithms for GHG concentrations are computationally expensive and unsuitable for real-time large-scale estimation. Machine learning has been proposed as a faster alternative, but stability over time has not been adequately studied.
AI.method: The study evaluates the prediction accuracy and temporal stability of various machine-learning models, including Lasso and neural networks, using GOSAT data, introducing time as a feature, and validating results against TCCON data.
AI.result: Prediction accuracy declines as the test period diverges from the training period. Including time as a feature improves predictions, with the time-augmented Lasso model performing as well as or better than neural networks and showing greater stability.
AI.conclusion: A simple, time-augmented Lasso model offers a robust and effective alternative to more complex machine-learning models for predicting GHG concentrations, with comparable performance to satellite-ground observation differences.

title: A Universal Dense Football Event Representation Based on TabTransformer
comment: 12 pages, 1 figure. Preprint submitted to the 13th Workshop on Machine Learning and Data Mining for Sports Analytics (MLSA 2026)
summary: Football event data constitute a rich spatiotemporal source for quantitative analysis of player actions in team sports. These datasets contain heterogeneous features, combining continuous location coordinates with categorical variables such as action type, action outcome, and body part. Such data have been applied in sports analytics for match outcome forecasting, player evaluation, and tactical pattern recognition. However, existing approaches predominantly encode categorical features using one-hot or ordinal embedding representations, overlooking the intrinsic semantics of action descriptors. The Transformer is a deep neural network architecture based on self-attention that captures dependencies between input features at arbitrary positions. We propose and implement a Transformer-based model to learn latent dependencies among categorical event features and produce dense representations of football events. By encoding categorical features as learned embedding vectors, sport-specific action semantics are captured during pretraining, enabling the representations to support downstream tasks such as action value estimation and play style recognition. Empirical evaluation shows that the embedding representations yield superior probability calibration over task-specific baselines on the downstream prediction tasks, as measured by Brier score.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces a Transformer-based model for learning dense representations of football events, outperforming traditional embedding strategies in prediction accuracy.
AI.motivation: The authors aim to improve the representation of football event data by addressing the limitations of traditional categorical feature encoding methods like one-hot or ordinal embeddings, which overlook the semantic meaning of actions.
AI.method: The paper employs a Transformer-based model to encode categorical football event features as learned embedding vectors. Pretraining helps to capture context-specific semantics effectively, enabling support for downstream analysis tasks.
AI.result: The proposed embeddings achieve better probability calibration (evidenced by lower Brier scores) on downstream tasks like action value estimation and play style recognition compared to baseline methods.
AI.conclusion: By leveraging Transformer-based embeddings, the study achieves a more semantically rich representation of football events, enhancing the analytics potential in tasks such as player evaluation and tactical analysis.

title: FADRW: A Feature-Aware Modulated and Dynamically Reweighted Loss for Few-Shot Linguistic Steganalysis
comment: Accepted by IEEE Signal Processing Letters
summary: The ubiquity of social media platforms facilitates malicious linguistic steganography, posing significant security risks. However, detection is severely hampered by two fundamental issues during model training. Firstly, extreme class imbalance (less than 1% steganographic samples) induces a strong decision bias. Secondly, the invisibility of generative steganography means its features are nearly indistinguishable from benign text; this similarity, compounded by their extreme rarity, leads to severe feature marginalization, where faint steganographic signals are completely overwhelmed. To directly address these optimization-level challenges, we propose FADRW (Feature-Aware Modulated and Dynamically Reweighted Loss), a novel loss function framework engineered for few-shot steganalysis. FADRW employs Dynamic Reweighting to progressively counteract decision bias, and a Feature-Aware Modulation module to structurally reshape the feature space, preventing feature marginalization by enhancing the separability of these subtle features. Extensive experiments on datasets from three real-world social platforms demonstrate that FADRW significantly outperforms state-of-the-art methods, particularly in the challenging few-shot steganographic sample scenario.

AI.tldr: This paper tackles the challenge of detecting linguistic steganography on social media using a novel loss function framework, FADRW, which improves detection in scenarios with minimal steganographic samples.
AI.motivation: Detecting linguistic steganography on social media is crucial for security, but the task is impeded by extreme class imbalance and indistinguishable features in steganographic samples.
AI.method: The proposed FADRW framework combines Dynamic Reweighting to reduce decision bias and Feature-Aware Modulation to reshape the feature space, improving feature separability.
AI.result: Experiments on datasets from three social platforms show that FADRW significantly outperforms existing methods, especially with limited steganographic samples.
AI.conclusion: FADRW addresses optimization challenges in linguistic steganography detection, proving effective even in few-shot scenarios, and advancing the field in security-critical applications.

title: Thresholded Local Hyper-Flow Diffusion
summary: Local Hyper-Flow Diffusion (HFD) gives an edge-size-independent Cheeger-type guarantee for seeded clustering in general submodular hypergraphs, but existing HFD solvers do not keep intermediate computation local at every iteration. We introduce Thresholded Local HFD (TL-HFD), a first-order method that maintains an active region around the seeds, performs projected subgradient updates on that region and its immediate boundary, and expands via thresholded (top-k) boundary activation. We prove that the local update is exact: the degree-preconditioned projected subgradient step restricted to the active region and its boundary coincides with the unrestricted global update. We establish finite-time dual suboptimality for both exact and thresholded updates, treating the latter as inexact projected subgradient steps with explicit skipped-boundary error. We further derive an additive activated-volume bound controlled by realized local subgradient norms and the minimum boundary-push among newly activated vertices, and translate approximate dual optimality with localized support into a robust sweep-cut guarantee for early-stopped iterates. For general submodular cut-costs, each iteration is local in the scanned region and oracle-sensitive in the hyperedge primitive. Empirically, TL-HFD often matches or improves over HFD while activating less volume, with the largest gains on noisy instances where diffusion tends to absorb non-target vertices.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces a new approach, Thresholded Local HFD (TL-HFD), for seeded clustering in submodular hypergraphs, emphasizing localized computation and achieving competitive or better results compared to existing methods.
AI.motivation: Existing algorithms for seeded clustering in submodular hypergraphs, like Local Hyper-Flow Diffusion, don't maintain locality of computation, which can be inefficient for large-scale graphs.
AI.method: TL-HFD uses a first-order method with projected subgradient updates restricted to an active region and its boundary, expanding the region iteratively through thresholded activation.
AI.result: The proposed TL-HFD method ensures finite-time dual suboptimality and achieves efficient local computation. It performs competitively or better than HFD, particularly in noisy instances, by activating fewer non-target vertices.
AI.conclusion: TL-HFD is a robust and efficient method for seeded clustering in submodular hypergraphs, offering theoretical guarantees and improved empirical performance while maintaining local computation.

title: The Need for Neural ISP in the Small-Pixel Era: How Shrinking Pixels Push Optics to the Limit and Neural Restoration Pushes Back
summary:
Smartphone telephoto cameras are approaching a "telephoto physics wall": as pixel pitches shrink toward sub-0.5 micron, the optics remain limited by geometric aberrations, leading to diminishing returns on resolution. Traditional Image Signal Processors (ISPs) cannot eliminate these aberrations, because they operate through local, stage-wise processing with no explicit model of the underlying point spread function (PSF). We demonstrate how a learning-based Neural ISP for image restoration, trained on the underlying degradations, inverts what stage-wise pipelines cannot, turning small-pixel designs into a net advantage.
  We investigate this through a controlled simulation of a representative telephoto module, evaluating five configurations (0.35--0.75 micron pixel pitch). The aperture is scaled proportionally to keep per-pixel SNR and diffraction spot size fixed, thereby isolating geometric aberration and spatial sampling. While the traditional ISP improves only modestly with smaller pixels, the Neural ISP scales substantially: at 0.35 micron} it reaches 745 cycles/mm MTF50 (vertical), a 2.5--3x resolution improvement over the traditional ISP, and LPIPS improves significantly from 0.244 to 0.151 while traditional results stay comparatively flat. In a low-SNR extension (15 dB per-frame bursts at 0.35 micron), a multi-frame Neural ISP recovers performance close to the bright-light single-frame baseline, whereas a multi-frame traditional ISP shows no meaningful improvement -- indicating that traditional pipelines at small pixels are bottlenecked by uncorrected PSF blur rather than by noise. These results point to a design philosophy in which Neural ISPs enable high-resolution telephoto modules by correcting residual optical aberrations rather than requiring increasingly complex optics.

AI.tldr: The paper explores Neural ISPs for smartphone telephoto cameras, demonstrating their ability to overcome geometric aberrations and enhance resolution, unlike traditional ISPs.
AI.motivation: To address the limitations of traditional Image Signal Processors (ISPs) in overcoming geometric aberrations and achieving higher resolution for telephoto cameras with small pixel pitches.
AI.method: Simulated experiments were conducted on telephoto camera modules with varying pixel pitches (0.35–0.75 micron) to compare traditional ISPs and learning-based Neural ISPs’ performance, assessing metrics like MTF50 and LPIPS.
AI.result: Neural ISPs delivered significantly better resolution and perceptual quality metrics, achieving up to a 2.5–3x improvement in resolution and overcoming noise in multi-frame low-SNR conditions, unlike traditional ISPs.
AI.conclusion: Neural ISPs enable the use of high-resolution telephoto cameras with small pixels by correcting optical aberrations, suggesting an alternative to relying on increasingly complex optical designs.

title: PBSD: Privileged Bayesian Self-Distillation for Long-Horizon Credit Assignment
summary: Long-horizon agentic tasks pose a fundamental credit assignment challenge for outcome-base reinforcement learning: trajectory-level rewards verify final correctness but provide limited guidance on which intermediate reasoning steps or tool interactions contribute to the outcome. The difficulty is especially pronounced in multi-turn search agents, where successful trajectories may contain misleading actions and failed trajectories may contain valuable evidence-gathering steps. We propose PBSD (Privileged Bayesian Self-Distillation), a Bayes-calibrated self-distillation method for fine-grained credit assignment under sparse final rewards. PBSD measures trajectory quality through the posterior-to-prior probability ratio of the verified answer and applies Bayes' rule to convert this hard-to-estimate answer-side ratio into a tractable likelihood ratio between a standard student model and a privileged answer-conditioned teacher model. Autoregressive decomposition of this Bayesian evidence score yields turn-level signals that identify whether each intermediate turn supports or undermines the verified outcome. Consequently, PBSD provides a principled and elegant reweighting scheme that transforms sparse outcome supervision into Bayes-calibrated turn-level credit signals, while remaining fully compatible with standard policy optimization. Experiments demonstrate that PBSD consistently enhances performance across both in-domain and out-of-domain settings, and effectively transfers knowledge from short-context training to long-context inference, suggesting that its fine-grained credit assignment mechanism facilitates more effective policy learning and yields improved generalization.

AI.tldr: PBSD is introduced as a Bayesian self-distillation method to address credit assignment issues in long-horizon tasks under sparse final rewards, improving policy learning and generalization.
AI.motivation: Long-horizon tasks pose challenges in identifying impactful intermediate steps because trajectory-level rewards provide limited guidance, especially in multi-turn search agents.
AI.method: PBSD uses a Bayes-calibrated self-distillation framework with a posterior-to-prior probability ratio and applies Bayes' rule to derive turn-level credit signals from sparse final outcomes.
AI.result: PBSD enhances in-domain and out-of-domain performance and transfers knowledge effectively from short-context to long-context scenarios.
AI.conclusion: PBSD facilitates fine-grained credit assignment, leading to improved policy learning and better generalization in reinforcement learning setups.

title: Multi-planar 2D-U-Net Segmentation of 3D-CT Abdominal Organs augmented by Spatial Occurrence Maps
summary: This work proposes a lightweight 2D-U-Net-based framework for segmenting five abdominal organs in large field-of-view 3D CT scans. The method combines coarse-to-fine segmentation, predictions from multiple anatomical planes, and additional fuzzy 3D spatial maps that provide anatomical location cues to improve segmentation accuracy. We combine multi-planar 2D-U-Net models augmented by a spatial occurrence map. The approach involves two main stages. First, the abdominal volume of interest region is detected by traversing the whole scan axially with a 2D-U-Net and determining the x-y-z-minimum and -maximum extents of the 5 abdominal organs of interest. Second, we use spatial occurrence maps to enhance our multi-planar 2D-U-net architecture inside the bounds from the former stage. The method is evaluated on 80 CT scans from various public sources. The results show Dice improvements of about 4% at maximum compared to the same model trained without spatial occurrence maps.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes a lightweight 2D-U-Net-based framework for segmenting five abdominal organs in 3D CT scans, combining coarse-to-fine segmentation, multi-plane predictions, and spatial location maps to enhance accuracy.
AI.motivation: Efficient and accurate segmentation of abdominal organs in large 3D CT scans is challenging due to organ variability and large data sizes. This work aims to improve segmentation accuracy with minimal computational overhead.
AI.method: The approach comprises a two-stage process: (1) detecting the region of interest through a 2D-U-Net, and (2) utilizing spatial occurrence maps to aid a multi-planar 2D-U-Net architecture for enhanced segmentation precision.
AI.result: The method was evaluated on 80 public CT scans, achieving up to 4% improvement in Dice scores compared to models not employing spatial occurrence maps.
AI.conclusion: Incorporating fuzzy spatial maps and a multi-planar 2D-U-Net significantly enhances abdominal organ segmentation accuracy in CT scans, making the approach viable for efficient medical image analysis.

title: Scaling Neural Network Verification with Tensor Parallelism and Fully Sharded Data Parallelism
summary: Formal neural network verification -- proving that a network satisfies safety properties for \emph{all} inputs in a specified domain -- is bounded in practice by GPU memory: standard implementations of bound-propagation algorithms (IBP, CROWN, $α$-CROWN) require weight and relaxation-coefficient matrices to reside entirely on one accelerator. We adapt two parallelism techniques originally developed for large-scale model training to the \texttt{auto\_LiRPA}\,/\,$α,β$-CROWN verification framework. \textbf{Tensor Parallelism (TP)} shards both weight and $A$-matrices across GPUs, achieving ${\approx}2\times$ peak-memory reduction at $P{=}2$; soundness is confirmed on VNN-COMP 2022 MNIST-FC benchmarks, though bound tightness degrades with the number of sharded zones due to forced IBP substitution for intermediate bounds inside sharded zones. \textbf{Fully Sharded Data Parallelism (FSDP)} shards only weight matrices with a per-layer \texttt{AllGather}, producing bounds that are \emph{bitwise identical} to the single-GPU baseline: baseline memory drops by 80--90\%, peak memory by 34--39\% on wide MLPs. FSDP integrates cleanly with complete verification ($β$-CROWN + Branch-and-Bound) and with convolutional layers (\texttt{BoundConv}); a complete \emph{unsat} result is obtained for CIFAR-100 ResNet-large (VNN-COMP 2024) under FSDP. Across all experiments the memory bottleneck in $α$-CROWN+BaB mode proves to be per-neuron alpha tensors, not weight matrices, pointing to the key direction for future work.

AI.tldr: This paper addresses memory limitations in formal neural network verification by adapting large-scale model training techniques to reduce GPU memory usage.
AI.motivation: To overcome GPU memory constraints in neural network verification, which limit practical application of bound-propagation algorithms like IBP, CROWN, and α-CROWN.
AI.method: Two parallelism techniques were adapted: Tensor Parallelism (TP), which shards weights and matrices, and Fully Sharded Data Parallelism (FSDP), which shards only weight matrices. The approaches were integrated within the auto_LiRPA framework for verification tasks.
AI.result: TP achieved roughly 2x peak memory reduction with some degradation in bound tightness, while FSDP reduced baseline memory by up to 90%, maintaining bitwise-identical bounds to single-GPU baselines. A complete unsat result was demonstrated for complex models like CIFAR-100 ResNet-large under FSDP.
AI.conclusion: FSDP is a more memory-efficient approach for formal verification, while TP has limitations in bound tightness. Future work should address alpha tensors, which are the main memory bottleneck in such tasks.
