  Three high-level findings: (1) engine classes (microVM, userspace kernel, OCI container) separate cleanly on every architectural axis, but products within a class do not; (2) product pin policy is the dominant operator-facing variable -- engine-side patch latency aggregates to ~0 days for coordinated disclosures, while downstream lag spans 0 days to 471+ days to "opaque" to infinity; (3) fuzzing investment splits into three tiers, and the strongest combination -- microVM x continuous public fuzzer -- is unoccupied in this set, leaving the "0 published CVEs x no upstream fuzzer x no academic study" intersection structurally unmeasured.
  We report per-axis orderings, per-product portraits, and a threat-model qualification matrix; no overall ranking is proposed. Companion repository (code, Apache-2.0): https://github.com/orbitalab/RnD-ai-sandboxes-sec-study-part-1. License: CC BY 4.0.

AI.tldr: This study evaluates the security measures of five AI-sandbox products across six metrics, highlighting varied architectural differences, patch policies, and fuzzing investments, without ranking them.
AI.motivation: The motivation is to provide a multi-dimensional security assessment of AI-sandbox products, identifying architectural and operational gaps for isolating guest code from host kernels.
AI.method: The authors analyzed six security-related metrics across five AI-sandbox products, focusing on cross-axis analysis to derive high-level conclusions.
AI.result: The study found (1) clear architectural differences between engine classes but not among products within a class, (2) significant variability in patch timelines based on product pin policies, and (3) a notable lack of investment in comprehensive fuzzing for microVMs.
AI.conclusion: AI-sandbox products exhibit clear class-level distinctions but room for improvement in fuzzing strategies and patch coordination. No single metric suffices for a complete assessment, emphasizing the importance of a nuanced, multi-axis approach.

title: SoK: Reconstruction Attacks on Synthetic Tabular Data (Insights from Winning the NIST CRC)
summary: Synthetic data is increasingly promoted as a privacy-preserving substitute for releasing sensitive tabular records, yet its central adversarial threat ("reconstruction", the recovery of an individual's hidden attribute values from a synthetic release and a handful of known quasi-identifiers) has been studied only in scattered, hard-to-compare settings. We present the first systematization of reconstruction (equivalently, attribute inference) attacks on de-identified and synthetic tabular data. We contribute a taxonomy that organizes attacks by the structure they exploit; the most systematic empirical evaluation to date, pitting fourteen attacks against nine synthetic data generation (SDG) methods across five benchmark datasets; and a set of new attacks that fill gaps in the taxonomy, one of which (CoBP-RA) is the strongest attack we measure. Crucially, we introduce a methodology for interpreting what attack success means: a memorization test that distinguishes reconstruction of the population distribution from memorization of training records, and a reduction that places reconstruction and membership inference on a single comparable scale. Our findings: the choice of SDG method governs risk far more than the choice of attack; differential privacy protects mainly at small budgets ($\varepsilon\lesssim1$), above which protection plateaus, bounded by the synthesizer's capacity rather than its noise; de-identification methods are the most exposed; and most reconstruction reflects distributional structure rather than memorization, concentrating individual risk on atypical records. The attacks and infrastructure are externally validated by our first-place finish among all red teams in the 2025 \textit{National Institute of Standards and Technology} (NIST) Collaborative Research Cycle.

AI.tldr: This paper presents a systematic study of reconstruction attacks on de-identified and synthetic tabular data, proposing a taxonomy, evaluating multiple attacks, and highlighting key findings.
AI.motivation: The paper addresses the lack of a systematic study on reconstruction attacks in synthetic data, which are key threats to privacy in releasing sensitive records.
AI.method: It conducts a taxonomy-led analysis, evaluates 14 attacks across 9 synthetic data generation methods and 5 datasets, introduces new attacks, and develops a method to interpret attack success.
AI.result: Key findings include that SDG method choice impacts risk more than attack choice, differential privacy protects only at small budgets, and most reconstruction outcomes arise from distributional structure.
AI.conclusion: The paper provides tools and insights for understanding reconstruction risks, emphasizing the limitations of certain protections while validating methods in competitive settings.

title: Predictive Coding with Bayesian Priors via Proximal Gradients
summary: We recast predictive coding as continuous-time proximal gradient descent applied to a regularized maximum-a-posteriori (MAP) objective. We study first a single-level problem and then a multi-level hierarchy. For the single-level problem, we show that proximal gradient descent is precisely a leaky firing-rate network: the membrane leak, the effective recurrent matrix, the local synaptic drive, and the static nonlinearity all follow from one optimization principle, and the resulting circuit is the one proposed by Rao and Ballard. The prior selects the nonlinearity through its proximal operator, and the likelihood precision sets the gain on the observation. For the hierarchy, we show that a classical variable-splitting relaxation of the deep MAP problem yields hierarchical predictive coding as the interconnection of local and distributed solvers. In probabilistic modeling terms, this relaxation replaces the directed generative chain by an undirected Markov random field whose node potentials are the level-wise priors. Each level then applies its own activation function, namely the proximal operator of its prior.

AI.tldr: This paper interprets predictive coding as proximal gradient descent applied to a regularized MAP objective, linking it to leaky firing-rate networks and proposing a hierarchical model based on a Markov random field.
AI.motivation: The authors aim to provide a principled optimization-based understanding of predictive coding and show how it emerges from well-defined mathematical frameworks.
AI.method: The study recasts predictive coding as proximal gradient descent on a MAP objective, analyzes single-level leaky firing-rate networks, and proposes a hierarchical predictive coding model based on variable-splitting relaxation and Markov random fields.
AI.result: The analysis demonstrates how leaky firing-rate networks and hierarchical predictive coding circuits naturally follow from the proximal gradient descent framework and optimization principles.
AI.conclusion: Predictive coding can be derived and generalized systematically through optimization principles, offering a unified framework for understanding and designing neural coding models.

title: Bayesian Optimization of a Multi-Product Chemical Reactor Using Composite Models and Partial Physics Knowledge
summary: We study data-driven real-time economic optimization of a multi-product chemical reactor when no reliable first-principles model is available beyond a steady-state energy balance. Instead of learning the economic objective directly as a black-box function, we use a composite formulation in which Gaussian process (GP) models predict physically meaningful outputs, including product concentrations and reactor temperature, while profit is computed analytically from these predictions together with raw-material, product, and utility prices. This preserves the structure of the economic objective, makes it parametric in changing prices without needing retraining, and allows candidate operating points to be checked against the available energy balance through a physics residual. The GPs also provide predictive uncertainty, which is exploited in a Bayesian optimization (BO) framework both for data-efficient exploration and for conservative enforcement of the reactor temperature constraint through an upper confidence bound. The acquisition function additionally penalizes large energy-balance mismatch obtained by substituting the GP-predicted outputs and candidate inputs into the available steady-state energy balance. The approach is demonstrated on a benchmark simulation of a non-isothermal multi-product reactor. Relative to a trust-region safe BO implementation, the proposed method achieves better simulated economic performance within the available iteration budget. Relative to a purely data-driven BO approach that does not use the available physics information, it avoids reactor temperature constraint violations.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes a data-driven optimization method for a chemical reactor lacking a full first-principles model.
AI.motivation: To optimize real-time economics of a chemical reactor without a reliable physics-based model, while ensuring safety and constraint satisfaction.
AI.method: Combines Gaussian process models and Bayesian optimization to predict outputs, compute profit analytically, and enforce physical constraints conservatively.
AI.result: Improves economic performance compared to a trust-region safe BO and ensures temperature constraint adherence unlike fully data-driven methods.
AI.conclusion: The method leverages limited physics-based information effectively, achieving both economic gains and safety in chemical reactor optimization.

title: Parameter Tuning with Generalization Guarantees for GPU-Accelerated Linear Programming
summary: Recent research has developed practical, parallelizable first-order methods for large scale linear programming, but performance is highly dependent on hyperparameter selection. We derive generalization guarantees for hyperparameter tuning within (cu)PDLP, a state-of-the-art first-order LP solver designed for modern hardware. First, we pin down the behavior of PDHG, the primal-dual hybrid gradient algorithm that underlies PDLP, as a function of its step size and primal weight, leading to linear sample complexity guarantees for learning those parameters. We then conduct a structural analysis of PDLP, which augments PDHG with several specialized techniques like preconditioning, adaptive step sizes, averaging, adaptive restarts, and smoothed primal weight updates. Our analysis captures the behavior of the solution trajectory as a function of the hyperparameters and leverages recent advances in data-driven algorithm design to obtain polynomial sample complexity guarantees for learning those hyperparameters. Finally, we conduct proof-of-concept experiments that demonstrate the need for data-driven PDLP parameter tuning. Our results showcase the versatility of the data-driven algorithm design toolkit for principled hyperparameter tuning within solver-grade implementations of complex modern optimization algorithms.

AI.tldr: The paper develops generalization guarantees for hyperparameter tuning within the state-of-the-art first-order LP solver (cuPDLP), employing data-driven algorithm design techniques.
AI.motivation: Hyperparameter tuning is critical for the performance of first-order methods in large-scale linear programming, and current methods lack principled frameworks for this process.
AI.method: The paper derives linear and polynomial sample complexity guarantees for tuning hyperparameters in PDLP through structural analysis and leveraging advances in data-driven algorithm design. It also integrates techniques like preconditioning and adaptive step sizes.
AI.result: The authors demonstrate proof-of-concept experiments showing that data-driven tuning of hyperparameters in PDLP improves performance, supported by theoretical guarantees.
AI.conclusion: The research highlights the necessity and effectiveness of using data-driven approaches for principled hyperparameter tuning in modern large-scale optimization solvers.

title: A Comparison of SSL-Based Feature Extractors and Back-End Classifiers for Spoofing Detection: A Multi-Corpus Training and Cross-Linguistic Analysis
summary: Voice biometric systems face growing threats from spoofing attacks, yet the evaluation of detection models remains inconsistent across datasets. To investigate these unpredictable fluctuations, we conduct a comprehensive benchmark of four self-supervised learning feature extractors paired with four back-end classifiers. We compare the hierarchical local feature extraction of ResNet with the global sequence and relational modeling of attention and graph-based back-ends. Through multi-corpus training across three scenarios and six evaluation datasets, our empirical analysis yields two critical findings. First, we expose a domain bias within the ASVspoof 5 dataset, showing that naive data scaling actively degrades performance. Second, our cross-linguistic analysis reveals that fine-tuning with just 8 hours of target-language data enhances detection robustness. Together, these findings emphasize the critical need for domain-aware and language-specific adaptation in spoofing detection.

AI.tldr: This paper assesses the performance of voice biometric spoofing detection methods using self-supervised feature extractors and back-end classifiers, highlighting domain biases and the benefits of language-specific fine-tuning.
AI.motivation: Spoofing attacks threaten voice biometric systems, yet inconsistent evaluations across datasets hinder effective detection.
AI.method: The authors benchmark four self-supervised learning feature extractors and pair them with four different back-end classifiers, analyzing their hierarchy and modeling approaches across multi-corpus training and evaluation.
AI.result: The study uncovers domain bias within the ASVspoof 5 dataset and demonstrates that fine-tuning with limited target-language data can significantly improve detection robustness.
AI.conclusion: Domain-aware and language-specific adaptation is essential for improving the robustness of spoofing detection systems in varied and cross-linguistic environments.

title: Speaker-Invariant Representation Learning for Spoofing Detection via Gradient Reversal and A Variational Information Bottleneck
summary: Sophisticated generative speech technology can undermined the reliability of voice biometrics. While spoofing detection systems excel when assessed under in-domain conditions, generalisation to out-of-domain settings is often poor. In this paper, we show that such issues could be caused by speaker bias, where models learn individual voice traits rather than markers of manipulation or generation. We propose a teacher-student framework for speaker-invariant spoofing detection that disentangles identity without requiring speaker labels. We leverage a pre-trained speaker recognition teacher to guide a student model via a gradient reversal layer. To control the balance between suppressing cues related to voice identity with the preservation of those related to spoofing detection, we integrate a Variational Information Bottleneck. Evaluations across nine datasets show our model achieves a 25.7% relative reduction to the EER compared to the MHFA baseline.

AI.tldr: This paper addresses speaker bias in spoofing detection and proposes a teacher-student framework using pre-trained speaker recognition and a Variational Information Bottleneck to improve generalization and reduce errors.
AI.motivation: The motivation is to address poor generalization of spoofing detection systems to out-of-domain conditions due to the learned speaker bias in the models.
AI.method: A teacher-student framework is proposed, where a pre-trained speaker recognition model acts as the teacher, guiding the student model via a gradient reversal layer. A Variational Information Bottleneck is incorporated to balance identity suppression and spoofing detection preservation.
AI.result: The proposed model achieves a 25.7% relative reduction in Equal Error Rate (EER) compared to the baseline method (MHFA) when tested across nine datasets.
AI.conclusion: The results demonstrate that addressing speaker bias using the proposed method significantly improves the robustness and generalization of spoofing detection systems.

title: Discovering and decoding latent mean-field structure with variational autoencoders
summary: Generative models are increasingly used to capture correlations in many-body systems, but the representations they learn remain largely opaque to physical interpretation. Here, we establish an intuitive criterion that quantifies the capacity of a variational autoencoder (VAE) to faithfully reconstruct the joint probability distribution of a many body system. In a nutshell, a bound on the VAE capacity is obtained by comparing the rate of the latent channel to the bipartite mutual information of the data. Using this bound, we show that the conditionally independent decoder of any successful VAE is structurally identical to a finite-size mean-field factorization. Hence, a successful reconstruction is direct evidence for a latent mean-field theory and the microscopic parameters of that theory can be read off the trained decoder. We validate these conclusions on a hierarchy of solvable models with scalar (Curie-Weiss), vector (Hopfield) and tensor (Maier-Saupe) order parameters, recovering the full Hopfield pattern matrix from equilibrium samples alone. We find that, when applied to Salamander retinal recordings, a two-latent VAE reproduces the population statistics with only two effective collective variables allowing us to recover the `stored patterns' of the neural population and write a generalized Hopfield model which correctly models the experimental data.

AI.tldr: The paper investigates how variational autoencoders (VAEs) can represent many-body systems, showing that VAEs can reconstruct these systems using a latent mean-field theory framework.
AI.motivation: Generative models are increasingly applied to many-body systems, but the learned representations are often not physically interpretable. The paper aims to bridge this gap by linking VAEs to theoretical frameworks in physics.
AI.method: The authors develop a criterion to evaluate the VAE's capacity to reconstruct many-body systems based on comparing the latent channel rate to the data's bipartite mutual information. They analyze this using solvable models and experimental data.
AI.result: The analysis shows that the VAE's decoder mirrors mean-field factorizations in successful reconstructions. Examples involving scalar, vector, and tensor order parameters validate the findings, including applications to salamander retinal data.
AI.conclusion: VAEs can provide interpretable representations of many-body systems, reducing them to mean-field theories and revealing their underlying microscopic parameters, which can aid in modeling real-world systems.

title: Compositional Approximation Can Strictly Outperform Superpositional Approximation
summary: Many classically studied function classes are known to be approximated optimally by superpositional methods, i.e. with approximants constructed as the linear combination of elements in some dictionary. Here optimality means that the uniform approximation error viewed as a function of the number of parameters used has polynomial decay of the highest order achievable by any parametrized method whose parameters can be encoded as a bit string of length proportional, up to logarithmic factors, to the number of parameters. While compositional methods like neural networks are structurally different, their approximation rates can be made comparable by imposing constraints that ensure such a proportional bit string encoding. In this work we study function classes exhibiting structural properties that limit superpositional approximation rates to be strictly lower than compositional approximation rates. In particular, we construct explicit examples for which there is an arbitrarily large gap.

AI.tldr: The paper explores instances where compositional methods, like neural networks, outperform superpositional methods in terms of approximation rates, even when constraints on bit string encoding are imposed.
AI.motivation: The authors aim to investigate function classes where structural properties inherently make compositional approximations outperform superpositional methods, addressing gaps in understanding their relative efficiencies.
AI.method: The study constructs explicit examples of function classes where compositional approximation rates exceed superpositional approximation rates, focusing on structural limitations of the latter.
AI.result: Explicit examples of function classes were identified where compositional methods achieve superior approximation rates compared to superpositional methods, showcasing an arbitrarily large performance gap.
AI.conclusion: Compositional methods like neural networks can achieve significantly superior approximation rates for specific function classes, challenging assumptions about the optimality of superpositional methods.

title: Sample-Efficient LLM-Based Detection of Malicious Web Server Logs with Forensically Explainable Reasoning
summary: Forensic analysis of web server logs demands both accurate detection and human-readable explanations that can satisfy legal requirements. We present CEF-Log, a context-enhanced few-shot chain-of-thought prompting strategy for Large Language Models that addresses this dual requirement. CEF-Log embeds expert investigative methodology through a structured five-step reasoning template, enabling the model to learn \textit{how} to analyze logs rather than \textit{what} patterns to memorize. Experimental evaluation demonstrates that CEF-Log achieves an F1-score of 0.99 on the CSIC 2010 dataset using only four examples while providing a $10\times$ improvement in sample efficiency compared to other prompting-based methods. We also introduce ForenWebLog, a new dataset that incorporates real-world attacks and multi-step attack sequences for comprehensive evaluation. Qualitative analysis confirms that CEF-Log generates traceable, accurate explanations suitable for forensic documentation, addressing the critical "black-box" limitation of traditional machine learning approaches.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces CEF-Log, a few-shot prompting strategy for large language models, tailored for forensic web server log analysis. It achieves high accuracy with minimal examples while generating human-readable, legal-grade explanations.
AI.motivation: The authors aim to address the challenge of requiring both high accuracy and interpretable, legally-compliant explanations in forensic web server log analysis while overcoming the limitations of traditional machine learning's black-box nature.
AI.method: CEF-Log employs a context-enhanced few-shot chain-of-thought prompting strategy. It uses a structured five-step reasoning template to embed forensic expertise, enabling the model to analyze logs effectively without relying on memorizing patterns.
AI.result: CEF-Log achieves an F1-score of 0.99 on the CSIC 2010 dataset with only four examples. It also improves sample efficiency by ten times compared to other prompting methods. Furthermore, a new dataset, ForenWebLog, was introduced for evaluation, showcasing real-world and multi-step attack scenarios.
AI.conclusion: CEF-Log effectively balances accuracy and explainability in web server log analysis. Its capability to generate traceable, accurate explanations overcomes traditional black-box model limitations, making it suitable for forensic applications.

title: OptMuon: Closed-Loop Orthogonalized Momentum Methods for Stochastic Optimization with Zero-Noise Optimality
summary: Orthogonalized momentum updates, as used in Muon-style optimizers, have recently shown strong empirical stability in large-scale deep learning. However, existing orthogonalized methods are typically paired with constant or open-loop magnitude rules, and therefore do not explicitly calibrate their update magnitudes from the observed optimization trajectory. Motivated by the closed-loop perspective behind Lipschitz-free and noise-adaptive methods, we propose OptMuon, a family of adaptive momentum orthogonalization methods for stochastic nonconvex optimization. OptMuon combines Muon-style polar-factor directions with a trajectory-dependent AdaGrad-Norm-type coefficient schedule, so that the update magnitude is determined by the observed gradient and momentum history rather than by a prescribed Lipschitz-dependent rule. The schedule does not use the smoothness constant, the variance level, or the bounded-gradient constant in parameter selection, and its running-maximum correction prevents isolated gradient spikes from causing excessive coefficient collapse. Under lower-boundedness, unbiased stochastic gradients with bounded variance, smoothness, and an almost-sure bounded stochastic-gradient condition, we prove two complementary guarantees. OptMuon-A achieves the noise-adaptive rate \(\tilde{\mathcal O}(T^{-1/2}+σ^{1/2}T^{-1/4})\) under average smoothness, while OptMuon-I achieves \(\tilde{\mathcal O}(T^{-1/2}+σ^{1/3}T^{-1/3})\) under individual smoothness. In the zero-noise regime, both bounds automatically reduce to a nearly optimal deterministic first-order rate \(\tilde{\mathcal O}(T^{-1/2})\) without manual hyperparameter retuning. These results show that closed-loop scalar adaptation can be combined with Muon-style momentum orthogonalization while retaining noise adaptivity and zero-noise optimality up to logarithmic factors.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces OptMuon, an adaptive optimization method that improves momentum updates using adaptive magnitude rules based on gradient and momentum history, achieving strong theoretical guarantees and empirical benefits.
AI.motivation: To address the limitations of existing Muon-style optimizers, specifically their reliance on constant or open-loop magnitude rules that neglect trajectory-dependent calibration.
AI.method: The authors propose OptMuon, which integrates Muon-style momentum orthogonalization with an adaptive AdaGrad-Norm-type schedule. This approach avoids reliance on certain constants (e.g., smoothness, variance) and eliminates sensitivity to gradient spikes, achieving robust closed-loop calibration.
AI.result: OptMuon guarantees two noise-adaptive convergence rates under different conditions (average smoothness and individual smoothness) and achieves nearly optimal performance in deterministic settings.
AI.conclusion: Closed-loop scalar adaptation can synergize with momentum orthogonalization, offering both theoretical robustness and practical benefits for stochastic nonconvex optimization.

title: Data Agents Under Attack: Vulnerabilities in LLM-Driven Analytical Systems
summary: Data agents integrate LLM-driven reasoning with relational data access, executable analytical tools, and multi-step workflow orchestration, making them increasingly central to enterprise analytics. This integration introduces new security vulnerabilities across data resources, database execution, and agent reasoning, recombining concerns from database security and general-purpose LLM-agent security into failure modes that neither line of work captures on its own. To address this gap, we present a systematic security study of data agents. Our contributions are threefold. First, we develop a layered vulnerability framework that identifies eight data agent-specific risks across interpretation, execution, and policy layers. Second, we introduce an attack taxonomy organized by adversary goal, tactic, and technique, covering three goals, seven tactics, and fourteen techniques, and pair it with an LLM-driven payload generation pipeline grounded in real database schemas. Third, we evaluate these attacks on six systems, including four open-source data agents and two production cloud analytics services. Our experiments reveal substantial security vulnerabilities across current systems and yield four key takeaways.

AI.tldr: The paper investigates security vulnerabilities in data agents that integrate LLM reasoning, databases, and analytics. It introduces a vulnerability framework, an attack taxonomy, and evaluates attacks on six systems.
AI.motivation: The motivation is to study and address the unique security challenges posed by data agents due to their integration of LLM-driven reasoning with database and analytics functionalities.
AI.method: The authors developed a layered framework to identify risks, crafted an attack taxonomy with adversary goals and payloads, and conducted experiments on six data agent systems.
AI.result: The experiments revealed significant security vulnerabilities in the analyzed systems, highlighting the need for better safeguards.
AI.conclusion: The study advances understanding of data agent security, providing a framework and taxonomy for risks, and underscores the urgency for security improvements in the field.

title: From A to B to A: Palindromic Zero-Shot Voice Conversion with Non-Parallel Data
summary: We present a voice conversion (VC) framework that utilizes K-Nearest Neighbors (KNN) retrieval over WavLM representations to align non-parallel source and target speech, constructing synthetic training pairs for supervised learning. The retrieved segments serve as synthetic inputs, while real target audio provides ground-truth outputs, forming a synthetic-to-real training paradigm that naturally supports multilingual data without requiring parallel corpora or explicit alignment. To ensure consistent target-speaker identity, we incorporate a speaker loss derived from a pretrained speaker verification model. Experiments across multiple languages demonstrate that the proposed approach achieves high naturalness and strong speaker similarity, outperforming competitive VC baselines, despite being trained exclusively on English data. Samples can be accessed at: https://palindromic-vc.github.io.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes a voice conversion framework using KNN search over WavLM representations to align non-parallel source and target speech for synthetic training.
AI.motivation: The authors aim to address the challenge of aligning non-parallel source and target speech for voice conversion without using explicit alignment or parallel corpora while enabling the framework to generalize across multiple languages.
AI.method: The proposed method uses K-Nearest Neighbors (KNN) retrieval over WavLM representations to construct synthetic training pairs. A speaker loss derived from a pretrained speaker verification model ensures consistent target-speaker identity.
AI.result: The framework achieves high naturalness and strong speaker similarity across multiple languages while outperforming competitive baselines, despite being trained solely on English data.
AI.conclusion: The method demonstrates the effectiveness of synthetic-to-real training for voice conversion, offering a scalable and multilingual-compatible solution without reliance on parallel corpora or explicit alignment.

title: Fourier Neural Operators with rank-1 lattice points and hyperbolic cross
summary: The \emph{Fourier neural operator} (FNO) is a neural network architecture that learns mappings between function spaces. Its efficient implementation is based on the multi-dimensional Fourier transform. By deriving general regularity bounds for the FNO with respect to both the spatial and parametric variables, we prove that the generalization error of the FNO can be improved by replacing spatial tensor product grids with purpose-built rank-1 lattice points, and by using a second lattice carefully constructed as training points in the parametric space. We achieve more accurate and efficient approximations from fewer network parameters, fewer spatial points, and fewer training samples. In addition, the architecture is simplified, because the high-dimensional Fourier transform on rank-1 lattices requires only a \emph{one-dimensional fast Fourier transform}, and we can use a \emph{hyperbolic cross} frequency index set with lattice points. We demonstrate the benefits of our \emph{lattice-based hyperbolic-cross FNOs} for an elliptic PDE on the torus.

AI.tldr: This paper enhances the Fourier neural operator (FNO) by employing specialized rank-1 lattice points and hyperbolic cross frequency index sets, resulting in improved efficiency and approximation accuracy for mappings between function spaces.
AI.motivation: The motivation is to improve the efficiency and approximation accuracy of the Fourier Neural Operator (FNO) by addressing its limitations in generalization error and computational complexity.
AI.method: The paper proposes replacing spatial tensor grids with rank-1 lattice points, utilizing a second lattice for parametric training points. It simplifies the high-dimensional Fourier transform by requiring only one-dimensional fast Fourier transforms with hyperbolic cross frequency sets.
AI.result: The proposed lattice-based hyperbolic-cross FNO achieves more accurate and efficient approximations with fewer parameters, spatial points, and training samples. It is demonstrated through an elliptic PDE example on a torus.
AI.conclusion: The findings suggest that the lattice-based modifications to the FNO enhance its generalization and computational efficiency, making it a more practical method for learning mappings between function spaces.

title: Few-shot Class-variable Incremental Audio Classification via Prototype Adaptation and Pseudo Class-variable Training
comment: This paper has been accepted for publication in Interspeech 2026. 4 Tables and 4 Figures
summary: In the task of few-shot class-incremental audio classification, the number of classes is assumed to always increase without considering the possibility of decrease. However, the number of classes generally increases or decreases in practice. In this paper, we investigate a problem of Few-shot Class-variable Incremental Audio Classification (FCIAC), in which the number of classes increases or decreases. We propose a FCIAC method using prototype adaptation and pseudo class-variable training. The model in our method consists of an encoder and a classifier. The classifier is initialized by a class-variable prototype adaptation network, whose structure dynamically changes with the change of classes. In addition, we design a pseudo class-variable training strategy to enhance the model's adaptability to changing classes. Experiments on three public datasets show that our method exceeds previous methods in average accuracy. The code is at: https://github.com/cgq2971-afk/FCIAC.

AI.tldr: This paper addresses the Few-shot Class-variable Incremental Audio Classification (FCIAC) problem, where class numbers can both increase and decrease, by proposing a novel method to handle such scenarios.
AI.motivation: Typical few-shot class-incremental audio classification assumes that the number of classes always increases, which does not align with real-world scenarios where classes can also decrease.
AI.method: The method introduces prototype adaptation via a class-variable prototype adaptation network and pseudo class-variable training to improve adaptability. The network structure dynamically updates based on the number of classes.
AI.result: Experiments on three public datasets demonstrate that this method outperforms previous approaches in terms of average accuracy.
AI.conclusion: The proposed method effectively addresses the variability in class changes, offering a practical solution for more dynamic audio classification tasks.

title: Hardening Agent Benchmarks with Adversarial Hacker-Fixer Loops
summary:
Agent benchmarks score submissions with outcome verifiers that are typically hand-written and brittle, leaving them open to reward hacking. We audit 1,968 tasks across five terminal-agent benchmarks and find 323 (16%) hackable by frontier models given only the task description. This corrupts both leaderboard rankings and RL training signal, yet the standard response is manual and reactive.
  We introduce the hacker-fixer loop, a method for building exploit-resistant verifiers without per-task manual patching. The loop alternates three LLM agents: a hacker tries to pass the verifier without solving the task, a fixer patches the verifier to reject each discovered exploit, and a solver confirms the patched verifier still admits legitimate solutions. The loop iterates: each patch reshapes what the verifier rewards, surfacing the next exploit. We further add verifier access, and let patches transfer across tasks, to broaden the exploits the loop discovers.
  On KernelBench, the loop drives the attack success rate from 62% to 0% on a held-out corpus of publicly reported exploits. We also find that weaker agents in the loop can defend against much stronger hackers: Gemini 3 Flash's loop drives the stronger Gemini 3.1 Pro and Claude Opus 4.7's attack success rate from 76% and 61% to 0% on KernelBench, and Gemini 3.1 Pro's from 39% to 17% on Terminal Bench across 77 tasks. We release Terminal Wrench (323 hackable environments, 3,632 hack trajectories) as a snapshot of the current attack surface, our patched verifiers, the exploits the loop discovered, and our implementation as a basis for future work.

AI.tldr: The paper addresses the vulnerabilities in agent benchmarks caused by reward hacking and proposes a novel solution called the hacker-fixer loop to mitigate these issues, demonstrating significant improvements across benchmarks.
AI.motivation: The motivation of this paper stems from the challenge of reward hacking in terminal-agent benchmarks, which undermines the reliability of leaderboard rankings and the reinforcement learning training signal.
AI.method: The proposed hacker-fixer loop involves three alternating LLM agents: a hacker to find exploits, a fixer to patch the verifier against those exploits, and a solver to ensure the verifier still admits legitimate solutions. Iterative cycles refine the verifier for exploit resistance.
AI.result: On KernelBench, the hacker-fixer loop reduces attack success rates from 62% to 0% on publicly reported exploits. Weaker agents in the loop defend successfully against stronger agents, showcasing robustness.
AI.conclusion: This paper successfully demonstrates the hacker-fixer loop as an innovative framework for improving the exploit resistance of verifiers in agent benchmarks. Terminal Wrench, a dataset and tool, is also released to facilitate further research.

title: Evaluating AI Investment Strategies
summary:
We study the problem of auditing a black-box algorithmic decision-maker from observable inputs and outputs alone. Our main result is an exact decomposition: under precisely characterized conditions, the cumulative \emph{regret} of a dynamic policy equals the sum of per-period covariances between the cost vector and the policy's decision. This extends the single-period identity of Aldridge~(2026) to the full multi-period setting of stochastic dynamic programming.
  We prove the identity holds exactly under i.i.d. costs and mean-unbiased Markov policies, derive closed-form bias corrections for non-stationary and time-varying cases, and establish the discounted-horizon analog. A Bellman recursion for the covariance regret functional connects the result to standard reinforcement learning algorithms; for rolling-window policies, the estimation-error bias is $O(d/w)$.
  The decomposition has direct implications for algorithmic auditing in strategic environments: in platform mechanism design, it provides a welfare-based audit metric without access to the agent's private type; in repeated games, covariance reduction is a sufficient condition for policy improvement; in procurement and ad auctions, the bias correction quantifies welfare loss from strategic misreporting. The associated trajectory estimator is consistent, asymptotically normal with HAC variance, and computable in $O(T \cdot nd)$ time. This makes the proposed approach a tractable, model-free audit tool for platform mechanisms, algorithmic portfolio strategies, and any sequential decision system subject to external performance review.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes a decomposition framework to audit black-box decision-making algorithms using observable inputs and outputs alone, connecting it to stochastic dynamic programming and reinforcement learning.
AI.motivation: The motivation is to create a tractable way to audit algorithmic decision-makers operating as black boxes, particularly in platform mechanisms, dynamic policy-making, and sequential decision systems, without requiring access to private agent data or internal mechanisms.
AI.method: The authors develop a decomposition formula linking cumulative regret and covariances of decision costs. They extend this into stochastic dynamic programming contexts, provide bias corrections for non-stationary policies, and leverage Bellman recursion to relate their results to existing reinforcement-learning frameworks.
AI.result: They present practical tools like a covariance-based audit metric that offers welfare quantifications and strategies for mitigating bias. They also deliver computationally efficient estimators consistent with theoretical guarantees.
AI.conclusion: The proposed decomposition approach and trajectory estimators provide a theoretically sound and computationally efficient way to audit black-box algorithms, offering applications in settings such as platform mechanism design and auctions.

title: A systematic investigation of molecular encoding methods for drug property predictions across neural network and Transformer encoder-based model
summary: Fundamental investigations into how different molecular encoding methods affect molecular property prediction remain relatively limited. In this study, we extensively examined the optimal molecular encoding methods for molecular properties prediction using two prevalent structure designs: a classical neural network model (MLP) and a Transformer encoder-based model (MLP+TL). For molecular encoding methods, we investigated several types of fingerprints, including traditional topological fingerprints, substructure-based fingerprints, and string-based representations. These two models were trained on seven well-known molecular datasets to evaluate different input molecular encoding methods based on evaluation metrics. On several biologically relevant classification tasks, including toxicity, mutagenicity, and side-effect prediction, our models consistently achieved average AUC values above 0.9. Rather than relying on external post-hoc explanation methods such as the local interpretable model-agnostic explanation (LIME) or the Deep SHapley Additive exPlanations (SHAP), we leveraged the model's intrinsic attention weights as an internal interpretability signal for identifying potentially important feature. The MLP+TL model using MACCS and PubChem as input can capture chemically interpretable groups that determined the major blood-brain barrier (BBB) permeability and mutagenicity in Salmonella typhimurium. In particular, a comparison between Morphine and Heroin highlighted the role of hydroxyl-related substructures in BBB permeability prediction, which was consistently reflected in the attention weights. Overall, our findings provide practical guidance for selecting effective molecular encoding methods and contribute to the development of interpretable molecular informatics approaches for drug discovery.

AI.tldr: The study evaluates the effectiveness of molecular encoding methods for molecular property predictions using neural and Transformer-based models, demonstrating high predictive accuracy and intrinsic interpretability in drug discovery applications.
AI.motivation: The work seeks to bridge the gap in understanding how different molecular encoding techniques influence the performance of predictive models for molecular properties.
AI.method: Two model architectures—MLP and MLP+Transformer—were employed to test various molecular encoding methods, such as fingerprints and string-based representations, on seven molecular datasets, focusing on biologically relevant classification tasks.
AI.result: The models achieved high predictive accuracy (average AUC > 0.9) and intrinsic interpretability, especially when using the MLP+Transformer model with MACCS and PubChem encodings. Attention weights helped identify chemically significant features like hydroxyl-related substructures.
AI.conclusion: This research offers insights into choosing effective molecular encodings and highlights the potential of attention-based interpretability in advancing drug discovery.

title: Families of Control-Cost-Parametrized Inverse-Optimal Universal Stabilizers
summary: A classical universal stabilization formula offers the practitioner no design freedom: it is a single, parameter-free object. We introduce a cost-parametrized family of stabilizing feedback laws, where (1) the user chooses a function that serves as the running cost on control in an inverse-optimal cost functional, and (2) obtains, through a formula, a nonlinear "expander" of a pre-existing universal controller, which solves an infinite-horizon optimal control problem with a meaningful cost on the state. The cost-to-expander formula is a three-step construction, involving, inter alia, cost differentiation and function inversion-overall, a nonlinear infinite-dimensional operator. The cost-to-expander operator is proven Lipschitz, which enables uniform neural operator approximation of the entire family and supports both offline performance exploration and online adaptation. Semiglobal practical asymptotic stability and second-order suboptimality bounds are established under the approximation. The operator learning and its use in semiglobal stabilization are illustrated numerically. We call the result 'half-direct-optimal' because the paper's design is less than a general 'direct optimal' (HJB-inducing) control, but more than the fully inverse optimal, since the user performs minimization for an arbitrary given cost on control. The dual to the half-direct problem we solve is the problem in which the cost on the state is arbitrary and given. This dual problem is easier and outside of the scope of the paper.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces a method to expand a universal control formula by incorporating user-chosen cost functions, enabling inverse-optimal control optimization, neural operator approximation, and practical stability.
AI.motivation: Traditional universal stabilization formulas lack design freedom for practitioners, creating a need for customizable and efficient control mechanisms.
AI.method: The paper proposes a three-step cost-to-expander operator that enables practitioners to design stabilization formulas by choosing running costs. The operator is Lipschitz and supports neural operator approximation for online and offline adaptability.
AI.result: The method achieves semiglobal practical asymptotic stability and suboptimality bounds under approximation, while illustrating efficiency through numerical experiments.
AI.conclusion: A novel 'half-direct-optimal' control design enables user customization, balances optimality with practicality, and advances stabilization techniques in control theory.

title: Alcmean's: Unsupervised community detection using local Laplacian, automatic detection of the number of centers
summary: Community detection is a fundamental problem in the analysis of complex networks. It has applications across social, biological, and financial domains. Traditional algorithms such as Louvain, LPA, and modularity optimization often require manual parameter tuning. They also suffer from inaccurate cluster center selection and struggle with scalability. To address these challenges, we propose Automatic Laplacian Centrality Means (ALCMeans), a novel community detection algorithm. ALCMeans combines Laplacian energy-based automatic center identification with DeepWalk embeddings for robust node representation. Unlike existing Laplacian-based and clustering methods, ALCMeans eliminates the need to predefine the number of communities, enhances cluster center selection using structural importance, and leverages representation learning for more accurate and stable assignments. Experimental results on benchmark datasets demonstrate 10 to 20 percent higher NMI and ARI scores compared to Louvain, Newman-Girvan, LPA, Fast-Greedy, and a recent GNN-based competitor (MAGI, KDD 2024). Additional evaluations with modularity and F1-scores confirm the superiority of ALCMeans. Ablation studies highlight the critical contributions of each component. Despite its reliance on DeepWalk parameters and increased runtime relative to lightweight heuristics, ALCMeans consistently outperforms state-of-the-art methods. This makes it a promising tool for real-world network analysis.

AI.tldr: ALCMeans, a new algorithm for community detection in networks, improves node representation and cluster selection while avoiding predefined community numbers, outscoring state-of-the-art methods by 10-20% in benchmark tests.
AI.motivation: Traditional community detection methods suffer from manual parameter tuning, inaccurate cluster center selection, and scalability issues.
AI.method: ALCMeans integrates Laplacian energy-based center identification and DeepWalk embeddings to enhance community detection accuracy and stability.
AI.result: ALCMeans achieves higher NMI and ARI scores compared to Louvain and other leading methods and demonstrates superiority through modularity and F1 tests.
AI.conclusion: ALCMeans is a robust and accurate algorithm for community detection, overcoming key limitations of traditional methods and proving effective in real-world applications.

title: Multi-View Speech Representation Learning for Parkinson's Disease Detection Using Context-guided Cross-modal Attention
summary: Parkinson's disease (PD) is a progressive neurodegenerative disorder that frequently causes speech impairments associated with hypokinetic dysarthria. As speech production relies on the precise coordination of complex neuromuscular mechanisms, speech analysis has emerged as a promising non-invasive and cost-effective biomarker for early PD detection. Recent deep learning approaches have shown encouraging results; however, most existing methods rely on a single speech representation, potentially overlooking complementary pathological information encoded across different feature spaces. In this work, we propose a multi-branch deep learning framework for automatic PD detection from speech. Each recording is segmented into 5-second chunks and represented using three complementary modalities: Log-Mel spectrograms, MFCCs, and HuBERT embeddings extracted from raw waveforms. The spectrograms are processed using a pre-trained ResNet-18 encoder, MFCC sequences are modeled through a BiLSTM network, and raw speech is encoded using a pre-trained HuBERT model. To effectively integrate these heterogeneous representations, we introduce a context-guided cross-modal attention mechanism that dynamically weights temporal HuBERT embeddings according to the global acoustic context derived from the spectrogram and MFCC branches. Experiments conducted on the publicly available Spanish PC-GITA corpus under strict speaker-independent 5-fold cross-validation demonstrate the effectiveness of the proposed approach. The proposed architecture achieves an accuracy of 91.51%, an F1-score of 91.24%, and an AUC of 95.97%. Furthermore, ablation studies confirm the contribution of both the proposed context-guided cross-modal attention mechanism and the integration of complementary speech representations. These findings highlight the potential of heterogeneous speech modeling for robust and clinically reliable PD detection.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces a multi-branch deep learning framework for Parkinson's disease detection from speech using complementary speech representations and a context-guided cross-modal attention mechanism, achieving high performance on a benchmark dataset.
AI.motivation: The study addresses the need for early Parkinson's disease detection by leveraging speech analysis as a non-invasive and cost-effective biomarker, improving upon limitations in current single-feature methods.
AI.method: The authors propose a framework where speech recordings are segmented into 5-second chunks and analyzed using three modalities: Log-Mel spectrograms with ResNet-18, MFCCs with BiLSTM, and HuBERT embeddings. A context-guided cross-modal attention mechanism integrates these features.
AI.result: Experiments on the Spanish PC-GITA corpus show a high performance with 91.51% accuracy, 91.24% F1-score, and 95.97% AUC. Ablation studies validate the importance of the proposed attention mechanism and multi-modal integration.
AI.conclusion: The proposed deep learning-based framework demonstrates the potential of combining heterogeneous speech representations for accurate and clinically reliable detection of Parkinson's disease.

title: Conan-embedding-v3: Fusing Modality-Specific Models for Omni-Modal Embedding
summary: Omni-modal retrieval promises a single embedding space for text, image, video, document, and audio inputs, but building such a unified retriever is difficult since these modalities differ in data distribution, architecture, and optimization dynamics. In this work, we present Conan-embedding-v3, a decouple--fuse--recover framework for omni-modal retrieval. Conan-embedding-v3 first trains modality specialists independently and fuses their task vectors into a single dense backbone, a strategy we call Decoupled Specialist Fusion. We show that this fusion composes visual, video, and document retrieval capabilities, but also exposes a failure mode for projector-based modalities: when audio is attached through an external encoder and projector, fusing the backbone leaves the projector calibrated to the audio-specialist backbone, causing a large audio retrieval regression despite copying all audio-specific modules unchanged. We call this failure Projector Drift. To repair it, Conan-embedding-v3 applies Projector Recovery (i.e., full-parameter fine-tuning of the projector while keeping the backbone frozen) followed by balanced multi-modal rehearsal. The resulting model supports these retrieval pathways in one backbone, achieving 74.9 scores on MMEB while obtaining 55.61 on the 30-task MAEB audio suite.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces Conan-embedding-v3, a unified framework for omni-modal retrieval across various data types like text, image, video, document, and audio.
AI.motivation: To address the challenges of creating a unified embedding space for multiple modalities that have differing data distributions, architectures, and optimization requirements.
AI.method: The approach involves a decouple--fuse--recover strategy: training modality-specific specialists independently, fusing their vectors into a unified backbone, addressing failure modes like "Projector Drift," and applying fine-tuning and multi-modal balancing techniques.
AI.result: Conan-embedding-v3 achieves strong benchmark scores: 74.9 on MMEB and 55.61 on the 30-task MAEB audio suite, demonstrating its versatile retrieval capabilities in a single backbone.
AI.conclusion: The proposed framework successfully integrates multiple modalities for omni-modal retrieval while resolving specific issues like Projector Drift through effective fine-tuning strategies.

title: Report on CHIIR 2026 Workshop on Generative AI and Academic Search (GAI&AS)
summary: This report summarizes the CHIIR 2026 Workshop on Generative AI and Academic Search (GAI\&AS), which examined how GenAI is reshaping academic search systems and research practices. The workshop brought together researchers in human information interaction and information retrieval to explore key challenges and opportunities in designing and evaluating future academic search systems that integrate GenAI, moving beyond traditional document retrieval to support summarization, recommendation, synthesis, and conversational interaction. Participants' interests and discussions focused on three thematic clusters: foundations and principles, applications and opportunities, and search-as-learning. Across these themes, the workshop highlighted the importance of academic search systems in supporting transparency, credibility, research integrity, and long-term scholarly needs, as well as in fostering higher-order cognitive processes. Participants discussed guiding theories, design principles, methodological approaches, partnerships, and community-building efforts aimed at advancing human-centered GenAI-enhanced academic search systems. Overall, the workshop demonstrated strong community interest and a diverse range of ongoing and emerging research initiatives at the intersection of GenAI and academic search.

AI.tldr: The CHIIR 2026 Workshop on GenAI and Academic Search explored integrating GenAI into academic search systems, covering themes like design principles, research integrity, and fostering cognitive processes.
AI.motivation: To investigate how Generative AI is transforming academic search systems and research practices.
AI.method: The workshop facilitated discussions on challenges, opportunities, and theoretical/methodological advancements in integrating GenAI for academic search, organized around thematic clusters.
AI.result: The workshop identified significant interest and ongoing initiatives in designing human-centered GenAI-enhanced academic search systems.
AI.conclusion: The integration of GenAI into academic search systems can enhance transparency, credibility, and cognitive processes, and foster community-driven advancements.

title: Now You (Still) See Me: Detecting Evasive Steganographic Payloads in LLMs
summary: Large language models can be fine-tuned to encode prompt-borne secrets into fluent, seemingly benign outputs. This creates a steganographic exfiltration risk that is difficult to detect with output-level steganalysis. Recent work proposes mechanistic detection using linear probes that recover the secret from internal activations. We show that this defense can be systematically evaded, but that detectability can be recovered through a targeted data-level intervention. First, we extend the detection setup to include a non-linear MLP probe. We then adversarially fine-tune steganographic trojans across five base models: Qwen3-8B, Llama-3.1-8B, Ministral-8B, Qwen3-14B, and Phi-4-14B. The resulting models retain $58$--$79\%$ exact-match secret recovery while evading both ridge and held-out MLP probes, with $1$--$8\%$ average capability degradation across six benchmarks. We then give an information-theoretic characterization of this evasion. Successful evasion preserves recoverability while reducing low-order extractability of the secret from the content-aligned representation, forcing the payload into synergistic interaction with residual degrees of freedom. This motivates a recontextualization dataset that restricts these residual degrees of freedom. On this distribution, both ridge and MLP detectability are restored across all five evasive trojans. Overall, our findings show that activation-based steganography detection is vulnerable to adaptive evasion, but also that theory-guided evaluation distributions can expose otherwise hidden payloads.

AI.tldr: The paper addresses the challenge of steganographic exfiltration in large language models by showing that it is possible to evade current mechanistic detection methods, but this can be mitigated using a targeted data-level intervention.
AI.motivation: The motivation is to address the security risk posed by steganographic techniques that encode secrets within language models, which existing detection techniques struggle to identify.
AI.method: The authors propose extending linear probe setups with non-linear MLP probes, adversarially fine-tuning steganographic trojans, conducting an information-theoretic analysis of evasion, and introducing a recontextualization dataset to counteract evasive tactics.
AI.result: They demonstrate that their adversarially fine-tuned models can evade detection with high levels of exact-match secret recovery, but also that the proposed dataset intervention restores detectability for both ridge and MLP probes.
AI.conclusion: Activation-based detection methods are vulnerable to evasion, but carefully designed evaluation distributions can counter these vulnerabilities and uncover hidden payloads effectively.

title: Sustainability and Artificial Intelligence: Necessary, Challenging, and Promising Intersections
comment: This is an author preprint version. For the final authenticated version of record, please use the official publication via the IEEE Xplore database. DOI: 10.1109/MSIEID52046.2020.00076
summary: Both digital economy and digital technology researchers increasingly recognize the need to better address the role that artificial intelligence (AI) plays in shaping the evolution of the environmental, social and governance aspects of development. It appears that sustainability and AI research converge on the features of wicked problems that are complex, interconnected and dynamic. Building off such convergence, this article aims to map out the necessary, challenging, and promising intersections by providing an overview of the state of art research. Based on 541 bibliographic data collected from the Web of Science (WoS) database, the findings reveal the increasingly central body of work on green and sustainable science and technology in bridging various disciplines, main journals and key topics and concepts. The findings reveal how such interactions can be necessary, challenging, and promising. The article concludes with few general arguments regarding how to diversify and expand the community of practice regarding AI for sustainable development, especially in the areas of expected AI application areas and institutions.

AI.tldr: The paper maps the intersection of AI and sustainability research, highlighting its current state, challenges, and future opportunities based on bibliographic analysis.
AI.motivation: The paper is motivated by the need to understand how AI influences the environmental, social, and governance aspects of development in light of sustainability challenges.
AI.method: The study uses bibliographic analysis of 541 articles from the Web of Science (WoS) database to explore AI's role in sustainable development research.
AI.result: The findings identify a central body of work focused on green and sustainable science, as well as key journals and concepts bridging disciplines. It highlights necessary, challenging, and promising aspects of AI-sustainability interactions.
AI.conclusion: The paper argues for diversifying and expanding practices in AI for sustainable development, especially in application areas and institutional support.

title: Automating the Expert Eye: A System-Agnostic Deep Learning Framework for Rare Event Discovery in Imbalanced Force Spectroscopy
summary: Single-Molecule Force Spectroscopy (SMFS) provides unprecedented insights into biomolecular mechanics, yet the high-throughput generation of force-extension trajectories creates a severe data curation bottleneck. Identifying rare molecular unbinding events within thousands of noise-dominated curves traditionally relies on tedious, non-scalable manual auditing. Here, we present a system-agnostic, interpretable deep learning framework tailored to overcome extreme class imbalance in automated SMFS triage. Utilizing 1D-to-2D rasterized geometric matrices, we deployed a modified ResNet18 architecture governed by an asymmetric Focal Loss objective function. We evaluated this framework on the complex mechanical unfolding pathways of the R. champanellensis cellulosome. Under hyper-imbalanced test conditions where the target interaction constituted only 1.34% of the dataset (13 true events out of 970 traces), the model achieved an overall accuracy of 0.9196 and a remarkable True Positive Rate (Recall) of 0.9231. By implementing an empirically calibrated dual-threshold triage system, the pipeline automatically discarded 880 unambiguous background noise traces , reducing the manual curation workload by over 90% while safely preserving high-value rare data. Finally, Gradient-weighted Class Activation Mapping (Grad-CAM) visually validated that the network's decisions are firmly anchored in the relevant geometric features of the force curves, specifically localizing on the structural unbinding regions, effectively mitigating 'black-box' skepticism. Built for free cloud-based execution, this open-source tool democratizes scalable, highly precise molecular discovery across the biophysics community.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces an interpretable deep-learning framework to automate the identification of rare molecular unbinding events in Single-Molecule Force Spectroscopy data, reducing manual auditing by over 90%.
AI.motivation: The study aims to address the severe bottleneck in curating vast amounts of noisy force-extension trajectory data produced by SMFS experiments, particularly when rare molecular interactions are involved.
AI.method: A modified ResNet18 model with asymmetric Focal Loss was trained on 1D-to-2D rasterized geometric matrices derived from SMFS data. A dual-threshold system was used for triage, complemented by Grad-CAM visualization for interpretability.
AI.result: The model achieved high accuracy (0.9196) and recall (0.9231) under extreme class imbalance conditions. The curated pipeline reduced manual oversight by over 90% while preserving rare, high-value events.
AI.conclusion: The open-source deep-learning tool is highly impactful for the biophysics field, democratizing scalable and precise molecular discovery while enhancing confidence through interpretability measures like Grad-CAM.

title: TLDR: Compressing Audio Tokens for Efficient Autoregressive Text-to-Speech
summary: Codec-based autoregressive (AR) speech language models have achieved strong text-to-speech (TTS) quality by modeling speech as sequences of discrete audio tokens with large pretrained backbones. However, this token-level formulation creates a structural efficiency bottleneck: speech-token sequences are much longer than text sequences, requiring the AR backbone to perform causal computation at every token position and maintain a KV cache that grows with the sequence length. We introduce TLDR, a patch-based autoregressive framework that accelerates codec-based AR-TTS by shifting the causal modeling from token-level speech sequences to patch-level sequences. TLDR groups consecutive codec tokens into compact latent patches using a lightweight compressor, models the resulting shorter patch sequence with a frozen pretrained AR-TTS backbone adapted by LoRA, and reconstructs fine-grained speech tokens within each patch using a speaker-conditioned extractor. With a patch size of 4, TLDR achieves a 1.8x inference speedup over the baseline AR-TTS model and reduces global KV-cache memory by up to 75%. Experimental results indicate that patch-level global causal modeling can be a practical way to reduce the inference cost of pretrained codec-based AR-TTS systems without replacing the existing modules.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces TLDR, a patch-based autoregressive framework that improves the efficiency of codec-based text-to-speech (TTS) by grouping audio tokens into compact patches.
AI.motivation: The motivation is to address the inefficiency of codec-based AR speech models, where token-level speech sequences are too long, leading to computational bottlenecks and excessive memory usage.
AI.method: The method involves grouping codec tokens into patches using a lightweight compressor, utilizing a pretrained AR-TTS backbone adapted by LoRA to model these compact patches, and reconstructing detailed speech tokens through a speaker-conditioned extractor.
AI.result: With a patch size of 4, TLDR achieves a 1.8x inference speedup and reduces KV-cache memory usage by 75%, demonstrating improved efficiency without sacrificing model quality.
AI.conclusion: The study demonstrates that patch-level global causal modeling is an effective and practical way to reduce inference costs in codec-based AR-TTS systems while preserving performance and utilizing existing modules.

title: Integrating gene regulatory priors into Transformer attention with scTransformer for interpretable scRNA-seq analysis
summary: Motivation: Transformer-based models are increasingly applied to large-scale single-cell transcriptomics, showing strong performance through self-supervised learning on millions of cells. However, most existing approaches treat genes as independent features, and largely ignore prior biological knowledge, which limits interpretability and robustness. In this paper, we explore whether explicitly incorporating gene regulatory information can improve both model performance and biological insight. Results: We present scTransformer, the first Transformer-based approach that builds a priori knowledge of biological mechanisms into the model's attention patterns. By constraining information flow according to known regulatory structures, the model learns representations that are more biologically meaningful. We evaluate scTransformer on a disease-relevant single-nucleus RNA-seq dataset using supervised cell-type classification. Compared to standard Transformers, our approach improves classification accuracy, enhances separation of cell types in embedding space, and produces attention patterns consistent with known regulatory programs. Overall, our results demonstrate that embedding biological structure into Transformer models can enhance interpretability without sacrificing performance, offering a principled step toward biologically grounded foundation models for single-cell omics.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces scTransformer, a Transformer-based model incorporating gene regulatory information to improve performance and interpretability in single-cell transcriptomics.
AI.motivation: Existing Transformer models for single-cell transcriptomics fail to incorporate prior biological knowledge, limiting their interpretability and robustness.
AI.method: The study integrates gene regulatory structures into the attention mechanism of a Transformer model, thereby influencing information flow based on biological mechanisms.
AI.result: scTransformer outperforms standard Transformers in classification accuracy, embedding space cell-type separation, and generates biologically relevant attention patterns.
AI.conclusion: Adding gene regulatory structure to Transformer models enhances biological interpretability and maintains high performance for single-cell omics data analysis.

title: BareWave: Waveform-Native Flow-Matching Text-to-Speech
summary: Removing intermediate representations and separately trained decoding stages has become an important direction in generative modeling. In text-to-speech, however, high-quality systems are still commonly built through an intermediate acoustic representation before waveform synthesis. In this work, we present BareWave, a fully waveform-native framework for direct text-to-wave generation in flow-matching TTS. We consider this setting to raise three training challenges: raw-waveform modeling lacks a strong pretrained representational scaffold, different stages of training benefit from different noise schedules, and data-space perceptual objectives do not automatically share the temporal structure of the velocity-space flow objective. As a result, direct waveform training is hard to optimize efficiently, hard to push toward a strong final operating point with a fixed recipe, and hard to integrate effective perceptual refinement. Guided by this view, we develop a direct text-to-wave training framework that combines training-time representation alignment, staged noise scheduling, and velocity-aware perceptual alignment (VAPA), while preserving a single waveform-native inference path without pretrained components at test time. Experiments on zero-shot voice cloning show that strong intelligibility, speaker similarity, and naturalness can be achieved under a fully waveform-native inference path, supporting waveform-native flow-matching TTS as a practical direction. Project page with audio demos is available at https://barewave.github.io/.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces BareWave, a text-to-wave TTS framework that eliminates intermediate acoustic steps, using novel techniques to overcome waveform training challenges.
AI.motivation: Most TTS systems rely on intermediate acoustic representations; this research aims to create a more direct and efficient text-to-waveform model with high-quality results.
AI.method: Develops BareWave using training-time representation alignment, staged noise scheduling, and velocity-aware perceptual alignment (VAPA) to train a direct text-to-wave system.
AI.result: Achieves strong intelligibility, speaker similarity, and naturalness in zero-shot voice cloning with a fully waveform-native process.
AI.conclusion: Waveform-native flow-matching TTS is shown to be viable and practical for high-quality synthesis, eliminating the need for intermediate stages.

title: What the Eyes See, the LLMs Miss: Exploiting Human Perception for Adversarial Text Attacks
comment: This work has been accepted for publication at USENIX Security 2026. This paper includes examples of harmful, hateful, or abusive language for research purposes. Reader discretion is advised
summary: Large language model (LLM)-powered content moderation systems have become a critical defense against harmful online content. However, these systems primarily operate on tokenized text and largely ignore the visual cues that humans naturally rely on when interpreting content. We show that this discrepancy creates a fundamental perceptual mismatch: content that is readily recognized as harmful by humans can become effectively invisible to automated moderation systems. To study this vulnerability, we introduce a class of Human-Perceptible Adversarial Attacks (HPAA), in which harmful expressions are embedded into otherwise benign text through visually salient typographic manipulations. Our key insight is that typographic features, including spacing, visual emphasis, and spatial arrangement, can be strategically combined to preserve human recognition of harmful content while substantially reducing machine detectability. Operating in black-box settings with only a small query budget, our attack automatically generates evasive content without requiring model access or gradient information. We evaluate the attack across multiple datasets and ten deployed moderation systems, including commercial APIs and state-of-the-art open-source guardrails. Results reveal a striking gap between human and machine perception: with only three detector queries, generated attacks achieve over 86\% human recognition while maintaining detection rates below 1\% across the evaluated systems. We further conduct ablation studies to identify the typographic factors driving successful evasion, analyze why current moderation architectures fail to capture these signals, and discuss practical defenses. Our findings expose a fundamental blind spot in today's LLM-based moderation ecosystem and highlight need for moderation systems that reason about content in a manner more consistent with human perceptual understanding.

AI.tldr: The paper reveals a significant vulnerability in LLM-based content moderation systems: visually manipulated text can evade detection by machines but remain harmful and recognizable to humans.
AI.motivation: The motivation is to address the perceptual mismatch in LLM-based content moderation systems, which fail to account for visual cues humans use to detect harmful content.
AI.method: The authors propose Human-Perceptible Adversarial Attacks (HPAA), which leverage typographic manipulations (e.g., spacing, emphasis, arrangement) to create harmful content that evades detection. They evaluate these attacks across multiple datasets and content moderation systems.
AI.result: The attacks achieve over 86% human recognition while maintaining below 1% detection by machines, exposing the flaws in the current moderation approaches.
AI.conclusion: The paper concludes that LLM-based moderation systems have a critical blind spot in detecting visually manipulated harmful content, emphasizing the need for systems that better align with human perceptual interpretation.

title: Context-Fractured Decomposition Attacks on Tool-Using LLM Agents: Exploiting Artifact Provenance Gaps
summary: Tool-using LLM agents interact with the world through actions that persist state in artifacts (e.g., workspace files or logs). Consequently, jailbreak defenses must reason about cross-step composition rather than isolated text. Yet most existing attacks and defenses, including ``multi-turn'' jailbreaks such as Crescendo and Tree of Attacks,still assume a single contiguous conversation visible to the defender. This assumption breaks down in real agent pipelines, where enforcement is fragmented across tools, modules, and time, and where artifact provenance is often not tracked. We operationalize a deployment failure mode for tool-using LLM agents, the \emph{provenance gap}, and study reproducible triggers for it: \emph{Context-Fractured Decomposition} (CFD), a family of cross-context multi-step jailbreaks that preserve benign-looking intermediate artifacts from an early interaction and elicit harmful behavior much later, potentially in a different agent instance or workflow stage, via individually innocuous tool actions whose risk emerges only under delayed artifact-mediated composition. We instrument the failure mode with trace-level diagnostics and outline a verifiable mitigation direction (provenance lineage tagging). Across agent-system jailbreak benchmarks, CFD improves success rates by up to 28.3 percentage points over state-of-the-art baselines, even against strong single-turn judges. Disclaimer: This paper contains examples of harmful or offensive language.

AI.tldr: The paper identifies a novel vulnerability in tool-using LLM agents called the 'provenance gap,' where multi-step attacks exploit the fragmented nature of state storage across tools and interactions, and proposes a partial solution through provenance lineage tagging.
AI.motivation: To address the overlooked deployment risk in tool-using LLM agents caused by fragmented state enforcement, where existing defenses fail to consider multi-step, cross-context interactions leading to harmful behavior.
AI.method: The authors introduce 'Context-Fractured Decomposition' (CFD), a category of cross-context multi-step jailbreak methodologies, and deploy trace-level diagnostics to study its impact. They also propose provenance lineage tagging as a partial mitigation strategy.
AI.result: Experiments highlight the potency of CFD attacks, which significantly increase jailbreak success rates by up to 28.3% over existing attack baselines, even against strong evaluation systems.
AI.conclusion: Tool-using LLM agents are vulnerable to cross-context multi-step attacks like CFD, and current defenses are inadequate. Provenance lineage tagging is a promising step toward mitigating these vulnerabilities, provided it is implemented effectively.

title: Adaptive directional gradients for parameterised quantum circuits
